topics.title: DC and AC Circuit Analysis with Dependent and Independent Sources Applying Superposition Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DC and AC Circuit Analysis with Dependent and Independent Sources Applying Superposition Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Independent Sources — Formal Definition

An **independent source** is an idealised energy-providing element whose voltage or current value is fixed by the source itself and is **not governed by any other variable (voltage or current) elsewhere in the circuit**. It is the "self-deciding" energy source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT417, Module 1):**
> Independent sources deliver prescribed values of voltage (Independent Voltage Source – IVS) or current (Independent Current Source – ICS) regardless of the network they are connected to.

#### 1.1.1 Ideal Independent Voltage Source (IVS)

A two-terminal element that maintains a **fixed terminal voltage** $v(t) = V_s$ across its terminals irrespective of the current drawn by the external load.

- For DC: $v(t) = V_s$ (constant)
- For AC (sinusoidal steady state): $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$
- Phasor representation: $\mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi$

**Internal impedance:** $Z_s = 0 \, \Omega$ (ideal).

#### 1.1.2 Ideal Independent Current Source (ICS)

A two-terminal element that delivers a **fixed current** $i(t) = I_s$ into the connected network, irrespective of the voltage developed across it.

- For DC: $i(t) = I_s$ (constant)
- For AC: $i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$
- Phasor: $\mathbf{I} = I_m \angle \phi$

**Internal impedance:** $Z_s \to \infty \, \Omega$ (ideal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reality:** Real sources have a small series resistance (voltage source) or a large parallel resistance (current source), but the KTU module treats them as **ideal** unless stated otherwise.

### 1.2 Dependent (Controlled) Sources — Formal Definition

A **dependent source** is an energy-providing element whose output voltage or current is **controlled by a voltage or current existing somewhere else in the same circuit**. The control variable and the output variable are linked through a **controlling constant** (gain coefficient).

The controlling constant has units that make the product dimensionally consistent:

- Voltage gain: $\text{V/V}$ (dimensionless)
- Current gain: $\text{A/A}$ (dimensionless)
- Transresistance: $\text{V/A} = \Omega$
- Transconductance: $\text{A/V} = \text{S}$ (Siemens)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Canonical Types of Dependent Sources (Master this table for KTU):**

| Type | Abbreviation | Controlling Variable | Output | Controlling Constant Unit |
|------|--------------|----------------------|--------|---------------------------|
| Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source | VCVS | $V_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $V_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{V/V}$ (dimensionless) |
| Voltage-Controlled Current Source | VCCS | $V_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $I_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{A/V} = \text{S}$ |
| Current-Controlled Voltage Source | CCVS | $I_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $V_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{V/A} = \Omega$ |
| Current-Controlled Current Source | CCCS | $I_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $I_{\text{out}}$ | $\text{A/A}$ (dimensionless) |

### 1.3 Superposition Principle — Formal Definition

The **Superposition Theorem** states that in any **linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources**, the response (voltage across or current through any element) is the **algebraic sum of the responses produced by each independent source acting alone, with all other independent sources replaced by their internal impedances** (voltage source → short circuit; current source → open circuit).

**Mathematical Statement:**

For a linear network with $n$ independent sources $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$, the response $y$ at any point in the network is:

$$y = k_1 x_1 + k_2 x_2 + \cdots + k_n x_n = \sum_{j=1}^{n} k_j x_j$$

where $k_j$ is the proportionality constant relating the response to source $x_j$.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Team of Lamps" Illumination Problem:**
> Imagine a dark room lit by **two independent lamps**, Lamp A (brightness controlled by a fixed switch) and Lamp B (also a fixed switch). The brightness at any point on the wall depends on the contribution of *both* lamps. **Superposition** says: "Don't try to compute the combined brightness directly — first, compute the brightness from Lamp A alone (with Lamp B switched off), then compute the brightness from Lamp B alone (with Lamp A switched off), and finally add the two brightnesses." 
> 
> The **room's walls (the linear medium)** allow you to add contributions. In a non-linear room (e.g., one with a smart-glass panel that darkens with cumulative light), this addition would fail — that is why superposition **only works for linear circuits**.
> 
> **Dependent sources**, in this analogy, are like *smart lamps* whose brightness automatically adjusts based on how much light another part of the room is receiving — you must keep them **active** in every sub-problem because they are *part of the circuit's structure*, not standalone energy inputs.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values used throughout PERUT417 Module 1:**
> - Power frequency (India): $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$, hence $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159 \, \text{rad/s}$
> - Phase sequence: R-Y-B (positive)
> - Standard DC test voltage: typically $V \leq 230 \, \text{V}$ for lab modules
> - Source impedance convention: voltage source in series with $R_s$; current source in parallel with $R_s$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Applicability of Superposition

The superposition principle is **only valid** when all the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **Linearity** — The circuit must obey the homogeneity and additivity properties. All elements must be linear (resistors, capacitors, inductors under small-signal, ideal transformers, etc.).
2. **Bilateral nature** — Elements conduct equally in both directions (resistors qualify; diodes do not).
3. **Multiple independent sources** — There must be at least two independent sources (or one independent + dependent).
4. **Dependent sources remain unaltered** — They are **never** switched off; their controlling variables change with the sub-circuit being solved.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often "turn off" dependent sources. **This is incorrect.** Dependent sources are part of the network's internal model (representing transistor gain, op-amp transfer, etc.) and must remain active in every sub-problem; only their controlling variable naturally takes the new sub-circuit value.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for Superposition

**Step 1 — Identify all independent sources.** List every independent voltage source $V_{s1}, V_{s2}, \ldots$ and independent current source $I_{s1}, I_{s2}, \ldots$.

**Step 2 — Deactivate all but one source.**
- Replace each independent **voltage source** with its internal impedance → a **short circuit** (for ideal sources, $R_s = 0$).
- Replace each independent **current source** with its internal impedance → an **open circuit** (for ideal sources, $R_s = \infty$).

**Step 3 — Keep dependent sources active.** Do not alter dependent sources; let their controlling variables assume values dictated by the new sub-circuit.

**Step 4 — Solve the sub-circuit.** Use mesh analysis, nodal analysis, source transformation, or Thévenin/Norton equivalents to find the contribution of the *active* source to the desired response (voltage or current).

**Step 5 — Repeat for every independent source.** Each independent source gets its own "deactivated-others" sub-problem.

**Step 6 — Algebraic summation.** Sum all partial responses (with their signs) to obtain the total response.

### 2.3 Application to AC Circuits (Phasor Domain)

For AC circuits operating in **sinusoidal steady state**, superposition is applied in the **phasor (frequency) domain**:

- All voltages and currents become complex phasors $\mathbf{V}, \mathbf{I}$.
- All impedances become frequency-dependent: $Z_R = R$, $Z_L = j\omega L$, $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$.
- The summation of partial responses is **complex addition** of phasors.
- After summing, convert back to time domain if required: $v(t) = \sqrt{2} \, V_{\text{rms}} \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does superposition still work in the frequency domain?** Because converting differential equations to algebraic phasor equations preserves linearity. If $y(t)$ is linear in $x(t)$, then $\mathbf{Y}$ is linear in $\mathbf{X}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in 80%+ of KTU Module 1 superposition questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Ohm's Law (Phasor) | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} Z$ | $\text{V}$, where $Z$ is complex impedance $\Omega$ |
| 2 | Resistor Impedance | $Z_R = R$ | $\Omega$ (real) |
| 3 | Inductor Impedance | $Z_L = j\omega L$ | $\Omega$ (purely imaginary, $+90°$) |
| 4 | Capacitor Impedance | $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$ | $\Omega$ (purely imaginary, $-90°$) |
| 5 | VCVS Output | $V_{\text{out}} = \mu V_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $\mu$ dimensionless |
| 6 | VCCS Output | $I_{\text{out}} = g_m V_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $g_m$ in Siemens (S) |
| 7 | CCVS Output | $V_{\text{out}} = r_m I_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $r_m$ in Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | CCCS Output | $I_{\text{out}} = \beta I_{\text{ctrl}}$ | $\beta$ dimensionless |
| 9 | Voltage Source "Off" | Replace by **short circuit** ($V = 0$) | $R_{\text{int}} = 0$ |
| 10 | Current Source "Off" | Replace by **open circuit** ($I = 0$) | $R_{\text{int}} = \infty$ |
| 11 | Superposition (Multi-source) | $y_{\text{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} y_k$ | Each $y_k$ is response from source $k$ alone |
| 12 | Nodal Analysis (Phasor) | $\displaystyle\sum \frac{\mathbf{V}_n - \mathbf{V}_k}{Z_{nk}} = \mathbf{I}_n$ | KCL at node $n$ |
| 13 | Mesh Analysis (Phasor) | $\displaystyle\sum Z_m \mathbf{I}_m = \mathbf{V}_s$ | KVL around mesh $m$ |
| 14 | Power (Phasor Domain) | $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{I}^*$ | $\mathbf{S} = P + jQ$ (VA) |
| 15 | Source Transformation | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot Z$ | Equivalence between Thèvenin and Norton |
| 16 | Voltage Divider (Phasor) | $\mathbf{V}_x = \mathbf{V}_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_x}{Z_1 + Z_2 + \cdots}$ | Only when no loading |
| 17 | Current Divider (Phasor) | $\mathbf{I}_x = \mathbf{I}_s \cdot \dfrac{Z_{\text{total-other}}}{Z_{\text{total}}}$ | For parallel branches |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where superposition is used in industry:**
> 
> 1. **Small-signal AC analysis of transistor amplifiers** — The DC operating point is solved first (with all AC sources zeroed), then the AC small-signal response is computed by zeroing the DC sources and re-activating AC sources. This is exactly superposition.
> 
> 2. **Power system fault analysis** — Symmetrical components decompose unbalanced faults into positive/negative/zero sequence networks, each solved independently and superimposed.
> 
> 3. **Antenna array design** — Total radiated field is the vector sum of fields from each radiator.
> 
> 4. **Operational amplifier circuits** — Inverting and non-inverting input contributions are computed separately and added at the output.
> 
> 5. **Harmonic analysis in power electronics** — Each harmonic of a non-sinusoidal waveform is treated as an independent source.

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Superposition

Mathematically, the linear network's response $y(t)$ is governed by a **linear differential equation** with constant coefficients:

$$a_n \frac{d^n y}{dt^n} + \cdots + a_1 \frac{dy}{dt} + a_0 y = b_m \frac{d^m x_1}{dt^m} + \cdots + b_m \frac{d^m x_2}{dt^m} + \cdots$$

By the **linearity property of differential equations**, the total solution is the sum of solutions to each forcing function treated individually. In the **phasor domain**, the differential equation collapses to a linear algebraic equation where superposition is a direct consequence of matrix linearity: $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$, with $\mathbf{H}$ a linear operator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Illustrative Derivations of Superposition on Canonical Topologies

We will work through **three exhaustive worked problems** — one purely resistive DC with two sources, one AC phasor with R-L-C, and one with a **dependent source**.

---

#### **Worked Problem 1 — DC Resistive Network with Two Independent Sources**

**Problem Statement (Canonical KTU Module 1 example):**

Consider the following circuit:

- A $12 \, \text{V}$ DC voltage source in series with $R_1 = 4 \, \Omega$, connected to a parallel combination of:
- $R_2 = 6 \, \Omega$ branch,
- $R_3 = 3 \, \Omega$ branch in series with a $2 \, \text{A}$ DC current source.

Find the current through $R_2$ using superposition.

**Circuit Topology (textual):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Node A} &\xrightarrow{R_1 = 4\,\Omega} \text{Node B} \\
12\,\text{V source} &\;\;|\;\;R_2 = 6\,\Omega \;\;|\;\;R_3 = 3\,\Omega + 2\,\text{A source (current flows into Node B)} 
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reference direction:** Positive current in $R_2$ flows from Node B to ground.

---

**Step 1: Activate the 12 V source alone. Deactivate the 2 A source (open circuit).**

When the current source is opened, the $R_3$ branch is broken → **no current flows through $R_3$** (open circuit carries zero current).

The active circuit becomes: $12 \, \text{V}$ source → $R_1 = 4 \, \Omega$ → Node B → $R_2 = 6 \, \Omega$ → ground.

This is a simple **series loop**. Total resistance seen by the 12 V source:

$$R_{\text{eq},1} = R_1 + R_2 = 4 + 6 = 10 \, \Omega$$

Current through the loop (this is the current in $R_2$ due to the 12 V source alone):

$$I_{2}^{(V)} = \frac{12 \, \text{V}}{10 \, \Omega} = 1.2 \, \text{A}$$

---

**Step 2: Activate the 2 A source alone. Deactivate the 12 V source (short circuit).**

When the 12 V source is replaced by a short circuit, the left side of $R_1$ is grounded. The circuit becomes:

- Node B is the top node.
- $R_1 = 4 \, \Omega$ from Node B to ground (the shorted voltage source).
- $R_2 = 6 \, \Omega$ from Node B to ground.
- $R_3 = 3 \, \Omega$ in series with the 2 A current source feeding Node B (current source still injects 2 A into Node B).

The 2 A current source pushes current into Node B; it then splits among the three parallel paths to ground: $R_1$, $R_2$, and the $R_3$-ground branch (if any). Wait — let me re-examine the topology.

**Re-examining topology (carefully):**

The 2 A current source is in **series** with $R_3$. The branch goes: Node B → $R_3 = 3 \, \Omega$ → (current source 2 A) → ground. This branch delivers a **fixed 2 A from Node B to ground** (depending on source orientation; assume 2 A flows from ground to Node B, so it injects 2 A into Node B).

**Apply KCL at Node B with the 12 V source shorted:**

Currents leaving Node B = Currents entering Node B.

$$\frac{V_B}{R_1} + \frac{V_B}{R_2} + 2 = 0$$

Wait — let me set up signs carefully. Assume $V_B$ is the voltage at Node B with respect to ground. The 2 A source is taken to **inject 2 A into Node B** (i.e., current flows from ground through the source and $R_3$ into Node B).

KCL at Node B (sum of currents leaving = 0):

$$\frac{V_B}{R_1} + \frac{V_B}{R_2} - 2 = 0$$

$$\frac{V_B}{4} + \frac{V_B}{6} = 2$$

Find common denominator (12):

$$\frac{3V_B}{12} + \frac{2V_B}{12} = 2$$

$$\frac{5V_B}{12} = 2$$

$$V_B = \frac{2 \times 12}{5} = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8 \, \text{V}$$

Current through $R_2$ (from Node B to ground) due to the 2 A source alone:

$$I_{2}^{(I)} = \frac{V_B}{R_2} = \frac{4.8}{6} = 0.8 \, \text{A}$$

---

**Step 3: Apply Superposition.**

Total current through $R_2$:

$$I_2 = I_{2}^{(V)} + I_{2}^{(I)} = 1.2 + 0.8 = 2.0 \, \text{A}$$

**Verification by Direct Nodal Analysis (all sources active):**

KCL at Node B with both sources active:

$$\frac{V_B - 12}{R_1} + \frac{V_B}{R_2} - 2 = 0$$

$$\frac{V_B - 12}{4} + \frac{V_B}{6} = 2$$

Multiply by 12:

$$3(V_B - 12) + 2V_B = 24$$

$$3V_B - 36 + 2V_B = 24$$

$$5V_B = 60 \implies V_B = 12 \, \text{V}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{V_B}{R_2} = \frac{12}{6} = 2.0 \, \text{A} \;\;\checkmark$$

The two methods agree. **Superposition is verified.**

---

#### **Worked Problem 2 — AC Phasor Network with R, L, C and Two Sources**

**Problem Statement:**

A series-parallel AC circuit operating at $\omega = 1000 \, \text{rad/s}$ consists of:
- An independent voltage source $\mathbf{V}_s = 10 \angle 0° \, \text{V}$ in series with $R_1 = 5 \, \Omega$,
- feeding a parallel combination of:
  - $R_2 = 10 \, \Omega$ branch,
  - $L = 5 \, \text{mH}$ branch,
  - $C = 200 \, \mu\text{F}$ branch in series with an independent current source $\mathbf{I}_s = 1 \angle 90° \, \text{A}$.

Find the voltage across the parallel combination using superposition.

---

**Step 0: Compute the phasor impedances.**

$$Z_{R_1} = 5 \, \Omega$$
$$Z_{R_2} = 10 \, \Omega$$
$$Z_L = j\omega L = j(1000)(5 \times 10^{-3}) = j5 \, \Omega$$
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1}{j(1000)(200 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{j \, 0.2} = -j5 \, \Omega$$

---

**Step 1: Activate $\mathbf{V}_s = 10 \angle 0°$ V alone. Deactivate $\mathbf{I}_s$ (open circuit).**

With $\mathbf{I}_s$ opened, the $C$-branch is broken. The circuit becomes: $\mathbf{V}_s$ → $R_1 = 5 \, \Omega$ → Node B → ($R_2 \parallel Z_L$) → ground.

Compute the parallel combination:

$$Z_{p}^{(V)} = \frac{Z_{R_2} \cdot Z_L}{Z_{R_2} + Z_L} = \frac{10 \cdot j5}{10 + j5}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator $(10 - j5)$:

$$Z_{p}^{(V)} = \frac{j50 \cdot (10 - j5)}{(10)^2 + (5)^2} = \frac{j500 - j^2 250}{100 + 25} = \frac{250 + j500}{125} = 2 + j4 \, \Omega$$

Total impedance seen by the voltage source:

$$Z_{\text{tot}}^{(V)} = R_1 + Z_{p}^{(V)} = 5 + 2 + j4 = 7 + j4 \, \Omega$$

Voltage across the parallel combination (voltage divider):

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(V)} = \mathbf{V}_s \cdot \frac{Z_{p}^{(V)}}{Z_{\text{tot}}^{(V)}} = 10 \angle 0° \cdot \frac{2 + j4}{7 + j4}$$

Compute the magnitude and phase of the ratio:

$$Z_{p}^{(V)} = 2 + j4 \implies |Z_{p}^{(V)}| = \sqrt{4 + 16} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5}, \quad \angle = \tan^{-1}(4/2) = 63.43°$$

$$Z_{\text{tot}}^{(V)} = 7 + j4 \implies |Z_{\text{tot}}^{(V)}| = \sqrt{49 + 16} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.062, \quad \angle = \tan^{-1}(4/7) = 29.74°$$

$$\frac{Z_{p}^{(V)}}{Z_{\text{tot}}^{(V)}} = \frac{2\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{65}} \angle (63.43° - 29.74°) = \frac{2 \times 2.236}{8.062} \angle 33.69° = \frac{4.472}{8.062} \angle 33.69° \approx 0.5547 \angle 33.69°$$

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(V)} = 10 \angle 0° \times 0.5547 \angle 33.69° = 5.547 \angle 33.69° \, \text{V}$$

---

**Step 2: Activate $\mathbf{I}_s = 1 \angle 90°$ A alone. Deactivate $\mathbf{V}_s$ (short circuit).**

With $\mathbf{V}_s$ shorted, $R_1 = 5 \, \Omega$ appears from Node B to ground (in parallel with the other branches). So Node B sees **four parallel branches** to ground: $R_1$, $R_2$, $Z_L$, and the $C$-branch carrying the current source.

For finding the **voltage at Node B** due to a current source feeding the node, we use:

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(I)} = \mathbf{I}_s \cdot Z_{\text{node}}$$

where $Z_{\text{node}}$ is the impedance from Node B to ground, looking into the network **with the current source removed** (i.e., the current source treated as an open while computing the equivalent impedance).

When the current source is opened, the $C$-branch is broken (it was: Node B → $C$ → current source → ground; with the source open, no current can flow). So $Z_{\text{node}} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel Z_L$.

$$Y_{\text{node}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{Z_L} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{j5}$$

$$Y_{\text{node}} = 0.2 + 0.1 - j0.2 = 0.3 - j0.2 \, \text{S}$$

$$Z_{\text{node}} = \frac{1}{0.3 - j0.2} = \frac{0.3 + j0.2}{(0.3)^2 + (0.2)^2} = \frac{0.3 + j0.2}{0.13} = 2.308 + j1.538 \, \Omega$$

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(I)} = (1 \angle 90°) \cdot (2.308 + j1.538)$$

Convert $1 \angle 90°$ to rectangular: $0 + j1$.

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(I)} = (j1)(2.308 + j1.538) = j2.308 + j^2 1.538 = -1.538 + j2.308 \, \text{V}$$

Convert to polar:

$$|\mathbf{V}_B^{(I)}| = \sqrt{(-1.538)^2 + (2.308)^2} = \sqrt{2.366 + 5.327} = \sqrt{7.693} \approx 2.774 \, \text{V}$$

$$\angle \mathbf{V}_B^{(I)} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2.308}{-1.538}\right) \text{ (in 2nd quadrant)} = 180° - \tan^{-1}(2.308/1.538) = 180° - 56.31° = 123.69°$$

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(I)} \approx 2.774 \angle 123.69° \, \text{V}$$

---

**Step 3: Apply Superposition.**

Convert $\mathbf{V}_B^{(V)}$ to rectangular for addition:

$$\mathbf{V}_B^{(V)} = 5.547 \angle 33.69° = 5.547 \cos 33.69° + j \, 5.547 \sin 33.69° = 4.615 + j3.078 \, \text{V}$$

Sum in rectangular form:

$$\mathbf{V}_B = \mathbf{V}_B^{(V)} + \mathbf{V}_B^{(I)} = (4.615 - 1.538) + j(3.078 + 2.308) = 3.077 + j5.386 \, \text{V}$$

Convert to polar:

$$|\mathbf{V}_B| = \sqrt{(3.077)^2 + (5.386)^2} = \sqrt{9.468 + 29.009} = \sqrt{38.477} \approx 6.203 \, \text{V}$$

$$\angle \mathbf{V}_B = \tan^{-1}(5.386/3.077) = \tan^{-1}(1.751) \approx 60.27°$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{V}_B \approx 6.20 \angle 60.27° \, \text{V}}$$

**Time-domain expression** (assuming RMS phasors and $\omega = 1000$ rad/s):

$$v_B(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot 6.203 \cos(1000 t + 60.27°) \approx 8.77 \cos(1000 t + 60.27°) \, \text{V}$$

---

#### **Worked Problem 3 — Network with a Dependent Source (VCVS)**

**Problem Statement:**

A circuit contains:
- An independent voltage source $V_s = 10 \, \text{V}$ (DC),
- A resistor $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$,
- A resistor $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$,
- A **voltage-controlled voltage source (VCVS)** with output voltage $V_d = 2 V_{R_1}$, where $V_{R_1}$ is the voltage across $R_1$.

The topology: $V_s$ in series with $R_1$, then in series with the VCVS, and $R_2$ is connected in parallel with the VCVS (i.e., across the VCVS terminals). Find the current through $R_2$.

**Topology (textual):**

$$V_s(+) \to R_1 = 2\,\Omega \to \text{Node A} \to R_2 = 4\,\Omega \to \text{ground} \to V_s(-)$$
$$\text{VCVS in parallel with } R_2: \text{positive terminal at Node A, negative at ground, output } V_d = 2 V_{R_1}$$

---

**Important Note for Superposition with Dependent Source:**

Since there is only **one independent source** ($V_s$), superposition is technically **trivial** here (only one sub-problem). However, this problem is valuable to **verify that dependent sources are kept active** even when there is only one independent source. The dependent source's output $V_d = 2V_{R_1}$ depends on $V_{R_1}$, which is solved from the same circuit.

**Direct Solution (KCL/KVL with the dependent source):**

Let $V_A$ be the voltage at Node A with respect to ground. The VCVS enforces $V_A = V_d = 2 V_{R_1}$. So:

$$V_A = 2 V_{R_1}$$

KVL around the loop ($V_s$, $R_1$, Node A, VCVS to ground):

$$V_s = V_{R_1} + V_A = V_{R_1} + 2 V_{R_1} = 3 V_{R_1}$$

$$V_{R_1} = \frac{V_s}{3} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.333 \, \text{V}$$

$$V_A = 2 \times 3.333 = 6.667 \, \text{V}$$

Current through $R_2$ (from Node A to ground):

$$I_{R_2} = \frac{V_A}{R_2} = \frac{6.667}{4} = 1.667 \, \text{A}$$

**Note:** No superposition needed because there's only one independent source. The dependent source is part of the network and is *always* active. This is the rule KTU tests.

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 4 — Full Superposition with BOTH Independent and Dependent Sources

**Problem Statement:**

A network has:
- Two independent sources: $V_s = 12 \, \text{V}$ (DC) and $I_s = 1 \, \text{A}$ (DC),
- Resistors: $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 6 \, \Omega$,
- One **CCCS** with output $I_d = 0.5 \, I_{R_1}$ (current-controlled current source; current $I_d$ flows through a branch containing $R_3$).

Topology: 
- $V_s$ in series with $R_1$ between Node A and ground.
- $I_s$ injecting current into Node A.
- $R_2$ between Node A and ground.
- $R_3$ in a branch from Node A to ground, with a CCCS (current $I_d = 0.5 I_{R_1}$ flowing from ground to Node A through this branch — this represents a controlled "pump" of current into Node A proportional to $I_{R_1}$).

Find the voltage $V_A$ at Node A.

**Wait — this is getting too complex. Let me revise to a clean KTU-style example.**

**Revised Problem:**

A two-node network with Node A and ground:
- $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$ between Node A and an independent $V_s = 12 \, \text{V}$ source (which is grounded on the other side).
- $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$ between Node A and ground.
- $R_3 = 6 \, \Omega$ between Node A and an independent $I_s = 1 \, \text{A}$ source (which is grounded on the other side; current $I_s$ flows from ground through the source to Node A).
- A **CCVS** between Node A and ground, with output voltage $V_d = 3 \cdot I_{R_1}$ (i.e., the voltage of the CCVS = 3 times the current through $R_1$, with polarity such that the + terminal is at Node A).

Find $V_A$ using superposition.

---

**Sub-problem A: $V_s = 12 \, \text{V}$ active, $I_s$ deactivated (open).**

With $I_s$ opened, the $R_3$ branch is broken → effectively absent.

Active components: $V_s$, $R_1$, $R_2$, and the CCVS (kept active).

**Nodal analysis at Node A:**

Let $V_A^{(V)}$ be the voltage due to $V_s$ alone. The CCVS imposes that the voltage at Node A is $V_d = 3 I_{R_1}$ (with respect to ground, assuming polarity convention). So $V_A^{(V)} = 3 I_{R_1}^{(V)}$.

Now $I_{R_1}^{(V)}$ is the current through $R_1$ from $V_s$ to Node A:

$$I_{R_1}^{(V)} = \frac{V_s - V_A^{(V)}}{R_1} = \frac{12 - V_A^{(V)}}{2}$$

Substitute into the CCVS constraint:

$$V_A^{(V)} = 3 \cdot \frac{12 - V_A^{(V)}}{2}$$

$$V_A^{(V)} = \frac{3}{2}(12 - V_A^{(V)})$$

$$V_A^{(V)} = 18 - 1.5 V_A^{(V)}$$

$$2.5 V_A^{(V)} = 18$$

$$V_A^{(V)} = 7.2 \, \text{V}$$

**Sub-problem B: $I_s = 1 \, \text{A}$ active, $V_s$ deactivated (short).**

With $V_s$ shorted, $R_1$ is between Node A and ground (i.e., $R_1 \parallel R_2$ from Node A's perspective, with the $R_3$-$I_s$ branch also feeding Node A).

The CCVS is still active. The current through $R_1$ (with $V_s$ shorted) is:

$$I_{R_1}^{(I)} = \frac{0 - V_A^{(I)}}{R_1} = -\frac{V_A^{(I)}}{2}$$

The CCVS constraint:

$$V_A^{(I)} = 3 I_{R_1}^{(I)} = 3 \cdot \left(-\frac{V_A^{(I)}}{2}\right) = -1.5 V_A^{(I)}$$

$$2.5 V_A^{(I)} = 0$$

$$V_A^{(I)} = 0 \, \text{V}$$

**Interpretation:** With $V_s$ shorted, the CCVS effectively "locks" Node A to ground (no current can build up).

**Apply Superposition:**

$$V_A = V_A^{(V)} + V_A^{(I)} = 7.2 + 0 = 7.2 \, \text{V}$$

**Verification by Direct KCL at Node A (all sources active):**

KCL at Node A (sum of currents leaving = 0):

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{R_1} + \frac{V_A}{R_2} - I_s + \frac{V_d - V_A}{R_3} = 0$$

Hmm, this is getting complex with the CCVS. Let me simplify the verification: the CCVS makes $V_A = 3 I_{R_1} = 3 \cdot \frac{12 - V_A}{2}$, which directly gives $V_A = 7.2$ V. The current source's contribution is suppressed by the dependent source's strong feedback. **This is a beautiful demonstration that dependent sources can dramatically alter superposition results.**

---

### 3.3 Generalised Proof of Superposition (for completeness in KTU answers)

Consider a linear network with two independent sources $x_1$ and $x_2$. The response $y$ at any branch is given by:

$$y = a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2$$

where $a_1$ and $a_2$ are constants determined by the network's topology and element values.

- **If $x_1$ is active and $x_2 = 0$:** $y_1 = a_1 x_1$
- **If $x_2$ is active and $x_1 = 0$:** $y_2 = a_2 x_2$

By linearity:

$$y = a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2 = y_1 + y_2$$

This proves superposition for **any number of sources** by induction.

For phasor AC circuits, replace $x_1, x_2$ with phasors $\mathbf{X}_1, \mathbf{X}_2$ and constants $a_1, a_2$ become complex transfer functions $H_1(j\omega), H_2(j\omega)$:

$$\mathbf{Y} = H_1(j\omega) \mathbf{X}_1 + H_2(j\omega) \mathbf{X}_2$$

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Superposition Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart visualises the **decision tree** a student should follow when solving any superposition problem in PERUT417 Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Circuit with N independent sources and possibly dependent sources]) --> identifyA[Step 1: Identify ALL independent sources]
    identifyA --> listA[Voltage sources V_s1, V_s2, ... and Current sources I_s1, I_s2, ...]
    listA --> checkA{Number of independent sources?}
    checkA -->|N = 0| trivialA[No sources — only dependent sources — Trivial: solve directly]
    checkA -->|N = 1| singleA[Single independent source — Solve directly with dependent sources active]
    checkA -->|N >= 2| proceedA[Proceed with superposition]
    proceedA --> initA[Step 2: Set source counter k = 1]
    initA --> activateA[Step 3: Activate source k; DEACTIVATE all other independent sources]
    activateA --> vsA{Source type being deactivated?}
    vsA -->|Voltage source| shortA[Replace with SHORT CIRCUIT]
    vsA -->|Current source| openA[Replace with OPEN CIRCUIT]
    shortA --> keepA[Keep ALL dependent sources ACTIVE in sub-circuit]
    openA --> keepA
    keepA --> solveA[Step 4: Solve sub-circuit using nodal or mesh analysis]
    solveA --> recordA[Step 5: Record response y_k due to source k alone]
    recordA --> incA[Step 6: Increment k]
    incA --> checkB{k = N?}
    checkB -->|No| activateA
    checkB -->|Yes| sumA[Step 7: Compute total response y = y_1 + y_2 + ... + y_N]
    sumA --> verifyA[Step 8: Verify with direct nodal or mesh analysis]
    verifyA --> endA([End: Final answer with units and polar/rectangular form as needed])
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Linear Circuit with Independent and Dependent Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[Input Sources Stage]
        IVS[Independent Voltage Source V_s]
        ICS[Independent Current Source I_s]
    end
    subgraph ControlStage[Dependent Source Control Stage]
        VSense[Voltage Sensor: Measures V_ctrl]
        ISense[Current Sensor: Measures I_ctrl]
        VCVS[VCVS mu V_ctrl]
        VCCS[VCCS g_m V_ctrl]
        CCVS[CCVS r_m I_ctrl]
        CCCS[CCCS beta I_ctrl]
    end
    subgraph NetworkStage[Linear Passive Network R, L, C]
        R[Resistors R]
        L[Inductors L]
        C[Capacitors C]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Response Stage]
        Vout[Output Voltage V_y]
        Iout[Output Current I_y]
    end
    IVS --> NetworkStage
    ICS --> NetworkStage
    NetworkStage --> VSense
    NetworkStage --> ISense
    VSense --> VCVS
    VSense --> VCCS
    ISense --> CCVS
    ISense --> CCCS
    VCVS --> NetworkStage
    VCCS --> NetworkStage
    CCVS --> NetworkStage
    CCCS --> NetworkStage
    NetworkStage --> Vout
    NetworkStage --> Iout
```

### 4.3 Superposition Signal-Flow Diagram (for AC phasor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SourceLayer1[Source Layer 1 - Voltage Source Sub-problem]
        Vs[Phasor V_s]
        VsClosed[All other V_sources: SHORT]
        IsOpen1[All other I_sources: OPEN]
    end
    subgraph SourceLayer2[Source Layer 2 - Current Source Sub-problem]
        Is[Phasor I_s]
        VsShorted[All other V_sources: SHORT]
        IsOpened[All other I_sources: OPEN]
    end
    subgraph SolveLayer[Sub-circuit Solver - Linear Methods]
        Nodal[Nodal Analysis]
        Mesh[Mesh Analysis]
        ThevNorton[Thevenin Norton Equivalents]
    end
    subgraph SumLayer[Superposition Summation]
        Sum1[y_1 from V_s alone]
        Sum2[y_2 from I_s alone]
        SumTotal[Total y = y_1 + y_2]
    end
    SourceLayer1 --> SolveLayer
    SourceLayer2 --> SolveLayer
    SolveLayer --> Sum1
    SolveLayer --> Sum2
    Sum1 --> SumTotal
    Sum2 --> SumTotal
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree — When to Use Superposition vs. Direct Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ([Given Circuit]) --> countQ{How many independent sources?}
    countQ -->|0| directQ[Use direct analysis with dependent sources only]
    countQ -->|1| directOnlyQ[Superposition trivial — Use direct nodal or mesh]
    countQ -->|2 or 3| supUsefulQ[Superposition HIGHLY recommended]
    countQ -->|4 or more| meshOrNodalQ[Consider direct mesh or nodal - superposition may be tedious]
    supUsefulQ --> typeQ{Is the circuit AC or DC?}
    typeQ -->|DC| dcSolveQ[Solve each sub-problem with resistors only]
    typeQ -->|AC| acSolveQ[Convert to phasors, solve each sub-problem with complex impedances]
    dcSolveQ --> sumQ[Sum the responses]
    acSolveQ --> sumQ
    sumQ --> verifyQ[Verify with direct analysis if time permits]
```
