## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, PERUT417, Module 1]`

**State and explain the superposition theorem. Mention the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **superposition theorem** states that in any linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources, the response (voltage across or current through any element) equals the algebraic sum of the responses produced by each independent source acting alone, with all other independent sources replaced by their internal impedances.
> 
> **Conditions for applicability:**
> 1. The network must be **linear** (all elements obey Ohm's law: $V = IR$ with constant $R$).
> 2. The network must be **bilateral** (the relation between voltage and current is the same in both directions).
> 3. The circuit must contain **two or more independent sources**.
> 4. **Dependent sources** must **never be deactivated**; they remain part of the circuit in every sub-problem.
> 
> **Mathematical form:** $y_{\text{total}} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} y_k$, where $y_k$ is the response from the $k$-th source alone.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, PERUT417, Module 1]`

**Differentiate between independent and dependent sources. List the four types of dependent sources with their controlling-constant units.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Independent Source:** Its voltage or current value is **fixed by the source itself** and is independent of any other variable in the circuit. Examples: a 12 V battery, a 5 A current generator.
> 
> **Dependent Source:** Its output (voltage or current) is **controlled by a voltage or current elsewhere in the same circuit**. The output is given by $y = k \cdot x$, where $x$ is the controlling variable and $k$ is the controlling constant.
> 
> **Four types of dependent sources:**
> 
> | Type | Output | Controlling Variable | Constant Unit |
> |------|--------|----------------------|---------------|
> | VCVS — Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source | Voltage | Voltage | V/V (dimensionless) |
> | VCCS — Voltage-Controlled Current Source | Current | Voltage | A/V = S (Siemens) |
> | CCVS — Current-Controlled Voltage Source | Voltage | Current | V/A = Ω (Ohm) |
> | CCCS — Current-Controlled Current Source | Current | Current | A/A (dimensionless) |
> 
> Dependent sources are used to model active devices like **transistors, operational amplifiers, and transformers**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, usually split as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts. The student is expected to **choose one of two alternative questions** from the same module.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, PERUT417, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the circuit shown below, determine the current through the $4 \, \Omega$ resistor using the superposition theorem.

> *Circuit Description:* A $24 \, \text{V}$ DC voltage source is connected in series with a $6 \, \Omega$ resistor. This combination is in parallel with a branch containing a $4 \, \Omega$ resistor. An independent $2 \, \text{A}$ current source is connected across the parallel combination (injecting current into the top node). All elements share a common ground.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An AC circuit operates at $\omega = 1000 \, \text{rad/s}$ and contains:
- An independent voltage source $\mathbf{V}_s = 20 \angle 0° \, \text{V}$ in series with $R_1 = 10 \, \Omega$ and $L = 10 \, \text{mH}$,
- in parallel with an independent current source $\mathbf{I}_s = 2 \angle -90° \, \text{A}$ feeding the same node,
- with a capacitor $C = 100 \, \mu\text{F}$ connected between the top node and ground.

Find the **voltage across the capacitor** using superposition.

**Complete Model Solution for Question B-A:**

**Part (a) — DC Superposition with V and I sources:**

Let the top node (junction of $V_s$, $R_1$, $R_2$, and $I_s$) be Node A. The 4 Ω resistor is $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$, the 6 Ω resistor is $R_1 = 6 \, \Omega$, $V_s = 24$ V, and $I_s = 2$ A injecting into Node A.

**Sub-problem 1: $V_s$ active, $I_s$ open-circuited.**

With the current source open, no current flows through its branch. The active circuit is: $V_s$ → $R_1 = 6 \, \Omega$ → Node A → $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$ → ground. A simple series loop.

$$I_{R_2}^{(V)} = \frac{V_s}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{24}{6 + 4} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4 \, \text{A}$$

**Sub-problem 2: $I_s = 2 \, \text{A}$ active, $V_s$ short-circuited.**

With the voltage source shorted, $R_1$ becomes a $6 \, \Omega$ resistor from Node A to ground (in parallel with $R_2$). The current source pushes 2 A into Node A; this current divides between the two parallel resistors.

By the current divider rule:

$$I_{R_2}^{(I)} = I_s \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 2 \cdot \frac{6}{6 + 4} = 2 \cdot \frac{6}{10} = 1.2 \, \text{A}$$

**Superposition — Total current through $R_2$ (4 Ω):**

$$I_{R_2} = I_{R_2}^{(V)} + I_{R_2}^{(I)} = 2.4 + 1.2 = 3.6 \, \text{A}$$

**Verification by direct nodal analysis (KCL at Node A):**

Let $V_A$ be the voltage at Node A.

$$\frac{V_A - 24}{6} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 2 = 0$$

Multiply by 12:

$$2(V_A - 24) + 3V_A - 24 = 0$$

$$2V_A - 48 + 3V_A - 24 = 0$$

$$5V_A = 72$$

$$V_A = 14.4 \, \text{V}$$

$$I_{R_2} = \frac{V_A}{R_2} = \frac{14.4}{4} = 3.6 \, \text{A} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Part (b) — AC Phasor Superposition:**

Compute impedances at $\omega = 1000$ rad/s:

$$Z_L = j\omega L = j(1000)(0.01) = j10 \, \Omega$$
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1}{j(1000)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{j \, 0.1} = -j10 \, \Omega$$
$$Z_{R_1} = 10 \, \Omega$$

**Sub-problem 1: $\mathbf{V}_s = 20 \angle 0°$ V active, $\mathbf{I}_s$ open.**

The circuit: $\mathbf{V}_s$ → $Z_{R_1} = 10 \, \Omega$ → $Z_L = j10 \, \Omega$ → Node A → $Z_C = -j10 \, \Omega$ → ground. The current source is opened, so its branch is absent.

Series impedance from $\mathbf{V}_s$ to Node A: $Z_s^{(V)} = Z_{R_1} + Z_L = 10 + j10 \, \Omega$.

The voltage across the capacitor (Node A to ground) by voltage divider:

$$\mathbf{V}_C^{(V)} = \mathbf{V}_s \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_s^{(V)} + Z_C} = 20 \angle 0° \cdot \frac{-j10}{10 + j10 - j10} = 20 \angle 0° \cdot \frac{-j10}{10}$$

$$\mathbf{V}_C^{(V)} = 20 \cdot (-j1) = -j20 = 20 \angle -90° \, \text{V}$$

**Sub-problem 2: $\mathbf{I}_s = 2 \angle -90°$ A active, $\mathbf{V}_s$ shorted.**

With the voltage source shorted, the path from Node A to ground through $R_1 + L$ becomes $Z_{R_1} + Z_L = 10 + j10 \, \Omega$. The capacitor $Z_C = -j10 \, \Omega$ is in parallel with this impedance.

The equivalent impedance from Node A to ground (with the current source removed):

$$Z_{\text{node}}^{(I)} = (Z_{R_1} + Z_L) \parallel Z_C = (10 + j10) \parallel (-j10)$$

$$Y_{\text{node}}^{(I)} = \frac{1}{10 + j10} + \frac{1}{-j10} = \frac{10 - j10}{200} + j0.1 = (0.05 - j0.05) + j0.1 = 0.05 + j0.05 \, \text{S}$$

$$Z_{\text{node}}^{(I)} = \frac{1}{0.05 + j0.05} = \frac{0.05 - j0.05}{(0.05)^2 + (0.05)^2} = \frac{0.05 - j0.05}{0.005} = 10 - j10 \, \Omega$$

The voltage across the capacitor (Node A voltage) due to the current source:

$$\mathbf{V}_C^{(I)} = \mathbf{I}_s \cdot Z_{\text{node}}^{(I)} = (2 \angle -90°) \cdot (10 - j10)$$

Convert $2 \angle -90°$ to rectangular: $0 - j2$.

$$\mathbf{V}_C^{(I)} = (-j2)(10 - j10) = -j20 + j^2 20 = -20 - j20 \, \text{V}$$

Convert to polar:

$$|\mathbf{V}_C^{(I)}| = \sqrt{400 + 400} = \sqrt{800} = 20\sqrt{2} \approx 28.28 \, \text{V}$$

$$\angle \mathbf{V}_C^{(I)} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-20}{-20}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1) \text{ in 3rd quadrant} = 180° + 45° = 225° \text{ or equivalently } -135°$$

$$\mathbf{V}_C^{(I)} = 28.28 \angle -135° \, \text{V}$$

**Apply Superposition:**

$$\mathbf{V}_C = \mathbf{V}_C^{(V)} + \mathbf{V}_C^{(I)} = (0 - j20) + (-20 - j20) = -20 - j40 \, \text{V}$$

Convert to polar:

$$|\mathbf{V}_C| = \sqrt{400 + 1600} = \sqrt{2000} = 20\sqrt{5} \approx 44.72 \, \text{V}$$

$$\angle \mathbf{V}_C = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-40}{-20}\right) \text{ in 3rd quadrant} = 180° + \tan^{-1}(2) = 180° + 63.43° = 243.43° \text{ or } -116.57°$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{V}_C = 44.72 \angle -116.57° \, \text{V (RMS)}}$$

**Time-domain:**

$$v_C(t) = \sqrt{2} \cdot 44.72 \cos(1000 t - 116.57°) \approx 63.25 \cos(1000 t - 116.57°) \, \text{V}$$

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, PERUT417, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** In the circuit below, find the voltage $V_{AB}$ across the $5 \, \Omega$ resistor using superposition.

> *Circuit Description:* A $10 \, \text{V}$ independent voltage source, a $2 \, \text{A}$ independent current source, and a **VCVS** with output $V_d = 3 V_x$ (where $V_x$ is the voltage across a $2 \, \Omega$ sensing resistor) are all connected to a network. Specifically:
> - $V_s = 10 \, \text{V}$ in series with $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$ between Node A and ground.
> - $I_s = 2 \, \text{A}$ injecting into Node A from a parallel branch containing $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$ to ground.
> - $R_3 = 5 \, \Omega$ between Node A and ground.
> - The VCVS is in series with the $R_1$ branch; its output is $V_d = 3 V_x$, where $V_x$ is the voltage across $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$.
> - $V_{AB}$ is the voltage across $R_3 = 5 \, \Omega$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two independent sources $V_s = 12 \, \text{V}$ (DC) and $I_s = 1 \, \text{A}$ (DC) are connected in a network with resistors $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$, and a **CCCS** with output $I_d = 0.5 I_{R_1}$ flowing through a branch parallel to $R_2$. Determine the current through $R_2$ using superposition.

**Complete Model Solution for Question B-B:**

**Part (a) — Superposition with VCVS:**

Let the VCVS output $V_d$ be in **series-aiding** polarity with $V_s$ (i.e., both push current in the same direction through $R_1$). Then the loop equation is:

$$V_s + V_d - I_{R_1} R_1 - V_{AB} = 0$$

But the VCVS makes $V_d = 3 V_x$ where $V_x$ is the voltage across $R_1$ (assumed equal to $I_{R_1} R_1$).

For the superposition approach, we treat the **VCVS as a dependent source that stays active**.

**Sub-problem 1: $V_s = 10 \, \text{V}$ active, $I_s$ opened.**

With $I_s$ open, the circuit is: $V_s$ → VCVS($V_d = 3 V_x$) → $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$ → Node A → $R_3 = 5 \, \Omega$ → ground.

Apply KVL around the loop:

$$V_s + V_d = I_{R_1} R_1 + I_{R_1} R_3$$

(Note: $I_{R_1} = I_{R_3}$ in this series loop since $I_s$ branch is open.)

Substitute $V_d = 3 V_x = 3 (I_{R_1} R_1) = 3 (I_{R_1} \cdot 2) = 6 I_{R_1}$:

$$10 + 6 I_{R_1} = 2 I_{R_1} + 5 I_{R_1}$$

$$10 + 6 I_{R_1} = 7 I_{R_1}$$

$$10 = I_{R_1}$$

$$I_{R_1}^{(V)} = 10 \, \text{A}$$

$$V_{AB}^{(V)} = I_{R_1}^{(V)} \cdot R_3 = 10 \times 5 = 50 \, \text{V}$$

**Sub-problem 2: $I_s = 2 \, \text{A}$ active, $V_s$ shorted.**

With $V_s$ shorted, the VCVS still has $V_d = 3 V_x = 3 (I_{R_1} R_1)$ in series with $R_1$. KCL at Node A:

$$I_{R_1}^{(I)} + I_s = I_{R_3}^{(I)} = \frac{V_{AB}^{(I)}}{R_3}$$

Also, the loop equation (taking $V_s$ = 0):

$$V_d = I_{R_1}^{(I)} R_1 + V_{AB}^{(I)}$$

$$3 (I_{R_1}^{(I)} \cdot 2) = 2 I_{R_1}^{(I)} + V_{AB}^{(I)}$$

$$6 I_{R_1}^{(I)} = 2 I_{R_1}^{(I)} + V_{AB}^{(I)}$$

$$V_{AB}^{(I)} = 4 I_{R_1}^{(I)}$$

From KCL: $I_{R_1}^{(I)} + 2 = \dfrac{V_{AB}^{(I)}}{5}$

Substitute $V_{AB}^{(I)} = 4 I_{R_1}^{(I)}$:

$$I_{R_1}^{(I)} + 2 = \frac{4 I_{R_1}^{(I)}}{5}$$

$$5 I_{R_1}^{(I)} + 10 = 4 I_{R_1}^{(I)}$$

$$I_{R_1}^{(I)} = -10 \, \text{A}$$

$$V_{AB}^{(I)} = 4 \times (-10) = -40 \, \text{V}$$

**Apply Superposition:**

$$V_{AB} = V_{AB}^{(V)} + V_{AB}^{(I)} = 50 + (-40) = 10 \, \text{V}$$

**Verification by direct KCL at Node A (all sources active):**

Let $V_{AB}$ be the unknown. The current through $R_1$ is $I_{R_1}$. The VCVS imposes $V_d = 6 I_{R_1}$ (in series-aiding with $V_s$).

KVL through $V_s$, VCVS, $R_1$, $R_3$:
$$V_s + V_d = I_{R_1} R_1 + V_{AB}$$
$$10 + 6 I_{R_1} = 2 I_{R_1} + V_{AB}$$
$$V_{AB} = 10 + 4 I_{R_1}$$

KCL at Node A: $I_{R_1} + I_s = V_{AB}/R_3$
$$I_{R_1} + 2 = V_{AB}/5$$
$$V_{AB} = 5 I_{R_1} + 10$$

Equating:
$$10 + 4 I_{R_1} = 5 I_{R_1} + 10$$
$$0 = I_{R_1}$$
$$I_{R_1} = 0$$
$$V_{AB} = 10 + 4(0) = 10 \, \text{V} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Part (b) — Superposition with CCCS:**

**Sub-problem 1: $V_s = 12 \, \text{V}$ active, $I_s$ opened.**

With $I_s$ open, the branch with the CCCS still has $I_d = 0.5 I_{R_1}$ flowing. Assume the CCCS branch is in parallel with $R_2 = 4 \, \Omega$ from Node A to ground.

KCL at Node A:
$$\frac{V_s - V_A}{R_1} = \frac{V_A}{R_2} + I_d$$
$$\frac{12 - V_A}{2} = \frac{V_A}{4} + 0.5 I_{R_1}$$

But $I_{R_1} = \dfrac{12 - V_A}{R_1} = \dfrac{12 - V_A}{2}$, so $0.5 I_{R_1} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{12 - V_A}{2} = \dfrac{12 - V_A}{4}$.

$$\frac{12 - V_A}{2} = \frac{V_A}{4} + \frac{12 - V_A}{4}$$

Multiply by 4:
$$2(12 - V_A) = V_A + (12 - V_A)$$
$$24 - 2V_A = 12$$
$$V_A = 6 \, \text{V}$$

$$I_{R_2}^{(V)} = \frac{V_A}{R_2} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \, \text{A}$$

**Sub-problem 2: $I_s = 1 \, \text{A}$ active, $V_s$ shorted.**

With $V_s$ shorted, $R_1 = 2 \, \Omega$ is between Node A and ground. KCL at Node A:

$$\frac{V_A}{R_1} + I_s = \frac{V_A}{R_2} - I_d$$

Wait, signs depend on direction. Let me re-state: assume the CCCS injects current $I_d$ into Node A (so it adds to $I_s$).

$$\frac{V_A}{2} + 1 = \frac{V_A}{4} - 0.5 I_{R_1}^{(I)}$$

But now $I_{R_1}^{(I)} = \dfrac{0 - V_A}{2} = -\dfrac{V_A}{2}$ (current flows from ground to Node A through $R_1$ when $V_s$ is shorted and $V_A > 0$).

So $0.5 I_{R_1}^{(I)} = 0.5 \cdot \left(-\dfrac{V_A}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{V_A}{4}$.

$$\frac{V_A}{2} + 1 = \frac{V_A}{4} - \left(-\frac{V_A}{4}\right) = \frac{V_A}{4} + \frac{V_A}{4} = \frac{V_A}{2}$$

$$1 = 0 \implies \text{No unique solution}$$

This indicates the dependent source's feedback creates a constrained relationship. Let me re-examine the topology assumption.

**Topology Re-clarification (more standard):**

- $V_s$ in series with $R_1$ between ground and Node A.
- $R_2$ between Node A and ground.
- $I_s$ injecting 1 A into Node A.
- CCCS branch between Node A and ground, with **current $I_d = 0.5 I_{R_1}$ flowing from ground to Node A** (i.e., it pumps current into Node A proportional to $I_{R_1}$).

KCL at Node A (sum of currents leaving = 0):
$$\frac{V_A - V_s}{R_1} + \frac{V_A}{R_2} - I_s - I_d = 0$$

With $V_s$ shorted (Sub-problem 2):
$$\frac{V_A}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 1 - 0.5 I_{R_1}^{(I)} = 0$$

With $I_{R_1}^{(I)} = -V_A/2$ (flowing from Node A to ground through $R_1$ when $V_A > 0$):
$$0.5 I_{R_1}^{(I)} = -V_A/4$$

$$\frac{V_A}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 1 - \left(-\frac{V_A}{4}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{V_A}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} + \frac{V_A}{4} = 1$$

$$V_A = 1 \, \text{V}$$

$$I_{R_2}^{(I)} = \frac{V_A}{R_2} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \, \text{A}$$

**Apply Superposition:**

$$I_{R_2} = I_{R_2}^{(V)} + I_{R_2}^{(I)} = 1.5 + 0.25 = 1.75 \, \text{A}$$

**Verification by direct KCL (all sources active):**

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 1 - 0.5 I_{R_1} = 0$$

With $I_{R_1} = (12 - V_A)/2$:

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{12 - V_A}{2} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 1 - \frac{12 - V_A}{4} = 0$$

Multiply by 4:
$$2(V_A - 12) + V_A - 4 - (12 - V_A) = 0$$
$$2V_A - 24 + V_A - 4 - 12 + V_A = 0$$
$$4V_A - 40 = 0$$
$$V_A = 10 \, \text{V}$$
$$I_{R_2} = V_A / R_2 = 10/4 = 2.5 \, \text{A}$$

**Wait — this disagrees with 1.75 A from superposition!** Let me recheck.

**Recheck Sub-problem 1:**

V_s active, I_s open. KCL at Node A:
$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - 0.5 I_{R_1} = 0$$
$$I_{R_1} = (12 - V_A)/2$$
$$0.5 I_{R_1} = (12 - V_A)/4$$

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A}{4} - \frac{12 - V_A}{4} = 0$$

Multiply by 4:
$$2(V_A - 12) + V_A - (12 - V_A) = 0$$
$$2V_A - 24 + V_A - 12 + V_A = 0$$
$$4V_A = 36$$
$$V_A = 9 \, \text{V}$$
$$I_{R_2}^{(V)} = V_A / 4 = 9/4 = 2.25 \, \text{A}$$

I made an arithmetic error before. Let me redo: the original calculation gave $V_A = 6$ V which was wrong. Correct: $V_A = 9$ V, $I_{R_2}^{(V)} = 2.25$ A.

**Recheck Sub-problem 2:** $V_A = 1$ V, $I_{R_2}^{(I)} = 0.25$ A.

**Superposition result:**

$$I_{R_2} = 2.25 + 0.25 = 2.5 \, \text{A} \;\;\checkmark$$

This now matches the direct KCL result. **The principle is verified.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Superposition Problems (Module 1):**
> 
> 1. **Deactivating dependent sources — 2 to 3 marks lost.** Students often short-circuit or open-circuit dependent sources like VCVS, VCCS, etc. This is **WRONG**. Dependent sources must remain active. The controlling variable will automatically take the new sub-circuit value.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to phasors for AC — 3 to 4 marks lost.** When the problem says $\omega = 1000$ rad/s, you MUST compute $Z_L = j\omega L$ and $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$ and work entirely in phasor form. Mixing time-domain and phasor-domain equations is a fatal error.
> 
> 3. **Sign errors when summing partial responses — 1 to 2 marks lost.** Always mark the assumed reference direction for each sub-problem. If a partial current comes out negative, the sign carries through to the final sum.
> 
> 4. **Not verifying with direct analysis — 1 mark lost.** KTU examiners reward verification. A 1-line "Verification by direct nodal analysis: ..." adds polish and may recover partial credit if superposition is slightly off.
> 
> 5. **Unit mismatch on dependent source constants — 1 to 2 marks lost.** If the problem says "a VCVS with output $V_d = 2 V_x$", then $V_d$ is in volts and $V_x$ is in volts; the constant 2 is dimensionless. If the constant has units (e.g., $g_m = 0.5 \, \text{S}$), you must include the unit in your final answer.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to convert polar phasor sums to rectangular before adding — 2 to 3 marks lost.** You cannot add phasors in polar form directly. Convert to rectangular: $A \angle \theta_1 + B \angle \theta_2 = (A\cos\theta_1 + B\cos\theta_2) + j(A\sin\theta_1 + B\sin\theta_2)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Superposition Principle: Rapid Revision Checklist**
> 
> ✅ **Core Theorem:** Total response = algebraic sum of individual source responses (linearity property).
> 
> ✅ **Deactivation Rules:**
> - Independent Voltage Source → **Short Circuit** ($V = 0$)
> - Independent Current Source → **Open Circuit** ($I = 0$)
> 
> ✅ **Golden Rule:** Dependent sources are **NEVER** deactivated. They stay active in every sub-problem, with their controlling variables taking the new sub-circuit values.
> 
> ✅ **Four Dependent Source Types (memorize symbols & units):**
> - VCVS (V/V), VCCS (A/V = S), CCVS (V/A = Ω), CCCS (A/A)
> 
> ✅ **AC Extension:** Convert all elements to phasor impedances ($R \to R$, $L \to j\omega L$, $C \to 1/j\omega C$) and work in the frequency domain. Add partial phasor responses as complex numbers (rectangular form first, then convert to polar if needed).
> 
> ✅ **Verification:** Always cross-check superposition result with direct nodal or mesh analysis — catches sign and arithmetic errors.
> 
> ✅ **Power is NOT Superposable:** Although voltages and currents superpose linearly, **power** ($P = VI$ or $P = I^2R$ or $P = V^2/R$) is a **quadratic** function and CANNOT be found by summing individual source contributions. You must find total $V$ and total $I$ first, then compute $P$.
> 
> ✅ **Conditions for Validity:** Linear + bilateral network with multiple independent sources. Diode circuits, circuits with saturation, switched circuits in transient — all **violate linearity**, so superposition does NOT apply directly.
> 
> ✅ **KTU Frequency:** 50 Hz mains, $\omega = 314.159$ rad/s, is the standard for Indian power engineering. Be ready to recognise it implicitly.
> 
> ✅ **Source Transformation Equivalence (use freely):** A voltage source $V_s$ in series with $R$ is equivalent to a current source $I_s = V_s/R$ in parallel with $R$. This is often the fastest way to simplify a sub-problem.
> 
> ✅ **Phasor Addition Rule:** Always convert to rectangular $(a + jb)$ form before adding; only then convert the sum back to polar if the answer must be in polar form.
> 
> ✅ **Mark Distribution Pattern in KTU ESE:** Part A questions on superposition typically test (i) statement + conditions (3 marks) and (ii) one numerical sub-problem. Part B questions (14 marks) usually have two 7-mark sub-problems, often one DC and one AC, with the second sometimes involving a dependent source.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Source Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Source Transformation** is a fundamental circuit analysis technique that allows the conversion of a practical (non-ideal) voltage source into an equivalent practical current source, and vice versa, without altering the external behavior of the circuit. This transformation is based on the principle of **circuit equivalence**, meaning the voltage-current (V-I) characteristics observed at the external terminals remain identical before and after the transformation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, source transformation is treated as a critical tool under **Norton’s Theorem** and **Thevenin’s Theorem**, forming the analytical backbone of Module 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Source transformation is the bridge between Thevenin and Norton equivalent circuits. A Thevenin equivalent (V_Th in series with R_Th) can be directly converted to a Norton equivalent (I_N in parallel with R_N) using this technique.

## Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two water pump systems delivering water to a garden:

- **Practical Voltage Source** = A pump pushing water at a **fixed pressure (Voltage, V)** through a partially clogged pipe that has some **internal resistance (R)**. The pressure drops as more water flows.
- **Practical Current Source** = A pump delivering a **fixed flow rate (Current, I)** that splits at a junction, where some water leaks back through a **parallel bypass pipe (R)**.

Both systems look *different physically*, but to the garden (external load), the water arrives in **exactly the same way**. Source transformation just lets us "redraw" the pump in the other style.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The internal resistance **R remains unchanged** in both forms. Only its position (series vs parallel) and the source type (voltage vs current) changes.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Voltage (V):** Measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Current (I):** Measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Resistance (R):** Measured in **Ohms (Ω)**
- **Ohm's Law Constant:** **1 Ω = 1 V/A**
- **Power (P):** Measured in **Watts (W)** — useful for verifying equivalence: P = I²R = V²/R

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic — How Source Transformation Works

The transformation follows a strict set of logical rules that every KTU examiner expects you to state explicitly:

### Step 1: Identify the Source-Resistor Configuration
- A **Voltage Source V in series with Resistance R** → ready to convert
- A **Current Source I in parallel with Resistance R** → ready to convert

### Step 2: Apply the Equivalence Relationship
Using **Ohm's Law**, the relationship between the source values and the shared resistance is:

$$I = \frac{V}{R}$$

### Step 3: Re-draw the Circuit
- Keep the **resistance value R** identical
- For Voltage → Current: Place the current source in **parallel** with R, with arrow pointing **toward** the positive terminal of the original voltage source
- For Current → Voltage: Place the voltage source in **series** with R, with **+ terminal** facing the arrow direction of the original current source

### Step 4: Verify External Behavior
The V-I curve at the open-circuit and short-circuit terminals must be **identical** for both representations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction Rule:** The arrow of the current source in the Norton equivalent always points **toward the positive (+) terminal** of the Thevenin voltage source.

## Special Case — Why Ideal Sources Cannot Be Transformed

- An **ideal voltage source** has R = 0 (internal resistance is zero). Transforming it would require dividing by zero to find I, which is **mathematically undefined**.
- An **ideal current source** has R = ∞ (infinite parallel resistance). Transforming it would require multiplying by infinity to find V, which is **physically meaningless**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students attempt to transform an ideal source (e.g., a pure 10V battery with no series resistor). This is **NOT** allowed. Only **practical sources** (with finite R) can be transformed.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Transformation | Input Form | Output Form | Governing Equation | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage → Current | V in series with R | I in parallel with R | $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ | R must be finite and non-zero |
| 2 | Current → Voltage | I in parallel with R | V in series with R | $V = I \cdot R$ | R must be finite and non-zero |
| 3 | Power Equivalence | Either form | Either form | $P = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Power delivered to external load is identical |
| 4 | Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc) | Thevenin form | Norton form | $V_{oc} = V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R$ | At output terminals with load removed |
| 5 | Short-Circuit Current (I_sc) | Norton form | Thevenin form | $I_{sc} = I_N = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R}$ | At output terminals with load shorted |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Analysis:** Simplifies large grid networks into manageable equivalents
- **Electronics Design:** Converts between battery models and transistor small-signal models
- **Control Systems:** Used in feedback amplifier analysis (Bode plots, stability)
- **PCB Design:** Helps reduce complex mixed-source circuits into single-loop analysis

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Proving Voltage-Source Equivalence to Current-Source

We will prove mathematically that the practical voltage source and practical current source produce **identical V-I characteristics** at the external terminals.

### Step 1: Start with the Practical Voltage Source
A practical voltage source has a voltage $V_s$ in series with an internal resistance $R$. Let $V_L$ be the voltage across the load and $I_L$ be the current through it.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop:

$$V_s - I_L \cdot R - V_L = 0$$

Solving for $V_L$:

$$V_L = V_s - I_L \cdot R$$

### Step 2: Express the Current-Source Equivalent
Now consider the practical current source with current $I_s$ in parallel with the same resistance $R$. The terminal voltage is $V_L$ and the load current is $I_L$.

By **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the top node, the current flowing out of the source splits between R and the load:

$$I_s = \frac{V_L}{R} + I_L$$

Rearranging to solve for $V_L$:

$$I_s - I_L = \frac{V_L}{R}$$

$$V_L = I_s \cdot R - I_L \cdot R$$

$$V_L = (I_s - I_L) \cdot R$$

### Step 3: Equate the Two Expressions for V_L
For the two circuits to be **electrically equivalent**, their $V_L$ expressions must be identical:

$$V_s - I_L \cdot R = I_s \cdot R - I_L \cdot R$$

Canceling $-I_L \cdot R$ from both sides:

$$V_s = I_s \cdot R$$

### Step 4: Final Equivalence Relationship

$$\boxed{I_s = \frac{V_s}{R}}$$

This is the **fundamental source transformation equation**. The proof is complete.

## Derivation 2: Power Dissipation Equivalence

To verify that the power delivered to the external load is **conserved**, we calculate P for both configurations.

### Voltage-Source Form (Power in R):

$$P_R = I_L^2 \cdot R$$

### Current-Source Form (Power in R):
Since the same current $I_L$ flows through R and the voltage across R is $V_R$:

$$P_R = \frac{V_R^2}{R} = I_L^2 \cdot R$$

Both yield the **same power dissipation**, confirming energy conservation across the transformation.

## Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A 12 V battery with internal resistance 4 Ω is connected to a 8 Ω load. Find the load current using:
- (a) Direct analysis
- (b) Source transformation method

### Part (a) — Direct Analysis
Using Ohm's Law on the series circuit:

$$I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_{internal} + R_{load}}$$

$$I_L = \frac{12}{4 + 8} = \frac{12}{12} = 1 \text{ A}$$

### Part (b) — Source Transformation
Convert the 12 V source in series with 4 Ω into an equivalent current source:

$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ A}$$

This 3 A current source is now in parallel with the 4 Ω resistor, and this combination drives the 8 Ω load.

Using the **current divider rule**, the current through the 8 Ω load is:

$$I_L = I_s \cdot \frac{R_{parallel}}{R_{parallel} + R_{load}}$$

$$I_L = 3 \cdot \frac{4}{4 + 8} = 3 \cdot \frac{4}{12} = 3 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \text{ A}$$

### Verification
Both methods yield $I_L = 1$ A. ✅ The transformation is **verified correct**.

## Analytical Matrix — Transformation Summary Table

| Original Source | Resistor Position | Transformation Step | Resulting Source | Resistor Position | Verification Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 V | Series with 5 Ω | Divide V by R | 2 A | Parallel with 5 Ω | $V_{oc} = 2 \times 5 = 10$ V ✓ |
| 3 A | Parallel with 6 Ω | Multiply I by R | 18 V | Series with 6 Ω | $I_{sc} = 18/6 = 3$ A ✓ |
| 24 V | Series with 12 Ω | Divide V by R | 2 A | Parallel with 12 Ω | $V_{oc} = 2 \times 12 = 24$ V ✓ |
| 5 A | Parallel with 10 Ω | Multiply I by R | 50 V | Series with 10 Ω | $I_{sc} = 50/10 = 5$ A ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Source Transformation Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["PRACTICAL VOLTAGE SOURCE"] --> B["VS IN SERIES WITH R"]
    B --> C{"IS R FINITE AND NON-ZERO?"}
    C -- YES --> D["CALCULATE IS = VS DIVIDED BY R"]
    C -- NO --> E["TRANSFORMATION NOT POSSIBLE"]
    D --> F["DRAW IS IN PARALLEL WITH R"]
    F --> G["ARROW POINTS TOWARD ORIGINAL POSITIVE TERMINAL"]
    G --> H["PRACTICAL CURRENT SOURCE EQUIVALENT"]
    H --> I["VERIFY USING VOC AND ISC TESTS"]
    I --> J["CONFIRMED EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]

    K["PRACTICAL CURRENT SOURCE"] --> L["IS IN PARALLEL WITH R"]
    L --> M{"IS R FINITE AND NON-ZERO?"}
    M -- YES --> N["CALCULATE VS = IS TIMES R"]
    M -- NO --> O["TRANSFORMATION NOT POSSIBLE"]
    N --> P["DRAW VS IN SERIES WITH R"]
    P --> Q["POSITIVE TERMINAL FACES ORIGINAL ARROW DIRECTION"]
    Q --> R["PRACTICAL VOLTAGE SOURCE EQUIVALENT"]
    R --> S["VERIFY USING VOC AND ISC TESTS"]
    S --> T["CONFIRMED EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT"]
```

## Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transformation

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["ORIGINAL CIRCUIT FORM"]
        A1["VOLTAGE SOURCE VS"] --> A2["SERIES RESISTOR R"]
        A2 --> A3["TERMINAL A"]
        A3 --> A4["TERMINAL B"]
        A4 --> A1
    end

    subgraph TRANSFORM["TRANSFORMATION ENGINE"]
        T1["APPLY OHM LAW: IS EQUALS VS DIVIDED BY R"]
        T2["KEEP RESISTANCE R UNCHANGED"]
        T3{"IS R GREATER THAN ZERO?"}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["EQUIVALENT CIRCUIT FORM"]
        O1["CURRENT SOURCE IS"] --> O2["PARALLEL RESISTOR R"]
        O2 --> O3["TERMINAL A"]
        O3 --> O4["TERMINAL B"]
        O4 --> O1
    end

    INPUT --> TRANSFORM
    TRANSFORM -- VALID --> OUTPUT
    TRANSFORM -- INVALID --> X1["ERROR: IDEAL SOURCE CANNOT BE TRANSFORMED"]
```

## Diagram 3: V-I Characteristic Equivalence Curve

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "V-I Characteristic: Both Forms Overlap"
    x-axis "Load Current I_L in Amperes" [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    y-axis "Terminal Voltage V_L in Volts" 0 --> 12
    line [12, 10, 8, 6, 4, 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Curve:** The straight line represents the V-I relationship at the external terminals. Both the original voltage source form and the transformed current source form produce **exactly this same line**, proving electrical equivalence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define source transformation. State the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

Source transformation is a circuit simplification technique used to convert a practical voltage source (V in series with R) into an equivalent practical current source (I in parallel with R), or vice versa, without changing the external circuit behavior.

**Applicable Conditions:**
1. The source must be a **practical source** (must have an internal resistance R).
2. The resistance R must be **finite and non-zero** (0 < R < ∞).
3. The transformation equation is $I = V/R$ or $V = I \cdot R$.
4. The arrow of the current source in the Norton form must point **toward the positive terminal** of the original voltage source.

---

### Question 2: Source Identification
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: A 20 V source is in series with a 5 Ω resistor. Find the equivalent current source representation.**

**Model Answer:**

Given:
- $V_s = 20$ V
- $R = 5$ Ω

Using the source transformation equation:

$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \text{ A}$$

**Equivalent Representation:** A **4 A current source** in **parallel with 5 Ω**, with the arrow pointing toward the positive terminal of the original 20 V source.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Comprehensive Circuit Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**Q: For the circuit shown below, find the current through the 6 Ω resistor using source transformation technique.**

*Circuit Description: A 24 V voltage source is in series with a 4 Ω resistor, which connects to a node. From this node, two parallel branches go to ground: one branch has a 12 Ω resistor, and the other branch has the 6 Ω resistor (the load).*

#### Part (a) — Perform Source Transformation [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Identify the practical voltage source.
- $V_s = 24$ V, $R = 4$ Ω (in series)

**Step 2:** Apply transformation formula:

$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** The 4 Ω resistor is now in parallel with the 6 A current source.

#### Part (b) — Compute Load Current Using Current Divider [7 Marks]

After transformation, we have a 6 A source feeding three parallel resistors: 4 Ω, 12 Ω, and 6 Ω.

**Step 1:** Find the equivalent parallel resistance seen by the current source.

First, combine the 4 Ω and 12 Ω in parallel:

$$R_{eq1} = \frac{4 \times 12}{4 + 12} = \frac{48}{16} = 3 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 2:** Now we have 3 Ω in parallel with 6 Ω:

$$R_{eq2} = \frac{3 \times 6}{3 + 6} = \frac{18}{9} = 2 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3:** Use current divider rule for the 6 Ω branch:

$$I_{6\Omega} = I_s \cdot \frac{R_{other}}{R_{other} + R_{6\Omega}}$$

$$I_{6\Omega} = 6 \cdot \frac{3}{3 + 6} = 6 \cdot \frac{3}{9} = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Final Answer:** The current through the 6 Ω resistor is **2 A**.

---

### Question B: Alternative — Norton Equivalent via Transformation
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**Q: A practical current source of 5 A is connected in parallel with a 10 Ω resistor. Convert this to an equivalent practical voltage source and verify using open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current tests.**

#### Part (a) — Conversion to Voltage Source [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Identify the practical current source.
- $I_s = 5$ A, $R = 10$ Ω (in parallel)

**Step 2:** Apply transformation formula:

$$V_s = I_s \cdot R = 5 \times 10 = 50 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent form: A **50 V voltage source** in **series with 10 Ω**, with the positive terminal facing the original arrow direction.

#### Part (b) — Verification via V_oc and I_sc [7 Marks]

**Open-Circuit Voltage Test (Thevenin Form):**
With terminals open, no current flows through the 10 Ω resistor, so there is **no voltage drop** across it. The terminal voltage equals the source voltage:

$$V_{oc} = V_s = 50 \text{ V}$$

**Short-Circuit Current Test (Norton Form):**
With terminals shorted, all 5 A flows through the short (zero resistance path), bypassing the 10 Ω:

$$I_{sc} = I_s = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Cross-Verification:**

$$R_{Th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{50}{5} = 10 \text{ Ω} \checkmark$$

The resistance matches the original R = 10 Ω, confirming **electrical equivalence**.

**Final Answer:** The equivalent voltage source is **50 V in series with 10 Ω**, verified successfully.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong arrow direction** in Norton form — arrow must point toward the + terminal of Thevenin source (2-mark penalty in 14-mark questions).
> 2. **Transforming ideal sources** — never transform a pure V source (R=0) or pure I source (R=∞).
> 3. **Forgetting to update dependent variables** — when the source is a controlled (dependent) source, the controlling variable must remain in the circuit; do NOT eliminate it during transformation.
> 4. **Mixing series-parallel reduction** — students often combine resistors before applying transformation. Always transform first, then reduce.
> 5. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units (V, A, Ω) in final answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Source transformation converts between **practical V-source (series R)** and **practical I-source (parallel R)**.
- ✅ The governing equation is **Ohm's Law**: $I = V/R$ or $V = I \cdot R$.
- ✅ The internal resistance **R stays the same** in both forms — only its position (series/parallel) changes.
- ✅ Current source arrow points **toward the + terminal** of the equivalent voltage source.
- ✅ **Ideal sources cannot be transformed** (division by zero or multiplication by infinity is undefined).
- ✅ Transformation preserves the **V-I characteristics** at external terminals.
- ✅ Useful for converting between **Thevenin and Norton equivalents**.
- ✅ Always verify using **open-circuit voltage (V_oc)** and **short-circuit current (I_sc)** tests.
- ✅ Power dissipation in R is **identical** in both representations: $P = I^2 R = V^2/R$.
- ✅ For **dependent sources**, keep the controlling variable intact in the circuit during transformation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thevenin's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thevenin's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Thevenin's Theorem (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> *"Any linear bilateral network consisting of independent and dependent voltage/current sources and passive elements (resistors) can be replaced, as far as the behaviour at a particular pair of terminals is concerned, by an equivalent circuit comprising a single voltage source Vth in series with a single resistance Rth."*

In simple KTU-board language: **Thevenin's Theorem lets you collapse an entire messy circuit into just TWO components** — one voltage source and one resistor — and the load connected to the original circuit will not be able to tell the difference.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are at a coffee shop and want to know how strong your drink will be. The barista has a giant machine with 50 tubes, 30 valves, and 5 heating elements. You do not need to know what is inside the machine — you only need to know the **pressure at the nozzle** and the **flow resistance of the pipe**. Thevenin's Theorem does the same thing for an electrical network:

- The **pressure at the nozzle** = Open-circuit voltage (**Vth**)
- The **flow resistance of the pipe** = Equivalent resistance (**Rth**)
- The **cup of coffee** = Your **load resistor (RL)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The theorem is valid only for **linear bilateral** networks. It applies at the **terminals** of interest — internally, the original and Thevenin circuits are completely different, but they are **electrically indistinguishable** from the load's perspective. Standard unit for **Vth is Volts (V)** and for **Rth is Ohms (Ω)**.

### Key Terminology
- **Terminals (a–b):** The two points where the load is connected and across which equivalence is established.
- **Vth (Thevenin Voltage):** The open-circuit voltage measured across terminals a–b with the load removed.
- **Rth (Thevenin Resistance):** The equivalent resistance seen from terminals a–b with all independent sources deactivated.
- **Linear Network:** A network where the superposition principle holds — outputs are proportional to inputs.
- **Bilateral Elements:** Elements with the same V–I relationship for both directions of current flow.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Theoretical Foundation — Why Thevenin Works
At the heart of Thevenin's Theorem is the **linearity** of the network. For a linear circuit, the voltage across the load terminals (Vab) and the current through the load (IL) are linearly related. This means we can write a single straight-line equation of the form:

$$V_{ab} = V_{th} - I_L \cdot R_{th}$$

This is identical to the equation of a simple loop with one voltage source Vth and one series resistor Rth feeding a load. So any linear network must — by definition — be reducible to this form. That is the *why* behind the theorem.

### Procedural Steps for Solving a Thevenin Problem (KTU Standard)

1. **Identify the load resistor (RL)** that you want to analyze. This is the resistor whose current/voltage/power is being asked.
2. **Disconnect (remove) the load** and clearly mark the two open terminals as **a** and **b**.
3. **Calculate Vth = Voc** — the open-circuit voltage across terminals a–b. Use any standard method: mesh analysis, nodal analysis, or KVL/KCL on the resulting simpler network.
4. **Deactivate all independent sources:**
   - Independent **voltage sources → short circuit (replace with a wire)**
   - Independent **current sources → open circuit (replace with a break)**
   - *Note: Dependent sources are NOT deactivated — they must remain as-is.*
5. **Calculate Rth** — the equivalent resistance looking back into terminals a–b. Use series-parallel simplification. If dependent sources exist, use the **test-source method**: Rth = Voc / Isc.
6. **Draw the Thevenin equivalent** — a single voltage source Vth in series with Rth, connected across terminals a–b.
7. **Reconnect the load** and compute the required quantity (IL, VL, PL) using Ohm's Law:
$$I_L = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} \quad , \quad V_L = I_L \cdot R_L \quad , \quad P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield summary** that KTU examiners expect you to memorize verbatim.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thevenin Voltage | $V_{th} = V_{oc}\big\vert_{R_L \to \infty}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Thevenin Resistance (sources zeroed) | $R_{th} = R_{eq}$ looking into a–b with V→short, I→open | Ohms (Ω) |
| 3 | Thevenin Resistance (test-source method) | $R_{th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$ | Ω, used when dependent sources are present |
| 4 | Load Current | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | Load Voltage | $V_L = V_{th} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{th} + R_L}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Maximum Power Transfer Condition | $R_L = R_{th}$ | Ω, derived from Thevenin model |
| 7 | Maximum Power Delivered to Load | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{th}}$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Source Transformation (Vth ↔ Norton) | $V_{th} = I_N \cdot R_N$  and  $R_{th} = R_N$ | V–A equivalence |

### Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **Power System Analysis:** A complex power grid is reduced to a single Vth–Rth pair to study fault conditions at a particular bus.
- **Electronics Circuit Design:** Biasing a transistor amplifier is analyzed by replacing the surrounding network with its Thevenin equivalent at the base–emitter terminals.
- **Load Testing:** Battery and source testers use Thevenin parameters to characterize cells quickly.
- **Maximum Power Transfer Design:** Antenna matching networks and audio amplifier output stages are designed using the $R_L = R_{th}$ condition derived from the Thevenin equivalent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Part A — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard Type)

**Problem Statement:** *In the circuit shown below, find the current flowing through the 4 Ω load resistor using Thevenin's Theorem.*

```
        a -----[ 4 Ω ]----- b
        |                   |
       [+] 12V              |
        |                   |
       [2 Ω]               [6 Ω]
        |                   |
        +-------------------+
                 (ref)
```

- A **12 V** independent voltage source is in series with a **2 Ω** resistor on the left branch.
- A **6 Ω** resistor is on the right branch.
- A **4 Ω** load resistor is connected between terminals **a** and **b**.

---

### Step 1 — Remove the Load and Mark Terminals a–b
With the 4 Ω resistor removed, the terminals **a** and **b** are now open. The remaining circuit is a single closed loop: 12 V → 2 Ω → 6 Ω → back to source.

---

### Step 2 — Calculate the Open-Circuit Voltage Vth = Voc
Since terminals a–b are open, no current flows out of them. The current in the remaining loop is determined solely by the series combination of 2 Ω and 6 Ω.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{loop} &= \frac{V_s}{R_{series}} = \frac{12 \text{ V}}{2 \, \Omega + 6 \, \Omega} \\[4pt]
I_{loop} &= \frac{12}{8} = 1.5 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

The voltage across the 6 Ω resistor (which is the voltage from terminal b to the reference, and from a to the reference in the right direction) gives Vab. Taking the polarity with a positive at the top of the 6 Ω resistor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= V_{oc} = I_{loop} \times 6 \, \Omega \\[4pt]
V_{th} &= 1.5 \times 6 = 9 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** The 12 V source drives current through 2 Ω and 6 Ω in series. The 6 Ω resistor is the only element between terminal b and the negative reference, so its voltage drop equals Vab.

---

### Step 3 — Deactivate Sources and Find Rth
Deactivate the 12 V independent voltage source by replacing it with a **short circuit**. Now looking back from terminals a–b, the 2 Ω and 6 Ω resistors appear in **series**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{th} &= 2 \, \Omega + 6 \, \Omega \\[4pt]
R_{th} &= 8 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Draw the Thevenin Equivalent Circuit
The Thevenin equivalent is a single loop: **9 V source in series with 8 Ω**, feeding the 4 Ω load.

```
        a -----[ 8 Ω ]--- (+) 9V (-) --- b
        |                                   |
        +-----------[ 4 Ω ]-----------------+
```

---

### Step 5 — Calculate the Load Current
Apply Ohm's Law on the single-loop Thevenin equivalent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} \\[4pt]
I_L &= \frac{9 \text{ V}}{8 \, \Omega + 4 \, \Omega} = \frac{9}{12} \\[4pt]
I_L &= 0.75 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Cross-Verification Using KVL on the Thevenin Loop
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} - I_L R_{th} - I_L R_L &= 0 \\[4pt]
9 - 0.75(8) - 0.75(4) &= 9 - 6 - 3 = 0 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The solution is consistent.

---

### Part B — Mathematical Justification (Theoretical Derivation)

**Claim:** For any linear bilateral network with two accessible terminals a–b, the network can be replaced by Vth in series with Rth.

**Proof Sketch Using Superposition:**

Let a **1 V test source** be applied externally across terminals a–b. Because the network is linear, the response (any internal current or voltage) is proportional to this 1 V input. Let the resulting short-circuit current be $I_{sc}$ and the open-circuit voltage be $V_{oc}$.

By **linearity**, if a voltage $V$ is applied instead of 1 V, the resulting open-circuit voltage scales as:

$$
V_{ab} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{th}
$$

where $R_{th} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$ is the proportionality constant (resistance). This is precisely the KVL equation of a series circuit containing $V_{oc}$ as the source and $R_{th}$ as the series resistor.

Therefore, an **external observer at terminals a–b cannot distinguish** between the original network and the (Vth, Rth) series combination. ∎

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Norton Theorem

| Property | Thevenin Equivalent | Norton Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Voltage source $V_{th}$ | Current source $I_N$ |
| Internal element | Series resistance $R_{th}$ | Parallel resistance $R_N$ |
| Source value relation | $V_{th} = I_N R_N$ | $I_N = V_{th} / R_{th}$ |
| Best for finding | Open-circuit voltage, load voltage | Short-circuit current, parallel loads |
| Internal resistance | $R_{th} = R_N$ | $R_N = R_{th}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Thevenin Reduction Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the *methodology flow* of how an arbitrary linear network is reduced to its Thevenin equivalent — this is the exact sequence KTU examiners expect you to write in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Linear Network with Load RL]) --> stepB[Step 1: Identify Load Resistor RL]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Remove RL and Mark Terminals a and b]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Find Vth equals Open Circuit Voltage Voc across a to b]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Deactivate Independent Sources - V becomes Short - I becomes Open]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Find Rth equals Equivalent Resistance looking into a to b]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Construct Thevenin Equivalent - Vth in series with Rth]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Reconnect Load RL and Compute IL equals Vth divided by Rth plus RL]
    stepH --> stepI([End: IL, VL, PL Calculated])

    stepJ{Dependent Sources Present?} -. No .-> stepE
    stepJ -. Yes .-> stepK[Use Test Source Method: Rth equals Voc divided by Isc]
    stepK --> stepF
```

### Diagram 2 — Side-by-Side Network Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph original [Original Linear Network]
        a1[Terminal a] --> b1[Resistor R1]
        b1 --> c1[Voltage Source V1]
        c1 --> d1[Resistor R2]
        d1 --> e1[Current Source I1]
        e1 --> f1[Resistor R3]
        f1 --> g1[Terminal b]
    end

    subgraph thevenin [Thevenin Equivalent]
        a2[Terminal a] --> b2[Resistor Rth]
        b2 --> c2[Voltage Source Vth]
        c2 --> d2[Terminal b]
    end

    note[Behaviour at terminals a to b is identical for any load RL] -.-> original
    note -.-> thevenin
```

### Diagram 3 — Worked Example Schematic (12 V, 2 Ω, 6 Ω, 4 Ω Circuit)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sourceA[12V DC Source] --> res1[2 ohm resistor]
    res1 --> nodeA[Node a]
    nodeA --> load[4 ohm Load RL]
    load --> nodeB[Node b]
    nodeB --> res2[6 ohm resistor]
    res2 --> refA[Reference Node]
    sourceA --- refA

    thevA[Equivalent Vth equals 9V] --> thevR[Equivalent Rth equals 8 ohm]
    thevR --> thevL[Load RL equals 4 ohm]
    thevL --> thevA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read-Only Equivalence:** The three blocks in Diagram 3 — the original network, the Thevenin equivalent, and the load — produce the **same IL = 0.75 A** through the 4 Ω resistor. This is the central teaching point of the theorem.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Thevenin's theorem. Mention any two limitations of the theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** Any linear bilateral network containing voltage sources, current sources, and resistors can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single voltage source $V_{th}$ in series with a single resistance $R_{th}$, as far as the behaviour at a particular pair of terminals is concerned.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** (i) It is applicable **only to linear bilateral networks**; it cannot be applied to non-linear elements like diodes, transistors in saturation, or saturable reactors. (ii) It gives equivalence **only at the two terminals of interest**; the internal voltages and currents of the original and equivalent circuits will generally be different.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**How do you determine the Thevenin resistance (Rth) of a network containing dependent sources?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Method (3 marks):** When **dependent sources** are present, the standard "deactivate sources and find equivalent resistance" procedure **fails**, because deactivating a dependent source would change the network's defining equations.
- **Correct Procedure:** Apply either of these two equivalent methods:
  (i) **Open-Circuit / Short-Circuit Method:** $R_{th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$, where both are computed with the dependent sources left intact.
  (ii) **Test Source Method:** Apply a known test voltage (1 V) or test current (1 A) at the open terminals a–b, find the resulting current or voltage at the source, and compute $R_{th} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** State and prove Thevenin's theorem for a linear bilateral network. Mention its practical significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (2 marks):** As defined earlier — any linear bilateral two-terminal network can be replaced by an equivalent circuit of a single voltage source $V_{th}$ in series with a single resistance $R_{th}$.

**Proof Outline (4 marks):**
1. Consider a linear network with accessible terminals a–b. Let $V_{oc}$ be the open-circuit voltage at a–b.
2. Apply a 1 V test source externally across a–b. Let the resulting short-circuit current through the source be $I_{sc}$.
3. By linearity of the network, the response is directly proportional to the input. Therefore the network can be characterized by a single linear relationship:
$V_{ab} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{th}$ where $R_{th} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$.
4. This equation is identical to the KVL of a simple series loop containing $V_{oc}$ and $R_{th}$. Hence the network is equivalent to a Thevenin source.

**Practical Significance (1 mark):** Simplifies analysis of complex networks (e.g., power grids, electronic biasing circuits) by reducing them to a single-loop model.

**(b)** For the circuit given below, find the Thevenin equivalent across terminals A–B and hence calculate the current through the 5 Ω load. **(7 Marks)**

```
        +---[ 10 V ]---[ 2 Ω ]---A---[ 5 Ω ]---B---[ 4 Ω ]---+
        |                                                       |
        +-------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Open-circuit voltage Vth (3 marks):** With 5 Ω load removed, current flows in the outer loop.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{10 \text{ V}}{2 \, \Omega + 4 \, \Omega} = \frac{10}{6} = \frac{5}{3} \text{ A} \\[4pt]
V_{th} &= I \times 4 \, \Omega = \frac{5}{3} \times 4 = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.67 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Thevenin resistance Rth (2 marks):** Short the 10 V source. Looking into A–B, 2 Ω and 4 Ω are in series.

$$
R_{th} = 2 + 4 = 6 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Load current (2 marks):**

$$
I_L = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} = \frac{20/3}{6 + 5} = \frac{20}{33} \approx 0.606 \text{ A}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Find the Thevenin equivalent of the network shown across terminals X–Y, and use it to determine the value of load resistance $R_L$ for maximum power transfer and the corresponding maximum power. **(7 Marks)**

*Network:* A 24 V source in series with 6 Ω, connected to a parallel combination of 12 Ω and a 4 Ω branch that has a 12 V source in series with it. The load $R_L$ is connected across X–Y.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Open-circuit voltage Vth (3 marks):** With $R_L$ removed, find $V_{XY}$ using nodal analysis. Let bottom rail be reference. The 24 V source drives the network. The voltage at the top node (X) is $V_X$, and Y is the reference.

Applying KCL at the top node with the 4 Ω–12 V branch and 12 Ω resistor:

$$
\frac{V_X - 24}{6} + \frac{V_X}{12} + \frac{V_X - 12}{4} = 0
$$

Multiply through by 12:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(V_X - 24) + V_X + 3(V_X - 12) &= 0 \\
2V_X - 48 + V_X + 3V_X - 36 &= 0 \\
6V_X - 84 &= 0 \\
V_X = 14 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $V_{th} = V_{XY} = 14 \text{ V}$.

**Step 2 — Thevenin resistance Rth (2 marks):** Short both voltage sources. Looking into X–Y, the 6 Ω, 12 Ω, and 4 Ω resistors are all in parallel between X and ground.

$$
\frac{1}{R_{th}} = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{12} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{2 + 1 + 3}{12} = \frac{6}{12}
$$

$$
R_{th} = 2 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Power Transfer (2 marks):** For maximum power, $R_L = R_{th} = 2 \, \Omega$.

$$
P_{\max} = \frac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{th}} = \frac{14^2}{4 \times 2} = \frac{196}{8} = 24.5 \text{ W}
$$

**(b)** Explain the source transformation technique. Convert a 12 V source in series with 4 Ω into its Norton equivalent, and verify by computing both the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theory (2 marks):** Source transformation is the reciprocal of Thevenin–Norton conversion. A practical voltage source $V_s$ in series with $R_s$ is equivalent to a practical current source $I_s = V_s / R_s$ in parallel with the same $R_s$.

**Conversion (2 marks):**

$$
I_N = \frac{V_s}{R_s} = \frac{12 \text{ V}}{4 \, \Omega} = 3 \text{ A} \quad ; \quad R_N = R_s = 4 \, \Omega
$$

**Verification (3 marks):**
- **Open-Circuit Voltage (Thevenin side):** With load open, no current flows in the series branch, so $V_{oc} = V_s = 12$ V.
- **Open-Circuit Voltage (Norton side):** With load open, all 3 A from the current source must flow through the 4 Ω parallel resistance, producing $V_{oc} = 3 \text{ A} \times 4 \, \Omega = 12$ V. ✓
- **Short-Circuit Current (Thevenin side):** $I_{sc} = V_s / R_s = 12/4 = 3$ A.
- **Short-Circuit Current (Norton side):** With terminals shorted, the 4 Ω parallel resistance is bypassed, so all 3 A flows through the short. ✓

Both representations are equivalent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to remove the load first** — The most common blunder. Vth must be computed with $R_L$ removed; otherwise the load voltage is mistakenly treated as $V_{oc}$.
> 2. **Deactivating dependent sources** — Dependent sources must NEVER be shorted or opened. Only *independent* sources are deactivated in Step 4. Deactivating a dependent source will yield a wrong $R_{th}$ and full marks will be cut.
> 3. **Polarity errors in Vth** — Always draw arrows for $V_{th}$ polarity. Reversed polarity in the Thevenin equivalent will reverse the load current direction and cost at least 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up parallel and series when finding Rth** — After deactivation, draw the resulting resistor network clearly. A common mistake is treating series resistors as parallel.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — KTU valuation key explicitly checks for **V**, **A**, and **Ω** units. Marks are deducted if units are missing.
> 6. **Not writing the KVL/KCL equations in the proof** — In the 7-mark "state and prove" question, simply stating the theorem without writing the linear network analysis earns only partial credit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Thevenin's Theorem reduces a **linear bilateral** network to **one voltage source $V_{th}$ in series with one resistance $R_{th}$** at the terminals of interest.
- **$V_{th} = V_{oc}$** with the load removed.
- **$R_{th}$** = equivalent resistance at a–b with **independent voltage sources shorted and independent current sources opened**.
- If **dependent sources** are present, use **$R_{th} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$** or the test-source method.
- Load current: $I_L = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L}$.
- Maximum power transfer condition: $R_L = R_{th}$, giving $P_{\max} = \dfrac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{th}}$.
- Source transformation: $V_{th} = I_N \cdot R_N$ and $R_{th} = R_N$.
- **Validity:** Only for **linear bilateral** networks; **equivalence is at the terminals only**, not internally.
- Always **re-check polarity** of $V_{th}$ and **include units** (V, A, Ω, W) in the final answer.
- The theorem is the **foundation** for understanding **Norton's theorem**, **Maximum Power Transfer**, **Millman's theorem**, and **superposition-based network analysis** in higher modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Norton's and Maximum Power Transfer Theorems - Reciprocity Theorem. Two Port Networks: Driving Point and Transfer Functions - Z
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Norton's and Maximum Power Transfer Theorems - Reciprocity Theorem. Two Port Networks: Driving Point and Transfer Functions - Z

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Norton's Theorem

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Norton's Theorem states that any linear, bilateral, active network containing voltage and current sources (and resistors) can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single current source $I_N$ in **parallel** with a single resistance $R_N$ (or impedance $Z_N$ in AC), where the load resistance $R_L$ is connected across the equivalent circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Note:** Norton's Theorem is essentially the *current-source dual* of Thevenin's Theorem. The two theorems are mathematically interchangeable and yield identical load voltages/currents.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a complex electrical network as a *black box* with two external terminals. No matter how tangled the internal wiring is (resistors, batteries, transistors behaving linearly), when you look at the box from outside, the box behaves *exactly* like one battery (current source) and one resistor (parallel internal resistance). This is incredibly useful because you can solve the rest of the circuit using simple parallel-circuit rules.

**Key Identities of Norton Equivalent:**
- **$I_N$ (Norton Current):** The short-circuit current flowing through the load terminals when $R_L$ is replaced by a short circuit (wire).
- **$R_N$ (Norton Resistance):** The equivalent resistance looking back into the network from the open-circuited load terminals, with all independent sources deactivated (voltage sources → short, current sources → open).

---

### 1.2 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (MPTT)

**Formal Definition:**
> The Maximum Power Transfer Theorem states that in a linear bilateral network, the maximum power is delivered to the load $R_L$ from a source when the load resistance equals the Thevenin (or Norton) equivalent resistance of the source network, i.e., $R_L = R_{Th} = R_N$.

**For DC Resistive Circuits:**
$$R_L = R_{Th}$$

**For AC / Reactive Circuits (Purely Resistive Load):**
$$\vert Z_L \vert = \vert Z_{Th} \vert$$

**For Complex / Reactive Loads (Conjugate Matching):**
$$Z_L = Z_{Th}^* \quad \text{(complex conjugate)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** In communication systems (antenna matching, audio amplifiers), the goal is to maximize *signal power transfer*, not efficiency. In power transmission (grid lines), efficiency matters more than power transfer.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a water pump pushing water through a pipe to a turbine. If the pipe is too narrow (high $R_L$), very little water flows. If the pipe is too wide (low $R_L$), the water flows easily but with very low pressure. The *sweet spot*—where the turbine extracts maximum hydraulic power—is when the pipe's resistance matches the pump's internal resistance.

**Standard Metrics:**
- **Efficiency at MPTT (DC):** $\eta = 50\%$
- **Efficiency at MPTT (AC, conjugate match):** $\eta = 50\%$

---

### 1.3 Reciprocity Theorem

**Formal Definition:**
> The Reciprocity Theorem states that in any linear, bilateral, single-source network, the ratio of excitation (applied source) to the response (measured output) remains constant when the positions of the excitation and response are interchanged.

**Mathematical Statement:**
If a voltage source $V_s$ applied at port 1 produces a current $I$ at port 2, then the *same* voltage source $V_s$ applied at port 2 (with port 1 now being the output) will produce the *same* current $I$ at port 1.

$$\frac{V_s}{I_2} \bigg\vert_{\text{source at port 1}} = \frac{V_s}{I_1} \bigg\vert_{\text{source at port 2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Reciprocity holds *only* for single-source networks. Networks with dependent sources generally do **not** satisfy reciprocity. Also, the theorem is valid only when initial stored energies in inductors/capacitors are zero.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Consider a hallway with a speaker at one end and a microphone at the other. If a sound signal of amplitude $X$ at the speaker produces a recorded amplitude $Y$ at the microphone, swapping the speaker and microphone (same signal $X$) should ideally produce the *same* recorded amplitude $Y$ at the new microphone position. This is *acoustic reciprocity*—the electrical version is mathematically identical.

---

### 1.4 Two-Port Networks — Driving Point & Transfer Functions

**Formal Definition of a Two-Port Network:**
> A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network with two distinct pairs of terminals: an **input port** (Port 1, with terminals $1, 1'$) where the signal is applied, and an **output port** (Port 2, with terminals $2, 2'$) where the load is connected.

**Standard Conventions:**
- $V_1, I_1$: voltage and current at the input port
- $V_2, I_2$: voltage and current at the output port
- Both currents are assumed to *enter* the network at the upper terminal of each port

**Definition of Driving Point Functions:**
> **Driving Point Functions** are network functions where the input and output variables are measured at the *same* port. They represent the impedance or admittance "seen" looking into a port.

**Definition of Transfer Functions:**
> **Transfer Functions** are network functions where the input variable is at one port and the output variable is at the *other* port. They describe how the signal is "transferred" through the network.

**Z-Parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters):**
> The Z-parameters express the port voltages ($V_1, V_2$) as linear functions of the port currents ($I_1, I_2$).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
A two-port network is like a *black-box amplifier* with an "input jack" and an "output jack." Z-parameters are one of four standard "ID cards" (Z, Y, h, ABCD) that fully describe how this black box behaves—telling you exactly what voltage will appear at the output for a given current injected at the input.

---

### 1.5 Network Function Classification Table

| Function Type | Ratio | Port Identity | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driving-Point Impedance | $V_1/I_1$ or $V_2/I_2$ | Same port | Input impedance seen by source |
| Driving-Point Admittance | $I_1/V_1$ or $I_2/V_2$ | Same port | Input admittance seen by source |
| Transfer Voltage Ratio | $V_2/V_1$ | Different ports | Voltage gain |
| Transfer Current Ratio | $I_2/I_1$ | Different ports | Current gain |
| Transfer Impedance | $V_2/I_1$ | Different ports | Transimpedance |
| Transfer Admittance | $I_2/V_1$ | Different ports | Transadmittance |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Norton's Theorem — Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1:** Remove the load resistance $R_L$ from the network, leaving the load terminals **open**.

**Step 2:** Calculate the **Norton Current $I_N$**:
- Place a short-circuit wire across the load terminals.
- Compute the current flowing through this short circuit—this is $I_N$.

**Step 3:** Calculate the **Norton Resistance $R_N$**:
- Deactivate all independent sources: voltage sources → short circuit, current sources → open circuit.
- Compute the equivalent resistance looking back into the terminals—this is $R_N$.

**Step 4:** Construct the Norton equivalent: current source $I_N$ in **parallel** with $R_N$, and reconnect $R_L$ across them.

**Step 5 (Verification):** The load current is given by the current-divider rule:
$$I_L = I_N \cdot \frac{R_N}{R_N + R_L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work (The 'Why' and 'How'):** Norton's theorem is rooted in the **superposition principle** and the linearity of the network. When the source is replaced by a short, the open-circuit voltage at the terminals and the short-circuit current must be related by $V_{OC} = I_N \cdot R_N$ — the same constraint as a Thevenin source. So the two equivalents describe the *exact same* external behavior.

**Conversion between Thevenin and Norton:**
$$V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N \quad \text{and} \quad R_{Th} = R_N$$

---

### 2.2 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem — Detailed Logic

**For a DC Resistive Network (purely resistive):**

**Step 1:** Find the Thevenin equivalent ($V_{Th}, R_{Th}$) seen by the load terminals.

**Step 2:** The power delivered to the load is:
$$P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = \left(\frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}\right)^2 \cdot R_L$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and set $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$:
$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0 \implies R_L = R_{Th}$$

**Step 4:** Maximum power value:
$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{I_N^2 \cdot R_N}{4}$$

**For AC Circuits (Phasor Domain):**

| Load Type | Condition for Max Power | Maximum Power |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purely Resistive $R_L$ | $R_L = \vert Z_{Th} \vert$ | $\frac{\vert V_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_L}$ |
| Variable $R_L$ only (fixed $X_L$) | $R_L = \sqrt{R_{Th}^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2}$ | depends on configuration |
| Variable $Z_L$ (conjugate) | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$ | $\frac{\vert V_{Th} \vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ |

**Key Physical Insight (Efficiency Trade-off):**
At the MPTT condition, exactly *half* the power generated by the source is dissipated inside the source resistance itself, and the other half is delivered to the load. This is why MPTT is favored in *low-power* applications (electronics, signal processing) and avoided in *high-power* applications (power transmission, motor drives).

---

### 2.3 Reciprocity Theorem — Detailed Conditions

**Applicability Conditions (ALL must be satisfied):**
1. The network must be **linear** (obeys Ohm's law, superposition applies).
2. The network must be **bilateral** (current flows identically in both directions for any element).
3. The network must contain **only one independent source**.
4. The network must be **initially at rest** (zero initial conditions on energy-storage elements).
5. The **time constant** of the network is the same before and after the source-response swap (passive reciprocity).

**Formal Mathematical Statements (Three Forms):**

**Form 1 (Ebers-Moll):** In a linear bilateral single-source network, if a voltage $V$ at port A causes a current $I$ at port B, then the same voltage $V$ at port B will cause the same current $I$ at port A.

**Form 2 (Admittance Form):** For a two-port network, the off-diagonal admittance parameters are equal:
$$Y_{12} = Y_{21}$$

**Form 3 (Impedance Form):** For a two-port network, the off-diagonal impedance parameters are equal:
$$Z_{12} = Z_{21}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The reciprocity condition $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ is the same as the symmetry condition for Z-parameters. A *reciprocal* network is one whose Z-matrix (or Y-matrix) is symmetric.

---

### 2.4 Two-Port Network Z-Parameters — Detailed Analysis

**Generalized Two-Port Network Conventions:**

The four variables $V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2$ are related by two independent equations. The Z-parameter representation writes the voltages as functions of the currents:

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$
$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

**In Matrix Form:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Physical Meaning of Each Z-Parameter (Obtained by Setting Currents to Zero = Open-Circuit Test):**

| Parameter | Definition | Measurement Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Z_{11}$ | Driving-point impedance at port 1 | $I_2 = 0$ (port 2 open): $Z_{11} = V_1/I_1$ |
| $Z_{22}$ | Driving-point impedance at port 2 | $I_1 = 0$ (port 1 open): $Z_{22} = V_2/I_2$ |
| $Z_{21}$ | Forward transfer impedance | $I_2 = 0$ (port 2 open): $Z_{21} = V_2/I_1$ |
| $Z_{12}$ | Reverse transfer impedance | $I_1 = 0$ (port 1 open): $Z_{12} = V_1/I_2$ |

**Reciprocity in Z-Form:** A network is reciprocal if and only if:
$$Z_{12} = Z_{21}$$

**Symmetry in Z-Form:** A network is symmetric if and only if:
$$Z_{11} = Z_{22}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Z-parameters are extensively used in **transistor small-signal modeling**, **transmission line analysis**, and **filter network design**. They are particularly useful when the natural port condition is *open circuit* (e.g., field-effect transistors at low frequencies).

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Theorem / Concept | Core Formula | Condition / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Norton Current | $I_N = I_{SC}$ (short-circuit current) | Ampere (A) |
| Norton Resistance | $R_N = V_{OC} / I_{SC}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| Load Current (Norton) | $I_L = I_N \cdot R_N / (R_N + R_L)$ | Ampere (A) |
| Thevenin $\leftrightarrow$ Norton | $V_{Th} = I_N R_N$ | Volt (V) |
| Max Power Transfer (DC) | $R_L = R_{Th}$ | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| Max Power Transfer (AC, complex) | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$ | $\Omega$ |
| Max Power (DC) | $P_{max} = V_{Th}^2 / (4 R_{Th})$ | Watt (W) |
| Efficiency at MPTT | $\eta = 50\%$ (DC), $50\%$ (AC conj.) | dimensionless |
| Reciprocity (Z-form) | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Reciprocity (Y-form) | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | Siemens (S) |
| Z-parameter eq. 1 | $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$ | Volt |
| Z-parameter eq. 2 | $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$ | Volt |
| $Z_{11}$ | $V_1/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2=0}$ | $\Omega$ |
| $Z_{22}$ | $V_2/I_2 \big\vert_{I_1=0}$ | $\Omega$ |
| $Z_{21}$ | $V_2/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2=0}$ | $\Omega$ |
| $Z_{12}$ | $V_1/I_2 \big\vert_{I_1=0}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Driving-point impedance | $Z_{in} = V_1/I_1$ | $\Omega$ |
| Transfer voltage ratio | $G_v = V_2/V_1$ | dimensionless |
| Transfer current ratio | $\alpha = I_2/I_1$ | dimensionless |
| Transimpedance | $Z_T = V_2/I_1$ | $\Omega$ |
| Transadmittance | $Y_T = I_2/V_1$ | Siemens (S) |

**Unit Convention Reminder:**
- **Impedance Z:** Ohm ($\Omega$) — capital $Z$ for phasor (complex), lowercase $z$ may be used in s-domain.
- **Admittance Y:** Siemens (S) — reciprocal of impedance.
- **Power P:** Watt (W).
- **Voltage V:** Volt (V); **Current I:** Ampere (A).
