# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Alternating Current (AC)** is a periodic electrical quantity (current or voltage) whose magnitude reverses direction periodically with time, while its polarity alternates between positive and negative half-cycles. The most commercially and academically significant form is the **sinusoidal AC waveform**, mathematically described as:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

where every term has a precise meaning in the **2024 Scheme B.Tech** syllabus. For the Indian power grid (operated by the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)** and the **Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (CERC)**), the standard supply frequency is **50 Hz**, while the standard domestic RMS voltage is **230 V (single-phase)** and **415 V (three-phase)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> This module transitions the learner from the steady-state DC analysis of Module 1 into the time-varying domain of **Single-Phase AC Circuits**. The Introduction module lays the conceptual foundation: generation of sinusoidal EMF, waveform parameters, RMS/Average values, and the **Phasor concept** — all of which are prerequisites for solving R, L, C series/parallel circuits in subsequent sub-modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a swing in a playground. The swing moves **back and forth** about a central equilibrium point. At the extreme ends, its velocity becomes zero, while at the center, its velocity is at its maximum. An **AC voltage/current behaves identically**:

- The **equilibrium point** is the zero-axis of the waveform.
- The **extreme positions** of the swing correspond to the **positive and negative peaks** $\pm V_m$.
- The **speed of the swing** at the center is analogous to the **rate of change of current**, which is maximum when crossing zero.

> A second, more engineering-oriented analogy is the **reciprocating water pump**: it pushes water forward, then sucks it back, producing a rhythmic, alternating flow — exactly the same principle on which an **alternator** (AC generator) works, where a coil rotates within a magnetic field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU Board Exams**
> - Standard Indian Grid Frequency: **f = 50 Hz** (Angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ rad/s)
> - Standard Domestic RMS Voltage: **$V_{rms} = 230$ V** (Peak $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 \approx 325$ V)
> - Form Factor of Pure Sine Wave: **$K_f = 1.11$**
> - Peak (Crest) Factor of Pure Sine Wave: **$K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$**

## 1.3 Why AC? — The Engineering Motivation

DC (Direct Current), while simple, suffers from a major commercial drawback: **voltage transformation**. AC, due to the principle of **electromagnetic induction** and Faraday's Law, can be efficiently stepped up or down using **transformers**, enabling economical long-distance power transmission. This is the single most important reason why **global power generation, transmission, and distribution networks are AC-based**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Relevance for Petroleum Engineers (Course Context PERUT417):** AC drives the prime movers, pumps, and compressor motors used extensively in upstream and downstream petroleum operations. Understanding AC fundamentals is therefore not merely an academic exercise but a direct prerequisite for the electrical side of oil-rig instrumentation and refinery motor control centers (MCCs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sinusoidal Waveform — Anatomy of an AC Signal

A general sinusoid has **three independent parameters**:

1. **Amplitude ($V_m$ or $I_m$)** — the peak (maximum) value reached by the waveform.
2. **Angular Frequency ($\omega$)** — the rate at which the waveform's argument advances, measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
3. **Phase Angle ($\phi$)** — the angular displacement of the waveform with respect to a chosen reference axis, measured in **radians or degrees**.

The **time period (T)** is the time required to complete one full cycle:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

The **frequency (f)** is the number of cycles per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.

## 2.2 Average Value, RMS Value, Form Factor, and Peak Factor

The **average value** of a pure sinusoid over a *complete cycle* is mathematically **zero**, because the positive half-cycle area cancels the negative half-cycle area. Hence, in KTU problems, "average value" always implicitly refers to the **half-cycle average** (i.e., the average over one half-wave, for either half-wave rectified or full-wave rectified interpretations).

The **Root Mean Square (RMS) value** (also called the *effective value*) of a periodic current is defined as the DC equivalent that produces the same heating effect ($I^2R$ losses) in a resistor over the same time period.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RMS?** A DC current of 1 A and an AC current of 1 A (RMS) will heat a resistor by the *same amount*. This makes RMS the only physically meaningful magnitude when computing real **power ($P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$)** in AC circuits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **high-yield formula set** that examiners expect to be applied without derivation during board examinations. Memorize every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula (Sine Wave) | Value for Standard Sine | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Instantaneous Voltage | $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | — | Value at any time $t$ |
| 2 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | $314$ rad/s (for 50 Hz) | Rate of phase change |
| 3 | Time Period | $T = 1 / f$ | $20$ ms (for 50 Hz) | Duration of one full cycle |
| 4 | RMS Value of Voltage | $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ | $0.707 \cdot V_m$ | DC-equivalent heating value |
| 5 | RMS Value of Current | $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$ | $0.707 \cdot I_m$ | DC-equivalent heating value |
| 6 | Average Value (Full-Wave) | $V_{avg} = 2 V_m / \pi$ | $0.637 \cdot V_m$ | Mean of $\vert \sin \vert$ over $T$ |
| 7 | Average Value (Half-Wave) | $V_{avg} = V_m / \pi$ | $0.318 \cdot V_m$ | Mean over half-cycle only |
| 8 | Form Factor | $K_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg}$ | $1.11$ | Ratio of RMS to average |
| 9 | Peak (Crest) Factor | $K_p = V_m / V_{rms}$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ | Ratio of peak to RMS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva & Board:** $V_{rms} : V_{avg} : V_m \; = \; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} : \frac{2}{\pi} : 1 \; \approx \; 0.707 : 0.637 : 1.000$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The RMS, average, form factor, and peak factor parameters are not merely academic. They are routinely used by engineers to:

- **Select insulation ratings** in transformers (designed for peak voltage, not RMS).
- **Size conductors** in residential wiring (ampacity is rated in RMS amperes).
- **Calibrate AC measuring instruments** — analog meters are inherently average-responding but RMS-scaled using the form factor $1.11$ (this is the principle of **rectifier-type voltmeters**).
- **Design rectifier and inverter circuits** in petroleum instrumentation, UPS systems, and variable-frequency drives (VFDs) used for pipeline pumps.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Each derivation is broken into a numbered *Logic Step*, the *mathematical operation*, and the *engineering justification*.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Value of a Sinusoidal AC Voltage

**Goal:** Prove that $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ for $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Definition of RMS.** By definition, the mean square value of a periodic function $v(t)$ over one full period $T$ is:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} [v(t)]^{2} \, dt$$

*Justification:* The RMS definition is derived from the equivalence of Joule heating. A DC voltage $V_{dc}$ produces power $V_{dc}^{2}/R$ in a resistor $R$. We demand that the AC waveform, averaged over time, produce the same power: $\langle v^{2}(t) \rangle / R = V_{dc}^{2} / R$, leading to the mean-square expression.

**Step 2 — Substitute the sinusoid.** Insert $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} V_m^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt$$

*Justification:* Squaring a sine gives a positive, non-zero integrand suitable for averaging — the basis of the mean-square method.

**Step 3 — Factor out the constant.** $V_m^{2}$ is independent of $t$ and is pulled outside the integral:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Apply the half-angle identity.** Use the standard trigonometric identity:

$$\sin^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$

Substituting $\theta = \omega t$:

$$\sin^{2}(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

*Justification:* This is the standard power-reduction identity, used universally in AC analysis to linearize squares of sinusoids.

**Step 5 — Substitute and split the integral.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, dt = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \int_{0}^{T} \big[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\big] \, dt$$

**Step 6 — Integrate term by term.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T} 1 \, dt - \int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t) \, dt \right]$$

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \left[ \left. t \right\vert_{0}^{T} - \left. \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right\vert_{0}^{T} \right]$$

*Justification:* $\int \cos(ax) \, dx = \sin(ax)/a$. The bounds are $0$ and $T$.

**Step 7 — Evaluate the boundary values.**

- The first term: $\left. t \right\vert_{0}^{T} = T - 0 = T$.
- The second term: $\sin(2\omega T) = \sin(2 \cdot (2\pi/T) \cdot T) = \sin(4\pi) = 0$, and $\sin(0) = 0$. So the second term vanishes entirely.

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2T} \cdot [T - 0] = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2}$$

*Justification:* The cosine term averages to zero over an integer number of cycles — a key property exploited in every AC analysis.

**Step 8 — Take the square root.**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_m$$

**Result Proven.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Value of a Full-Wave Rectified Sine Wave

**Goal:** Prove that $V_{avg} = 2 V_m / \pi$ for $\vert v(t) \vert = V_m \vert \sin(\omega t) \vert$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Average.** The average value over a period $T$ is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Symmetry argument.** A full-wave rectified sinusoid is symmetric about $T/2$, so the average over $T$ equals twice the average over the first half-cycle:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, dt$$

*Justification:* The full-wave rectified waveform repeats every half-period, hence integrating over $[0, T/2]$ and multiplying by $2$ covers the full period.

**Step 3 — Factor and integrate.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \sin(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{2 V_m}{T} \left[ -\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the bounds.**

- At $t = T/2$: $\cos(\omega T/2) = \cos(\pi) = -1$.
- At $t = 0$: $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{T \omega} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{0}^{T/2} = \frac{2 V_m}{T \omega} \left[ -(-1) - (-(1)) \right] = \frac{2 V_m}{T \omega} \cdot 2 = \frac{4 V_m}{T \omega}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify using $\omega = 2\pi / T$.**

$$T \omega = T \cdot \frac{2\pi}{T} = 2\pi$$

Therefore:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{4 V_m}{2 \pi} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \cdot V_m$$

**Result Proven.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Form Factor and Peak Factor

Using the two proven results above, the dimensionless ratios follow by direct substitution:

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2 V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2 \sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$$

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{V_m / \sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Both $K_f$ and $K_p$ are *constants independent of amplitude or frequency* for a pure sine wave. This is why they are called **waveform-shape factors** — they are intrinsic properties of the waveform's geometry, not of its scale.

---

## 3.4 The Phasor Concept — A Qualitative Justification

A **phasor** is a complex-number (or rotating-vector) representation of a sinusoid in the *frequency domain*. The time-domain sinusoid:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

is represented in the phasor domain as:

$$\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \, \angle \phi = V_{rms} (\cos\phi + j \sin\phi)$$

This conversion is justified because, in linear circuits driven at a single frequency $\omega$, all voltages and currents share the same $\omega$. Hence, $\omega$ can be suppressed, and only the **amplitude ($V_m$ or $V_{rms}$)** and **phase ($\phi$)** matter for circuit analysis. The transformation converts **differential equations into algebraic equations** — a tremendous simplification that will be exploited in KTU Module 2 sub-topics on R, L, C and R-L, R-C, R-L-C circuits.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **KTU Examiner Note:** The diagrams below are *functional block diagrams*, not physical circuit drawings. KTU Module-2 questions (3-mark or 14-mark) frequently demand a "neat block diagram" of AC generation or measurement chains. Use these as templates.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generation of Single-Phase AC EMF

This diagram traces the energy flow from a mechanical prime mover (e.g., a diesel engine on an offshore platform) to the AC output terminals of an alternator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Prime Mover] --> B[Alternator Rotor Field Winding]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field]
    C --> D[Stator Armature Winding]
    D --> E[Induced Sinusoidal EMF]
    E --> F[Slip Rings and Brushes]
    F --> G[AC Output Terminals]
    G --> H[Load Bank or Grid]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **prime mover** (turbine/diesel engine) provides mechanical torque.
- The **rotor** carries a DC-excited field winding.
- Rotation at synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / P$ (where $P$ = number of poles) produces a **rotating magnetic field**.
- **Faraday's Law** of electromagnetic induction causes the stator winding to cut field lines, generating a sinusoid: $e(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- The output is delivered to the load through slip rings.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: AC Measurement & Analysis Chain

This functional block shows how a time-domain AC signal is converted into meaningful engineering metrics (RMS, form factor, phasor).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Source Signal] --> B[Signal Conditioning]
    B --> C[Rectification Stage]
    C --> D[Full Wave Rectifier]
    D --> E[Integrator for Average Value]
    D --> F[Squarer and Averager]
    E --> G[Form Factor Computer]
    F --> H[Square Root Stage]
    H --> I[RMS Value Output]
    G --> J[Form Factor Output]
    I --> K[Phasor Transform Module]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Phasor Domain Output]
```

**Engineering Significance:** The full-wave rectifier extracts the magnitude envelope (used for average), the squarer-averager path extracts RMS, and the phasor transform module yields the complex-domain equivalent for circuit analysis. This pipeline is implemented in **digital multimeters, oscilloscopes, and power-quality analyzers**.

## 4.3 Waveform Anatomy — Visual Summary of Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sinusoid v of t] --> B[Peak Value Vm]
    A --> C[Time Period T]
    A --> D[Phase Angle phi]
    A --> E[Angular Frequency omega]
    A --> F[Peak to Peak Value Vpp equals 2 Vm]
    A --> G[RMS Value Vm divided by root 2]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Drawing Hack:** When asked to label a sinusoid in the answer script, always mark the **peak $V_m$** on the y-axis, the **time period $T$** on the x-axis (from one positive-going zero crossing to the next), and the **phase shift $\phi$** as the horizontal offset from the origin to the nearest positive-going zero crossing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Mark Distribution Note (As per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Part A carries 3 marks each (short answer). Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between two full questions — typically subdivided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define RMS value of an alternating current. Why is RMS value used in AC power calculations instead of the average value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value of an AC current is defined as the square root of the mean of the squares of all instantaneous values over one complete cycle. Mathematically:

$$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} i^{2}(t) \, dt}$$

For a pure sine wave, $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$. RMS value is used in AC power calculations because the **average value of a pure sinusoid over a complete cycle is zero** (the positive half-cycle cancels the negative half-cycle). RMS, on the other hand, gives the **DC-equivalent heating effect** in a resistor, which directly corresponds to the real power dissipated: $P = I_{rms}^{2} R$. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for sine value, 1 mark for power justification.)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Define Form Factor and Peak Factor. State their values for a pure sinusoidal waveform.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Form Factor ($K_f$):** The ratio of RMS value to the average value of an AC waveform. $K_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg}$. (1 mark)
- **Peak Factor ($K_p$):** The ratio of peak (maximum) value to the RMS value. $K_p = V_m / V_{rms}$. (1 mark)
- For a **pure sine wave**: $K_f = 1.11$ and $K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Examination Pattern:** Two full questions Q1 and Q2 (or Q3 and Q4) are provided. The student attempts ONE, with sub-parts (a) and (b) each carrying 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the RMS value of a sinusoidal alternating voltage. (7 marks)
**(b)** A sinusoidal AC voltage is given by $v(t) = 311 \sin(314 t)$ volts. Calculate: (i) RMS value, (ii) Average value (full-wave), (iii) Form factor, (iv) Peak factor, (v) Frequency, (vi) Time period. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks
**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the RMS definition: $V_{rms}^{2} = (1/T) \int_0^T v^2(t) \, dt$.
**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$: $V_{rms}^{2} = (V_m^{2}/T) \int_0^T \sin^{2}(\omega t) \, dt$.
**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$ and split the integral.
**Step 4 (1 mark):** Evaluate both integrals; the cosine term vanishes.
**Step 5 (1 mark):** Obtain $V_{rms}^{2} = V_m^{2} / 2$.
**Step 6 (1 mark):** Take the square root: $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$.
**Step 7 (1 mark):** Mention that the same result applies to current: $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$.

> *Full derivation is presented in Section 3.1 of these notes; students may reproduce it as-is in the answer script for full marks.*

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks
Given: $v(t) = 311 \sin(314 t)$ V.

**Identification (1 mark):** Compare with $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:
- $V_m = 311$ V
- $\omega = 314$ rad/s

**(i) RMS value (1 mark):**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{311}{1.414} = 219.94 \, \text{V} \approx 220 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Average value (full-wave) (1 mark):**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 311}{3.1416} = 198.0 \, \text{V}$$

**(iii) Form factor (1 mark):**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{219.94}{198.0} = 1.11$$

**(iv) Peak factor (1 mark):**

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{311}{219.94} = 1.414 = \sqrt{2}$$

**(v) Frequency (1 mark):**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{314}{2 \times 3.1416} = \frac{314}{6.2832} = 49.97 \, \text{Hz} \approx 50 \, \text{Hz}$$

**(vi) Time period (1 mark):**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \, \text{s} = 20 \, \text{ms}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of single-phase alternating EMF. Define the terms: waveform, cycle, time period, frequency, and amplitude. (7 marks)
**(b)** The instantaneous current in a circuit is $i(t) = 10 \sin(100\pi t + \pi/6)$ A. Find: (i) Maximum value, (ii) RMS value, (iii) Phase angle (in degrees), (iv) Frequency, (v) Time taken to reach the first positive peak. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks
**Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw a flow block from *Prime Mover → Rotor → Magnetic Field → Stator Winding → Induced EMF → Output Terminals*. (Refer Section 4.1.)
**Generation explanation (2 marks):** When a coil rotates in a uniform magnetic field at angular velocity $\omega$, the flux linkage $\Phi = NBA \cos(\omega t)$, and by Faraday's law, the induced EMF is $e = -d\Phi/dt = NBA \omega \sin(\omega t)$, which is sinusoidal with peak $E_m = NBA\omega$.
**Definitions (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
- **Waveform:** Graph of instantaneous value vs. time.
- **Cycle:** One complete set of positive and negative half-cycles.
- **Time period ($T$):** Time to complete one cycle.
- **Frequency ($f$):** Number of cycles per second, $f = 1/T$.
- **Amplitude ($V_m$):** Maximum (peak) value of the waveform.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks
Given: $i(t) = 10 \sin(100\pi t + \pi/6)$ A.

**Identification (1 mark):** Compare with $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$:
- $I_m = 10$ A
- $\omega = 100\pi$ rad/s
- $\phi = \pi/6$ rad

**(i) Maximum value (1 mark):** $I_m = 10$ A.

**(ii) RMS value (1 mark):**

$$I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{1.414} = 7.07 \, \text{A}$$

**(iii) Phase angle in degrees (1 mark):**

$$\phi = \frac{\pi}{6} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 30^{\circ}$$

**(iv) Frequency (1 mark):**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{100\pi}{2\pi} = 50 \, \text{Hz}$$

**(v) Time to first positive peak (2 marks):** The first positive peak occurs when the argument of sine equals $\pi/2$:

$$100\pi t + \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2} \implies 100\pi t = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{3} \implies t = \frac{1}{300} \, \text{s} = 3.33 \, \text{ms}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Read Before You Write**
> 1. **Average Value ≠ Zero in practical problems:** Students often write "average value of a sine wave is zero" and leave it at that. Examiners expect you to state: "Average value over a *complete cycle* is zero; the **half-wave or full-wave average** is $2V_m/\pi$." This distinction is worth **1 mark** easily.
> 2. **Confusion between peak and peak-to-peak:** Peak-to-peak $V_{pp} = 2 V_m$. Writing $V_{pp} = V_m$ costs a full mark on sub-parts that ask "peak-to-peak value."
> 3. **Units in RMS calculations:** Always write units (V, A, Hz) in the final answer. Numerical answers without units receive partial credit only.
> 4. **Phase angle conversion:** $\pi/6$ rad must be written as $30^{\circ}$ when asked. Mixing units is a common error.
> 5. **Standard form of sinusoidal equation:** Examiners expect $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ with **all** three parameters explicitly identified before plugging in numerical values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AC Definition:** A periodic current/voltage that alternates direction sinusoidally with time; standard equation $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$.
- **Standard Indian Supply:** $f = 50$ Hz, $V_{rms} = 230$ V (single-phase), $415$ V (three-phase).
- **Three Key Parameters of a Sine Wave:** Amplitude ($V_m$), Angular Frequency ($\omega = 2\pi f$), Phase Angle ($\phi$).
- **RMS Value:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ — the DC-equivalent heating magnitude, used in **all** power calculations.
- **Average Value (Full-Wave):** $V_{avg} = 2 V_m / \pi$ — the mean of the rectified sine wave.
- **Average Value (Half-Wave):** $V_{avg} = V_m / \pi$ — used in half-wave rectifier contexts.
- **Form Factor:** $K_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg} = 1.11$ for a pure sine wave — dimensionless.
- **Peak Factor:** $K_p = V_m / V_{rms} = \sqrt{2}$ for a pure sine wave — dimensionless.
- **Phasor Representation:** A complex-number equivalent of a sinusoid: $\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \angle \phi$. Converts differential equations to algebraic equations.
- **Faraday's Law Foundation:** AC EMF is generated by rotating a coil in a magnetic field; $e = NBA \omega \sin(\omega t)$.
- **Memory Anchor for Board:** "**R**MS gives **R**eal heat, **A**verage gives rectified **A**mplitudes, **P**eak gives **P**ower-insulation limit."
- **Cross-link to KTU Module 2 sub-topics:** R-L, R-C, R-L-C series circuits, phasor diagrams, resonance, and power triangle — all build directly on this Introduction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Series Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Resonance** is the operating condition of a **series RLC circuit** in which the **inductive reactance ($X_L$)** exactly cancels the **capacitive reactance ($X_C$)**, causing the **net reactance to become zero**. At this condition, the circuit impedance ($Z$) is purely resistive and attains its **minimum value ($Z_{min} = R$)**, while the circuit current ($I$) reaches its **maximum value ($I_{max} = V/R$)** for a given supply voltage.

The frequency at which this phenomenon occurs is called the **Resonant Frequency ($f_0$ or $f_r$)** or **Natural Frequency** of the circuit.

**Key Properties at Resonance:**
- The circuit behaves as a **purely resistive circuit**.
- The **power factor is unity (cos $\phi$ = 1)**.
- The voltage and current are **in phase** with each other.
- The reactive powers of L and C are equal and opposite, hence net reactive power is zero.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing.

- If you push **randomly** (at wrong timing/rhythm), the swing goes nowhere — very little motion.
- If you push **exactly in sync** with the swing's natural back-and-forth rhythm, even a tiny push makes the swing go very high.

**A series RLC circuit behaves exactly the same way:**
- The supply voltage (the "push") has a frequency.
- The inductor and capacitor store and exchange energy in their own natural rhythm, determined by L and C.
- When the supply frequency matches this natural rhythm, the **energy oscillates** smoothly between L and C, and the current shoots up to its maximum.
- This perfect synchronization = **Resonance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - Resonant angular frequency: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ (rad/s)
> - Resonant frequency: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ (Hz)
> - Quality factor $Q$ is **dimensionless**.

## 1.3 Why is Series Resonance Important in Engineering?

Series resonance is the foundational principle behind:
- **Radio & TV tuning circuits** (selecting one station frequency).
- **Band-pass and band-stop filters**.
- **Oscillators** in communication systems.
- **Induction heating** (where high currents at resonance heat metals).
- **Antenna matching networks**.
- **Wireless power transfer** systems.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Series RLC Circuit — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Consider a series circuit containing:
- A resistor $R$ (in $\Omega$)
- An inductor $L$ (in Henry, H)
- A capacitor $C$ (in Farad, F)
- All connected in series across an AC source $V$ of frequency $f$ Hz.

**Step 1: Express the reactances.**
- Inductive reactance: $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$
- Capacitive reactance: $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$

**Step 2: Write the total impedance.**
- $Z = R + j(X_L - X_C)$
- Magnitude: $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$

**Step 3: Define the resonance condition.**
At resonance, $X_L = X_C$.
This makes the imaginary part of impedance zero, leaving only $R$.

**Step 4: Solve for the resonant frequency.**

$$\omega_0 L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C} \implies \omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC} \implies \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 5: Examine current behaviour.**

$$I = \dfrac{V}{\vert Z \vert} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}}$$

- At $f = f_0$: $X_L - X_C = 0 \implies I_{max} = V/R$.
- As $f \to 0$: $X_C \to \infty \implies I \to 0$.
- As $f \to \infty$: $X_L \to \infty \implies I \to 0$.

**Step 6: Examine phase angle.**
- $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$
- At resonance, $\phi = 0^\circ$ (voltage and current in phase).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **At Resonance** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inductive Reactance | $X_L = \omega L$ | $X_L = \omega_0 L$ | $\Omega$ |
| Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Net Reactance | $X = X_L - X_C$ | $X = 0$ | $\Omega$ |
| Impedance Magnitude | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2}$ | $\vert Z \vert = R$ (minimum) | $\Omega$ |
| Current Magnitude | $I = V / \vert Z \vert$ | $I = V / R$ (maximum) | A |
| Resonant Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | — | rad/s |
| Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | — | Hz |
| Quality Factor (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C R}$ | $\dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | dimensionless |
| Bandwidth (BW) | $BW = \dfrac{f_0}{Q} = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$ | — | Hz |
| Half-Power Frequencies | $f_1, f_2 = f_0 \mp \dfrac{R}{4\pi L}$ | $f_1 f_2 = f_0^2$ | Hz |
| Voltage across L | $V_L = I X_L$ | $V_L = QV$ (magnified) | V |
| Voltage across C | $V_C = I X_C$ | $V_C = QV$ (magnified) | V |
| Power Factor | $\cos\phi$ | $1$ (unity) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage Magnification Phenomenon:** At resonance, the voltage across L (or C) is **$Q$ times the supply voltage**. This is why $Q$ is called the **voltage magnification factor** in series resonance. If $Q > 1$, individual component voltages can far exceed the supply voltage.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Quality Factor $Q$ controls how "sharp" the resonance peak is.
- **High-Q circuits** (e.g., $Q > 100$): Used in radio tuners where you must pick exactly one station and reject others.
- **Low-Q circuits** (e.g., $Q \approx 1$–$10$): Used in audio equalizers, broadband filters, and induction heating where a wider band of frequencies is desired.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resonant Frequency $f_0$

**Given:** A series RLC circuit with parameters $R$, $L$, $C$, and supply of angular frequency $\omega$.

**Condition for resonance:** Net reactance is zero.

$$X_L - X_C = 0$$

$$\omega_0 L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

**Cross-multiplying:**

$$\omega_0^2 \cdot L \cdot C = 1$$

**Solving for $\omega_0$:**

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Converting to frequency in Hz** (since $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$):

$$2\pi f_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Notice that $f_0$ depends **only on L and C**, not on $R$. So the resonant frequency is a property of the energy-storing elements alone.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quality Factor (Q Factor)

**Definition of Q-factor in terms of energy:**
Q is the ratio of energy stored per cycle to energy dissipated per cycle, multiplied by $2\pi$.

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{\text{Maximum Energy Stored}}{\text{Energy Dissipated per Cycle}}$$

**Derivation from circuit parameters:**

Starting from the condition $X_L = X_C$ at resonance:

$$Q = \dfrac{X_L}{R} = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$

**Substituting $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{L}{R\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{L}}{R\sqrt{C}} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

**Equivalently, using $X_C$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{X_C}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

Both forms are **identical**, confirming the result.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth (BW)

**Definition:** Bandwidth is the range of frequencies for which the current is at least $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., 70.7%) of the maximum current. These are the **half-power frequencies** $f_1$ and $f_2$.

**Condition for half-power:**
At these frequencies, the resistance equals the magnitude of the net reactance:

$$\vert X_L - X_C \vert = R$$

For the upper half-power frequency $f_2$ (where $X_L > X_C$):

$$2\pi f_2 L - \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_2 C} = R$$

For the lower half-power frequency $f_1$ (where $X_C > X_L$):

$$\dfrac{1}{2\pi f_1 C} - 2\pi f_1 L = R$$

**Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$$

**Combining with the Q-factor:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{2\pi f_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$$

$$\boxed{BW = \dfrac{f_0}{Q}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Half-Power Frequencies in Terms of $f_0$ and $BW$

The two half-power frequencies are symmetrically placed around $f_0$ in geometric (not arithmetic) sense:

$$f_1 = f_0 - \dfrac{BW}{2} \quad \text{(approximate, but not exact)}$$

**The exact closed-form expressions:**

$$f_1 = -\dfrac{R}{4\pi L} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{R}{4\pi L}\right)^2 + \dfrac{1}{4\pi^2 LC}}$$

$$f_2 = +\dfrac{R}{4\pi L} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{R}{4\pi L}\right)^2 + \dfrac{1}{4\pi^2 LC}}$$

**Useful exact relations:**

$$f_1 \cdot f_2 = f_0^2$$

$$f_1 + f_2 \approx \text{slightly greater than } 2f_0 \text{ (not exactly equal)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The product $f_1 \cdot f_2 = f_0^2$ is an **exact** relation and is a favourite 3-mark short question. The sum $f_1 + f_2 = 2f_0$ is only an approximation valid for high-Q circuits.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Voltage Magnification Across L and C at Resonance

The current at resonance is:

$$I_{max} = \dfrac{V}{R}$$

Voltage across the inductor:

$$V_L = I_{max} \cdot X_L = \dfrac{V}{R} \cdot \omega_0 L = V \cdot \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = QV$$

Voltage across the capacitor:

$$V_C = I_{max} \cdot X_C = \dfrac{V}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C} = V \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = QV$$

**Note:** $V_L$ and $V_C$ are **180° out of phase** with each other and equal in magnitude, so they cancel in KVL (which they must, since the source voltage is only $V$). However, each individual voltage is $Q$ times the source voltage.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
