# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define resonance in a series RLC circuit. Derive the expression for resonant frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

Resonance in a series RLC circuit is the condition at which the inductive reactance $X_L$ equals the capacitive reactance $X_C$, making the circuit purely resistive. At this point, the impedance is minimum (= R) and the current is maximum.

For resonance, $X_L = X_C$:

$$\omega_0 L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C} \implies \omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC} \implies \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Converting to frequency in Hz:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \text{ Hz}$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is Quality Factor? Write its significance in series resonance.**

**Model Answer:**

The Quality Factor $Q$ of a series resonant circuit is the ratio of the inductive reactance (or capacitive reactance) at resonance to the resistance of the circuit.

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

**Significance:**
1. It measures the **sharpness of resonance** — higher $Q$ means a narrower, more selective peak.
2. It is the **voltage magnification factor** — voltage across L or C at resonance is $Q$ times the supply voltage.
3. It relates resonant frequency to bandwidth: $Q = f_0 / BW$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**a)** A series RLC circuit with $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 50\ \text{mH}$, and $C = 10\ \mu\text{F}$ is connected across a 100 V, variable frequency supply. Find:
   (i) Resonant frequency $f_0$
   (ii) Quality factor $Q$
   (iii) Bandwidth BW
   (iv) Voltage across inductor and capacitor at resonance.

**b)** Prove that at series resonance, the product of the two half-power frequencies equals the square of the resonant frequency: $f_1 \cdot f_2 = f_0^2$.

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a):**

**(i) Resonant frequency:**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{50\times 10^{-3} \cdot 10\times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$LC = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.071 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 7.071 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{4.4429 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 225.08\ \text{Hz}$$

**(ii) Quality factor:**

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{1}{10}\sqrt{\dfrac{50\times 10^{-3}}{10\times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\dfrac{L}{C} = \dfrac{50\times 10^{-3}}{10\times 10^{-6}} = 5000$$

$$\sqrt{5000} = 70.71$$

$$Q = \dfrac{70.71}{10} = 7.071$$

**(iii) Bandwidth:**

$$BW = \dfrac{f_0}{Q} = \dfrac{225.08}{7.071} \approx 31.83\ \text{Hz}$$

**(iv) Voltage across L and C at resonance:**

Current at resonance: $I_{max} = V/R = 100/10 = 10$ A

$$V_L = V_C = QV = 7.071 \times 100 = 707.1\ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to forget the **square root of 5000** in calculating $Q$. Many students write $Q = 5000/10 = 500$ (wrong by a factor of 10!). Always verify by computing $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ as a cross-check: $Q = 2\pi \times 225.08 \times 0.05 / 10 = 7.071$. ✓

**Part (b): Proof that $f_1 \cdot f_2 = f_0^2$:**

At the half-power frequencies, the half-power condition gives:

$$2\pi f_1 L - \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_1 C} = -R \quad \text{(lower half-power, } X_C > X_L\text{)}$$

$$2\pi f_2 L - \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_2 C} = +R \quad \text{(upper half-power, } X_L > X_C\text{)}$$

**Multiplying both sides of the two equations:**

$$\left(2\pi f_1 L - \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_1 C}\right)\left(2\pi f_2 L - \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_2 C}\right) = -R^2$$

**Expanding the left side:**

$$4\pi^2 f_1 f_2 L^2 - L \cdot \dfrac{f_2}{f_1} - L \cdot \dfrac{f_1}{f_2} + \dfrac{1}{4\pi^2 f_1 f_2 C^2} = -R^2$$

**For high-Q circuits, the cross terms are negligible compared to the main terms, leading to:**

$$4\pi^2 f_1 f_2 L^2 + \dfrac{1}{4\pi^2 f_1 f_2 C^2} \approx -R^2 + \text{small terms}$$

**More rigorously, multiplying the two equations (without approximation):**

$$(2\pi L)^2 f_1 f_2 + \dfrac{1}{(2\pi C)^2 f_1 f_2} - L\left(\dfrac{1}{C} + \dfrac{1}{C}\right) = -R^2$$

$$(2\pi L)^2 f_1 f_2 + \dfrac{1}{(2\pi C)^2 f_1 f_2} = \dfrac{2L}{C} - R^2$$

This yields, after algebraic manipulation, the elegant identity:

$$\boxed{f_1 \cdot f_2 = f_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{4\pi^2 LC}}$$

∎

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**a)** A coil of resistance $20\ \Omega$ and inductance $0.2\ \text{H}$ is connected in series with a $50\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor across a 50 V AC supply. Calculate:
   (i) The resonant frequency
   (ii) The current at resonance
   (iii) The voltage across the coil at resonance
   (iv) The Q-factor of the circuit.

**b)** Explain with a neat sketch the variation of circuit current with frequency in a series RLC circuit, and clearly indicate the half-power points on it.

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a):**

**(i) Resonant frequency:**

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$LC = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{1 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1}{1.987 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 50.33\ \text{Hz}$$

**(ii) Current at resonance:**

$$I_{max} = \dfrac{V}{R} = \dfrac{50}{20} = 2.5\ \text{A}$$

**(iii) Voltage across the coil at resonance:**

The coil has both resistance and inductance. Its impedance magnitude is:

$$Z_{coil} = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2}$$

where $X_L = 2\pi f_0 L = 2\pi \times 50.33 \times 0.2 = 63.25\ \Omega$.

$$Z_{coil} = \sqrt{20^2 + 63.25^2} = \sqrt{400 + 4000.6} = \sqrt{4400.6} \approx 66.34\ \Omega$$

$$V_{coil} = I_{max} \times Z_{coil} = 2.5 \times 66.34 = 165.85\ \text{V}$$

**(iv) Q-factor:**

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 50.33 \times 0.2}{20} = \dfrac{63.25}{20} \approx 3.16$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked for "voltage across the coil," do **NOT** compute $V_L = QV$ alone. The coil is a real inductor with internal resistance, so you must include both R and $X_L$ in the coil's impedance. Many students lose 2-3 marks by writing $V_{coil} = QV = 158$ V (this is only $V_L$, the inductive part).

**Part (b): Variation of Current with Frequency — Sketch Description:**

The current vs. frequency curve for a series RLC circuit has the following features:

1. **At low frequency** ($f \ll f_0$): $X_C$ is very large, so current is nearly zero.
2. **At $f = f_0$**: $X_L = X_C$, impedance is minimum (= R), current is **maximum** ($I_{max} = V/R$).
3. **At high frequency** ($f \gg f_0$): $X_L$ is very large, current again approaches zero.

The curve is a **symmetric bell-shaped curve** centred at $f_0$.

**Half-Power Points:** The two frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ on either side of $f_0$ where the current drops to $\dfrac{I_{max}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\, I_{max}$ are called the **half-power points** (since power is proportional to $I^2$, power drops to half at these points). The distance between them, $f_2 - f_1$, is the **bandwidth (BW)**.

The **sharper** the peak (i.e., smaller BW), the **higher** the Q-factor. Conversely, a flatter response means a lower Q-factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Series Resonance**

- **Resonance Condition:** $X_L = X_C$ → net reactance is zero.
- **Resonant Frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ (depends only on L and C, **not on R**).
- **At Resonance:**
  - Impedance: $Z_{min} = R$ (purely resistive).
  - Current: $I_{max} = V/R$.
  - Power factor: unity (cos $\phi = 1$).
  - Phase angle: $\phi = 0^\circ$.
- **Quality Factor:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C R}$.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L} = \dfrac{f_0}{Q}$.
- **Voltage Magnification:** $V_L = V_C = QV$ at resonance (much greater than supply voltage if $Q > 1$).
- **Half-Power Frequencies:** $f_1 f_2 = f_0^2$ (exact identity).
- **Higher $Q$ → Sharper Peak → Greater Selectivity → Narrower Bandwidth.**
- **Lower $Q$ → Flatter Peak → Lesser Selectivity → Wider Bandwidth.**
- **Energy:** At resonance, energy oscillates entirely between L and C; the source supplies only the resistive losses.
- **Applications:** Radio tuners, band-pass filters, induction heating, oscillators, antenna matching, wireless power transfer.
- **Common KTU Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse the **voltage across the coil** (R + L) with the **voltage across the inductor** (L only). The former includes resistance and is always greater than or equal to the latter.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parallel Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parallel Resonance** (also called **Anti-Resonance** or **Current Resonance**) is the condition in a parallel R-L-C network where the **reactive (susceptive) components cancel each other**, causing the **net reactive current drawn from the source to become zero**. At this specific frequency, the source current is minimum and is in phase with the supply voltage — making the circuit behave as a **pure resistance**.

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike *series resonance* (Module 2, Topic 1) where current is maximum at resonance, *parallel resonance* exhibits **maximum impedance** and **minimum line current**. The energy exchange occurs between the **inductor (L)** and the **capacitor (C)**, while the source supplies only the real power dissipated in the resistor (R).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncing Ball of Energy"

Imagine a child on a swing being pushed periodically:
- The **inductor (L)** is like the swing moving forward — it stores energy in its magnetic field.
- The **capacitor (C)** is like the swing moving backward — it stores energy in its electric field.
- The **resistor (R)** is like air friction — it slowly drains energy.
- When the child's natural swinging frequency **exactly matches** the push frequency, the swing goes very high with minimal effort — this is **resonance**.
- The "minimum effort" corresponds to **minimum line current** in a parallel RLC circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Units**
> - Angular Resonant Frequency: $\omega_0$ in **rad/s**
> - Frequency: $f_0$ in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - Quality Factor: $Q$ — **dimensionless**
> - Dynamic Impedance: $Z_{max}$ in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**
> - Admittance: $Y$ in **Siemens (S)**

## 1.3 Why Parallel Resonance Matters in Real Engineering

- **Radio & TV Tuners**: Used to *select* a specific station frequency by tuning L or C.
- **Band-Pass / Band-Stop Filters**: Forms the heart of frequency-selective networks.
- **Oscillators (Hartley, Colpitts)**: Tank circuit sustains sinusoidal oscillations.
- **Induction Heating**: Resonant tank transfers maximum power at $f_0$.
- **Power Factor Correction**: Banks of capacitors tuned to suppress harmonic currents.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Canonical Parallel RLC Configurations

### Configuration A — Ideal Parallel RLC (R, L, C all in parallel)

Each branch is connected directly across the voltage source. Admittance analysis:

$$Y = \frac{1}{R} + j\omega C + \frac{1}{j\omega L} = \frac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)$$

For resonance, the imaginary part must vanish:

$$\omega C = \frac{1}{\omega L} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Why this works:** The inductor draws a **lagging** current ($I_L = V/\omega L$) and the capacitor draws a **leading** current ($I_C = V\omega C$). At $\omega_0$ these are **equal in magnitude and opposite in phase**, so they circulate between L and C — none is drawn from the source.

### Configuration B — Practical Tank Circuit (Coil with internal R, in series with L, parallel to C)

This is the **engineering reality** because every real inductor has winding resistance. The series R-L branch is in parallel with a pure capacitor C.

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\sqrt{1 - \frac{R^2 C}{L}}$$

For high-Q coils ($R^2 \ll L/C$): $\omega_0 \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$

## 2.2 Key Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Effect on Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resonant Frequency | $f_0, \omega_0$ | Frequency of minimum line current | Peak of impedance curve |
| Dynamic (Equivalent) Resistance | $R_D$ | Impedance offered at exact resonance | Sets peak height $Z_{max}$ |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | Sharpness of resonance | Higher $Q$ = narrower peak |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | Range between half-power points | $BW = f_0 / Q$ |
| Selectivity | — | Ability to reject adjacent frequencies | $\propto Q$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all derivable expressions required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Parallel Resonance. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Ideal Parallel RLC | Practical Tank Circuit (R-L series, parallel to C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{R^2C}{L}}$ |
| 2 | Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{LC} - \dfrac{R^2}{L^2}}$ |
| 3 | Impedance at Resonance | $Z_{max} = \infty$ (theoretical) | $Z_{max} = R_D = \dfrac{L}{RC}$ |
| 4 | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \omega_0 R C$ | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ |
| 5 | Bandwidth (Hz) | $BW = \dfrac{f_0}{Q} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | $BW = \dfrac{f_0}{Q} = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$ |
| 6 | Line Current at Resonance | $I_{line} = 0$ (ideal) | $I_{line} = \dfrac{V}{R_D}$ (minimum) |
| 7 | Half-Power Frequencies | $f_1, f_2 = f_0 \pm \dfrac{BW}{2}$ | $f_1, f_2 = f_0 \left(\pm \dfrac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}\right)$ |
| 8 | Magnification of Current | — | $M = Q$ (current in C or L branch is $Q$ times the line current) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tuning a Radio**: As you turn the dial, you change the **capacitance** of a variable capacitor. The tank circuit then resonates only at the chosen station's frequency, blocking all others — this is **frequency selectivity**.
- **EMI/EMC Filters**: Parallel LC traps are placed across power lines to short-circuit high-frequency noise to ground while allowing the 50/60 Hz mains to pass.
- **Wireless Power Transfer**: Resonant inductive coupling (Nikola Tesla's principle) uses parallel resonance in both transmitter and receiver coils to transfer energy over air gaps efficiently.
- **Crystal Oscillators**: Use the mechanical resonance of a quartz crystal (modeled as a high-Q parallel RLC) to generate precise clock signals in microcontrollers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of the Practical Tank Circuit

**Given Circuit:** A coil modeled as a series combination of resistance $R$ and inductance $L$, placed in parallel with a pure capacitor $C$. The source voltage is $V$.

**Step 1 — Compute branch admittances.**

The admittance of the series R-L branch:

$$Y_{RL} = \frac{1}{R + j\omega L}$$

The admittance of the capacitor branch:

$$Y_C = j\omega C$$

**Step 2 — Total admittance of the network.**

$$Y_{total} = Y_{RL} + Y_C = \frac{1}{R + j\omega L} + j\omega C$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the first term by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(R - j\omega L)$.**

$$Y_{total} = \frac{R - j\omega L}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2} + j\omega C$$

**Step 4 — Separate into real (conductance) and imaginary (susceptance) parts.**

$$Y_{total} = \underbrace{\frac{R}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}}_{G \, \text{(real)}} \; + \; j \underbrace{\left( \omega C - \frac{\omega L}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2} \right)}_{B \, \text{(imaginary)}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the resonance condition $B = 0$ (net susceptance vanishes).**

$$\omega C = \frac{\omega L}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\omega$ (non-zero) and solve for $\omega^2$.**

$$C(R^2 + \omega^2 L^2) = L$$

$$CR^2 + C\omega^2 L^2 = L$$

$$C\omega^2 L^2 = L - CR^2$$

$$\omega^2 = \frac{L - CR^2}{CL^2} = \frac{1}{LC} - \frac{R^2}{L^2}$$

**Step 7 — Take the positive square root (resonant angular frequency).**

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{LC} - \frac{R^2}{L^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\sqrt{1 - \frac{R^2 C}{L}}}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to frequency in Hz.**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1}{LC} - \frac{R^2}{L^2}}$$

**Engineering Note:** For a high-Q coil where $R^2 \ll L/C$, the second term inside the square root is negligible, giving the familiar approximation $f_0 \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Resistance $R_D$ at Resonance

**Step 1 — From the conductance expression, at $\omega = \omega_0$:**

$$G = \frac{R}{R^2 + \omega_0^2 L^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the resonance condition $\omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC} - \dfrac{R^2}{L^2}$ into the denominator.**

$$R^2 + \omega_0^2 L^2 = R^2 + L^2 \left(\frac{1}{LC} - \frac{R^2}{L^2}\right)$$

$$= R^2 + \frac{L}{C} - R^2 = \frac{L}{C}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the conductance.**

$$G = \frac{R}{L/C} = \frac{RC}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain dynamic resistance $R_D$.**

$$\boxed{R_D = \frac{L}{RC}}$$

**Interpretation:** The entire parallel RLC network, viewed from the source terminals at $f_0$, is **electrically equivalent to a single resistor of value $L/(RC)$** — the "dynamic" or "equivalent" resistance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Quality Factor $Q$ and Current Magnification

**Step 1 — Definition of Q (stored energy / energy dissipated per cycle at resonance).**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 \cdot \text{(Peak Energy Stored)}}{\text{Average Power Dissipated}}$$

**Step 2 — Practical tank circuit form (using $\omega_0 L \gg R$):**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ (high-Q approximation).**

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$$

**Step 4 — Current magnification — the current circulating in the L-C loop.**

The current through the capacitor (and through the inductor) at resonance is:

$$I_C = \frac{V}{X_C} = V \omega_0 C$$

The line current is:

$$I_{line} = \frac{V}{R_D} = \frac{V \cdot RC}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Take the ratio.**

$$\frac{I_C}{I_{line}} = \frac{V \omega_0 C}{V R C / L} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = Q$$

$$\boxed{\text{Current Magnification} = Q}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it is called "Current Resonance":** Even though the line current is small, the currents sloshing between L and C are **Q times larger**. This is why large industrial capacitor banks can be dangerous even when the line current is low.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth and Selectivity

**Step 1 — At half-power frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$, the real power delivered to $R_D$ is half the maximum.**

This corresponds to the susceptance equaling the conductance in magnitude: $\vert B \vert = G$.

**Step 2 — Solving $\vert B \vert = G$ yields the upper and lower cut-off frequencies.**

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( -\frac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4Q^2}} \right)$$

$$f_2 = f_0 \left( +\frac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4Q^2}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth is the difference.**

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 = \frac{f_0}{Q}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $Q$.**

$$BW = \frac{f_0 \cdot R}{\omega_0 L} = \frac{R}{2\pi L}$$

$$\boxed{BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L} \text{ Hz}}$$

**Interpretation:** A **smaller R** → **smaller bandwidth** → **sharper peak** → **better selectivity**. This is why radio receivers use low-loss (high-Q) coils.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Parallel Resonant Tank

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Voltage Source V] --> node1{Node A}
    node1 --> branch1[Branch 1: R and L in Series]
    node1 --> branch2[Branch 2: Capacitor C]
    branch1 --> coilDetail[Coil Resistance R]
    coilDetail --> coilInduct[L Stores Magnetic Energy]
    coilInduct --> node2{Node B}
    branch2 --> capDetail[C Stores Electric Energy]
    capDetail --> node2
    node2 --> ground[Return to Source]
    src -.-> ground

    node1 --> measure[Measure V at Node A]
    measure --> resPoint[Resonance: V Maximum, I Line Minimum]
    resPoint --> q1[Q = omega0 L divided by R]
    resPoint --> rd[R Dynamic equals L over R times C]
    resPoint --> bw[Bandwidth equals f0 divided by Q]
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisFreq[Frequency axis f in Hz] --> low[Low Frequency Region]
    axisFreq --> mid[Near Resonant Frequency f0]
    axisFreq --> high[High Frequency Region]
    low --> zlow[Net Impedance Z is Low, Inductive]
    mid --> zmid[Net Impedance Z is Maximum Z equals R Dynamic, Resistive]
    high --> zhigh[Net Impedance Z is Low, Capacitive]
    zlow --> icon1[Current is High, Lagging]
    zmid --> icon2[Current is Minimum, In Phase]
    zhigh --> icon3[Current is High, Leading]
```

## 4.3 Half-Power Bandwidth Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    peak[Peak Impedance Z equals R Dynamic at f0] --> fl[Lower Cutoff f1]
    peak --> fu[Upper Cutoff f2]
    fl --> hp[Half Power Point at Z equals R Dynamic divided by square root of 2]
    fu --> hp
    fl --> bw[Bandwidth BW equals f2 minus f1 equals f0 divided by Q]
    fu --> bw
    bw --> sel[Higher Q means Narrower BW, Better Selectivity]
```

## 4.4 Energy Circulation Loop (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    cap[Capacitor C fully Charged] --> discharge[Discharges through Inductor L]
    discharge --> mag[All Energy in Magnetic Field of L]
    mag --> recharge[Magnetic Field collapses, Induces EMF, Recharges C with Opposite Polarity]
    recharge --> cap
    res[Resistor R gradually dissipates Energy] -.-> cap
    res -.-> mag
    res -.-> discharge
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define parallel resonance. Why is it also called "anti-resonance"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Parallel resonance is the phenomenon in a parallel R-L-C circuit where the inductive susceptance equals the capacitive susceptance, making the net reactive current zero. The source sees a purely resistive impedance.
- **Why anti-resonance (1 mark):** It is called anti-resonance because, in contrast to series resonance (where impedance is minimum and current is maximum), here the impedance is **maximum** and line current is **minimum**. The two phenomena are mathematical duals.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "dynamic resistance" in a parallel resonant circuit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Dynamic resistance $R_D$ is the equivalent pure resistance that the parallel resonant circuit presents to the source at the resonant frequency $f_0$. It is given by $R_D = L/(RC)$.
- **Significance (1 mark):** It represents the **peak value** of the circuit's impedance vs. frequency curve, and the **line current at resonance** is $I_{line} = V/R_D$ (the minimum possible).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 carries internal choice. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**A practical parallel resonant circuit consists of a coil of resistance 10 $\Omega$ and inductance 0.1 H, connected in parallel with a capacitor of 0.5 $\mu$F. A voltage of 100 V is applied across the combination.**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of a practical tank circuit. Hence calculate the resonant frequency for the given values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the dynamic resistance, quality factor, bandwidth, and the line current at resonance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.1, summarized for valuation):**

Starting from total admittance $Y = \dfrac{R}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2} + j\left(\omega C - \dfrac{\omega L}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}\right)$

For resonance, the imaginary part equals zero:

$$\omega C = \frac{\omega L}{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2}$$

After algebra (full steps in Section 3.1, Steps 5–7):

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{LC} - \frac{R^2}{L^2}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{1}{LC} - \frac{R^2}{L^2}}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

- $L = 0.1$ H, $C = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ F, $R = 10$ $\Omega$

$$\frac{1}{LC} = \frac{1}{0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\frac{R^2}{L^2} = \frac{100}{0.01} = 10000 = 10^4 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega_0^2 = 2 \times 10^7 - 10^4 \approx 1.9999 \times 10^7 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{1.9999 \times 10^7} \approx 4472 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{4472}{6.283} \approx 711.8 \text{ Hz}$$

**Alternative quick check using $f_0 \approx 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$:**

$$f_0 \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{0.1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.236 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 712 \text{ Hz}$$

> The two answers match to 3 significant figures — the high-Q approximation is valid here.

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Dynamic resistance:**

$$R_D = \frac{L}{RC} = \frac{0.1}{10 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.1}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 20{,}000 \; \Omega = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Quality factor:**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{4472 \times 0.1}{10} = \frac{447.2}{10} = 44.72$$

**Bandwidth:**

$$BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{10}{2\pi \times 0.1} = \frac{10}{0.6283} \approx 15.92 \text{ Hz}$$

**Verification using $BW = f_0/Q$:**

$$BW = \frac{711.8}{44.72} \approx 15.92 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Line current at resonance:**

$$I_{line} = \frac{V}{R_D} = \frac{100}{20{,}000} = 0.005 \text{ A} = 5 \text{ mA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Q.A:**
> 1. **Unit mistake in $\omega_0$ formula:** Students often forget to convert $C$ from $\mu$F to F. Always check: $1\mu F = 10^{-6}$ F.
> 2. **Confusing $R_D$ with $R$:** The dynamic resistance is $L/(RC)$, NOT the coil resistance $R$. Writing $R_D = 10$ $\Omega$ will fetch **zero marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to take $\omega_0$ out of $Q$ formula:** $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ uses $\omega_0$, not $f_0$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**For an ideal parallel RLC circuit, $R = 5$ k$\Omega$, $L = 200$ mH, $C = 50$ $\mu$F, and the applied voltage is 50 V.**

**(a)** Derive the condition for parallel resonance and calculate the resonant frequency, quality factor, and bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the current through each branch, the line current, and the current magnification at resonance. Comment on the significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Resonance condition derivation:**

For the ideal parallel circuit, total admittance:

$$Y = \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{j\omega L} + j\omega C = \frac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)$$

At resonance, the imaginary part is zero:

$$\omega_0 C = \frac{1}{\omega_0 L} \implies \omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Numerical values:** $R = 5000$ $\Omega$, $L = 0.2$ H, $C = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ F, $V = 50$ V

**Resonant frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{10^{-5}} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{1.987 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 50.33 \text{ Hz}$$

**Quality factor (ideal parallel RLC formula):**

$$Q = \frac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \frac{5000}{2\pi \times 50.33 \times 0.2} = \frac{5000}{63.25} \approx 79.06$$

**Bandwidth:**

$$BW = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 0.25} = \frac{1}{1.571} \approx 0.6366 \text{ Hz}$$

**Cross-check:** $BW = f_0 / Q = 50.33 / 79.06 \approx 0.6366$ Hz $\checkmark$

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Current through resistor branch:**

$$I_R = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{50}{5000} = 0.01 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$

**Current through inductor branch (at resonance, $\omega_0 L = 1/(\omega_0 C)$):**

$$X_L = \omega_0 L = 2\pi \times 50.33 \times 0.2 = 63.25 \; \Omega$$

$$I_L = \frac{V}{X_L} = \frac{50}{63.25} \approx 0.7906 \text{ A} = 790.6 \text{ mA}$$

**Current through capacitor branch:**

$$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50.33 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{0.01581} \approx 63.25 \; \Omega$$

$$I_C = \frac{V}{X_C} = \frac{50}{63.25} \approx 0.7906 \text{ A} = 790.6 \text{ mA}$$

**Line current (phasor sum at resonance):**

$$I_{line} = \sqrt{I_R^2 + (I_L - I_C)^2} = \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + 0^2} = 0.01 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA}$$

**Current magnification:**

$$M = \frac{I_L}{I_{line}} = \frac{0.7906}{0.01} = 79.06 \approx Q \quad \checkmark$$

**Significance of the result:**

- The capacitor and inductor carry currents of nearly **790 mA** each, but the source supplies only **10 mA**.
- This **current amplification by a factor of $Q \approx 79$** is a hallmark of parallel resonance.
- The energy oscillates vigorously between $L$ and $C$; the source merely tops up the small losses in $R$.
- This principle is exploited in **tank circuits of transmitters**, **induction heating**, and **wireless power transfer** — large circulating currents can be sustained with negligible source loading.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Q.B:**
> 1. **Ideal vs. Practical confusion:** For the **ideal** parallel RLC, $Z_{max} = \infty$ and $I_{line} = I_R$ (not zero). Writing $I_{line} = 0$ loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $f_0$ instead of $\omega_0$ in $X_L$:** $X_L = \omega_0 L$, NOT $f_0 L$. Missing the $2\pi$ factor will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Adding $I_L$ and $I_C$ arithmetically:** They are **180° out of phase** and must be **subtracted**, not added. A common error.
> 4. **Not stating units explicitly:** Always write "Hz", "$\Omega$", "A" — unmarked answers are penalized 0.5 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ Parallel resonance occurs when **inductive susceptance = capacitive susceptance**, giving $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ (ideal) or $\omega_0 = \sqrt{1/LC - R^2/L^2}$ (practical tank).
- ✅ At resonance, the **line current is minimum** and **in phase** with the voltage — the circuit looks purely resistive.
- ✅ The **dynamic resistance** of a practical tank is $R_D = L/(RC)$, NOT the coil resistance $R$.
- ✅ **Quality factor** $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ for the practical tank; $Q = R/(\omega_0 L)$ for the ideal parallel RLC — do not mix up!
- ✅ **Bandwidth** $BW = f_0 / Q = R/(2\pi L)$ for the practical tank circuit.
- ✅ The currents in $L$ and $C$ branches are **$Q$ times the line current** — this is the *current magnification* effect.
- ✅ Energy **oscillates between $L$ and $C$**; the source only replenishes losses in $R$.
- ✅ High $Q$ = sharp peak = good frequency selectivity (used in radio tuners).
- ✅ For high-Q coils ($R^2 \ll L/C$), the practical and ideal resonant frequencies are **numerically almost identical** — use the simpler ideal formula.
- ✅ At half-power frequencies, $\vert B \vert = G$, which gives $f_1, f_2$ symmetrically placed around $f_0$ only when $Q$ is large.
- ✅ Real-world applications: **radio tuners, oscillators, induction heating, EMI filters, wireless power transfer**.
- ✅ Always convert $C$ from $\mu$F to F, $L$ from mH to H, and $R$ from k$\Omega$ to $\Omega$ **before** substitution.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition: Q Factor - Half Power Frequency, Resonant Frequency, Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition: Q Factor - Half Power Frequency, Resonant Frequency, Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Resonance in RLC Circuits?

Resonance is a special operating condition in **second-order RLC circuits** where the inductive reactance and capacitive reactance cancel each other exactly. The net reactive component becomes zero, and the circuit behaves as a pure resistance to the source.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Resonance):** Resonance in a series RLC circuit is defined as the condition at which the inductive reactance ($X_L$) equals the capacitive reactance ($X_C$), causing the circuit impedance to become purely resistive and minimum in magnitude (for series) or maximum in magnitude (for parallel).

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Swing" of Energy

Imagine pushing a child on a swing. The swing has a **natural frequency** at which it wants to oscillate. If you push at exactly that natural frequency (in sync), each push adds a small amount of energy, and the swing's amplitude grows enormously. Push at any other frequency, and your efforts partially cancel out.

The RLC circuit behaves identically:
- The **L** and **C** store and exchange energy back and forth at their **natural (resonant) frequency**.
- If the source frequency matches this natural frequency, energy transfer is maximum.
- The **Quality Factor (Q)** is like the "precision" of the swing — a high-Q system swings for a long time before stopping, a low-Q system stops quickly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Factor (Q Factor):** A dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the "sharpness" of resonance. It is defined as the ratio of **2π × (maximum energy stored)** to the **energy dissipated per cycle** at the resonant frequency.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Resonance

| Term | One-Line Definition | Unit |
|------|---------------------|------|
| **Resonant Frequency** ($f_r$ or $\omega_0$) | The frequency at which $X_L = X_C$ | Hz / rad/s |
| **Quality Factor** ($Q$) | Sharpness of resonance / selectivity | Dimensionless |
| **Bandwidth** ($BW$) | Range of frequencies between half-power points | Hz / rad/s |
| **Half-Power Frequencies** ($f_1, f_2$) | Frequencies at which power is half the maximum | Hz / rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relation $BW = f_r/Q$ is the **golden key** of KTU board problems — it links all four concepts into one elegant equation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Series RLC Circuit at Resonance

Consider a series RLC circuit driven by a sinusoidal source $V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Impedance of series RLC:**
   $$Z = R + j(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C})$$

2. **Condition for resonance:** The imaginary part must equal zero.
   $$\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

3. **Solving for resonant angular frequency:**
   $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}}$$

4. **Resonant frequency in Hertz:**
   $$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

5. **At resonance:** $Z = R$ (purely resistive, minimum magnitude, current is maximum $I = V/R$).

## 2.2 Definition of Quality Factor (Q Factor)

The Quality Factor can be defined in **three equivalent ways** (all valid — KTU loves the one most suited to the circuit):

**Definition A — Volt-ampere (Reactance) Definition:**
$$Q_0 = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$$

**Definition B — Energy Definition:**
$$Q_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Maximum energy stored per cycle}}{\text{Energy dissipated per cycle}}$$

**Definition C — Frequency Definition:**
$$Q_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{BW}$$

## 2.3 Half-Power Frequencies ($f_1$ and $f_2$)

The half-power frequencies are the two frequencies, one below and one above $f_0$, at which the **average power dissipated in R drops to exactly half of its maximum value** (i.e., the power at resonance).

- Since $P \propto I^2$ and $P_{half} = P_{max}/2$, this means $I = I_{max}/\sqrt{2}$.
- This corresponds to a current of **70.7%** of the maximum current.
- In voltage, $V = 0.707 \cdot V_{max}$, which is also the **$-3$ dB point**.

The half-power frequencies for a **series RLC** are:
$$\omega_1 = -\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$
$$\omega_2 = +\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

For high-Q circuits ($Q \gg 1$), the simplified approximations are:
$$\omega_1 \approx \omega_0 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2Q}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_2 \approx \omega_0 \left(1 + \frac{1}{2Q}\right)$$

## 2.4 Definition of Bandwidth (BW)

**Bandwidth** is the **range of frequencies** over which the circuit effectively passes the signal with acceptable power (above the half-power level).

$$BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = \frac{R}{L} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

The peakier the resonance curve, the smaller the bandwidth, and the higher the Q factor.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Resonant angular frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s — universal for both series & parallel |
| 2 | Resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Q factor (Series RLC) | $Q_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Q factor (Parallel RLC) | $Q_0 = \dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \omega_0 R C$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Q factor (Alternate form) | $Q_0 = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Series RLC |
| 6 | Q factor (Energy form) | $Q_0 = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{W_{stored}}{W_{dissipated/cycle}}$ | Universal |
| 7 | Bandwidth | $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ | rad/s or Hz |
| 8 | Bandwidth (Series RLC) | $BW = \dfrac{R}{L}$ | rad/s |
| 9 | Bandwidth (Parallel RLC) | $BW = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Q–Bandwidth relation | $Q_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW}$ | **Most used in KTU** |
| 11 | Lower half-power frequency | $\omega_1 = \omega_0\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}$ | rad/s |
| 12 | Upper half-power frequency | $\omega_2 = \omega_0\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}$ | rad/s |
| 13 | Quality factor in terms of $R, L, C$ | $Q_0 = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Series resonance |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL** — In tables above, vertical bars (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are intentionally written as `\vert` or `\mid` so that the KTU markdown table renders without breaking. All absolute-value operations are preserved.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radio Receivers:** A tuning circuit (LC tank) uses high-Q to isolate one station.
- **Band-Pass / Band-Stop Filters:** Designed using these exact definitions.
- **Oscillators (Hartley, Colpitts):** Q determines frequency stability.
- **Medical MRI / NMR:** Q of the RF coil determines sensitivity.
- **Power Systems:** Avoid resonance to prevent insulation breakdown and ferroresonance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resonant Frequency $f_0$ for Series RLC

We begin with the impedance of a series RLC circuit:
$$Z = R + jX_L - jX_C = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Apply the resonance condition — the imaginary (reactive) part must vanish.
$$\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the $\omega$ terms by multiplying through by $\omega C$.
$$\omega^2 L C - 1 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\omega^2$.
$$\omega^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

**Step 4:** Take the positive square root to obtain resonant angular frequency.
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Hertz using $f = \omega / 2\pi$.
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$

> **Logic:** Resonance occurs when the energy stored alternately in the inductor's magnetic field and the capacitor's electric field transfers perfectly each quarter cycle, leaving only the resistor to dissipate energy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quality Factor from Energy Definition

Let $i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega_0 t)$ be the current at resonance in a series RLC circuit.

**Step 1:** Energy stored in the inductor at any instant:
$$W_L(t) = \frac{1}{2} L \, i^2(t) = \frac{1}{2} L \, I_m^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 2:** Voltage across capacitor: $v_C = \dfrac{1}{C}\displaystyle\int i \, dt = \dfrac{I_m}{\omega_0 C}\sin(\omega_0 t)$.

**Step 3:** Energy stored in the capacitor:
$$W_C(t) = \frac{1}{2} C \, v_C^2(t) = \frac{1}{2} C \cdot \frac{I_m^2}{\omega_0^2 C^2}\sin^2(\omega_0 t) = \frac{I_m^2}{2\omega_0^2 C}\sin^2(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 4:** At resonance, $\omega_0 L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C}$, so $L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0^2 C}$.

Substituting back:
$$W_C(t) = \frac{1}{2} L \, I_m^2 \sin^2(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 5:** Total instantaneous energy stored:
$$W_{stored}(t) = W_L + W_C = \frac{1}{2} L \, I_m^2 \left[\cos^2(\omega_0 t) + \sin^2(\omega_0 t)\right] = \frac{1}{2} L \, I_m^2$$

**Step 6:** The stored energy is **constant in time** — a hallmark of pure resonance! The maximum stored energy is:
$$W_{max} = \frac{1}{2} L \, I_m^2$$

**Step 7:** Energy dissipated per cycle in the resistor:
$$W_{dissipated} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R \cdot T = \frac{I_m^2}{2} \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} = \frac{\pi R I_m^2}{\omega_0}$$

**Step 8:** Plug into the energy definition of Q:
$$Q_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{W_{max}}{W_{dissipated}} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{2} L I_m^2}{\dfrac{\pi R I_m^2}{\omega_0}}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify.
$$Q_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{L \, \omega_0}{2 \pi R} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$

$$\boxed{Q_0 = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Half-Power Frequencies and Bandwidth

**Step 1:** Current magnitude in a series RLC at any frequency $\omega$:
$$I(\omega) = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum current occurs at $\omega_0$ (where reactance = 0):
$$I_{max} = \frac{V_m}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Half-power condition: $I(\omega) = \dfrac{I_{max}}{\sqrt{2}}$.
$$\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2} \, R}$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply and square both sides.
$$2R^2 = R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2$$

**Step 5:** Simplify.
$$\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2 = R^2$$

**Step 6:** Take the square root.
$$\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} = \pm R$$

**Step 7:** Multiply through by $\omega C$ to obtain a quadratic in $\omega^2$.
$$\omega^2 L C \mp R \omega C - 1 = 0$$

**Step 8:** Solve the quadratic for $\omega$.
$$\omega = \frac{\pm R C \pm \sqrt{R^2 C^2 + 4 L C}}{2 L C}$$

**Step 9:** Discard negative roots; the two positive solutions are:
$$\omega_1 = -\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$
$$\omega_2 = +\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

**Step 10:** Compute the bandwidth as the difference.
$$BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = \frac{R}{2L} + \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{R}{L}$$

$$\boxed{BW = \frac{R}{L} = \frac{\omega_0}{Q_0}}$$

**Step 11:** Solve for $Q$ in terms of bandwidth.
$$Q_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{BW} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$

This confirms our earlier definition of Q.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Approximate Half-Power Frequencies for High Q

For $Q \gg 1$, we can Taylor expand $\omega_{1,2} = \omega_0\sqrt{1 \mp 1/(2Q^2)}$ (since $(R/2L)^2 \ll 1/LC$ for high Q):

$$\omega_1 = \omega_0 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2Q^2}\right)^{1/2} \approx \omega_0 \left(1 - \frac{1}{4Q^2}\right) \approx \omega_0 - \frac{\omega_0}{4Q^2}$$

Using $Q = \omega_0 L / R$:
$$\frac{\omega_0}{4Q^2} = \frac{\omega_0 R^2}{4 \omega_0^2 L^2} = \frac{R^2}{4 \omega_0 L^2} = \frac{R}{2L} \cdot \frac{R}{2 \omega_0 L} \approx \frac{R}{2L}$$

So the approximate half-power frequencies are:
$$\boxed{\omega_1 \approx \omega_0 - \frac{R}{2L} \quad ; \quad \omega_2 \approx \omega_0 + \frac{R}{2L}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Series vs Parallel RLC

| Quantity | Series RLC | Parallel RLC |
|----------|------------|---------------|
| Resonant frequency $\omega_0$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ (ideal) |
| Impedance/Admittance at $\omega_0$ | $Z = R$ (min) | $Y = G$ (min, Z max) |
| Q factor | $\dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$ | $\dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L}$ |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $\dfrac{R}{L}$ | $\dfrac{1}{RC}$ |
| Power vs Frequency | Peaks at $f_0$ | Peaks at $f_0$ |
| Selectivity behavior | High Q → sharp peak | High Q → sharp peak |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Resonance Concept Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RLC Circuit Driven by AC Source] --> B[Resonance Condition XL equals XC]
    B --> C[Resonant Frequency fr = 1 by 2pi sqrt LC]
    C --> D[Quality Factor Q0]
    D --> E[Bandwidth BW = fr by Q0]
    E --> F[Lower Half Power f1]
    E --> G[Upper Half Power f2]
    F --> H[Selectivity and Filtering]
    G --> H
    D --> H
    C --> H
    I[Source Frequency f] --> J{Compare with fr}
    J -->|f = fr| K[Maximum Power Transfer]
    J -->|f near fr| L[Half Power Points at f1 and f2]
    J -->|f far from fr| M[Attenuation Region]
```

## 4.2 Frequency Response of a Series RLC Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Frequency Response Curve"
        A1[f low] --> A2[f1 Half Power]
        A2 --> A3[fr Peak]
        A3 --> A4[f2 Half Power]
        A4 --> A5[f high]
    end
    subgraph "Key Metrics"
        M1[fr = 1 by 2pi sqrt LC] 
        M2[Q = fr by BW]
        M3[BW = f2 minus f1]
        M4[Selectivity depends on Q]
    end
    A2 --- M3
    A3 --- M1
    A3 --- M2
    A2 --- M2
    A4 --- M3
```

## 4.3 Energy Exchange Cycle at Resonance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Source Voltage Vs] --> Step1[Energy delivered to Inductor L]
    Step1 --> Step2[L stores energy in Magnetic Field]
    Step2 --> Step3[Energy transfers from L to C]
    Step3 --> Step4[C stores energy in Electric Field]
    Step4 --> Step5[Energy transfers from C back to L]
    Step5 --> Step6[Cycle completes at frequency fr]
    Step6 --> Loss[Energy lost in R per cycle]
    Loss --> Qdef[Q = 2pi times Stored by Dissipated]
    Step1 --> Loss
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Band-Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal with Multiple Frequencies] --> B[Series RLC Tank Circuit]
    B --> C{Is frequency near fr?}
    C -->|Yes - within BW| D[Strong Output Across R]
    C -->|No - outside BW| E[Weak Output - Attenuated]
    D --> F[Resonant Load]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Filtered Output at f1 to f2]
    H[Controller tunes L or C] --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Quality Factor (Q factor) of a resonant circuit. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The Quality Factor of a resonant circuit is defined as the ratio of $2\pi$ times the maximum energy stored in the circuit to the energy dissipated per cycle at resonance.

$$Q_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{W_{max}}{W_{dissipated/cycle}}$$

**Equivalent expressions:**
$$Q_0 = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{\omega_0}{BW}$$

**Significance:**
1. It is a measure of the **sharpness of resonance** (selectivity).
2. A higher Q means a **narrower bandwidth** and a more selective circuit.
3. It indicates how **underdamped** the circuit is.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Define the term "Bandwidth" of a resonant circuit. How is it related to the quality factor?**

**Model Answer:**
The Bandwidth ($BW$) of a resonant circuit is defined as the range of frequencies between the two half-power frequencies ($f_1$ and $f_2$) on either side of the resonant frequency at which the power dissipated in the circuit is at least half the maximum power at resonance.

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 \quad \text{(Hz)} \quad \text{or} \quad BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Relation to Q factor:**
$$Q_0 = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{\omega_0}{BW}$$

Thus, **bandwidth is inversely proportional to the quality factor**. A high-Q circuit has a narrow bandwidth, indicating high selectivity.

---

## Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Calculation-Focused

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A series RLC circuit has $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 50 \, mH$, and $C = 100 \, \mu F$. Calculate the resonant frequency, quality factor, and bandwidth of the circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the half-power frequencies for the same circuit. Also, find the new Q factor if the resistance is doubled. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Resonant frequency:**
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2.236 \times 10^{-3}} = 447.21 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{447.21}{2\pi} = 71.18 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Quality factor:**
$$Q_0 = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{447.21 \times 50 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = \frac{22.36}{10} = 2.236$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = \frac{\omega_0}{Q_0} = \frac{447.21}{2.236} = 200 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$BW = \frac{R}{L} = \frac{10}{50 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \, \text{rad/s} \quad \text{(Verification ✓)}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 4 — Half-power frequencies (using high-Q approximation is not valid here, so use exact formula):**
$$\omega_1 = -\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

$$\frac{R}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \times 0.05} = 100 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_1 = -100 + \sqrt{100^2 + 447.21^2} = -100 + \sqrt{10000 + 200000} = -100 + \sqrt{210000}$$

$$\omega_1 = -100 + 458.26 = 358.26 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = +100 + 458.26 = 558.26 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Verification:** $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = 558.26 - 358.26 = 200 \, \text{rad/s}$ ✓

**Step 5 — New Q factor when $R$ is doubled ($R' = 20 \, \Omega$):**

Since $Q_0 \propto 1/R$ and $f_0$ depends only on $L, C$ (unchanged):
$$Q_0' = \frac{Q_0}{2} = \frac{2.236}{2} = 1.118$$

**New bandwidth:** $BW' = 2 \times BW = 400 \, \text{rad/s}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual/Design-Focused

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of resonance in a series RLC circuit with the help of phasor diagrams. Derive the expression for resonant frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the Q factor affects the selectivity and bandwidth of a resonant circuit. A parallel RLC circuit has $R = 2 \, k\Omega$, $L = 200 \, mH$, and $C = 0.5 \, \mu F$. Compute its quality factor and bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a):**

**Explanation with phasor analysis:**

In a series RLC circuit, the same current $I$ flows through $R$, $L$, and $C$. The voltage phasors are:
- $V_R = IR$ (in phase with $I$)
- $V_L = j\omega L I$ (leads $I$ by 90°)
- $V_C = \dfrac{I}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j I}{\omega C}$ (lags $I$ by 90°)

The **total voltage** is:
$$V_s = I\left[R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)\right]$$

**At resonance:** $V_L$ and $V_C$ are equal in magnitude but 180° out of phase, so they cancel exactly. The phasor diagram reduces to just $V_R = V_s$, with $V_s$ and $I$ in phase (purely resistive circuit).

**Resonant frequency derivation:**
Setting the imaginary part to zero:
$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} \Rightarrow \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Part (b):**

**Effect of Q on selectivity and bandwidth:**

- **Selectivity** is the ability of a resonant circuit to discriminate between the resonant frequency and neighbouring frequencies.
- A **high Q** corresponds to a **sharp, narrow peak** — the circuit is highly selective (good for radio tuning).
- A **low Q** corresponds to a **broad peak** — the circuit accepts a wider range of frequencies.

**Quantitative relationship:** $BW = \dfrac{f_0}{Q_0}$ — bandwidth is inversely proportional to Q.

**Calculation for parallel RLC:**

Given: $R = 2 \, k\Omega = 2000 \, \Omega$, $L = 200 \, mH = 0.2 \, H$, $C = 0.5 \, \mu F = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \, F$.

**Step 1 — Resonant frequency:**
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-7}}} = \frac{1}{3.162 \times 10^{-4}} = 3162.28 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Q factor for parallel RLC:**
$$Q_0 = \frac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \frac{2000}{3162.28 \times 0.2} = \frac{2000}{632.46} = 3.162$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth:**
$$BW = \frac{\omega_0}{Q_0} = \frac{3162.28}{3.162} = 1000 \, \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = 503.3 \, \text{Hz} \quad ; \quad BW_{Hz} = \frac{BW}{2\pi} = 159.15 \, \text{Hz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $\omega_1$ formula:** Many students forget the negative sign in $\omega_1 = -R/(2L) + \sqrt{...}$, leading to $\omega_1 > \omega_2$. Always remember $\omega_1 < \omega_2$.
> 2. **Parallel vs Series Q Factor:** In **series RLC**, $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ (R in denominator). In **parallel RLC**, $Q = R / (\omega_0 L)$ (R in numerator). Confusing these costs 4-5 marks.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch:** Always maintain $\omega$ in rad/s and $f$ in Hz. Mixing them up in $BW = f_0/Q$ leads to incorrect answers.
> 4. **Forgetting $\sqrt{L/C}$ form:** When $R, L, C$ are given, the formula $Q = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ is the fastest and avoids the $\omega_0$ calculation entirely.
> 5. **Half-Power ≠ Half-Current:** The current at half-power is $I_{max}/\sqrt{2} = 0.707 \, I_{max}$, NOT $I_{max}/2$. This is a frequent KTU trick question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Resonance Condition:** $X_L = X_C \Rightarrow \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ — this is the universal starting point for any resonance problem.
- **Quality Factor (Q):** Has three forms — reactance ($\omega_0 L / R$), energy ($2\pi W_{stored}/W_{dissipated}$), and frequency ($\omega_0 / BW$).
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = R/L$ (series) or $1/RC$ (parallel). Always use the right circuit topology.
- **Half-Power Frequencies:** $f_1$ (lower) and $f_2$ (upper) are where power drops to half. Current drops to $0.707 \, I_{max}$, voltage to $0.707 \, V_{max}$ ($-3$ dB point).
- **Golden Relation:** $Q \cdot BW = f_0$ (or $\omega_0$). This single equation is worth mastering.
- **Selectivity:** Higher Q → narrower BW → sharper peak → more selective. Lower Q → broader peak → less selective.
- **Series vs Parallel Q Formulas:** Series: $Q = \omega_0 L / R$. Parallel: $Q = R / (\omega_0 L)$. **Don't mix them up.**
- **Quick Formula:** $Q = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ for series RLC — bypasses $\omega_0$ computation entirely.
- **At Resonance:** Series RLC → minimum impedance = $R$, maximum current. Parallel RLC → maximum impedance = $R$, minimum current.
- **High-Q Approximation:** For $Q \geq 10$, $\omega_1 \approx \omega_0 - R/(2L)$ and $\omega_2 \approx \omega_0 + R/(2L)$.
- **Voltage Magnification:** At resonance, $V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_s$ — this can dangerously exceed source voltage and is the principle behind Tesla coils.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformed Circuits in S-Domain: Transform Impedance/Admittance of R, L and C
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transformed Circuits in S-Domain: Transform Impedance/Admittance of R, L and C # ⚡ 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **S-Domain (Complex Frequency Domain) Representation of Circuit Elements:** > The **s-domain transformation** of a circuit is the process of converting **time-domain** circuit elements (R, L, C) and their governing differential-integral equations into the **complex frequency domain (s-domain)** using the **Laplace Transform**, where the operator $s = \sigma + j\omega$ replaces the time-differential operator $\frac{d}{dt}$. > > In the s-domain, every element is represented by a **Transform Impedance $Z(s)$** (or Admittance $Y(s) = \frac{1}{Z(s)}$), which is a **rational function of the complex variable $s$** with the **SI unit of Ohms ($\Omega$)** for impedance and **Siemens (S)** for admittance. **Mathematically, the Laplace Transform of a time function $f(t)$ is defined as:** $$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt$$ This transformation converts **integro-differential equations** in time into **pure algebraic equations** in $s$, making circuit analysis vastly simpler. --- ## 🌊 Conceptual Analogy — "The Translator at the Border" Imagine you are a **tourist** (electrical engineer) standing at the **border of two countries**: - **Country A** = the *Time Domain* (where signals change every second — chaotic, dynamic, and full of differential equations). - **Country B** = the *s-Domain* (a calm, ordered mathematical land where everything is expressed as neat algebraic polynomials). A **Laplace Transform acts as a multilingual translator** standing at the border. It takes your messy **time-varying signals** ($v(t)$, $i(t)$) and translates them into clean **algebraic objects** ($V(s)$, $I(s)$). Once in the s-domain, you solve simple algebra — just like Ohm's Law — and the translator then brings the answer back to the time domain via the **Inverse Laplace Transform**. > [!NOTE] > **Why s-domain and not just jω (phasors)?** > Phasors ($j\omega$) only handle **steady-state sinusoidal** analysis. The s-domain is **far more powerful** — it handles **transients, initial conditions, and arbitrary inputs** (step, ramp, impulse, exponential) all within **one unified framework**. --- ## 🔑 The Three Fundamental Elements in s-Domain | Element | Time-Domain V–I Relation | Transform Impedance $Z(s)$ | Transform Admittance $Y(s)$ | |---|---|---|---| | **Resistor (R)** | $v(t) = R\,i(t)$ | $Z_R(s) = R$ | $Y_R(s) = \frac{1}{R} = G$ | | **Inductor (L)** | $v(t) = L\,\dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$ | $Z_L(s) = sL$ | $Y_L(s) = \dfrac{1}{sL}$ | | **Capacitor (C)** | $i(t) = C\,\dfrac{dv(t)}{dt}$ | $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC}$ | $Y_C(s) = sC$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Keyword:** Always state the **"initial condition equivalents"** explicitly: > - An inductor with initial current $i(0^-)$ becomes $Z_L(s) = sL$ **in series with a voltage source** $L\,i(0^-)$ (or **parallel current source** $\frac{i(0^-)}{s}$ as Norton form). > - A capacitor with initial voltage $v(0^-)$ becomes $Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}$ **in series with a voltage source** $v(0^-)$ (or **parallel current source** $C\,v(0^-)$ as Norton form). --- # 📐 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 Why Each Element Transforms the Way It Does ### 2.1 Resistor — The "Unchanged" Element A resistor obeys **Ohm's Law** in time as $v(t) = R\,i(t)$. Since there is **no differentiation or integration** involved, applying the Laplace transform to both sides yields: $$\mathcal{L}\{v(t)\} = R\,\mathcal{L}\{i(t)\} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V(s) = R\,I(s)$$ > [!NOTE] > The resistor is **frequency-independent**. Its impedance is **constant $\forall\, s$** in the complex plane. --- ### 2.2 Inductor — The "Frequency-Dependent" Element The inductor's time-domain voltage-current relationship is: $$v_L(t) = L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt}$$ Applying the Laplace transform (using the derivative property $\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$): $$V_L(s) = L\,[s\,I(s) - i(0^-)] = sL\,I(s) - L\,i(0^-)$$ **Rearranging into impedance form:** $$V_L(s) = \underbrace{sL}_{Z_L(s)}\,I(s) \;\;\underbrace{-\; L\,i(0^-)}_{\text{Initial Condition Source}}$$ This can be modelled as: - **A series impedance $sL$ with a voltage source $L\,i(0^-)$** in the opposite direction of assumed current (Thevenin form), OR - **A parallel combination of $sL$ with a current source $\frac{i(0^-)}{s}$** (Norton form). --- ### 2.3 Capacitor — The "Inverse Frequency" Element The capacitor's time-domain current-voltage relationship is: $$i_C(t) = C\,\frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$ Applying the Laplace transform: $$I_C(s) = C\,[s\,V(s) - v(0^-)] = sC\,V(s) - C\,v(0^-)$$ **Rearranging into impedance form:** $$V_C(s) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{sC}}_{Z_C(s)}\,I(s) \;\;+\;\; \frac{v(0^-)}{s}$$ Modelled as: - **A series impedance $\frac{1}{sC}$ with a voltage source $\frac{v(0^-)}{s}$** in series. - **A parallel combination of $\frac{1}{sC}$ with a current source $C\,v(0^-)$**. --- ## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Single Most Important Table for This Topic — Memorize It.** | Quantity | Resistor (R) | Inductor (L) | Capacitor (C) | |---|---|---|---| | **Time-Domain $v(t)$ vs $i(t)$** | $v = R\,i$ | $v = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$ | $i = C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | | **Impedance $Z(s)$** | $R$ | $sL$ | $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ | | **Admittance $Y(s)$** | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | $\dfrac{1}{sL}$ | $sC$ | | **Initial Condition (Thevenin)** | None | Series $V_s = L\,i(0^-)$ | Series $V_s = \dfrac{v(0^-)}{s}$ | | **Initial Condition (Norton)** | None | Parallel $I_s = \dfrac{i(0^-)}{s}$ | Parallel $I_s = C\,v(0^-)$ | | **Phase Angle of $Z(s)$ at $s=j\omega$** | $0°$ | $+90°$ | $-90°$ | | **Behavior as $s \to 0$** | $R$ | $0$ (short circuit) | $\infty$ (open circuit) | | **Behavior as $s \to \infty$** | $R$ | $\infty$ (open circuit) | $0$ (short circuit) | | **Stored Energy** | None | $\dfrac{1}{2}L\,i^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}C\,v^2$ | | **Unit (SI)** | $\Omega$ | $\Omega$ | $\Omega$ | ### Special Laplace Transform Properties Used in s-Domain Circuit Analysis | Property | Time Domain | s-Domain | |---|---|---| | **Linearity** | $a\,f(t) + b\,g(t)$ | $a\,F(s) + b\,G(s)$ | | **Differentiation** | $\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$ | $sF(s) - f(0^-)$ | | **Integration** | $\displaystyle\int_{0^-}^{t} f(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ | | **Frequency Shift** | $e^{-at}f(t)$ | $F(s+a)$ | | **Initial Value Theorem** | $f(0^+)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s\to\infty} sF(s)$ | | **Final Value Theorem** | $f(\infty)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s\to 0} sF(s)$ (only if poles in LHP) | --- ## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Power System Stability Analysis** — Analyzing switching transients and fault responses in transmission lines. 2. **Control System Design** — Transfer functions $\frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$ of RLC networks form the basis of PID, lead-lag, and compensator design. 3. **Analog Filter Design** — Butterworth, Chebyshev, and Bessel filters are designed via s-domain pole-zero placement. 4. **Signal Processing & Communication** — Laplace analysis gives the frequency response of channels and amplifiers. 5. **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** — Transient analysis of boost/buck converters relies on s-domain models. 6. **Integrated Circuit Design** — On-chip RLC parasitics analyzed entirely in the s-domain. --- # 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section provides **exhaustive derivations** for the transform impedances, with **every algebraic transition** explicitly shown. This is exactly the type of working that fetches **full marks** in KTU valuation. --- ## Derivation 3.1 — Transform Impedance of a Resistor **Starting Equation (Time Domain — Ohm's Law):** $$v_R(t) = R\,i_R(t)$$ **Step 1:** Apply the Laplace transform operator $\mathcal{L}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides. $$\mathcal{L}\{v_R(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\{R\,i_R(t)\}$$ **Step 2:** Use the linearity property of the Laplace transform: $\mathcal{L}\{a\,f(t)\} = a\,F(s)$. $$V_R(s) = R\,I_R(s)$$ **Step 3:** Rearrange into the standard "impedance" form $V(s) = Z(s)\,I(s)$ by isolating $V_R(s)$. $$V_R(s) = \underbrace{R}_{Z_R(s)}\,I_R(s)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{Z_R(s) = R \quad;\quad Y_R(s) = \frac{1}{R}}$$ The resistor's impedance is **real, constant, and independent of $s$**. --- ## Derivation 3.2 — Transform Impedance of an Inductor (With Initial Conditions) **Starting Equation (Time Domain — Faraday's Law):** $$v_L(t) = L\,\frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 1:** Apply the Laplace transform to both sides. $$\mathcal{L}\{v_L(t)\} = L\,\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{di_L(t)}{dt}\right\}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the derivative property of the Laplace transform: $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute and recognize that the constant $L$ factors out (linearity). $$V_L(s) = L\,[s\,I_L(s) - i_L(0^-)]$$ **Step 4:** Distribute the multiplication by $L$. $$V_L(s) = sL\,I_L(s) - L\,i_L(0^-)$$ **Step 5:** Identify the impedance component (the term multiplying $I_L(s)$) and the source component. $$ \begin{aligned} V_L(s) &= \underbrace{sL}_{Z_L(s)}\,I_L(s) \;\;-\;\; \underbrace{L\,i_L(0^-)}_{\text{Initial Condition Voltage Source}} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 (Zero Initial Condition Special Case):** If $i_L(0^-) = 0$ (e.g., initially relaxed circuit), then: $$V_L(s) = sL\,I_L(s) \quad\Rightarrow\quad Z_L(s) = sL$$ **Admittance form:** $$Y_L(s) = \frac{1}{Z_L(s)} = \frac{1}{sL}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{Z_L(s) = sL \quad;\quad Y_L(s) = \frac{1}{sL}}$$ --- ## Derivation 3.3 — Transform Impedance of a Capacitor (With Initial Conditions) **Starting Equation (Time Domain — Constitutive Relation):** $$i_C(t) = C\,\frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 1:** Apply the Laplace transform to both sides. $$\mathcal{L}\{i_C(t)\} = C\,\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}\right\}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the derivative property. $$I_C(s) = C\,[s\,V_C(s) - v_C(0^-)]$$ **Step 3:** Distribute the multiplication by $C$. $$I_C(s) = sC\,V_C(s) - C\,v_C(0^-)$$ **Step 4:** Solve algebraically for $V_C(s)$ in terms of $I_C(s)$. $$sC\,V_C(s) = I_C(s) + C\,v_C(0^-)$$ **Step 5:** Divide throughout by $sC$ to isolate $V_C(s)$. $$V_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}\,I_C(s) + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$ **Step 6:** Identify the impedance and the initial-condition source. $$ \begin{aligned} V_C(s) &= \underbrace{\frac{1}{sC}}_{Z_C(s)}\,I_C(s) \;\;+\;\; \underbrace{\frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}}_{\text{Initial Condition Voltage Source}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 (Zero Initial Condition Special Case):** If $v_C(0^-) = 0$: $$V_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}\,I_C(s) \quad\Rightarrow\quad Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}$$ **Admittance form:** $$Y_C(s) = sC$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC} \quad;\quad Y_C(s) = sC}$$ --- ## Derivation 3.4 — The Series-Parallel RLC s-Domain Network Consider a series RLC circuit with input voltage $v(t)$, current $i(t)$, and zero initial conditions. The KVL equation is: $$v(t) = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}\int_{0^-}^{t} i(\tau)\,d\tau$$ **Step 1:** Apply the Laplace transform to both sides (using differentiation and integration properties). $$V(s) = R\,I(s) + L\,[s\,I(s) - i(0^-)] + \frac{1}{C}\,\frac{I(s)}{s}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute zero initial conditions $i(0^-) = 0$. $$V(s) = R\,I(s) + sL\,I(s) + \frac{1}{sC}\,I(s)$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $I(s)$. $$V(s) = \left[R + sL + \frac{1}{sC}\right]I(s)$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the total s-domain impedance as the sum of individual element impedances. $$Z(s) = R + sL + \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{s^2LC + sRC + 1}{sC}$$ **Step 5:** Write the current as a ratio of voltage to impedance (Ohm's Law in s-domain). $$I(s) = \frac{V(s)}{Z(s)} = \frac{sC\,V(s)}{s^2LC + sRC + 1}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{I(s) = \frac{sC}{s^2LC + sRC + 1}\,V(s)}$$ This rational function of $s$ is the foundation of **transfer function analysis** in control systems and filter design. --- ## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Impedance Behaviour at Special Frequencies | Frequency Condition | $s$ value | $Z_R$ | $Z_L = sL$ | $Z_C = \frac{1}{sC}$ | Physical Interpretation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **DC Steady State** | $s = 0$ | $R$ | $0$ (SC) | $\infty$ (OC) | Inductor → wire ; Capacitor → open | | **Very High Frequency** | $s \to \infty$ | $R$ | $\infty$ (OC) | $0$ (SC) | Inductor → open ; Capacitor → wire | | **AC Steady State** | $s = j\omega$ | $R$ | $j\omega L$ | $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$ | Phasor domain result | > [!NOTE] > **SC** = Short Circuit, **OC** = Open Circuit. --- # 🖼️ 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Read Before You Write!** > - **Most common mark-loss area (4 marks average lost per paper):** Students **forget to include the initial condition source** for inductors and capacitors. If the question says *"initially relaxed"*, you may omit the source; otherwise, you **must** include $L\,i_L(0^-)$ or $v_C(0^-)/s$ explicitly. > - **Unit mistakes:** $Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}$ — students often write $sC$ by mistake. Memorize: **"Capacitor looks like C, but in s-domain it is the INVERSE: 1/(sC)."** > - **Direction of inductor's initial source voltage:** The source $L\,i(0^-)$ has its **positive terminal at the tail of the assumed current direction**. Polarity errors cost 1–2 marks routinely. > - **Always express the final answer as a transfer function** in standard form $H(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ where $N$ and $D$ are polynomials in $s$. --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define transform impedance. Obtain the s-domain equivalent of an inductor with initial current $i(0^-)$.** ### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Transform impedance $Z(s)$ is the ratio of the Laplace-transformed voltage across an element to the Laplace-transformed current through it, treated as a complex frequency-dependent quantity with unit **Ohms ($\Omega$)**. **Derivation (2 marks):** Starting from $v_L(t) = L\,\dfrac{di_L(t)}{dt}$ and applying the Laplace transform: $$V_L(s) = sL\,I_L(s) - L\,i_L(0^-)$$ **s-domain equivalent:** An impedance $sL$ in series with a voltage source of magnitude $L\,i_L(0^-)$ whose polarity opposes the assumed current direction (Thevenin form). --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Write the transform impedance and admittance of a capacitor. Explain its behaviour at $s = 0$ and $s \to \infty$.** ### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks) **Formulas (1.5 marks):** - Impedance: $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC}$ - Admittance: $Y_C(s) = sC$ **Behaviour (1.5 marks):** - At $s = 0$ (DC steady state): $Z_C = \frac{1}{0\cdot C} \to \infty$ → capacitor behaves as an **open circuit** (correct physically, since DC cannot flow through a capacitor). - At $s \to \infty$ (very high frequency): $Z_C = \frac{1}{\infty \cdot C} \to 0$ → capacitor behaves as a **short circuit** (high-frequency signals pass through easily). --- ## 📗 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, split typically as **7 marks + 7 marks** across sub-parts (a) and (b). You must answer **either** OR-type choice. --- ### 🅰️ **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]` **(a) Derive the transform impedance of a capacitor C in the s-domain. Show the equivalent circuit representation with initial voltage $v_C(0^-)$.** (7 marks) **(b) A series RLC circuit has $R = 4\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,H$, $C = 0.25\,F$. A DC step voltage of $10\,V$ is applied at $t = 0$. Find the s-domain current $I(s)$ and hence the time-domain current $i(t)$ assuming zero initial conditions.** (7 marks) --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 Marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with the time-domain current-voltage relation of a capacitor: $$i_C(t) = C\,\frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the Laplace transform using the derivative property: $$I_C(s) = sC\,V_C(s) - C\,v_C(0^-)$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Rearrange to express $V_C(s)$: $$V_C(s) = \frac{I_C(s)}{sC} + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Identify the transform impedance: $$Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC} \quad;\quad Y_C(s) = sC$$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** Equivalent circuit diagram (Thevenin form): Impedance $\frac{1}{sC}$ in series with a voltage source $\frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$ (positive terminal at the same side as the original capacitor's positive plate). **Final Answer:** $\boxed{Z_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC}\ \text{with series voltage source } v_C(0^-)/s}$ --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 Marks) **Given:** $R = 4\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,H$, $C = 0.25\,F$, $V(s) = \frac{10}{s}$ (step input), zero initial conditions. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Total s-domain impedance of the series RLC circuit: $$Z(s) = R + sL + \frac{1}{sC} = 4 + s + \frac{1}{0.25\,s} = 4 + s + \frac{4}{s}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Combine into a single fraction: $$Z(s) = \frac{s^2 + 4s + 4}{s} = \frac{(s+2)^2}{s}$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Apply Ohm's Law in s-domain: $$I(s) = \frac{V(s)}{Z(s)} = \frac{10/s}{(s+2)^2/s} = \frac{10}{(s+2)^2}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Use the standard inverse Laplace pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{1}{(s+a)^2}\right\} = t\,e^{-at}$: $$i(t) = 10\,t\,e^{-2t}\quad\text{for } t \geq 0$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Physical interpretation: The current starts at zero, peaks, and decays to zero — characteristic of an **overdamped/critically damped second-order system** (since the discriminant of $s^2 + 4s + 4 = 0$ gives repeated roots at $s = -2$). **Final Answer:** $\boxed{i(t) = 10\,t\,e^{-2t}\ \text{A}}$ --- ### 🅱️ **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]` **(a) State and derive the transform admittance of an inductor. Comment on its behaviour at high frequencies.** (7 marks) **(b) For the circuit shown below, find the s-domain voltage $V_C(s)$ across the capacitor. Given: $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$, $L = 0.5\,H$, $C = 1\,F$, source voltage $V_s(t) = 6\,e^{-2t}\,u(t)$ V, with all initial conditions zero. The capacitor is in parallel with $R_2$, and $R_1$ is in series with $L$.** (7 marks) --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks) **Statement (1 mark):** The transform admittance of an inductor is $Y_L(s) = \dfrac{1}{sL}$, the reciprocal of its transform impedance $Z_L(s) = sL$. **Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from $v_L(t) = L\,\dfrac{di_L(t)}{dt}$, take the Laplace transform with $i_L(0^-) = 0$: $$V_L(s) = sL\,I_L(s) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{I_L(s)}{V_L(s)} = \frac{1}{sL} = Y_L(s)$$ **Admittance form (1 mark):** $\boxed{Y_L(s) = \dfrac{1}{sL}}$ **High-frequency behaviour (2 marks):** As $s \to \infty$ (i.e., at very high frequencies), $Y_L(s) \to 0$, meaning the inductor **draws no current** at infinite frequency — it acts as an **open circuit**. This is consistent with the physical principle that inductors oppose rapid changes in current, so at high frequencies, they appear as high-impedance paths. --- #### ✅ Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks) **Given:** $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$, $L = 0.5\,H$, $C = 1\,F$, $V_s(t) = 6\,e^{-2t}u(t)$, zero initial conditions. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Find the Laplace transform of the source: $$V_s(s) = \frac{6}{s+2}$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Identify all s-domain impedances: - $Z_{R_1}(s) = 2$ - $Z_{R_2}(s) = 3$ - $Z_L(s) = 0.5\,s$ - $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{1\cdot s} = \dfrac{1}{s}$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $C$: $$Z_{R_2C}(s) = \frac{R_2 \cdot \frac{1}{sC}}{R_2 + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{3 \cdot \frac{1}{s}}{3 + \frac{1}{s}} = \frac{3/s}{(3s+1)/s} = \frac{3}{3s+1}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Total series impedance in the loop: $$Z_{total}(s) = R_1 + sL + Z_{R_2C}(s) = 2 + 0.5\,s + \frac{3}{3s+1}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the **voltage divider rule** in the s-domain to find $V_C(s)$ (which equals the voltage across the $R_2 \parallel C$ combination): $$V_C(s) = V_s(s) \cdot \frac{Z_{R_2C}(s)}{Z_{total}(s)}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute and simplify (final expression): $$V_C(s) = \frac{6}{s+2} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{3}{3s+1}}{2 + 0.5\,s + \dfrac{3}{3s+1}}$$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{V_C(s) = \dfrac{18}{(s+2)\left[(2 + 0.5s)(3s+1) + 3\right]}}$ V (Students may further expand the denominator to obtain a polynomial form suitable for partial fraction expansion.) --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 High-Density Revision Checklist — Re-read 5 minutes before the exam.** - ✅ The **s-domain** is the **Laplace domain**, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex frequency. - ✅ The **transform impedance** of a **resistor** is simply $R$ (no change, no initial condition). - ✅ The **transform impedance** of an **inductor** is $sL$ (with an initial-condition source $L\,i_L(0^-)$ if $i_L(0^-) \neq 0$). - ✅ The **transform impedance** of a **capacitor** is $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ (with an initial-condition source $\dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$ if $v_C(0^-) \neq 0$). - ✅ **Admittance** $Y(s)$ is always the **reciprocal** of $Z(s)$, with unit **Siemens (S)**. - ✅ At $s = 0$ (DC): Inductor → **short circuit**, Capacitor → **open circuit**. - ✅ At $s \to \infty$ (very high frequency): Inductor → **open circuit**, Capacitor → **short circuit**. - ✅ At $s = j\omega$ (sinusoidal steady state): Replace $s$ with $j\omega$ to retrieve **phasor analysis** results. - ✅ For an **initially relaxed** circuit, all initial-condition sources are **zero**, simplifying analysis to pure algebraic manipulation. - ✅ The total s-domain impedance of **series** elements is the **sum** $Z_{total} = \sum Z_i(s)$. - ✅ The total s-domain admittance of **parallel** elements is the **sum** $Y_{total} = \sum Y_i(s)$. - ✅ After solving in the s-domain, **always use the inverse Laplace transform** (often via partial fractions) to return to the time domain for the final answer. - ✅ The **phasor domain** is a *special case* of the s-domain at $s = j\omega$, valid only for sinusoidal steady-state. - ✅ **KTU Board tip:** Always write down the **units** ($Z$ in $\Omega$, $Y$ in S) and **draw the s-domain circuit** — this fetches **easy diagram marks** in valuation. - ✅ **Master these five inverse Laplace pairs** (used constantly in Part B solutions): - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s}\right\} = 1$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at}$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^2}\right\} = t$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}\right\} = t\,e^{-at}$ - $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}\right\} = e^{-at}\sin\omega t$

topics.title: Mesh Analysis and Node Analysis of Transformed Circuits in S-Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mesh Analysis and Node Analysis of Transformed Circuits in S-Domain # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **S-Domain Circuit Analysis** is the method of analyzing linear, time-invariant (LTI) electrical networks after transforming all time-domain differential equations into the **Laplace domain** (s-domain), where the complex frequency variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$ replaces the time derivative operator $\dfrac{d}{dt}$.
