topics.title: EMF Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** EMF Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMF Equation (KTU 2024 Definition):** The **EMF Equation** of a DC generator expresses the relationship between the generated electromotive force (E_g) and the fundamental machine parameters — number of poles (**P**), flux per pole (**Φ**), armature speed (**N**), total armature conductors (**Z**), and number of parallel paths (**A**).

The standard form of the EMF equation is:

$$E_g = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A}$$

This equation is derived from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and is the cornerstone for analyzing the voltage generation in rotating DC machines.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Wheel of Electricity"**
>
> Imagine a **water wheel** with buckets arranged around its rim. As water flows into the buckets, the wheel rotates. The faster the wheel spins (**N**), the more water each bucket scoops. Now, replace the water with **magnetic flux lines** and the buckets with **conductors moving through a magnetic field**. Each conductor "cuts" flux lines as it rotates, generating a small voltage. Many conductors working together (combined in **parallel paths**) produce the total EMF.
>
> - **More poles (P)** → more magnetic regions → conductors cut flux more often.
> - **More flux per pole (Φ)** → stronger magnetic field → more voltage per cut.
> - **More conductors (Z)** → more "mini-voltages" added together.
> - **Fewer parallel paths (A)** → conductors add in series → higher total voltage.

---

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in the EMF Equation:**
> - **SI Unit of Flux (Φ):** Weber (Wb)
> - **SI Unit of Speed (N):** Revolutions Per Minute (RPM)
> - **SI Unit of EMF (E_g):** Volts (V)
> - **Number of Parallel Paths:**
>   - **Wave Winding:** A = 2 (always)
>   - **Lap Winding:** A = P (equals number of poles)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The EMF equation emerges from applying **Faraday's Law** to a rotating armature. The derivation follows a structured logical flow:

- **Step 1 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution:**
  In one complete revolution, each conductor cuts the flux passing under **all P poles**.
  Total flux cut per conductor per revolution = **P × Φ** Webers.

- **Step 2 — Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Second:**
  If the armature rotates at **N revolutions per minute**, then in one second it makes **N/60 revolutions**.
  Flux cut per conductor per second = **(P × Φ × N) / 60** Webers per second.

- **Step 3 — EMF Induced Per Conductor (Faraday's Law):**
  By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced in one conductor = rate of change of flux.
  **Average EMF per conductor = (PΦN) / 60** Volts.

- **Step 4 — Total EMF of the Armature:**
  There are **Z** total conductors on the armature. These Z conductors are divided into **A parallel paths**, so the number of conductors in **series per parallel path = Z/A**.
  Total EMF (E_g) = EMF per conductor × Conductors in series per path.
  **E_g = (PΦN / 60) × (Z / A) = (PΦNZ) / (60A)**

- **Step 5 — Lap vs Wave Winding Distinction:**
  - **Lap Winding:** A = P → E_g = (ΦNZ) / 60
  - **Wave Winding:** A = 2 → E_g = (PΦNZ) / 120

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of Poles | P | — | P | P |
| Flux per Pole | Φ | Weber (Wb) | Φ | Φ |
| Armature Speed | N | RPM | N | N |
| Total Conductors | Z | — | Z | Z |
| Parallel Paths | A | — | A = P | A = 2 |
| Conductors per Path | Z/A | — | Z/P | Z/2 |
| **Generated EMF** | **E_g** | **Volts** | **ΦNZ/60** | **PΦNZ/120** |
| **General Formula** | **E_g** | **Volts** | **PΦNZ/(60A)** | **PΦNZ/(60A)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Examination Tip:** In KTU board exams, students frequently confuse the value of **A** for lap and wave windings. Remember: **Lap = P**, **Wave = 2** — this single mistake costs full marks.

---

## Real-World Utility of the EMF Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The EMF equation is not just a textbook formula — it is used in:
> - **Generator Design:** Determining the number of turns required for a desired output voltage.
> - **Motor Analysis:** Calculating the back-EMF, which directly governs the motor's speed-torque characteristics.
> - **Voltage Regulation Studies:** Understanding how speed and field current variations affect terminal voltage.
> - **Power System Applications:** Sizing DC generators for substations, traction systems, and battery charging stations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

### Assumptions for the Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation assumes:
> 1. The armature is **rotated at a uniform speed** N (in RPM).
> 2. The **flux per pole (Φ)** is constant and uniformly distributed.
> 3. The conductors are **symmetrically placed** on the armature periphery.
> 4. The machine operates in **steady-state** conditions (no transients).

---

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1: Consider one armature conductor.**

In one complete revolution of the armature, the conductor cuts all the flux passing under all **P** poles.

$$
\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P \times \Phi \quad \text{(Weber)}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate flux cut per second.**

Since the armature makes **N** revolutions in **60 seconds**, it makes **N/60** revolutions per second.

$$
\text{Flux cut per conductor per second} = P \times \Phi \times \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{(Weber/second)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.**

According to Faraday's Law, the EMF induced in a conductor is equal to the **rate of change of flux linkages**.

$$
\text{Average EMF induced per conductor} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{60} \quad \text{(Volts)}
$$

**Step 4: Account for the total number of conductors.**

The armature has **Z** total conductors. These are distributed among **A** parallel paths.

$$
\text{Number of conductors in series per parallel path} = \frac{Z}{A}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the total generated EMF.**

The EMFs of conductors in series within one path add up. Since there are **Z/A** conductors in series per path:

$$
E_g = \left( \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{60} \right) \times \left( \frac{Z}{A} \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(Volts)}}
$$

---

### Special Cases — Simplification for Winding Types

**Case 1: Lap-Wound DC Machine (A = P)**

$$
E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, P} = \frac{\Phi \, N \, Z}{60}
$$

**Case 2: Wave-Wound DC Machine (A = 2)**

$$
E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \times 2} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{120}
$$

---

### Alternative Derivation Using Flux Cut Per Minute (Alternative Approach)

**Method 2 — Using Total Flux Cut Per Minute:**

Total flux cut per minute by all Z conductors = **P × Φ × N × Z** Weber per minute.

Since **A** parallel paths are in **parallel**, the conductors in **one path** = **Z/A**, and they are in **series**.

$$
E_g = \frac{\text{Total flux cut per minute}}{60 \times A} = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by 60?** Because the flux cut is computed **per minute** (since N is in RPM), but EMF requires flux cut **per second**. Hence the conversion factor **60**.
> **Why divide by A?** Because **A paths are in parallel**, the total EMF is that of **one path** — conductors in **one path = Z/A**.

---

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 4-pole, lap-wound DC generator has **1200 armature conductors** and runs at **1000 RPM**. The flux per pole is **0.05 Wb**. Calculate the generated EMF.

**Solution:**

Given:
- P = 4, A = P = 4 (lap wound), Z = 1200, N = 1000 RPM, Φ = 0.05 Wb.

$$
E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} = \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 1200}{60 \times 4}
$$

$$
E_g = \frac{4 \times 0.05 \times 1000 \times 1200}{240}
$$

$$
E_g = \frac{240000}{240} = 1000 \text{ Volts}
$$

**Verification using simplified lap formula:**

$$
E_g = \frac{\Phi \, N \, Z}{60} = \frac{0.05 \times 1000 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{60000}{60} = 1000 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Mermaid Block Diagram — Derivation Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT PARAMETERS: P, Phi, N, Z, A"] --> B["STEP 1: Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution"]
    B --> B1["Flux = P x Phi Weber"]
    B1 --> C["STEP 2: Convert RPM to Revolutions per Second"]
    C --> C1["Rev/sec = N / 60"]
    C1 --> D["STEP 3: Apply Faraday Law"]
    D --> D1["EMF per Conductor = P x Phi x N / 60 Volts"]
    D1 --> E["STEP 4: Identify Series Conductors per Path"]
    E --> E1["Series Conductors = Z / A"]
    E1 --> F["STEP 5: Multiply for Total EMF"]
    F --> F1["E_g = P x Phi x N x Z / 60A"]
    F1 --> G{"WINDING TYPE?"}
    G -->|Lap: A = P| H["E_g = Phi x N x Z / 60"]
    G -->|Wave: A = 2| I["E_g = P x Phi x N x Z / 120"]
    H --> J["FINAL OUTPUT: Generated EMF in Volts"]
    I --> J
```

---

## Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical & Functional Architecture of a DC Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["MECHANICAL INPUT"]
        M1["Prime Mover Turbine or Motor"]
    end

    subgraph MACHINE["DC GENERATOR ASSEMBLY"]
        M2["Armature Core with Conductors Z"]
        M3["Field Poles with Flux Phi"]
        M4["Commutator and Brushes"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT"]
        M5["Generated EMF E_g at Terminals"]
    end

    M1 -->|Rotational Speed N RPM| M2
    M3 -->|Provides Magnetic Field| M2
    M2 -->|Faraday Induction| M4
    M4 -->|Rectified DC Output| M5
    M3 -.->|Field Excitation Current| M3
```

---

## Mermaid Comparison Chart — Lap vs Wave Winding Impact on EMF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DC GENERATOR"] --> B{"WINDING TYPE"}
    B -->|LAP WINDING| C["A = P"]
    B -->|WAVE WINDING| D["A = 2"]
    C --> E["E_g = Phi x N x Z / 60"]
    D --> F["E_g = P x Phi x N x Z / 120"]
    E --> G["LOW VOLTAGE HIGH CURRENT APPLICATION"]
    F --> H["HIGH VOLTAGE LOW CURRENT APPLICATION"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define the EMF equation of a DC generator and list the factors affecting the generated EMF.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC generator gives the relationship between the generated voltage and the machine parameters:

$$E_g = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 \, A} \quad \text{(Volts)}$$

Where **P** = number of poles, **Φ** = flux per pole in Wb, **N** = speed in RPM, **Z** = total armature conductors, **A** = number of parallel paths.

**Factors affecting generated EMF:**
1. **Magnetic Flux (Φ):** E_g is directly proportional to flux per pole.
2. **Speed of Armature (N):** E_g is directly proportional to speed.
3. **Number of Conductors (Z):** E_g is directly proportional to Z.
4. **Number of Poles (P):** E_g is directly proportional to P (for wave winding).
5. **Type of Winding:** Affects the value of A, which inversely affects E_g.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for factors)*

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Distinguish between lap winding and wave winding with respect to the number of parallel paths and the resulting EMF equation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Lap Winding | Wave Winding |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Parallel Paths (A) | A = P | A = 2 |
| EMF Equation | E_g = ΦNZ / 60 | E_g = PΦNZ / 120 |
| Application | High current, low voltage | High voltage, low current |
| Number of Brushes | Equal to number of poles (P) | Always 2 |

*(1.5 marks for A values, 1.5 marks for EMF equations)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 6-pole, lap-connected DC generator has **720 armature conductors** and a flux per pole of **0.04 Wb**. It runs at a speed of **900 RPM**. Calculate:
> - (i) The generated EMF.
> - (ii) The generated EMF if the same machine is rewound as a wave generator with all other parameters unchanged. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Let the armature rotate at **N RPM**. The flux per pole is **Φ** Wb and there are **P** poles.

**Step 2:** Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = **P × Φ** Wb.

**Step 3:** Number of revolutions per second = **N / 60**.

**Step 4:** Flux cut per conductor per second = **P × Φ × N / 60** Wb/s.

**Step 5:** By Faraday's Law, average EMF per conductor:

$$
e = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N}{60} \quad \text{Volts}
$$

**Step 6:** Total conductors = Z, distributed in A parallel paths. Conductors in series per path = Z/A.

**Step 7:** Total EMF:

$$
\boxed{E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}}
$$

*(1 mark each for steps 1, 3, 5, 7 and 0.5 marks for steps 2, 4, 6 — 7 marks total)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- P = 6, Φ = 0.04 Wb, N = 900 RPM, Z = 720.

**Part (b)(i) — Lap Wound (A = P = 6):**

$$
E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A} = \frac{6 \times 0.04 \times 900 \times 720}{60 \times 6}
$$

$$
E_g = \frac{155520}{360} = 432 \text{ Volts}
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Wave Wound (A = 2):**

$$
E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \times 2} = \frac{6 \times 0.04 \times 900 \times 720}{120}
$$

$$
E_g = \frac{155520}{120} = 1296 \text{ Volts}
$$

**Observation:** The wave-wound generator produces **3 times** the EMF of the lap-wound machine (since A decreased from 6 to 2, and 6/2 = 3).

*(3.5 marks for each part with full working shown)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the constructional features of a DC generator and identify the role of each component in EMF generation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator delivers a current of **50 A** at **250 V**. The armature has **500 conductors** and the flux per pole is **30 mWb**. Calculate:
> - (i) The speed of the armature in RPM.
> - (ii) If the load is suddenly removed and the flux remains unchanged, what is the new terminal voltage (assuming no armature reaction)? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Constructional Features (7 Marks)

A DC generator consists of the following key parts:

1. **Yoke:** Provides mechanical support and acts as a magnetic flux return path. Made of **cast iron or cast steel**.
2. **Pole Core and Pole Shoe:** The pole core carries the field winding, and the pole shoe spreads the flux uniformly across the air gap.
3. **Field Winding (Poles):** Produces the magnetic flux **Φ** when excited by DC current. Number of poles = **P**.
4. **Armature Core:** A laminated cylindrical structure that houses the armature conductors. Lamination reduces **eddy current losses**.
5. **Armature Winding:** Contains the total **Z** conductors where EMF is induced by **Faraday's Law** as they rotate through the magnetic field.
6. **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of copper segments that converts the internally generated **AC** to **DC** at the output terminals.
7. **Brushes:** Carbon blocks that maintain sliding contact with the commutator and deliver the generated EMF to the external circuit.

**Role in EMF Generation:** The interaction between the **rotating armature conductors (Z)**, the **magnetic flux (Φ)**, and the **commutator-brushe assembly** produces the DC output E_g = PΦNZ/(60A).

*(1 mark per component for 6 components + 1 mark for role summary)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- P = 4, A = 2 (wave), Z = 500, Φ = 30 × 10⁻³ Wb = 0.03 Wb, V = 250 V, I = 50 A.

**Part (b)(i) — Find Speed N:**

Using the EMF equation:

$$
E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, N \, Z}{60 \, A}
$$

Assuming no armature reaction and negligible armature resistance, **E_g ≈ V = 250 V**.

$$
250 = \frac{4 \times 0.03 \times N \times 500}{60 \times 2}
$$

$$
250 = \frac{60 \, N}{120} = \frac{N}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 500 \text{ RPM}}
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — New Terminal Voltage (No-Load):**

When load is removed and flux remains constant, the speed remains 500 RPM (assuming prime mover speed is unchanged). Since E_g depends only on P, Φ, N, Z, and A — all of which are unchanged:

$$
E_{g,new} = 250 \text{ V}
$$

> **Note:** In practice, terminal voltage rises on no-load due to **armature reaction** being eliminated. With the assumption stated in the problem (no armature reaction), E_g remains **250 V**.

*(3.5 marks for each part with formula application and substitution)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong value of A:** Students often write **A = 2 for lap** and **A = P for wave** — this is the **#1 most common error**. Remember: **Lap → A = P**, **Wave → A = 2**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert RPM to RPS:** The formula requires **N/60** (revolutions per second). If you substitute N directly without the 60, your answer will be **60 times larger** — a full-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Mixing units of flux:** Flux must be in **Weber (Wb)**, not milliweber or maxwell. **1 mWb = 10⁻³ Wb**, **1 Maxwell = 10⁻⁸ Wb**. Always convert before substitution.
>
> 4. **Not writing the final unit:** Always end with **"Volts"** or **"V"**. Omitting units is a **0.5 mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** The full EMF equation derivation carries **7 marks** in a 14-mark question. Skipping intermediate steps like "flux cut per second" or "conductors in series per path" leads to **3-4 mark loss** even if the final answer is correct.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EMF Equation (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- **Core Formula:** **E_g = (P × Φ × N × Z) / (60 × A)** — memorize this verbatim.
- **Winding Rule:** **Lap → A = P**, **Wave → A = 2** — never confuse these.
- **Direct Proportionality:** E_g is directly proportional to **P, Φ, N, and Z**; inversely proportional to **A**.
- **Units Vigilance:** Φ in **Wb**, N in **RPM**, E_g in **Volts**.
- **Derivation Origin:** Based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.
- **Practical Insight:** A **wave-wound** generator produces a **higher EMF** than a **lap-wound** generator with identical P, Φ, N, Z — because A is smaller (2 vs P).
- **Back-EMF in Motors:** The same equation applies to DC motors, but E_g is called **back-EMF (E_b)** and opposes the applied voltage.
- **Lap Winding Application:** Used for **high-current, low-voltage** machines (e.g., electroplating, battery charging).
- **Wave Winding Application:** Used for **high-voltage, low-current** machines (e.g., lighting circuits, small power supplies).
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic typically appears as a **full 14-mark question** in Part B and a **3-mark question** in Part A of the End Semester Examination.
- **One-Line Mantra:** **"PFNZ over 60A"** — a mnemonic for the EMF equation's numerator and denominator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phasor Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phasor Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phasor** is a complex number (or rotating vector in the complex plane) that represents a sinusoidal function of time, capturing its **magnitude (RMS/Peak value)** and **phase angle** at a specific angular frequency $\omega$. The technique of representing sinusoids as phasors is called **Phasor Transformation**, and the geometric arrangement of these phasors depicting the relationship between voltages and currents in an AC circuit or machine is termed a **Phasor Diagram**.

Mathematically, a sinusoidal quantity:
$$v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$
is represented in phasor form as:
$$\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \angle \phi = V_{rms}(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Phasor diagrams are the primary tool for analyzing **single-phase transformers**, **synchronous machines**, and **AC circuit networks**. The reference phasor is *always* chosen along the positive real axis by convention, unless otherwise specified in the problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Clock Hand

Imagine a **giant clock face** lying flat on a table:
- The **length of the clock hand** = magnitude of the sinusoid (say, voltage $V_m$).
- The **angle the hand makes with 12 o'clock (positive real axis)** = phase angle $\phi$.
- The **hand rotates** at angular velocity $\omega$ (radians/second).

Now, if you take a **photograph of the clock at $t=0$**, the frozen image of the hand *is* the phasor. The vertical projection (shadow) of the hand at any instant gives the instantaneous value of the sinusoid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** A phasor is NOT a physical rotating vector in the wire — it is a **mathematical tool** that lets us convert differential equations (hard) into algebraic equations (easy) by suppressing the time-domain rotation $\omega t$.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Conventions (KTU Board Standard)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| RMS Voltage | $V$ | **Volts (V)** |
| RMS Current | $I$ | **Amperes (A)** |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | **rad/s** |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| Phase Angle | $\phi$ | **Degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)** |
| Power | $P, Q, S$ | **Watts, VAR, VA** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always take **flux $\Phi$** as the reference phasor in transformer phasor diagrams (since induced EMF $E$ lags $\Phi$ by 90°). For pure R-L-C circuits, **current $I$** is taken as the reference phasor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phasor Transformation Rules

A sinusoidal quantity $x(t) = X_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ can be represented in three equivalent ways:

1. **Time Domain (Instantaneous):** $x(t) = X_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$
2. **Phasor Domain (Complex):** $\mathbf{X} = \dfrac{X_m}{\sqrt{2}} \angle \phi = X_{rms} \angle \phi$
3. **Rectangular Form:** $\mathbf{X} = X_{rms}\cos\phi + jX_{rms}\sin\phi$

### 2.1.1 Conversion Logic (Why this works)

Since $e^{j\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)$, multiplying a complex phasor by $e^{j\omega t}$ gives a vector rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. Its real part projection yields the instantaneous sinusoid:
$$x(t) = \text{Re}\left[\sqrt{2}\,\mathbf{X}\,e^{j\omega t}\right]$$

## 2.2 Elementary R, L, C Phasor Relationships

For an AC circuit at angular frequency $\omega$, the voltage-current phasor relationships are:

| Element | Impedance $\mathbf{Z}$ | Phase of $V$ w.r.t. $I$ | Power Consumed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor ($R$) | $R \angle 0°$ | In-phase | $P = I^2 R$ (real) |
| Inductor ($L$) | $j\omega L = \omega L \angle 90°$ | Leads by 90° | $Q = I^2 X_L$ (reactive) |
| Capacitor ($C$) | $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} \angle -90°$ | Lags by 90° | $Q = I^2 X_C$ (reactive) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** **"ELI the ICE man"** — In an Inductor ($L$), EMF ($E$) **L**eads Current. In a Capacitor ($C$), Current **L**eads EMF. In a Resistor, $E$ and $I$ are **I**n-phase.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}$ | Ohm's law in phasor form | All AC circuits |
| 2 | $\mathbf{Z} = R + jX_L - jX_C$ | Series RLC impedance | Series resonance |
| 3 | $\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ | Impedance magnitude | RLC circuits |
| 4 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$ | Impedance phase angle | Power factor |
| 5 | $S = V I$ (VA) | Apparent power | Power triangle |
| 6 | $P = V I \cos\phi$ (W) | Real (active) power | Power triangle |
| 7 | $Q = V I \sin\phi$ (VAR) | Reactive power | Power triangle |
| 8 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_m$ | EMF equation | Transformers |
| 9 | $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2 = E_1/E_2$ (ideal) | Transformer ratio | Transformers |
| 10 | $E = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \Rightarrow E \text{ lags } \Phi \text{ by } 90°$ | Faraday's law | Transformer phasors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** In the phasor domain, $\dfrac{d}{dt} \leftrightarrow j\omega$ and $\int dt \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{j\omega}$. This conversion is what allows solving differential equations as algebraic ones.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phasor diagrams are indispensable in:
- **Power System Analysis:** Load flow studies, short-circuit calculations.
- **Transformer Design:** Magnetizing current analysis, regulation studies.
- **Synchronous Machines:** Power factor control, V-curve analysis.
- **Filter Design:** Determining resonance conditions.
- **Protection Engineering:** Directional relay coordination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Phasor Representation of a Pure Inductor

**Given:** A pure inductor $L$ carries current $i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 1:** Write the voltage equation using Faraday's law.
$$v(t) = L \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate the current with respect to time.
$$v(t) = L \cdot \dfrac{d}{dt}\left[I_m \cos(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the derivative of cosine.
$$v(t) = L \cdot I_m \cdot (-\omega \sin(\omega t))$$

**Step 4:** Convert sine to cosine (shift phase by $-90°$).
$$v(t) = \omega L \, I_m \cos(\omega t + 90°)$$

**Step 5:** Identify magnitude and phase.
$$V_m = \omega L \, I_m, \quad \phi_v = 90°$$

**Step 6:** Write phasor form (dividing by $\sqrt{2}$ for RMS).
$$\mathbf{V} = I_{rms} \cdot (j\omega L) = j X_L \mathbf{I}$$

**Conclusion:** Voltage leads current by **90°** in a pure inductor, and inductive reactance is $X_L = \omega L$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series RLC Phasor Sum

**Given:** $R$, $L$, $C$ in series, current $\mathbf{I}$ is the reference phasor.

**Step 1:** Express each voltage phasor with $\mathbf{I}$ as reference.

- Across R: $\mathbf{V}_R = I \angle 0° \cdot R = IR \angle 0°$
- Across L: $\mathbf{V}_L = I \angle 0° \cdot jX_L = IX_L \angle 90°$
- Across C: $\mathbf{V}_C = I \angle 0° \cdot (-jX_C) = IX_C \angle -90°$

**Step 2:** Apply KVL in phasor form.
$$\mathbf{V}_S = \mathbf{V}_R + \mathbf{V}_L + \mathbf{V}_C$$

**Step 3:** Substitute rectangular forms.
$$\mathbf{V}_S = IR + jIX_L - jIX_C$$

**Step 4:** Factor out common term.
$$\mathbf{V}_S = I\left[R + j(X_L - X_C)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the complex impedance.
$$\mathbf{V}_S = \mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{Z}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{Z} = R + j(X_L - X_C)$$

**Step 6:** Compute magnitude and phase.
$$\vert \mathbf{V}_S \vert = I\sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Phasor Diagram for an Ideal Transformer on Load

**Given:** Single-phase ideal transformer, secondary delivering current $I_2$ at phase angle $\phi_2$ with respect to $V_2$.

**Step 1:** Place secondary terminal voltage $\mathbf{V}_2$ along the reference axis.
$$\mathbf{V}_2 = V_2 \angle 0°$$

**Step 2:** Draw load current $\mathbf{I}_2$ lagging $\mathbf{V}_2$ by $\phi_2$ (lagging PF load).
$$\mathbf{I}_2 = I_2 \angle -\phi_2$$

**Step 3:** The induced EMF $\mathbf{E}_2$ must overcome $\mathbf{V}_2$ plus the voltage drop across secondary impedance (for an *ideal* transformer with no impedance, $\mathbf{E}_2 = \mathbf{V}_2$).

**Step 4:** The primary induced EMF $\mathbf{E}_1$ is related by turns ratio.
$$\dfrac{\mathbf{E}_1}{\mathbf{E}_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = K \text{ (transformation ratio)}$$
$$\mathbf{E}_1 = K \mathbf{E}_2 = K V_2 \angle 0°$$

**Step 5:** Apply KVL on primary side (ideal transformer, no winding resistance).
$$\mathbf{V}_1 = \mathbf{E}_1 = K V_2 \angle 0°$$

**Step 6:** Primary current $\mathbf{I}_1$ is determined by mmf balance: $N_1 \mathbf{I}_1 = N_2 \mathbf{I}_2$ (with no load current for ideal case).
$$\mathbf{I}_1 = \dfrac{\mathbf{I}_2}{K} \angle -\phi_2$$

**Conclusion:** In an ideal transformer, the phasor diagram shows $\mathbf{V}_1$ and $\mathbf{V}_2$ in phase, and $\mathbf{I}_1$ and $\mathbf{I}_2$ in phase, both maintaining the same load phase angle $\phi_2$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Resolving Phasors in the Complex Plane

To convert a phasor $\mathbf{V} = V \angle \phi$ into rectangular form:
$$\mathbf{V} = V\cos\phi + jV\sin\phi$$

To convert rectangular form back to polar:
$$\vert \mathbf{V} \vert = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi)^2 + (V\sin\phi)^2}$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{V\sin\phi}{V\cos\phi}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When drawing phasor diagrams, **always maintain consistent angular scale** and label **all magnitudes AND all angles** explicitly. Missing labels is the #1 reason for mark deductions in KTU board exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Phasor Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Sinusoidal Signal x of t equals Xm cos omega t plus phi"] --> nodeB["Identify Amplitude Xm and Phase phi"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Compute RMS Value Xrms equals Xm divided by sqrt 2"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Form Phasor X equals Xrms angle phi"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Element Type?"}
    nodeE -->|R| nodeF["V equals I times R angle 0 degree"]
    nodeE -->|L| nodeG["V equals I times j omega L angle 90 degree"]
    nodeE -->|C| nodeH["V equals I times 1 over j omega C angle minus 90 degree"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Sum Vectors using KVL or KCL"]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Draw Phasor Diagram in Complex Plane"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Compute Magnitude and Phase"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Convert Back to Time Domain"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Series RLC Phasor Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRef["Reference Axis Positive Real Direction"]
    nodeIR["I phasor along Reference"]
    nodeVR["VR along I direction In Phase"]
    nodeVL["VL 90 degrees above I Leading"]
    nodeVC["VC 90 degrees below I Lagging"]
    nodeVnet["Vnet equals VR plus VL minus VC"]
    nodeAngle["Phase angle phi between Vnet and I"]

    nodeRef --> nodeIR
    nodeIR --> nodeVR
    nodeIR --> nodeVL
    nodeIR --> nodeVC
    nodeVR --> nodeVnet
    nodeVL --> nodeVnet
    nodeVC --> nodeVnet
    nodeVnet --> nodeAngle
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Power Triangle from Phasor Data

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeV["Voltage Phasor V angle 0 deg Reference"]
    nodeI["Current Phasor I angle minus phi Lagging"]
    nodeP["Real Power P equals VI cos phi Watts Horizontal"]
    nodeQ["Reactive Power Q equals VI sin phi VAR Vertical"]
    nodeS["Apparent Power S equals VI VA Hypotenuse"]
    nodePF["Power Factor cos phi equals P divided by S"]

    nodeV --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeP
    nodeI --> nodeQ
    nodeP --> nodeS
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodePF
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Single-Phase Transformer Phasor Diagram (Lagging PF Load)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePhi["Flux Phi along Reference Axis Primary"]
    nodeE1["E1 Lags Phi by 90 degrees"]
    nodeE2["E2 Lags Phi by 90 degrees"]
    nodeV1["V1 equals minus E1 for Ideal Transformer"]
    nodeV2["V2 equals E2 for Ideal Transformer"]
    nodeI0["No Load Current I0 Leads Phi by Phi0"]
    nodeIw["Working Component Iw In Phase with Phi"]
    nodeIm["Magnetizing Component Im 90 degrees behind V1"]
    nodeI1["Primary Current I1 equals I0 plus I2 dash"]
    nodeI2["Secondary Current I2 Lags V2 by phi2"]

    nodePhi --> nodeE1
    nodePhi --> nodeE2
    nodeE1 --> nodeV1
    nodeE2 --> nodeV2
    nodePhi --> nodeI0
    nodeV1 --> nodeIm
    nodePhi --> nodeIw
    nodeI0 --> nodeIm
    nodeI0 --> nodeIw
    nodeV1 --> nodeI1
    nodeV2 --> nodeI2
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Phasor Diagram Construction Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start Identify Circuit Type and Known Quantities"]
    nodeCh1["Choose Reference Phasor I for Series and V for Parallel"]
    nodeCh2["Mark Reference along Positive Real Axis"]
    nodeD1["Draw Element Voltages with Correct Phase Shifts"]
    nodeD2["Resistor 0 degrees, Inductor plus 90, Capacitor minus 90"]
    nodeAdd["Apply KVL or KCL to Sum Phasors Tip to Tail"]
    nodeRes["Resolve into Resultant Magnitude and Angle"]
    nodeVerify["Verify using Complex Arithmetic"]
    nodeDone["Final Phasor Diagram Complete"]

    nodeStart --> nodeCh1
    nodeCh1 --> nodeCh2
    nodeCh2 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeRes
    nodeRes --> nodeVerify
    nodeVerify --> nodeDone
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** In KTU board exams, the **physical phasor diagram** is expected to be drawn on **graph paper** with proper scaling (e.g., 1 cm = 50 V). The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **logical structure** of the phasor relationships — students should translate these into precise geometric phasor diagrams during exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define a phasor. Why is the phasor representation of sinusoidal quantities useful in AC circuit analysis?

**Model Answer:**
A phasor is a complex number representation of a sinusoidal function of time, capturing its RMS magnitude and phase angle at a specific angular frequency. A sinusoidal quantity $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$ is represented as $\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \angle \phi$.

The phasor representation is useful because:
1. It converts **differential equations** (in time domain) into **algebraic equations** (in phasor domain) by replacing $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ with $j\omega$.
2. It allows **graphical visualization** of voltage-current phase relationships through phasor diagrams.
3. It simplifies **addition and subtraction** of sinusoidal quantities of the same frequency using vector rules.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** In a purely inductive AC circuit, draw the phasor diagram and write the expression for impedance.

**Model Answer:**
For a purely inductive circuit, the voltage **leads** the current by **90°**. The phasor diagram shows current $\mathbf{I}$ along the reference axis and voltage $\mathbf{V}$ along the positive imaginary axis (90° counter-clockwise from $\mathbf{I}$).

Impedance expression:
$$\mathbf{Z}_L = j\omega L = X_L \angle 90° \, \Omega$$

where $X_L = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ is the inductive reactance in **Ohms (Ω)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** A series RLC circuit has $R = 30 \, \Omega$, $L = 0.4 \, \text{H}$, and $C = 50 \, \mu\text{F}$. It is supplied by a 230 V, 50 Hz AC source. **Draw the phasor diagram and calculate the total impedance, current, and power factor** of the circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a single-phase ideal transformer with turns ratio 10:1, the secondary delivers 100 A at 230 V to a load with power factor 0.8 lagging. **Draw the phasor diagram and find the primary voltage and primary current.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1:** Calculate inductive reactance.
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 0.4 = 125.66 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2:** Calculate capacitive reactance.
$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = 63.66 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3:** Calculate net reactance.
$$X = X_L - X_C = 125.66 - 63.66 = 62.00 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Calculate impedance magnitude.
$$\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 62^2} = \sqrt{900 + 3844} = \sqrt{4744} = 68.88 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5:** Calculate phase angle.
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{62}{30}\right) = 64.20° \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate current.
$$I = \dfrac{V}{\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert} = \dfrac{230}{68.88} = 3.34 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate power factor.
$$\cos\phi = \cos(64.20°) = 0.435 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Phasor Diagram Description:** $\mathbf{I}$ along reference axis; $\mathbf{V}_R$ along $\mathbf{I}$ (magnitude 100.2 V); $\mathbf{V}_L$ at +90° (magnitude 419.7 V); $\mathbf{V}_C$ at -90° (magnitude 212.6 V); $\mathbf{V}_S$ as resultant at 64.2° ahead of $\mathbf{I}$.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b):

**Step 1:** Identify given data.
- Turns ratio $K = N_1/N_2 = 10$
- Secondary voltage $V_2 = 230 \, \text{V}$, secondary current $I_2 = 100 \, \text{A}$
- Power factor $\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$ lagging, so $\phi_2 = 36.87°$

**Step 2:** Take $\mathbf{V}_2$ as reference.
$$\mathbf{V}_2 = 230 \angle 0° \, \text{V}$$
$$\mathbf{I}_2 = 100 \angle -36.87° \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** For ideal transformer, $V_1/V_2 = N_1/N_2$.
$$V_1 = K \times V_2 = 10 \times 230 = 2300 \, \text{V}$$
$$\mathbf{V}_1 = 2300 \angle 0° \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** For ideal transformer, $I_1 \cdot N_1 = I_2 \cdot N_2$.
$$I_1 = \dfrac{I_2}{K} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10 \, \text{A}$$
$$\mathbf{I}_1 = 10 \angle -36.87° \, \text{A}$$

**Phasor Diagram Description:** Both $\mathbf{V}_1$ and $\mathbf{V}_2$ along reference axis; $\mathbf{I}_1$ and $\mathbf{I}_2$ both lagging their respective voltages by 36.87°.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** A coil of resistance $12 \, \Omega$ and inductance $0.1 \, \text{H}$ is connected in series with a $100 \, \mu\text{F}$ capacitor across a 200 V, 50 Hz supply. **Draw the phasor diagram and find the current drawn, the power consumed, and the power factor.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with the help of a phasor diagram how the **no-load phasor diagram of a single-phase transformer** is constructed. Indicate the role of the no-load current components. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a):

**Step 1:** Compute reactances.
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 0.1 = 31.42 \, \Omega$$
$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = 31.83 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2:** Net reactance.
$$X = X_L - X_C = 31.42 - 31.83 = -0.41 \, \Omega \text{ (capacitive)}$$

**Step 3:** Impedance magnitude.
$$\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{12^2 + 0.41^2} = \sqrt{144 + 0.168} = 12.007 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Phase angle.
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{-0.41}{12}\right) = -1.957°$$

Since $X$ is negative, the circuit is **slightly capacitive** and current **leads** voltage.

**Step 5:** Current magnitude.
$$I = \dfrac{V}{\vert \mathbf{Z} \vert} = \dfrac{200}{12.007} = 16.66 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 6:** Power consumed.
$$P = I^2 R = (16.66)^2 \times 12 = 3330.7 \, \text{W} \approx 3.33 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 7:** Power factor.
$$\cos\phi = \cos(-1.957°) = 0.9994 \text{ (leading)}$$

**Phasor Diagram:** $\mathbf{I}$ leads $\mathbf{V}_S$ by 1.957°; $\mathbf{V}_R$ in phase with $\mathbf{I}$; $\mathbf{V}_L$ at +90° from $\mathbf{I}$; $\mathbf{V}_C$ at -90° from $\mathbf{I}$ (slightly larger than $\mathbf{V}_L$).

---

#### Solution to Question B(b):

**Construction of No-Load Phasor Diagram of a Single-Phase Transformer:**

**Step 1:** Take **mutual flux $\Phi$** along the reference (positive real) axis.

**Step 2:** Draw induced EMFs $\mathbf{E}_1$ and $\mathbf{E}_2$ lagging $\Phi$ by **90°** (by Faraday's law, $e = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$).

**Step 3:** Apply KVL on primary: $\mathbf{V}_1 = -\mathbf{E}_1 + \mathbf{I}_0 \mathbf{Z}_1$. Ignoring primary impedance drop (ideal approximation), $\mathbf{V}_1 = -\mathbf{E}_1$, so $\mathbf{V}_1$ leads $\Phi$ by **90°** (opposite to $\mathbf{E}_1$).

**Step 4:** The no-load current $\mathbf{I}_0$ has two components:
- **Magnetizing component $\mathbf{I}_m$**: In phase with $\Phi$ (or 90° behind $\mathbf{V}_1$), produces the flux. $I_m = I_0 \sin\phi_0$.
- **Core loss (working) component $\mathbf{I}_w$**: In phase with $\mathbf{V}_1$, supplies hysteresis and eddy current losses. $I_w = I_0 \cos\phi_0$.

**Step 5:** $\mathbf{I}_0 = \mathbf{I}_w + \mathbf{I}_m$, leading $\Phi$ by angle $\phi_0$ (typically 20°–30° for iron-core transformers).

**Step 6:** The phasor $\mathbf{V}_1$ leads $\mathbf{I}_0$ by angle $(90° - \phi_0)$. The no-load power factor is $\cos(90° - \phi_0) = \sin\phi_0 \approx 0.2$ to $0.3$.

**Phasor Diagram Layout (Reference):**
- $\Phi$ along $+x$ axis
- $\mathbf{E}_1, \mathbf{E}_2$ along $-y$ axis
- $\mathbf{V}_1$ along $+y$ axis
- $\mathbf{I}_w$ along $+y$ axis (in phase with $\mathbf{V}_1$)
- $\mathbf{I}_m$ along $+x$ axis (in phase with $\Phi$)
- $\mathbf{I}_0$ at angle $\phi_0$ ahead of $\Phi$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Peak and RMS values** in calculations → Always convert $V_m$ to $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ when using phasors.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $X_C$** in series RLC → $X_C$ is subtracted, not added: $X = X_L - X_C$.
> 3. **Wrong reference phasor** choice → For transformers, use $\Phi$ (flux) as reference; for R-L-C circuits, use $I$ (current) for series and $V$ for parallel.
> 4. **Not drawing arrows on phasors** → Each phasor must have an arrowhead showing the direction of rotation (counter-clockwise convention).
> 5. **Confusing leading and lagging** → In a phasor diagram, the phasor that is "ahead in counter-clockwise direction" leads.
> 6. **Skipping units in the answer** → Always write $\Omega$, V, A, W, VAR explicitly.
> 7. **Wrong angle conversion** → Calculator must be in **DEG mode** when inputting values in degrees.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **phasor** is a complex number representing a sinusoid's magnitude (RMS) and phase, **not** a physical rotating vector.
- The phasor transformation converts time-domain differential equations to algebraic equations by replacing $\dfrac{d}{dt} \rightarrow j\omega$.
- **"ELI the ICE man"** is the master mnemonic: Inductor voltage leads current by 90°; Capacitor voltage lags current by 90°; Resistor voltage is in phase with current.
- In **series circuits**, current $\mathbf{I}$ is the natural reference; in **parallel circuits**, voltage $\mathbf{V}$ is the natural reference.
- For **transformer phasor diagrams**, flux $\Phi$ is the universal reference because $\mathbf{E}$ lags $\Phi$ by 90° (Faraday's law).
- The **impedance triangle** ($R$, $X$, $\vert Z \vert$) and the **power triangle** ($P$, $Q$, $S$) are directly related by $\cos\phi$.
- The **phase angle** $\phi$ of the impedance determines the power factor: $\text{PF} = \cos\phi$.
- At **resonance** in series RLC: $X_L = X_C$, the impedance is purely resistive ($\mathbf{Z} = R$), and the current is maximum.
- The phasor diagram must be drawn with **proper scale**, **labeled magnitudes**, and **labeled angles** for full marks in KTU exams.
- **Impedance in rectangular form:** $\mathbf{Z} = R + j(X_L - X_C)$.
- **Impedance in polar form:** $\mathbf{Z} = \vert Z \vert \angle \phi$, where $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right)$.
- **Apparent power** $S = VI$ (VA), **Real power** $P = VI\cos\phi$ (W), **Reactive power** $Q = VI\sin\phi$ (VAR).
- For an **ideal transformer**: $\mathbf{V}_1 / \mathbf{V}_2 = N_1/N_2$ and $\mathbf{I}_1 \cdot N_1 = \mathbf{I}_2 \cdot N_2$ (phasor form, magnitudes only for ideal case).
- The **no-load current** of a transformer is typically **2–5%** of full-load current and is highly reactive (low lagging PF, around 0.2–0.3).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Circuit Model (of a Transformer)** is an *electrical network of passive lumped parameters* — comprising **resistances (R), leakage reactances (X), and a magnetising branch (Rc ∥ jXm)** — that mathematically replicates the **input-output terminal behaviour** of an actual physical transformer under sinusoidal steady-state excitation, without solving the coupled electromagnetic field equations.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for `PERUT417`, the equivalent circuit of a single-phase two-winding transformer is derived by translating its *T-model* parameters to one common voltage level — typically the **primary side** — and is then further approximated into an **approximate equivalent circuit** by shifting the shunt (magnetising) branch across the primary terminals to simplify phasor arithmetic during load analysis.

The standard equivalent circuit contains **five lumped elements** that represent physical phenomena:

| Circuit Element | Physical Origin |
| :--- | :--- |
| $R_1$ | Ohmic resistance of primary winding |
| $X_1$ | Primary leakage reactance (flux that links only primary) |
| $R_2'$ | Referred ohmic resistance of secondary winding |
| $X_2'$ | Referred leakage reactance of secondary winding |
| $R_c$ | Core-loss resistance (represents hysteresis + eddy current) |
| $X_m$ | Magnetising reactance (represents mutual/core flux) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **approximate equivalent circuit** is preferred in KTU numerical problems because it shifts the shunt branch to the input terminals — eliminating the need to solve a **T-network** using simultaneous equations and reducing computations to a single KVL loop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumbing Analogy"

Imagine a real transformer as a **complex underground water pipeline** that carries fluid (electrical energy) from a reservoir (source) to a city (load). We cannot see inside the pipe, but we can measure *flow rate* and *pressure drop* at the inlet and outlet.

An **equivalent circuit** is the engineer's *transparent plastic replica* of that pipe:

- The **two series resistors** $R_1$ and $R_2'$ → act like *narrow pipe sections* that cause friction (copper losses).
- The **two series reactances** $X_1$ and $X_2'$ → act like *elbows and bends* that restrict flow oscillation (leakage flux drop).
- The **parallel capacitor-like $X_m$** → represents the *storage capacity* of the pipe wall that absorbs/releases energy (magnetising energy).
- The **parallel resistor $R_c$** → represents *small leaks* from the pipe wall (hysteresis and eddy current losses in the core).

By measuring the pressure (voltage) and flow (current) at the ends of this *plastic model*, we get **identical numbers** to the real pipeline — without ever opening the ground. This is the genius of the **lumped-parameter equivalent circuit**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEUTICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Construction Logic — From Physical Transformer to Equivalent Circuit

The equivalent circuit is **not assumed**; it is **derived** from the coupled-circuit equations of a two-winding transformer. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **draw, label, and justify** every transition. The construction proceeds in **five ordered steps**:

1. **Start with the T-Model** — Two windings, mutual flux linkage $M$, and per-coil resistances and leakage reactances.
2. **Refer secondary to primary** — Multiply secondary voltages by turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$ and divide secondary currents by $a$, eliminating the ideal transformer core.
3. **Combine series elements** — Define $R_{01} = R_1 + R_2'$ (equivalent resistance referred to primary) and $X_{01} = X_1 + X_2'$ (equivalent reactance referred to primary).
4. **Add the magnetising branch** — The shunt branch ($R_c \parallel jX_m$) is connected **across the primary EMF** $E_1$ in the **exact** circuit, and **across the primary terminals** $V_1$ in the **approximate** circuit.
5. **Solve using KVL & KCL** — Apply Kirchhoff's laws on the simplified loop to find regulation, efficiency, and voltage drop.

## 2.2 Why "Referring" is Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transformer has **two physically isolated windings** with **different voltages and currents**. Combining them into one algebraic circuit requires that **all secondary quantities be mathematically re-scaled to the primary voltage level** so that the entire network has a single reference.

The transformation uses the turns ratio $a = N_1/N_2$:

$$
V_2' = a \cdot V_2
$$

$$
I_2' = \dfrac{I_2}{a}
$$

$$
R_2' = a^2 \cdot R_2
$$

$$
X_2' = a^2 \cdot X_2
$$

> The factor $a^2$ appears because **both voltage scales by $a$ and current scales by $1/a$**, and $R = V/I$, so $R$ scales by $a \cdot a = a^2$. **Power remains invariant** under referring, which is the physical correctness check.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Approximate Circuit

The exact equivalent circuit has a **T-network** feeding a **shunt branch** at an internal node. Solving requires the **two-loop mesh method** (KCL at the shunt node + KVL on each loop). The KTU board examiner typically tests whether a student understands the **approximation validity condition**:

> The approximation **shifts the shunt branch from the internal EMF node to the primary terminals**, valid only when the **no-load current $I_0$ is small compared to the full-load current** — i.e., when the transformer carries significant load.

This is why the **approximate equivalent circuit is used for load calculations** but the **exact circuit is used for no-load and magnetising current analysis**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Turns ratio | $a = N_1 / N_2$ | Dimensionless |
| Voltage transformation | $V_2' = a \cdot V_2$ | Volts (V) |
| Current transformation | $I_2' = I_2 / a$ | Amperes (A) |
| Impedance transformation | $Z_2' = a^2 \cdot Z_2$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Equivalent resistance | $R_{01} = R_1 + R_2' = R_1 + a^2 R_2$ | $\Omega$ |
| Equivalent reactance | $X_{01} = X_1 + X_2' = X_1 + a^2 X_2$ | $\Omega$ |
| Equivalent impedance | $Z_{01} = R_{01} + jX_{01}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Voltage drop (approx.) | $\Delta V \approx I_2 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 + X_{01} \sin\phi_2)$ | Volts |
| Voltage regulation | $\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{V_{1,\text{NL}} - V_{1,\text{FL}}}{V_{1,\text{FL}}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Copper loss (full load) | $P_{cu,\text{FL}} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ | Watts (W) |
| Core (iron) loss | $P_i = V_1^2 / R_c = I_0^2 R_c$ | Watts (W) |
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi_2}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi_2 + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}}$ | Per-unit |
| Phasor of secondary induced EMF | $E_1 = V_1 - I_1 (R_1 + jX_1)$ | Volts |
| KVL for primary loop | $V_1 = E_1 + I_1 (R_1 + jX_1)$ | Volts |
| KVL for secondary loop | $E_2' = V_2' + I_2' (R_2' + jX_2')$ | Volts |
| No-load current magnitude | $I_0 = \sqrt{I_w^2 + I_\mu^2}$ | Amperes |
| Core-loss component | $I_w = P_i / V_1$ | Amperes |
| Magnetising component | $I_\mu = V_1 / X_m$ | Amperes |

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $x$ in the efficiency formula represents the **fraction of full load** (e.g., $x = 0.5$ for half load, $x = 1.5$ for 150% overload). $S$ is the apparent power rating in VA, and $\cos\phi_2$ is the load power factor.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equivalent circuit is the **working tool of every transformer design engineer** because it enables:

- **Protection relay setting** — Calculating fault currents and through-fault stability.
- **Tap-changer design** — Determining voltage regulation limits under varying load.
- **Parallel operation** — Matching impedance ratios of two transformers sharing a load.
- **Power-quality studies** — Predicting voltage sag and reactive power demand.
- **Energy audit** — Quantifying no-load (iron) and load (copper) losses for efficiency labelling.

> Without the equivalent circuit, every transformer calculation would require solving **Maxwell's field equations** in a nonlinear magnetic medium — computationally impossible for routine design work.


<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Voltage Regulation from the Phasor Diagram

The **voltage regulation** of a transformer is the change in secondary terminal voltage when the load is removed (full-load to no-load), expressed as a percentage of the full-load voltage. The KTU 2024 board consistently asks for its derivation from the **approximate equivalent circuit phasor diagram**.

### Step 1: Write the KVL for the secondary side (referred to primary)

Starting from the secondary induced EMF $E_2'$ and traversing the secondary series impedance $R_2' + jX_2'$, we get the secondary terminal voltage $V_2'$:

$$
E_2' = V_2' + I_2' (R_2' + jX_2')
$$

**Logic:** The induced EMF must overcome the internal impedance drop before reaching the load terminals.

### Step 2: Substitute the primary quantities

For the **approximate** circuit, $V_1 \approx E_1$ (neglecting the small drop across the primary series impedance due to no-load current), and $E_1 = E_2' = a E_2$. The primary applied voltage at no load equals $E_1$, while at full load it equals $V_1$:

$$
V_{1,\text{NL}} = E_1 = a E_2
$$

$$
V_{1,\text{FL}} = V_1 \approx V_2' = a V_2
$$

**Logic:** At no load, no current flows, so no series drop exists — applied voltage equals induced EMF.

### Step 3: Construct the phasor diagram

Take $V_2'$ as the **reference phasor** along the positive real axis. The current $I_2'$ lags by angle $\phi_2$ for a lagging power-factor load.

The induced EMF $E_2'$ is obtained by adding the drop $I_2' R_2'$ (in phase with $I_2'$) and $I_2' X_2'$ (leading $I_2'$ by 90°).

### Step 4: Resolve the drop into orthogonal components

Drop the perpendicular from the tip of $E_2'$ onto the $V_2'$ axis extension. The total voltage drop is:

$$
\Delta V = I_2' R_2' \cos\phi_2 + I_2' X_2' \sin\phi_2
$$

**Logic:** The drop has two orthogonal components — the *in-phase* part adds directly to regulation, while the *quadrature* part must be projected using the small-angle approximation $\sqrt{1 - \epsilon^2} \approx 1$.

### Step 5: Convert to percentage

$$
\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_2'} \times 100 = \dfrac{I_2' (R_2' \cos\phi_2 + X_2' \sin\phi_2)}{V_2'} \times 100
$$

**Logic:** Regulation is normalised by the rated voltage to give a dimensionless quality metric.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 10 kVA, 2000/200 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has $R_1 = 5\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 0.05\,\Omega$, $X_1 = 12\,\Omega$, $X_2 = 0.12\,\Omega$. Find the **voltage regulation at 0.8 power factor lagging**.

**Step A: Compute turns ratio**

$$
a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{2000}{200} = 10
$$

**Step B: Refer secondary resistance to primary**

$$
R_2' = a^2 R_2 = (10)^2 \times 0.05 = 5\,\Omega
$$

**Step C: Refer secondary reactance to primary**

$$
X_2' = a^2 X_2 = (10)^2 \times 0.12 = 12\,\Omega
$$

**Step D: Compute full-load primary current**

$$
I_{1,\text{FL}} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{2000} = 5\,\text{A}
$$

**Step E: Compute equivalent resistance and reactance referred to primary**

$$
R_{01} = R_1 + R_2' = 5 + 5 = 10\,\Omega
$$

$$
X_{01} = X_1 + X_2' = 12 + 12 = 24\,\Omega
$$

**Step F: Compute voltage drop at 0.8 pf lagging**

For $\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$, $\sin\phi_2 = 0.6$:

$$
\Delta V = I_1 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 + X_{01} \sin\phi_2)
$$

$$
\Delta V = 5 \times (10 \times 0.8 + 24 \times 0.6)
$$

$$
\Delta V = 5 \times (8 + 14.4) = 5 \times 22.4 = 112\,\text{V}
$$

**Step G: Compute percentage regulation**

$$
\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{112}{2000} \times 100 = 5.6\%
$$

**Result:** Voltage regulation = **5.6 %** at 0.8 power factor lagging.

## 3.2 Derivation of Efficiency from the Equivalent Circuit

The **efficiency** $\eta$ of a transformer is the ratio of output power to input power. Because the equivalent circuit gives direct access to the two loss components, the efficiency formula emerges cleanly.

### Step 1: Define output power

$$
P_{\text{out}} = V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi_2
$$

where $x$ is the load fraction and $S$ is the rated apparent power.

**Logic:** Output power scales linearly with load current.

### Step 2: Define losses

- **Core (iron) loss** $P_i$ — depends on applied voltage (constant at all loads):

$$
P_i = \dfrac{V_1^2}{R_c} = \text{constant}
$$

- **Copper loss** $P_{cu}$ — depends on $I^2$ (varies with load square):

$$
P_{cu} = I_1^2 R_{01} = x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}
$$

**Logic:** Core loss is voltage-driven (no load exists even at full load voltage), while copper loss is current-driven (zero at no load, maximum at full load).

### Step 3: Assemble the efficiency expression

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_i + P_{cu}} = \dfrac{x S \cos\phi_2}{x S \cos\phi_2 + P_i + x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}}
$$

**Logic:** Input power equals output plus all losses; substituting into the efficiency ratio yields the standard expression.

### Step 4: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

To find the load at which $\eta$ is maximum, differentiate $\eta$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero. Equivalently, note that the derivative vanishes when **core loss equals copper loss**:

$$
P_i = x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}
$$

Thus, the **load fraction for maximum efficiency** is:

$$
x_{\max\eta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu,\text{FL}}}}
$$

**Logic:** The maximum-efficiency condition is where the two loss curves (constant iron loss and quadratically rising copper loss) intersect — this balances the *always-on* iron loss against the *load-dependent* copper loss.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Exact Equivalent Circuit (T-Model with Shunt at EMF Node)

The exact equivalent circuit preserves the **T-topology** by placing the magnetising branch at the **internal EMF node** rather than at the primary terminals. This model is mandatory for **no-load analysis** and for problems requiring computation of the **magnetising current** explicitly.

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1in[V1 Applied Voltage] --> R1A[R1 Primary Resistance]
    R1A --> X1A[X1 Primary Leakage Reactance]
    X1A --> NodeEMF{Internal EMF Node E1}
    NodeEMF --> RcL[Rc Core Loss Resistance]
    NodeEMF --> XmL[jXm Magnetising Reactance]
    RcL --> GndA[Reference]
    XmL --> GndA
    NodeEMF --> R2p[R2 Prime Secondary Resistance]
    R2p --> X2p[X2 Prime Secondary Leakage Reactance]
    X2p --> V2term[V2 Prime Terminal Voltage]
    V2term --> LoadA[Load ZL]
    LoadA --> GndA

    classDef source fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef pass fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef shunt fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000

    class V1in source
    class R1A,X1A,R2p,X2p,V2term pass
    class RcL,XmL,NodeEMF shunt
    class LoadA,GndA load
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left vertical branch** is the primary series impedance $R_1 + jX_1$.
- The **central node** is where the **shunt branch** ($R_c \parallel jX_m$) is connected, modelling the magnetising current drawn from the induced EMF.
- The **right vertical branch** is the secondary series impedance (referred), $R_2' + jX_2'$, terminated by the load.

## 4.2 Approximate Equivalent Circuit (Shunt at Primary Terminals)

For **load analysis**, the KTU board prefers the approximate circuit, where the magnetising branch is shifted to the input terminals. This trade-off introduces a small error in voltage regulation but drastically simplifies the arithmetic.

```mermaid
graph LR
    V1in2[V1 Applied Voltage] --> RcL2[Rc Core Loss Resistance]
    V1in2 --> XmL2[jXm Magnetising Reactance]
    RcL2 --> GndB[Reference]
    XmL2 --> GndB
    V1in2 --> R01A[R01 Equivalent Resistance]
    R01A --> X01A[X01 Equivalent Reactance]
    X01A --> V2term2[V2 Prime Terminal Voltage]
    V2term2 --> LoadB[Load ZL]
    LoadB --> GndB

    classDef source2 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef pass2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef shunt2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef load2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,color:#000

    class V1in2 source2
    class R01A,X01A,V2term2 pass2
    class RcL2,XmL2 shunt2
    class LoadB,GndB load2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **shunt branch** is now at the **input terminals** (no longer at the internal EMF node).
- A **single series impedance** $R_{01} + jX_{01}$ replaces the four individual series elements.
- The circuit is now a **simple series-parallel network** solvable in one KVL loop.

## 4.3 Phasor Diagram for Lagging Power-Factor Load

The phasor diagram is the **graphical equivalent of the KVL equations**. It is essential for KTU derivations involving regulation at different power factors.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RefAxis [Reference Axis]
        V2ref[V2 Prime Reference Phasor]:::phasor
    end

    V2ref --> Add1((Phasor Sum))
    I2ref[I2 Prime Lagging Current]:::current --> DropR[I2R2 Prime Drop]
    I2ref --> DropX[I2X2 Prime Drop]

    DropR --> Add1
    DropX --> Add1

    Add1 --> E2ref[E2 Prime Induced EMF]:::phasor
    E2ref -.->|Shifted by a| E1ref[E1 Induced EMF on Primary Side]:::phasor
    E1ref --> V1ref[V1 Applied Voltage]:::phasor

    V1ref --> Drop1R[I1R1 Drop]:::phasor
    V1ref --> Drop1X[I1X1 Drop]:::phasor

    classDef phasor fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#000
    classDef current fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $V_2'$ is the reference.
- $I_2' R_2'$ is in phase with $I_2'$ (along the current direction).
- $I_2' X_2'$ is perpendicular to $I_2'$ (leading by 90°).
- $E_2' = V_2' + I_2' R_2' + j I_2' X_2'$ is the vector sum.
- $E_1 = a E_2'$ (same phase, scaled magnitude).
- $V_1 = E_1 + I_1 R_1 + j I_1 X_1$ closes the primary loop.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transformer Analysis

This block diagram maps the *engineering workflow* from raw machine data to actionable design parameters using the equivalent circuit as the central computational kernel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Machine Data: Ratings, OC Test, SC Test] --> B[Identify Turns Ratio a]
    B --> C[Refer All Secondary Quantities to Primary Side]
    C --> D[Construct Equivalent Circuit: Exact or Approximate]
    D --> E[Apply KVL and KCL on Lumped Network]
    E --> F[Compute Voltage Regulation]
    D --> G[Identify Loss Components Pi and Pcu]
    G --> H[Compute Efficiency at Any Load x]
    E --> I[Compute Secondary Terminal Voltage]
    F --> J[Design Output: Tap Changer Setting]
    H --> K[Design Output: Cooling Rating]
    I --> L[Design Output: Protection Relay Setting]

    classDef inputBox fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef processBox fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000

    class A,B,C inputBox
    class D,E,G processBox
    class F,H,I processBox
    class J,K,L outputBox
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: Define the term "equivalent circuit" of a transformer. Why is it necessary to refer secondary quantities to the primary side?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> An **equivalent circuit** of a transformer is a lumped-parameter electrical network (comprising resistances, leakage reactances, and a magnetising branch) that replicates the electrical behaviour of the actual two-winding machine at its terminals, without involving the magnetic coupling equations.

**Necessity of referring (3 points expected):**
1. The primary and secondary windings are **electrically isolated** but magnetically coupled; referring provides a **common electrical reference** for phasor arithmetic.
2. Referring preserves **power invariance** — output power computed on the referred side equals the actual secondary power.
3. It enables the use of **standard circuit theorems** (Thevenin, superposition) which require a single-port network description.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Draw the approximate equivalent circuit of a single-phase transformer referred to the primary. State the approximation made and the condition under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer:**

**Diagram (described in text):**
The approximate equivalent circuit has a shunt branch ($R_c$ in parallel with $jX_m$) connected directly across the **primary input terminals**, followed by a **single series branch** $R_{01} + jX_{01}$ in series with the load.

**Approximation made:** The **magnetising branch is shifted from the internal EMF node to the primary terminals**, eliminating the need to solve a T-network.

**Validity condition:** The no-load current $I_0$ must be **negligibly small** (typically $< 5\%$) compared to the full-load current. Under heavy load, the small drop across $R_1$ and $X_1$ due to $I_0$ is insignificant compared to the drop due to load current.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**
**`[KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]`**

A single-phase, 5 kVA, 200/400 V, 50 Hz transformer has the following parameters referred to the low-voltage (primary) side:

- $R_1 = 0.2\,\Omega$, $X_1 = 0.5\,\Omega$
- $R_2 = 0.8\,\Omega$, $X_2 = 2.0\,\Omega$ (actual secondary side values)
- Core loss resistance $R_c = 600\,\Omega$, Magnetising reactance $X_m = 300\,\Omega$

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Draw the exact equivalent circuit referred to the primary side and calculate the **voltage regulation at 0.85 power factor lagging**.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Calculate the **efficiency at full load and at half load** for the same power factor. Also find the load at which maximum efficiency occurs.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Compute turns ratio**

$$
a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{200}{400} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2: Refer secondary resistance and reactance to primary**

$$
R_2' = a^2 R_2 = (0.5)^2 \times 0.8 = 0.25 \times 0.8 = 0.2\,\Omega
$$

$$
X_2' = a^2 X_2 = (0.5)^2 \times 2.0 = 0.25 \times 2.0 = 0.5\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3: Compute equivalent resistance and reactance**

$$
R_{01} = R_1 + R_2' = 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4\,\Omega
$$

$$
X_{01} = X_1 + X_2' = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Compute full-load primary current**

$$
I_{1,\text{FL}} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{5000}{200} = 25\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 5: Compute voltage drop at 0.85 pf lagging**

Given $\cos\phi_2 = 0.85$, $\sin\phi_2 = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} = \sqrt{0.2775} \approx 0.5268$.

$$
\Delta V = I_1 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 + X_{01} \sin\phi_2)
$$

$$
\Delta V = 25 \times (0.4 \times 0.85 + 1.0 \times 0.5268)
$$

$$
\Delta V = 25 \times (0.34 + 0.5268) = 25 \times 0.8668 = 21.67\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 6: Compute percentage regulation (referred to primary)**

$$
\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{V_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{21.67}{200} \times 100 = 10.84\%
$$

**Result:** Voltage regulation = **10.84 %** at 0.85 pf lagging.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Compute core (iron) loss**

$$
P_i = \dfrac{V_1^2}{R_c} = \dfrac{(200)^2}{600} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{600} \approx 66.67\,\text{W}
$$

**Step 2: Compute full-load copper loss**

$$
P_{cu,\text{FL}} = I_{1,\text{FL}}^2 \times R_{01} = (25)^2 \times 0.4 = 625 \times 0.4 = 250\,\text{W}
$$

**Step 3: Efficiency at full load ($x = 1.0$)**

$$
P_{\text{out,FL}} = S \cos\phi_2 = 5000 \times 0.85 = 4250\,\text{W}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{FL}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out,FL}}}{P_{\text{out,FL}} + P_i + P_{cu,\text{FL}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{FL}} = \dfrac{4250}{4250 + 66.67 + 250} \times 100 = \dfrac{4250}{4566.67} \times 100 \approx 93.06\%
$$

**Step 4: Efficiency at half load ($x = 0.5$)**

At half load, copper loss becomes $x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}} = (0.5)^2 \times 250 = 62.5\,\text{W}$. Output power is $0.5 \times 5000 \times 0.85 = 2125\,\text{W}$.

$$
\eta_{0.5} = \dfrac{2125}{2125 + 66.67 + 62.5} \times 100 = \dfrac{2125}{2254.17} \times 100 \approx 94.27\%
$$

**Step 5: Load at maximum efficiency**

$$
x_{\max\eta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{cu,\text{FL}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{66.67}{250}} = \sqrt{0.2667} \approx 0.516
$$

**Result:**
- $\eta_{\text{FL}} \approx 93.06\%$
- $\eta_{0.5} \approx 94.27\%$
- Maximum efficiency occurs at **51.6 % of full load**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2** (Alternative Selection)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A 100 kVA, 1100/220 V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following test data:
- **OC Test (LV side open):** 110 V, 5 A, 200 W
- **SC Test (HV side open):** 50 V, 90.9 A, 1000 W

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** From the above test data, determine the **equivalent circuit parameters referred to the HV side**. Draw the equivalent circuit.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Using the approximate equivalent circuit, calculate the **voltage regulation and efficiency at full load, 0.8 power factor lagging**.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Identify which side corresponds to which test**

Since the **OC test** is conducted with the **LV side open**, the instruments are on the **HV side** (1100 V rating). The test reads 110 V (which is the rated HV voltage scaled by core saturation; here actually, OC test is usually done by applying rated voltage to the LV side with HV open — but the problem states LV side open, so OC is on HV side, and the applied voltage of 110 V is the LV side rated voltage scaled up). 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reinterpretation:** The OC test is performed on the **side with instruments connected**. The applied voltage (110 V) and current (5 A) are on the instrumented side. Since 110 V matches the LV rating (220 V × 0.5 = 110 V not matching — let us accept the given values at face value). For SC test, 50 V and 90.9 A on HV side with LV shorted.

Let us treat the **HV side as the reference** (1100 V) and the **LV side as the secondary** (220 V).

Turns ratio:

$$
a = \dfrac{V_{\text{HV}}}{V_{\text{LV}}} = \dfrac{1100}{220} = 5
$$

**Step 2: Extract magnetising branch parameters from OC test**

The OC test gives **core loss** and **magnetising branch** parameters. The applied voltage 110 V is unusual (LV side is 220 V); we accept it as a reduced-voltage OC test.

Working component of no-load current:

$$
I_w = \dfrac{P_{\text{oc}}}{V_{\text{oc}}} = \dfrac{200}{110} \approx 1.818\,\text{A}
$$

Magnetising component of no-load current:

$$
I_\mu = \sqrt{I_0^2 - I_w^2} = \sqrt{5^2 - 1.818^2} = \sqrt{25 - 3.306} = \sqrt{21.694} \approx 4.658\,\text{A}
$$

Core-loss resistance (referred to instrumented side):

$$
R_c = \dfrac{V_{\text{oc}}^2}{P_{\text{oc}}} = \dfrac{(110)^2}{200} = \dfrac{12{,}100}{200} = 60.5\,\Omega
$$

Magnetising reactance (referred to instrumented side):

$$
X_m = \dfrac{V_{\text{oc}}}{I_\mu} = \dfrac{110}{4.658} \approx 23.61\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3: Refer magnetising branch to HV side**

Multiplying by $a^2 = 25$:

$$
R_{c,\text{HV}} = 60.5 \times 25 = 1512.5\,\Omega
$$

$$
X_{m,\text{HV}} = 23.61 \times 25 = 590.25\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4: Extract series impedance from SC test**

The SC test on the HV side reads $V_{\text{sc}} = 50\,\text{V}$ and $I_{\text{sc}} = 90.9\,\text{A}$. The equivalent impedance referred to HV:

$$
Z_{01,\text{HV}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{sc}}}{I_{\text{sc}}} = \dfrac{50}{90.9} \approx 0.55\,\Omega
$$

Equivalent resistance referred to HV:

$$
R_{01,\text{HV}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{sc}}}{I_{\text{sc}}^2} = \dfrac{1000}{(90.9)^2} = \dfrac{1000}{8262.81} \approx 0.121\,\Omega
$$

Equivalent reactance referred to HV:

$$
X_{01,\text{HV}} = \sqrt{Z_{01,\text{HV}}^2 - R_{01,\text{HV}}^2} = \sqrt{(0.55)^2 - (0.121)^2} = \sqrt{0.3025 - 0.01464} = \sqrt{0.2879} \approx 0.5365\,\Omega
$$

**Result:** Equivalent circuit parameters referred to HV:
- $R_{01} = 0.121\,\Omega$
- $X_{01} = 0.5365\,\Omega$
- $R_c = 1512.5\,\Omega$
- $X_m = 590.25\,\Omega$

**Equivalent Circuit (described):** The approximate circuit has $R_c = 1512.5\,\Omega$ in parallel with $jX_m = j590.25\,\Omega$ across the HV terminals, in series with $R_{01} = 0.121\,\Omega$ and $X_{01} = 0.5365\,\Omega$, feeding the referred load.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Compute full-load HV current**

$$
I_{1,\text{FL}} = \dfrac{S}{V_1} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{1100} \approx 90.91\,\text{A}
$$

**Step 2: Compute voltage drop at 0.8 pf lagging**

$\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$, $\sin\phi_2 = 0.6$.

$$
\Delta V = I_1 (R_{01} \cos\phi_2 + X_{01} \sin\phi_2)
$$

$$
\Delta V = 90.91 \times (0.121 \times 0.8 + 0.5365 \times 0.6)
$$

$$
\Delta V = 90.91 \times (0.0968 + 0.3219) = 90.91 \times 0.4187 \approx 38.06\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3: Compute percentage regulation**

$$
\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{38.06}{1100} \times 100 \approx 3.46\%
$$

**Step 4: Compute losses at full load**

Core loss: $P_i = P_{\text{oc}} = 200\,\text{W}$ (already measured on HV side at rated voltage).

Copper loss at full load: $P_{cu,\text{FL}} = P_{\text{sc}} = 1000\,\text{W}$ (SC test was done at full-load current 90.9 A).

**Step 5: Compute efficiency at full load**

Output power:

$$
P_{\text{out}} = S \cos\phi_2 = 100{,}000 \times 0.8 = 80{,}000\,\text{W}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{FL}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}} + P_i + P_{cu}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{FL}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{80{,}000 + 200 + 1000} \times 100 = \dfrac{80{,}000}{81{,}200} \times 100 \approx 98.52\%
$$

**Result:**
- Voltage regulation = **3.46 %**
- Full-load efficiency = **98.52 %**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Equivalent Circuit Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the turns ratio for impedance referring** — Students often use $a$ instead of $a^2$ for $R_2'$ and $X_2'$. This is the **single most common error**, costing up to 3 marks per sub-question.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong voltage in the regulation formula** — Regulation must be computed using the voltage of the **side to which the equivalent circuit is referred**, not the original secondary voltage. Using $V_2$ instead of $V_1$ gives a wrong answer that examiners will detect.
>
> 3. **Sign error in the regulation expression for leading pf** — For a leading power-factor load, the $\sin\phi_2$ term becomes **negative**, and the regulation can become *negative* (voltage rise on load addition). Students often use the lagging formula blindly.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to include the iron loss in efficiency** — Many students compute efficiency as $P_{\text{out}} / (P_{\text{out}} + P_{cu})$ only, ignoring $P_i$. This yields an efficiency that is **always higher than correct** and costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 5. **Using $V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{rated}}$ in the SC test analysis** — The SC test is done at *reduced* voltage, not rated voltage. Iron loss during the SC test is **negligible** (because $V_{\text{sc}} \ll V_{\text{rated}}$), so $P_{\text{sc}}$ represents **only copper loss**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up which side is "primary"** — In a step-up transformer used as step-down, the **physical primary** (input) becomes the LV side. The choice of reference side is arbitrary but must be **consistent** throughout the problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Equivalent Circuit Model:**

- The equivalent circuit is a **lumped-parameter replica** of the transformer for sinusoidal steady-state analysis.
- It contains **two series impedances** ($R_1, X_1, R_2', X_2'$) and **one shunt branch** ($R_c \parallel jX_m$).
- **Referring** is mandatory — secondary quantities are scaled by $a = N_1/N_2$; **impedances scale by $a^2$**, currents by $1/a$, voltages by $a$.
- The **exact equivalent circuit** has the shunt at the internal EMF node; the **approximate circuit** has it at the input terminals.
- The approximation is valid when **$I_0 \ll I_{\text{FL}}$** (always true for power transformers).
- **Voltage regulation** formula: $\%\text{Reg} = \dfrac{I (R \cos\phi + X \sin\phi)}{V} \times 100$.
- For **leading pf**, the $\sin\phi$ term becomes negative — regulation can be **negative**.
- **Efficiency** = Output / (Output + Iron Loss + Copper Loss).
- **Iron loss** is **constant** (voltage-driven); **Copper loss** varies as **$I^2$** (current-driven).
- **Maximum efficiency** occurs when **$P_i = x^2 P_{cu,\text{FL}}$** i.e. at $x = \sqrt{P_i / P_{cu,\text{FL}}}$.
- **OC test** gives **magnetising branch** parameters; **SC test** gives **series impedance** parameters.
- During **SC test**, $P_{\text{sc}}$ is **entirely copper loss** (iron loss is negligible at reduced voltage).
- During **OC test**, $P_{\text{oc}}$ is **entirely core loss** (copper loss is negligible at no load).
- The **phased diagram** always has $V_2$ as reference; $I_2$ lags/leads depending on load pf; series drops are $IR$ (in phase) and $IX$ (quadrature).
- KTU board expects **both** the diagram and the formula-based solution in long-answer questions.
- **Units check**: all resistances in $\Omega$, all reactances in $\Omega$, all voltages in V, all currents in A, all powers in W.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Losses and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Losses and Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Electrical Machines**, the terms *Losses* and *Efficiency* describe the quantitative relationship between the input power supplied to a machine and the useful output power delivered by it. Since no practical machine is ideal, a portion of the input energy is inevitably converted into **non-useful forms** (primarily heat and sound). These dissipated portions are termed **Power Losses (P_loss)**, and the ratio of useful output power to input power is known as the **Efficiency (η)** of the machine. Mathematically, **Efficiency** is expressed as: $$\eta = \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} = \frac{P_{input} - P_{losses}}{P_{input}} = 1 - \frac{P_{losses}}{P_{input}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3 of PERUT417, the classification of losses applies uniformly across **DC Machines, Transformers, and Induction Motors**. The Board Examiner expects you to identify the type, location, and nature (constant vs. variable) of each loss for every machine topology. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are filling a 100-litre water tank using a long pipe with small leaks along its length. You pour in 100 litres, but only 85 litres actually reach the tank. The 15 litres lost through the leaks represent **Power Losses**. The efficiency of the system is then $\frac{85}{100} = 85\%$.
