Each tangential force acts at a radius of $D/2$ from the shaft axis. Torque = Force × radius:

$$T_a = F_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{Z \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{\pi D \cdot A} \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

The $D$ cancels beautifully:

$$T_a = \frac{Z \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

Or in decimal form:

$$\boxed{T_a = 0.159 \, \Phi \, Z \, I_a \, \left(\frac{P}{A}\right) \text{ (N·m)}}$$

where the constant $1/(2\pi) \approx 0.159$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Quantity Wanted | Given | Use This Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $E_g$ | $\Phi, Z, N, P, A$ | $E_g = \Phi Z N P / (60 A)$ |
| $E_b$ in a motor | $V, I_a, R_a$ | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ |
| $V$ in a generator | $E_g, I_a, R_a$ | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ |
| $T_a$ (developed torque) | $\Phi, Z, I_a, P, A$ | $T_a = P Z \Phi I_a / (2 \pi A)$ |
| $T_{sh}$ (shaft torque) | $T_a$, rotational losses | $T_{sh} = T_a - \text{losses} \approx (P_{out} \cdot 60)/(2 \pi N)$ |
| Mechanical power developed | $T_a$ and $N$ | $P_m = T_a \cdot (2 \pi N / 60) = T_a \cdot \omega_m$ |
| Electrical power converted | $E_g$ and $I_a$ | $P_e = E_g \cdot I_a$ |
| Verification of $P_e = P_m$ | — | $E_g \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega_m$ |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Model Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V, lap-wound DC shunt motor has 800 armature conductors. The armature resistance is $0.12 \, \Omega$ and it draws an armature current of 50 A at a speed of 1000 rpm. Find: (a) The flux per pole, (b) The back-EMF, (c) The armature torque developed.

**Solution:**

**(a) Flux per pole:**

For a lap winding, $A = P = 4$. Back-EMF:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50)(0.12) = 250 - 6 = 244 \text{ V}$$

Using EMF equation with $E_b$:

$$\Phi = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{Z \cdot N \cdot P} = \frac{244 \times 60 \times 4}{800 \times 1000 \times 4} = \frac{58560}{3200000} = 0.0183 \text{ Wb}$$

**(b) Back-EMF** (already computed): $E_b = 244$ V.

**(c) Armature torque developed:**

$$T_a = \frac{P \cdot Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{4 \times 800 \times 0.0183 \times 50}{2 \pi \times 4}$$

$$T_a = \frac{2928}{25.133} \approx 116.5 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using power balance:**
> $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 244 \times 50 = 12200$ W
> $\omega_m = 2 \pi \times 1000 / 60 = 104.72$ rad/s
> $T_a = P_m / \omega_m = 12200 / 104.72 \approx 116.5$ N·m ✓
> Both methods agree — an excellent self-verification technique for KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Flow & Equivalence in a DC Machine

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions** between the EMF, torque, current, flux, and power domains in a DC machine. Although the underlying physics is a single rotating conductor, this block view is the standard KTU-style schematic representation for conceptual questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc1["V SUPPLY"] -->|Voltage| nodeA1["ARMATURE CIRCUIT Ra La"]
    nodeA1 -->|Back EMF Eb| nodeB1["ENERGY CONVERSION REGION Phi Z P A"]
    nodeSrc2["FIELD CIRCUIT Vf Rf"] -->|Excitation| nodeB1
    nodeB1 -->|Mechanical Coupling| nodeC1["SHAFT LOAD"]
    nodeB1 -->|Eq Ta PZ Phi Ia over 2 pi A| nodeD1["ARMATURE TORQUE Ta"]
    nodeB1 -->|Eq Eg Phi Z N P over 60 A| nodeE1["INDUCED EMF Eg"]
    nodeD1 -->|Ta minus losses| nodeF1["SHAFT TORQUE Tsh"]
    nodeE1 -->|Eg Ia equals Ta omega m| nodeG1["MECHANICAL POWER Pm"]
    nodeF1 -->|Tsh omega m| nodeG1
    nodeG1 -->|minus rotational losses| nodeH1["OUTPUT POWER Pout"]
    nodeA1 -->|Current Ia| nodeI1["ARMATURE CURRENT Ia"]
    nodeI1 -->|interacts with Phi| nodeD1
    nodeB1 -->|Speed N rpm| nodeE1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **ARMATURE CIRCUIT** (top-left) and **FIELD CIRCUIT** (middle-left) both feed the **ENERGY CONVERSION REGION**, which is the heart of the machine.
> - Inside this region, two outputs are generated simultaneously: an **INDUCED EMF** $E_g$ and an **ARMATURE TORQUE** $T_a$.
> - The **MECHANICAL POWER** $P_m = T_a \cdot \omega_m = E_g \cdot I_a$ is the bridge between the two — this identity is what KTU loves to test as a 3-mark question.

### 4.2 Conceptual Mapping: EMF vs Torque Roles in Generator vs Motor

| Operating Mode | EMF $E_g$ acts as | Torque $T_a$ acts as | KVL Relation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Generator** | **Source** of electrical energy | **Opposing** torque (prime-mover must overcome it) | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ |
| **Motor** | **Opposing** voltage (back-EMF) | **Driving** torque (turns the load) | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The two equations are structurally symmetric: $E_g$ depends on $\Phi, N$ and $T_a$ depends on $\Phi, I_a$. This duality is the foundation of **reversible energy conversion** in DC machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the EMF equation of a DC machine and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The average EMF induced in a DC machine is:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

where:
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole in **Weber (Wb)**
- $Z$ = total number of **armature conductors**
- $N$ = **speed** of the armature in **rpm**
- $P$ = **number of poles**
- $A$ = **number of parallel paths** in the armature winding

**Significance:**
- This is the *output voltage* in a generator and the *back-EMF* in a motor.
- It is directly proportional to speed and flux, making it a **speed-flux-voltage** relation used extensively in motor control (e.g., Ward-Leonard, chopper control).
- The factor $A$ depends on winding type: $A = P$ for lap winding and $A = 2$ for wave winding.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define armature torque and shaft torque. How are they related in a DC motor?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Armature Torque ($T_a$):** The gross electromagnetic torque developed by the interaction of armature current and main field flux, given by $T_a = P Z \Phi I_a / (2\pi A)$. It is the torque available *inside* the machine at the armature surface.
- **Shaft Torque ($T_{sh}$):** The net torque available at the motor shaft for doing useful work, after subtracting mechanical rotational losses (friction, windage) and stray-load losses.

**Relation:**

$$T_{sh} = T_a - T_{\text{rotational losses}}$$

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega_m} = \frac{P_{out} \cdot 60}{2 \pi N}$$

In a motor, $T_{sh} > 0$ for motoring action; the difference $T_a - T_{sh}$ represents the torque needed to overcome the no-load rotational losses.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has an internal choice. Two full alternative questions are given below.**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC machine, starting from Faraday's law. Discuss how the EMF differs between lap-wound and wave-wound machines for the same armature.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Flux cut per conductor per revolution:*
A conductor passing under $P$ poles cuts a flux of $P \Phi$ per revolution.

*Step 2 — Time per revolution:*
A speed of $N$ rpm corresponds to $N/60$ revolutions per second.

*Step 3 — Average EMF per conductor:*
By Faraday's law, the average EMF is the rate of flux cutting:
$$e_{cond} = \Phi \cdot P \cdot \frac{N}{60} \text{ V}$$

*Step 4 — Series conductors per path:*
The $Z$ conductors are split into $A$ parallel paths, so there are $Z/A$ conductors in series per path.

*Step 5 — Total EMF:*
$$E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\Phi P N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

**Comparison of lap vs wave winding:**

| Winding | $A$ | $E_g$ Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Lap | $A = P$ | $E_g = \Phi Z N / 60$ |
| Wave | $A = 2$ | $E_g = \Phi Z N P / (120)$ |

For a given $Z, P, N, \Phi$, the lap-wound machine produces a **lower EMF** because more parallel paths reduce the conductors in series. Hence lap windings are preferred for *low-voltage, high-current* machines (e.g., traction motors), and wave windings for *high-voltage* machines (e.g., small generators).

---

**Q. (b)** A 6-pole, 440 V, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 1200 armature conductors. The armature and field resistances are $0.3 \, \Omega$ and $220 \, \Omega$ respectively. At no-load, the motor runs at 1000 rpm drawing an armature current of 8 A. At full load, the armature current is 80 A. Assuming the flux is constant, calculate:
(i) The no-load speed,
(ii) The full-load speed,
(iii) The armature torque developed at full load.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $P = 6$, $V = 440$ V, wave $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $Z = 1200$, $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$.

**Step 1 — Compute back-EMF at no-load and full-load:**

$$E_{b,N\!L} = V - I_{a,N\!L} R_a = 440 - (8)(0.3) = 440 - 2.4 = 437.6 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{b,F\!L} = V - I_{a,F\!L} R_a = 440 - (80)(0.3) = 440 - 24 = 416 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — No-load speed:**

Since flux is constant, $E \propto N$. So:

$$E_{b,N\!L} = \frac{\Phi Z N_{N\!L} P}{60 A} = 437.6$$

$$\Phi = \frac{E_{b,N\!L} \cdot 60 \cdot A}{Z \cdot N_{N\!L} \cdot P} = \frac{437.6 \times 60 \times 2}{1200 \times 1000 \times 6} = \frac{52512}{7200000} = 7.293 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$

*(i) No-load speed is **1000 rpm** (given) — verified.*

**Step 3 — Full-load speed:**

$$N_{F\!L} = N_{N\!L} \times \frac{E_{b,F\!L}}{E_{b,N\!L}} = 1000 \times \frac{416}{437.6} = 950.6 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N_{F\!L} \approx 951 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 4 — Armature torque at full load:**

$$T_a = \frac{P \, Z \, \Phi \, I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{6 \times 1200 \times 7.293 \times 10^{-3} \times 80}{2 \pi \times 2}$$

$$T_a = \frac{4200.77}{12.566} = 334.3 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 334.3 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for armature torque developed in a DC motor, starting from the Lorentz force law. Show that the mechanical power developed in the armature equals the electrical power converted ($E_b \cdot I_a$).

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1 — Lorentz force on a conductor:*
$$F = B_{av} \cdot i \cdot l$$
where $i = I_a / A$.

*Step 2 — Express $B_{av}$ in terms of $\Phi$:*
$$B_{av} = \frac{P \Phi}{\pi D L}$$

*Step 3 — Total tangential force on $Z$ conductors:*
$$F_{total} = Z \cdot \frac{P \Phi}{\pi D L} \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot l = \frac{Z P \Phi I_a}{\pi D A}$$
(since $l = L$).

*Step 4 — Torque = Force × radius:*
$$T_a = F_{total} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{Z P \Phi I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

*Step 5 — Power identity:*

Mechanical power developed:
$$P_m = T_a \cdot \omega_m = \frac{P Z \Phi I_a}{2 \pi A} \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \cdot I_a = E_b \cdot I_a$$

$$\boxed{P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega_m}$$

This proves that the **electrical power converted** ($E_b \cdot I_a$) and the **mechanical power developed** ($T_a \cdot \omega_m$) are equal — a cornerstone of electromechanical energy conversion.

---

**Q. (b)** A 4-pole, 250 V, lap-wound DC series motor has 800 armature conductors and 4 series field turns per pole. The series field resistance is $0.05 \, \Omega$ and the armature resistance is $0.15 \, \Omega$. When drawing 60 A from the supply, the flux per pole is 25 mWb. Calculate:
(i) The speed of the motor,
(ii) The armature torque developed,
(iii) The shaft torque if rotational losses total 400 W.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $P = 4$, $V = 250$ V, lap $\Rightarrow A = P = 4$, $Z = 800$, $R_a = 0.15 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05 \, \Omega$, $I_a = 60$ A, $\Phi = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb.

**Step 1 — Back-EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a (R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 60(0.15 + 0.05) = 250 - 12 = 238 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Speed:**

$$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot Z \cdot P} = \frac{238 \times 60 \times 4}{25 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 \times 4}$$

$$N = \frac{57120}{80} = 714 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N = 714 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 3 — Armature torque:**

$$T_a = \frac{P \cdot Z \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{4 \times 800 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 60}{2 \pi \times 4}$$

$$T_a = \frac{4800}{25.133} = 190.99 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_a \approx 191 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Shaft torque:**

Mechanical power developed: $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 238 \times 60 = 14280$ W.

Rotational losses: $P_{rot} = 400$ W.

Output power: $P_{out} = P_m - P_{rot} = 14280 - 400 = 13880$ W.

$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{out}}{\omega_m} = \frac{13880 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 714} = \frac{832800}{4485.5} = 185.7 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{sh} \approx 185.7 \text{ N·m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $A$ and $P$:** In lap winding, $A = P$, *not* $A = 1$. A frequent mistake is to plug $A = 1$ for lap winding.
> 2. **Series motor resistance inclusion:** When using $E_b = V - I_a R_{total}$, *all* series resistances (armature, series field, interpole, compensating) must be summed. Omitting $R_{se}$ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 60 in EMF equation:** The $N$ in $E_g$ is in **rpm**, so the 60 is mandatory when converting to seconds.
> 4. **Shaft torque vs armature torque:** Never compute shaft torque directly from the torque equation — always subtract rotational losses.
> 5. **Units of $\Phi$:** Always use **Weber**, not milliWeber, in the EMF and torque equations. A common slip-up is to substitute mWb directly.
> 6. **Power cross-check:** Always verify $E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega_m$ — if these don't match to within rounding, you have a sign or factor error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3**

- ✅ The **EMF equation** $E_g = \Phi Z N P / (60 A)$ is the **backbone** of all DC machine numericals. Memorize it cold.
- ✅ **Lap winding** $\Rightarrow A = P$ (high current, low voltage); **Wave winding** $\Rightarrow A = 2$ (high voltage, low current).
- ✅ The **torque equation** $T_a = P Z \Phi I_a / (2 \pi A)$ is derived from the Lorentz force and is *independent of speed* (unlike EMF which depends on speed).
- ✅ **$E_g$ depends on $\Phi$ and $N$**; **$T_a$ depends on $\Phi$ and $I_a$**. This is the duality to remember.
- ✅ In a **motor**: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (back-EMF opposes supply).
- ✅ In a **generator**: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ (terminal voltage is EMF minus drop).
- ✅ The **power-conversion identity** $E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \omega_m$ is a 3-mark derivation guaranteed to appear.
- ✅ **Shaft torque** = Armature torque − Torque-equivalent of rotational losses.
- ✅ For **DC series motors**, all series resistances (armature + series field) are summed in the KVL loop.
- ✅ **Cross-check numericals** using both the power-balance identity and the direct formulas — this catches arithmetic errors before submission.
- ✅ Maintain unit consistency: $\Phi$ in **Wb**, $N$ in **rpm**, $I_a$ in **A**, $R$ in **$\Omega$**, $T$ in **N·m**, $P$ in **W**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Stages and Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Stages and Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Stages and Efficiency** refers to the systematic accounting of electrical and mechanical power as it flows through an electrical machine (DC generator, DC motor, or transformer), starting from the input power, passing through various conversion stages involving losses, and finally emerging as useful output power.

In any rotating electrical machine, the total power flow follows a strict sequence:
$$\text{Input Power} \rightarrow \text{Losses} \rightarrow \text{Output Power}$$

The **efficiency** ($\eta$) is the ratio of useful output power to the total input power, expressed as a percentage:
$$\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU examiner expects you to master three pillars for this topic:
> 1. The **power flow diagram** (also called the Sankey-like chart of energy conversion).
> 2. Categorization of **all losses** into electrical, magnetic, and mechanical groups.
> 3. Mathematical derivation of the condition for **maximum efficiency**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **water pumping system**: water enters a pump (electrical input), but some energy is lost as heat in the motor windles (copper losses), some leaks through the pump seals (friction/windage), and the remaining water reaches the tank usefully (output).

Similarly, in a **DC machine**:
- **Input Stage:** Electrical energy enters (motor) OR mechanical energy enters (generator).
- **Intermediate Stage:** Some power is "siphoned off" as losses — heat, sound, magnetic hum, bearing friction.
- **Output Stage:** What remains is the useful mechanical work (motor) or electrical energy (generator).

> [!NOTE]
> Think of efficiency like a **fuel tank in a car**: if the tank holds 50 liters (input) but only 12 liters reach the engine as useful combustion energy (output), the conversion efficiency is just 24%. The rest is lost to friction, heat, and exhaust. Electrical machines behave identically — they are energy converters with inherent losses at every stage.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **1 horsepower (HP)** = **746 W** (exact conversion factor for KTU numericals).
- **1 kW** = **1000 W** (SI unit of power).
- All copper losses vary as **$I^2R$** (square of current).
- Iron losses depend on **voltage** (and hence flux), independent of load current to first order.

> [!TIP]
> Always keep units consistent. KTU numericals often mix HP and kW in the same problem. **Convert everything to Watts first**, then solve.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Losses in DC Machines

In a DC machine, losses are broadly divided into three categories:

### (a) Electrical / Copper Losses (Variable Losses)
These are $I^2R$ losses in:
- **Armature winding copper loss:** $I_a^2 R_a$
- **Series field winding loss (if any):** $I_{se}^2 R_{se}$
- **Brush contact loss:** $V_b \times I_a$ (sometimes treated as a constant voltage drop of ~2V per brush set)

> [!NOTE]
> Copper losses are **load-dependent** — they vary with the square of the current drawn.

### (b) Magnetic / Iron / Core Losses (Constant Losses at Given Voltage/Speed)
- **Hysteresis loss:** $P_h = \eta_h \, B_{\max}^{1.6} \, f \, V$
- **Eddy current loss:** $P_e = \eta_e \, B_{\max}^{2} \, f^2 \, t^2 \, V$
- Total iron loss: $P_i = P_h + P_e$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Iron losses are **practically constant** as long as flux and speed remain constant (i.e., for a given supply voltage and frequency/speed).

### (c) Mechanical Losses (Constant Losses at Given Speed)
- **Friction loss** (bearing friction)
- **Windage loss** (air friction on rotating parts)
- Total mechanical loss: $P_m = P_f + P_w$

**Stray load losses** are additional minor losses (~1% of output) that occur in the armature due to flux distortion.

---

## 2.2 Power Flow Stages in a DC Generator

For a **DC generator**, the power stages from input to output are:

| Stage | Symbol | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical Power Input | $P_{in}$ | $T_{sh} \times \omega$ | From prime mover (e.g., turbine) |
| Iron + Mechanical Loss | $P_{i+m}$ | $P_i + P_m$ | Lost before conversion |
| Gross Mechanical Power (Developed Power) | $P_d$ | $P_{in} - (P_i + P_m)$ | Air-gap power = $E_b \times I_a$ |
| Armature Copper Loss | $P_{cu}$ | $I_a^2 R_a$ | In armature winding |
| Net Output Power | $P_{out}$ | $V \times I_L$ | Delivered to load |

$$\boxed{\text{Generator: } P_{in} \to (P_i + P_m) \to P_d = E_b I_a \to (I_a^2 R_a) \to P_{out} = V I_L}$$

---

## 2.3 Power Flow Stages in a DC Motor

For a **DC motor**, power flows in the opposite direction:

$$\boxed{\text{Motor: } P_{in} = V I_L \to (I_a^2 R_a) \to P_d = E_b I_a \to (P_i + P_m) \to P_{out} = T_{sh} \omega}$$

The **back-EMF power** $E_b I_a$ is the most important intermediate quantity — it represents the power actually converted from electrical to mechanical form (or vice versa).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Solving)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Basic efficiency |
| 2 | $P_d = E_b \times I_a$ | Developed (air-gap) power |
| 3 | $P_{cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss |
| 4 | $P_i = P_h + P_e$ | Total iron (core) loss |
| 5 | $P_m = P_f + P_w$ | Total mechanical loss |
| 6 | $P_{const} = P_i + P_m$ | Constant losses (independent of load) |
| 7 | $P_{var} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Variable losses (load-dependent) |
| 8 | $\eta_{\max}$ when $P_{const} = P_{var}$ | Condition for maximum efficiency |
| 9 | $I_{L,\max \eta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{const}}{R_a}}$ | Load current at maximum efficiency |
| 10 | $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{const} + P_{var}$ | Power balance equation |
| 11 | $P_{in} = V I_L$ | For a motor (DC shunt) |
| 12 | $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$ | Shaft power in watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 8** is the **most-tested KTU concept**. Whenever a problem asks "at what load is efficiency maximum?" — the answer is always: **when constant losses equal variable losses**.

---

## 2.5 Power Stages in a Transformer (Supplementary, Same Module)

A transformer has only two loss categories:
- **Iron (core) loss** $P_i$ — depends on voltage
- **Copper loss** $P_{cu}$ — depends on current

$$\eta_T = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_i + P_{cu}}$$

**All-day efficiency** (for distribution transformers that operate 24 hours with varying load):
$$\eta_{all-day} = \frac{\text{Energy output in 24 hours}}{\text{Energy input in 24 hours}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For transformers, the design choice of iron-to-copper loss ratio differs depending on whether the unit is a **power transformer** (high load factor) or a **distribution transformer** (low load factor, hence all-day efficiency matters).

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Motor Selection:** Engineers use efficiency curves to choose motors that operate at peak efficiency at typical industrial loads (e.g., 75–100% of rated load).
- **Energy Audits:** Power loss calculations are central to KTU-style problems on energy conservation in industries.
- **Generator Sizing:** Power stage calculations ensure that prime movers are correctly matched to the electrical load.
- **Transformer Design:** Trade-off between iron loss and copper loss directly determines transformer economics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

We will derive the condition under which a DC machine (or transformer) achieves **maximum efficiency** at some specific load current.

### Step 1: Express Total Losses

Let:
- $P_{const}$ = constant losses (iron + mechanical) — independent of load
- $I_L$ = load current
- $R_a$ = effective resistance in the current path (so that $I_L^2 R_a$ gives all variable losses)

Total losses:
$$P_{loss} = P_{const} + I_L^2 R_a$$

### Step 2: Express Efficiency

For a motor, the input power is:
$$P_{in} = V I_L$$

The output power is:
$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = V I_L - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a$$

Therefore, efficiency is:
$$\eta = \frac{V I_L - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a}{V I_L}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Maximum Condition

For maximum efficiency, we differentiate with respect to $I_L$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_L} = 0$$

Using the quotient rule on $\eta = \dfrac{N(I_L)}{D(I_L)}$ where $N = V I_L - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a$ and $D = V I_L$:

$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_L} = \frac{N' \cdot D - N \cdot D'}{D^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N' \cdot D = N \cdot D'$$

Computing the derivatives:
$$N' = V - 2 I_L R_a, \quad D' = V$$

Substituting:
$$(V - 2 I_L R_a) \cdot (V I_L) = (V I_L - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a) \cdot V$$

Divide both sides by $V$ (assuming $V \neq 0$):
$$(V - 2 I_L R_a) \cdot I_L = (V I_L - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a)$$

Expand the left side:
$$V I_L - 2 I_L^2 R_a = V I_L - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a$$

Cancel $V I_L$ from both sides:
$$- 2 I_L^2 R_a = - P_{const} - I_L^2 R_a$$

Rearrange:
$$P_{const} = 2 I_L^2 R_a - I_L^2 R_a = I_L^2 R_a$$

Therefore, the condition for **maximum efficiency** is:
$$\boxed{P_{const} = I_L^2 R_a = P_{var}}$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum efficiency occurs when **constant losses equal the variable losses** at that particular load.

### Step 4: Find the Load Current at Maximum Efficiency

From $P_{const} = I_L^2 R_a$:
$$\boxed{I_{L,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{const}}{R_a}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The variable losses at maximum efficiency = constant losses. Just plug $I_{L,\max}$ into $I_L^2 R_a$ to get the loss values at that point.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Efficiency in Terms of Fractional Load $x$

Let $x$ be the **fractional load** (i.e., $x = 1$ means full load). Then $I_L = x \cdot I_{FL}$ where $I_{FL}$ is the full-load current.

**Variable loss at fractional load $x$:**
$$P_{var}(x) = x^2 \cdot I_{FL}^2 \cdot R_a = x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}$$

**Constant loss:** $P_{const}$ (unchanged)

**Input power at fractional load $x$:**
$$P_{in}(x) = V \cdot x \cdot I_{FL} = x \cdot P_{in,FL}$$

**Efficiency at fractional load:**
$$\eta(x) = \frac{x \cdot P_{in,FL} - P_{const} - x^2 \cdot P_{cu,FL}}{x \cdot P_{in,FL}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $P_{in,FL}$:
$$\eta(x) = 1 - \frac{P_{const} + x^2 P_{cu,FL}}{x \cdot P_{in,FL}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This compact form is extremely useful for KTU problems where efficiency at $1/2$ load, $3/4$ load, or $1.5$ times full load is asked.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Standard Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A 250 V DC shunt motor draws an armature current of 50 A and field current of 2 A at full load. The armature resistance is $0.3 \, \Omega$ and shunt field resistance is $125 \, \Omega$. The iron loss is 500 W and mechanical loss is 300 W. Calculate:
1. Back-EMF $E_b$
2. Developed power $P_d$
3. Output power and shaft torque at 1500 rpm
4. Efficiency

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Back-EMF**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - 50 \times 0.3 = 250 - 15 = 235 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Developed Power**

$$P_d = E_b \cdot I_a = 235 \times 50 = 11750 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Output Power**

Total constant losses: $P_{const} = 500 + 300 = 800 \text{ W}$

Armature copper loss: $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a = 50^2 \times 0.3 = 750 \text{ W}$

Input power:
$$P_{in} = V I_L = 250 \times (50 + 2) = 250 \times 52 = 13000 \text{ W}$$

Output power:
$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{const} - P_{cu} = 13000 - 800 - 750 = 11450 \text{ W}$$

Shaft torque at 1500 rpm:
$$T_{sh} = \frac{P_{out} \times 60}{2 \pi N} = \frac{11450 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 1500} = \frac{687000}{9424.78} \approx 72.89 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{11450}{13000} \times 100 = 88.08\%$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix for Quick Decision-Making in KTU Problems

| Given Quantity | What to Find First | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $V$, $I_a$, $R_a$ | Back-EMF $E_b$ | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ |
| $E_b$, $I_a$ | Developed power | $P_d = E_b I_a$ |
| $P_d$, $P_{i+m}$ | Shaft power | $P_{out} = P_d - (P_i + P_m)$ |
| $P_{out}$, $N$ | Shaft torque | $T = P_{out}/\omega = 60 P_{out}/(2\pi N)$ |
| $P_{const}$, $R_a$ | Load at max efficiency | $I_L = \sqrt{P_{const}/R_a}$ |
| $\eta$ at two loads | Constant + variable losses | Set up simultaneous equations |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Diagram — DC Motor

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the power flow stages in a **DC shunt motor**, with all losses explicitly shown:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Input VxIL] --> B[Armature Cu Loss Ia^2 Ra]
    B --> C[Developed Power Eb x Ia]
    C --> D[Iron Loss Pi]
    C --> E[Mechanical Loss Pm]
    D --> F[Shaft Power Output T x omega]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Useful Mechanical Work]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### Block-Level Functional Description

- **Block A (Yellow — Input):** $P_{in} = V \cdot I_L$ — the total electrical power drawn from the supply.
- **Block B (Red — Loss 1):** $I_a^2 R_a$ — the first stage of energy loss as heat in the armature winding.
- **Block C (Teal — Conversion):** $P_d = E_b \cdot I_a$ — the air-gap power where the actual electromechanical conversion occurs.
- **Block D (Red — Loss 2):** $P_i$ — iron/core losses (hysteresis + eddy current), constant in nature.
- **Block E (Red — Loss 3):** $P_m$ — friction + windage losses, also constant.
- **Block F (Light Green — Output):** Shaft power $P_{out} = T_{sh} \cdot \omega$.
- **Block G (Green — Useful):** Final mechanical work delivered to the load.

---

## 4.2 Power Flow Diagram — DC Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input T x omega] --> B[Iron Loss Pi]
    A --> C[Mechanical Loss Pm]
    B --> D[Developed Power Eb x Ia]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Armature Cu Loss Ia^2 Ra]
    E --> F[Electrical Output V x IL]
    F --> G[Useful Electrical Power to Load]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Efficiency vs Load Characteristic Curve (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Load 0% No Load] --> E0[Eta is 0]
    L25[Load 25%] --> E25[Eta increases]
    L50[Load 50%] --> E50[Eta increases further]
    L75[Load 75%] --> E75[Eta approaching peak]
    L100[Load 100% Full Load] --> E100[Eta near maximum]
    L100 --> EMAX[Eta MAX when Pconst equals Pvar]
    L150[Overload 150%] --> E150[Eta drops again]
    L0 --> E0
    style EMAX fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E0 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E150 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The peak of the efficiency curve occurs at the load where $P_{const} = I_L^2 R_a$. This is typically **less than full load** for shunt machines and **near or above full load** for series machines.

---

## 4.4 Transformer Power Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Power V1 x I1 cos phi1] --> B[Iron Loss Pi Constant]
    A --> C[Full Copper Loss Pcu Variable]
    B --> D[Output Power V2 x I2 cos phi2]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Delivered to Load]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transformer has **no mechanical losses** (no rotating parts). Only iron loss and copper loss exist. This is why transformer efficiencies are very high (95–99%) compared to rotating machines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Developed Power" in a DC machine. [3 Marks]
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

The developed power (also called **air-gap power** or **electromagnetic power**) in a DC machine is the power actually converted from electrical to mechanical form (in a motor) or vice versa (in a generator). It is the power that crosses the air gap between the armature and the field poles.

$$P_d = E_b \times I_a$$

where $E_b$ is the back-EMF and $I_a$ is the armature current. This is the most important intermediate power stage because it represents the boundary where **electromagnetic energy conversion** physically occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory hook:** All armature copper loss $I_a^2 R_a$ is subtracted *before* $P_d$, and all iron + mechanical losses are subtracted *after* $P_d$. This sequence is critical for KTU problems.

---

### Question 2: Why does the efficiency of a DC machine fall to zero at no-load? [3 Marks]
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

At no-load, the **output power is zero** since no useful mechanical work (motor) or electrical work (generator) is delivered. However, the machine still draws input power to:
- Sustain the field current (in a motor)
- Supply iron losses (hysteresis + eddy current)
- Supply mechanical losses (friction + windage)

Since $P_{out} = 0$ but $P_{in} \neq 0$:
$$\eta = \frac{0}{P_{in}} \times 100 = 0\%$$

Thus, efficiency is zero at no-load and rises to a maximum at some intermediate load (where constant losses equal variable losses), then drops again as copper losses grow quadratically with load.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Note:** Module 3 questions in the End Semester Exam (ESE) generally include a choice between two questions. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) Derive the condition for maximum efficiency of a DC machine. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

We have already derived this in Section 3.1. The key steps for the answer sheet are:

Step 1: Let $P_c$ = constant losses and $I_L$ = load current, $R_a$ = effective resistance. Total losses: $P_{loss} = P_c + I_L^2 R_a$.

Step 2: Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{V I_L - P_c - I_L^2 R_a}{V I_L}$$

Step 3: Differentiate $\eta$ w.r.t. $I_L$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{d\eta}{dI_L} = 0$$

Step 4: After applying the quotient rule and simplifying:
$$V - 2 I_L R_a = V - \frac{P_c}{I_L} - I_L R_a$$

Step 5: This reduces to:
$$I_L^2 R_a = P_c$$

**Final condition:**
$$\boxed{P_{\text{constant losses}} = P_{\text{variable losses}}}$$

The load current at which this occurs:
$$I_{L,\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{R_a}}$$

---

**(b) A 200 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.5 \, \Omega$ and field resistance of $200 \, \Omega$. It draws a line current of 25 A at a certain load. The iron and mechanical losses total 450 W. Calculate: (i) Back-EMF, (ii) Developed torque, (iii) Output power, and (iv) Efficiency at a speed of 1200 rpm. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Back-EMF:**

Field current: $I_f = V/R_f = 200/200 = 1 \text{ A}$

Armature current: $I_a = I_L - I_f = 25 - 1 = 24 \text{ A}$

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 200 - 24 \times 0.5 = 200 - 12 = 188 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Developed Torque:**

Developed power: $P_d = E_b I_a = 188 \times 24 = 4512 \text{ W}$

Angular speed: $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \text{ rad/s}$

Developed torque:
$$T_d = \frac{P_d}{\omega} = \frac{4512}{40\pi} = \frac{4512}{125.66} \approx 35.91 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Output Power:**

Armature copper loss: $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a = 24^2 \times 0.5 = 288 \text{ W}$

Total losses: $P_{loss} = P_{cu} + (P_i + P_m) = 288 + 450 = 738 \text{ W}$

Input power: $P_{in} = V I_L = 200 \times 25 = 5000 \text{ W}$

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = 5000 - 738 = 4262 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{4262}{5000} \times 100 = 85.24\%$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a) Explain the various power stages in a DC generator with the help of a power flow diagram. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A DC generator converts **mechanical input** into **electrical output**. The power flow proceeds through the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Mechanical Power Input ($P_{in}$):**
$$P_{in} = T_{sh} \times \omega$$
This is the power supplied by the prime mover (e.g., engine, turbine).

**Stage 2 — Mechanical and Iron Losses (subtracted *before* air gap):**
- Friction loss $P_f$ in bearings
- Windage loss $P_w$ due to air
- Iron loss $P_i$ (hysteresis + eddy current)

These are constant losses, independent of load. They consume part of the mechanical input before the air gap.

**Stage 3 — Developed (Air-Gap) Power ($P_d$):**
$$P_d = E_b \times I_a = P_{in} - (P_i + P_m)$$
This is the actual power converted from mechanical to electrical form across the air gap.

**Stage 4 — Armature Copper Loss ($P_{cu}$):**
$$P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$$
Lost as heat in the armature winding.

**Stage 5 — Net Electrical Output ($P_{out}$):**
$$P_{out} = V \times I_L = P_d - P_{cu}$$
This is the power delivered to the external load.

**Power Flow Diagram (as per Section 4.2 above):**
The Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 represents this exact flow.

**Final Power Equation:**
$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cu} + P_i + P_m$$

**Generator Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{gen} = \frac{V I_L}{V I_L + P_{cu} + P_i + P_m} \times 100$$

---

**(b) A 100 kVA single-phase transformer has an iron loss of 1.2 kW and full-load copper loss of 1.6 kW. Calculate: (i) Full-load efficiency at unity power factor, (ii) The load at which maximum efficiency occurs, and (iii) Maximum efficiency at unity power factor. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Full-load efficiency at UPF:**

Output at full load: $P_{out} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ kW}$

Total losses at full load: $P_{loss} = P_i + P_{cu} = 1.2 + 1.6 = 2.8 \text{ kW}$

Input power: $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss} = 100 + 2.8 = 102.8 \text{ kW}$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{100}{102.8} \times 100 = 97.28\%$$

**(ii) Load at maximum efficiency:**

Maximum efficiency occurs when $P_i = x^2 P_{cu,FL}$:
$$x^2 = \frac{P_i}{P_{cu,FL}} = \frac{1.2}{1.6} = 0.75$$
$$x = \sqrt{0.75} = 0.866 \text{ (i.e., 86.6\% of full load)}$$

Load at maximum efficiency: $0.866 \times 100 = 86.6 \text{ kVA}$

**(iii) Maximum efficiency:**

At max efficiency, variable loss = constant loss = 1.2 kW, so total loss = 2.4 kW

Output at this load: $P_{out} = 86.6 \times 1 = 86.6 \text{ kW}$

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{86.6}{86.6 + 2.4} \times 100 = \frac{86.6}{89.0} \times 100 = 97.30\%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Confusing shaft power with developed power:** Developed power $P_d = E_b I_a$ is *before* mechanical losses. Shaft power $P_{out} = T_{sh} \omega$ is *after* mechanical losses. Many students subtract armature copper loss twice or forget the iron/mechanical losses entirely. **Always draw the power flow diagram first** before plugging numbers.
> 2. **Mixing up $I_L$ and $I_a$:** In a **shunt motor**, $I_L = I_a + I_f$. In a **series motor**, $I_L = I_a = I_f$. Wrongly setting $I_L = I_a$ in a shunt motor costs ~2 marks.
> 3. **Unit mistakes in torque:** $T = P/\omega$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s. If you use rpm directly without converting, your torque answer will be off by a factor of ~9.55.
> 4. **Forgetting the maximum efficiency condition:** KTU often asks "at what load is efficiency maximum?" — and the answer is *not* "at full load." It's wherever $P_{const} = P_{var}$. **State this explicitly** in your answer.
> 5. **Treating brush contact loss incorrectly:** Some textbooks include $V_b I_a$ as a constant loss; others absorb it into the armature resistance. **Follow the assumption stated in the problem**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Input → Losses → Output** is the universal power flow in every electrical machine.
- [x] **Developed power** $P_d = E_b I_a$ is the heart of every DC machine efficiency problem.
- [x] **Copper loss** is **variable** ($I^2R$); **Iron + Mechanical** losses are **constant**.
- [x] **Condition for maximum efficiency:** $P_{const} = P_{var}$, giving $I_{L,\max} = \sqrt{P_c / R_a}$.
- [x] Efficiency is **zero at no-load** and **falls at extreme overload** — it peaks at one specific intermediate load.
- [x] **1 HP = 746 W** — always convert when the problem mixes units.
- [x] For **shunt machines**, $I_L = I_a + I_f$; for **series machines**, $I_L = I_a$.
- [x] **Transformers** have only iron + copper losses (no mechanical losses) → higher efficiency (95–99%).
- [x] **All-day efficiency** matters for distribution transformers with varying 24-hour load profiles.
- [x] KTU's most-tested concept: **Derivation of maximum efficiency condition** (carry this derivation as a 7-mark answer template).
- [x] **Always draw the power flow block diagram** before starting any numerical — it prevents 80% of the common errors.
- [x] At max efficiency, both constant and variable losses are **equal numerically** — use this to solve reverse problems (find constant loss from given efficiency curve).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** If a problem gives you efficiency at two different loads and asks you to find constant and variable losses separately, set up two simultaneous equations using the formula $\eta = 1 - (P_c + x^2 P_{cu})/(x \cdot P_{in,FL})$ and solve. This is a guaranteed 14-mark question pattern.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Application of DC Generators and DC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Application of DC Generators and DC Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 DC Generator — Formal Definition

A **DC Generator** is an electrical machine that converts mechanical energy into direct current electrical energy based on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. When a closed armature coil rotates in a uniform magnetic field produced by the field winding, an alternating EMF is induced in the coil, which is then converted to unidirectional (DC) EMF using a mechanical commutator and brush assembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The DC generator classification is based on the **method of field excitation**, namely — (i) Separately Excited, (ii) Self-Excited (Shunt, Series, Compound). For KTU 2024, focus on self-excited types.

### 1.2 DC Motor — Formal Definition

A **DC Motor** is an electrical machine that converts direct current electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational). It operates on the principle that a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force (**Lorentz Force**), given by $\vec{F} = I \vec{L} \times \vec{B}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** DC Generator and DC Motor are **physically identical** in construction. The same machine can operate as either depending on whether mechanical or electrical power is supplied.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pump & Turbine

Imagine a **bicycle air pump**:
- When **you push the handle down** (mechanical input), air flows out as a stream (electrical output) → This is a **DC Generator**.
- When **compressed air is pushed in** (electrical input), the handle jumps up (mechanical output) → This is a **DC Motor**.

The "valve" that ensures the flow remains one-directional is the **commutator**, exactly analogous to the one-way valve in the pump.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Magnetic flux density (B):** measured in **Tesla (T)**
- **Magnetic flux ($\Phi$):** measured in **Weber (Wb)**
- **Permeability of free space ($\mu_0$):** $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- **Rated speed of typical DC machine:** **1500 RPM** to **3000 RPM**
- **Standard armature voltage ratings:** **220 V DC**, **440 V DC**

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for students:** Think of **Field winding** as the *throttle* of a car and **Armature** as the *engine body*. The interaction between them produces the actual power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of DC Machines

DC machines are classified by the **type of field excitation** and **armature-field connection**:

**A. DC Generators:**
1. **Separately Excited Generator** — Field winding energized from an external DC source.
2. **Self-Excited Generators:**
   - **Shunt Generator** — Field winding connected in **parallel** with the armature.
   - **Series Generator** — Field winding connected in **series** with the armature.
   - **Compound Generator:**
     - *Cumulatively Compound* — Series and shunt field MMFs **add up**.
     - *Differentially Compound* — Series and shunt field MMFs **oppose** each other.

**B. DC Motors:**
1. **DC Shunt Motor**
2. **DC Series Motor**
3. **DC Compound Motor** (Cumulative & Differential)

### 2.2 EMF Equation of a DC Generator

The EMF induced in a DC machine is governed by the flux cutting law. For a wave-wound armature:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

For a lap-wound armature, $A = P$, simplifying the equation to:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N}{60}$$

### 2.3 Torque Equation of a DC Motor

The electromagnetic torque developed in the armature is given by:

$$T_a = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$$

For a lap-wound machine, the shaft torque equation simplifies to:

$$T_{sh} = 9.55 \cdot \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{N} \quad \text{(in N·m)}$$

### 2.4 Speed Equation of a DC Motor

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \cdot \Phi} = \frac{E_b}{k \Phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Speed is **inversely proportional to flux** ($\Phi$). This is the basis of **field flux control** method of speed regulation.

### 2.5 Armature Reaction

When current flows through the armature winding, it produces its own magnetic field (**cross-magnetizing effect**) that **distorts** the main field flux. This phenomenon is called **Armature Reaction**, leading to:
- **Demagnetizing effect** in the leading pole tip.
- **Magnetizing effect** in the trailing pole tip.
- **Commutation problems** (sparking at brushes).

The remedy is to use **Interpoles** (commutating poles) or **Compensating Windings**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Induced EMF (general) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N}{60}$ | Induced EMF (lap-wound, A=P) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V = E_g - I_a R_a$ | Generator terminal voltage | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Motor back EMF relation | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $T_a = 0.159 \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \frac{P}{A}$ | Armature torque | N·m |
| 6 | $T_a = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque in motor form | N·m |
| 7 | $E_b = k_E \cdot \Phi \cdot N$ | Back EMF in motor | Volts (V) |
| 8 | $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ | Speed of DC motor | RPM |
| 9 | $P_{developed} = T_a \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T_a}{60}$ | Mechanical power developed | Watts (W) |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{Output}{Input} \times 100$ | Efficiency | Percentage (%) |
| 11 | $V_{sh} = I_{sh} \cdot R_{sh}$ | Shunt field voltage drop | Volts (V) |
| 12 | $I_L = I_a + I_{sh}$ | Generator load current (shunt) | Amperes (A) |
| 13 | $I_L = I_a = I_{se}$ | Series machine line = armature = series current | Amperes (A) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the foundation for **sizing motors in electric vehicles (EVs)**, **DC traction systems in railways (Indian Railways uses series motors)**, and **industrial drives** like rolling mills and cranes.

### 2.7 Characteristics of DC Generators

The three main characteristics studied for DC generators are:

**a) Open Circuit Characteristics (OCC) / Magnetization Curve:**
- Plot of **No-load EMF ($E_0$) vs Field Current ($I_f$)** at constant speed.
- Represents the **magnetic saturation** behavior of the field poles.

**b) Internal Characteristics ($E_g/I_a$):**
- Plot of **Generated EMF ($E_g$) vs Armature Current ($I_a$)**.
- Shows the effect of armature reaction (drop in $E_g$ due to demagnetization).

**c) External Characteristics ($V/I_L$):**
- Plot of **Terminal Voltage (V) vs Load Current ($I_L$)**.
- Shows the **voltage regulation** behavior of the generator.

#### 2.7.1 External Characteristics of Shunt Generator

$$V = E_g - I_a R_a = E_g - (I_L + I_{sh}) R_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> The terminal voltage **drops** as load increases due to: (i) Armature resistance drop $I_a R_a$, (ii) Armature reaction demagnetization, (iii) Reduced field current (since $V$ drops, $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ also drops).

#### 2.7.2 External Characteristics of Series Generator

For a series generator, the field winding is in series with the armature. So the field current equals the armature current. As load increases, flux increases, but eventually **saturation** limits the rise, and the $I_a R_a$ drop dominates, causing the curve to droop.

#### 2.7.3 External Characteristics of Compound Generator

- **Cumulative Compound:** Voltage rises initially, then drops (over-compounded, level-compounded, under-compounded based on series winding strength).
- **Differential Compound:** Voltage drops sharply with load (used as welding generators).

### 2.8 Characteristics of DC Motors

The three primary motor characteristics are:

**a) Torque vs Armature Current ($T_a/I_a$):**
- **Shunt Motor:** $T_a \propto I_a$ (linear, since $\Phi$ is approximately constant)
- **Series Motor:** $T_a \propto I_a^2$ (parabolic, before saturation)
- **Compound Motor:** Between shunt and series (cumulative gives high starting torque)

**b) Speed vs Armature Current ($N/I_a$):**
- **Shunt Motor:** Speed drops slightly with $I_a$ (good speed regulation)
- **Series Motor:** Speed varies inversely with $I_a$ (dangerous at no-load!)
- **Compound Motor:** Cumulative compound has limited no-load speed rise.

**c) Speed vs Torque ($N/T_a$):**
- **Shunt Motor:** Nearly **horizontal** straight line (constant speed drive)
- **Series Motor:** Steeply falling curve (inverse relation: $N \propto 1/\sqrt{T_a}$)
- **Compound Motor:** Drooping but safer than series.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Safety Note:** A **DC Series Motor must NEVER be started on no-load**. The speed can rise to dangerously high values causing **mechanical destruction** of the armature. It is always belt-coupled or geared to a load.

### 2.9 Applications of DC Machines

| Machine Type | Typical Applications |
|--------------|---------------------|
| **DC Shunt Generator** | Battery charging, lighting circuits, as exciters for AC generators |
| **DC Series Generator** | Boosters, special applications (rarely used as generator) |
| **Cumulative Compound Generator** | General power supply, DC welding (over-compounded) |
| **Differential Compound Generator** | Welding generators, arc lamps |
| **DC Shunt Motor** | Lathes, drills, fans, blowers, printing presses, machine tools (constant speed) |
| **DC Series Motor** | Electric traction (trains, trams), cranes, hoists, elevators, trolleys |
| **Cumulative Compound Motor** | Rolling mills, heavy punches, shears, elevators, presses |
| **Differential Compound Motor** | Limited use; servo applications where stable speed is needed |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the EMF Equation of a DC Generator

**Given:**
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in **Weber (Wb)**
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $N$ = Speed of armature in **RPM**
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature

**Step 1: Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution**

A conductor passing under one pair of poles (N and S) cuts flux equivalent to $P\Phi$ Webers. Therefore, flux cut per revolution per conductor = $P\Phi$ Wb.

**Step 2: Number of revolutions per second**

The armature rotates at $N$ RPM, so revolutions per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 3: Flux cut per conductor per second (average rate of flux cutting)**

$$\text{Average EMF per conductor} = \frac{P \Phi \cdot N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

This follows directly from Faraday's law: $e = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

**Step 4: Total EMF of the armature**

Since there are $Z$ conductors connected in **$A$ parallel paths**, the number of conductors in series per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

The total induced EMF in the armature:

$$E_g = \left(\frac{Z}{A}\right) \times \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \text{ Volts}}$$

**Step 5: Special case — Lap winding**

For lap winding: $A = P$ (number of parallel paths equals number of poles).

Substituting $A = P$:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 6: Special case — Wave winding**

For wave winding: $A = 2$ (always two parallel paths regardless of poles).

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{120} \text{ Volts}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Starting principle:** Force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \text{ Newtons}$$

where:
- $B$ = Flux density (T)
- $I$ = Current through the conductor (A)
- $L$ = Active length of conductor (m)

**Step 1: Torque developed by one conductor**

Torque per conductor = Force × radius of armature

$$T_{\text{per conductor}} = F \cdot r = B I L r$$

We know that flux per pole $\Phi = B \cdot A_{\text{pole face}}$ (where $A_{\text{pole face}} \approx \pi D L / P$ for a smooth-core assumption; in practice, $B = \Phi / \text{area}$).

For a simpler practical approach using flux directly:

The **average force per conductor** is given by:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P \Phi I_{\text{conductor}}}{\pi D}$$

where $D$ is the armature diameter.

**Step 2: Torque per conductor**

$$T_{\text{per conductor}} = F_{\text{avg}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{P \Phi I_{\text{conductor}}}{2\pi}$$

**Step 3: Total armature torque**

Total current in the armature = $I_a$ amperes, distributed over $A$ parallel paths.

So, current per conductor: $I_{\text{conductor}} = \dfrac{I_a}{A}$

Number of conductors in the armature: $Z$

Total armature torque:

$$T_a = Z \times \frac{P \Phi}{2\pi} \times \frac{I_a}{A}$$

$$\boxed{T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \text{ Newton-metres}}$$

**Step 4: Power equivalence check**

Mechanical power developed = $T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$

$$P_{\text{developed}} = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P I_a}{60 A}$$

But $\dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = E_g$ (from the EMF equation).

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{developed}} = E_g \cdot I_a \text{ Watts}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful result:** Mechanical power developed in the armature equals the **electrical power converted** in the armature. This is the **Law of Conservation of Energy** in action — the basis of energy conversion in all rotating machines.

### 3.3 Derivation of Speed Equation (DC Shunt Motor)

**Starting from back EMF equation:**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = k \Phi N \quad \text{(where } k = \frac{Z P}{60 A} \text{)}$$

**Applying KVL around the armature circuit:**

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Substituting $E_b$:

$$V = k \Phi N + I_a R_a$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$k \Phi N = V - I_a R_a$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi} = \frac{E_b}{k \Phi}}$$

**Step: Speed regulation derivation**

Speed regulation = $\dfrac{N_0 - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\%$

For a shunt motor, $I_a R_a$ is small compared to $V$, so the regulation is excellent (typically **2-5%**).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Characteristic Curve Behavior

| Machine | $N$ vs $I_a$ (Slope) | $T_a$ vs $I_a$ (Shape) | No-load Safety | Starting Torque |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Shunt Motor** | Small negative slope (very stable) | Linear: $T_a = k_T \Phi I_a$ | Safe | Moderate (1.2-2× rated) |
| **Series Motor** | Inverse square relation: $N \propto 1/I_a$ | Parabolic: $T_a \propto I_a^2$ | **Dangerous — never no-load** | Very high (5-7× rated) |
| **Cumulative Compound** | Moderate drop | Above shunt, below series | Safe | High (2.5-3× rated) |
| **Differential Compound** | Nearly constant | Low (opposing flux) | Safe | Very low (rarely used) |

### 3.5 Numerical Example — Worked Out

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 540 wave-connected conductors. The armature resistance is $0.1 \ \Omega$ and shunt field resistance is $100 \ \Omega$. At a certain load, the armature current is 50 A and the flux per pole is 25 mWb. Calculate:
(a) Speed of the motor
(b) Developed torque

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** For wave winding, $A = 2$.

**Step 2 (a): Back EMF**

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50)(0.1) = 250 - 5 = 245 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 (a): Speed calculation**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$

$$245 = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-3})(540)(N)(4)}{60 \times 2}$$

$$245 = \frac{0.025 \times 540 \times 4 \times N}{120}$$

$$245 = \frac{54 \times N}{120}$$

$$245 = 0.45 N$$

$$N = \frac{245}{0.45} = 544.44 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 4 (b): Developed torque**

$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = \frac{(4)(25 \times 10^{-3})(540)(50)}{2 \pi (2)}$$

$$T_a = \frac{2700}{4\pi} = \frac{2700}{12.566}$$

$$T_a \approx 214.86 \text{ N·m}$$

**Verification using power:**

$$P_{\text{developed}} = E_b \cdot I_a = 245 \times 50 = 12250 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{\text{developed}} = \frac{2 \pi N T_a}{60} = \frac{2 \pi (544.44)(214.86)}{60}$$

$$= \frac{734{,}820}{60} \approx 12247 \text{ W} \approx 12250 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
> **Distinguish between a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor with respect to their speed-torque characteristics and one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Field Connection | Parallel with armature | In series with armature |
| Speed Regulation | Excellent (2-5%) | Poor |
| Torque vs $I_a$ | Linear ($T_a \propto I_a$) | Parabolic ($T_a \propto I_a^2$) |
| No-Load Speed | Safe (within limit) | Dangerously high |
| Application | Lathe machines, fans, centrifugal pumps | Electric traction, cranes, hoists |

**Keywords for full 3 marks:** Shunt → constant speed, safe no-load, light starting torque. Series → high starting torque, variable speed, never no-load.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]`
> **What is armature reaction in a DC machine? Mention its effect and one method to minimize it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Armature reaction is the effect of the magnetic field produced by the armature current on the main field flux produced by the field winding.

**Effects:**
- **Cross-magnetization:** Distortion of the main flux distribution.
- **Demagnetization:** Net reduction in the main flux.
- **Commutation difficulties:** Sparking at the brushes.

**Minimization Method:** Use of **interpoles** (commutating poles) connected in series with the armature. These produce a flux that **neutralizes** the cross-magnetizing flux in the commutating zone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write only the definition and forget to mention **the specific neutralization point (magnetic neutral axis shift)**. Always mention the **shift of MNA in the direction of rotation** for a generator and **opposite to rotation** for a motor — this fetches 1 mark easily.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Pattern]`

**a) Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator. (7 Marks)**

*Step-by-step model solution:*

**Given:** Flux per pole = $\Phi$ Wb; Total conductors = $Z$; Poles = $P$; Speed = $N$ RPM; Parallel paths = $A$.

**Step 1:** Flux cut per conductor per revolution = $P\Phi$ Wb (since one conductor cuts flux under $N$ and $S$ pole pair).

**Step 2:** Revolutions per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$

**Step 3:** Flux cut per conductor per second = $P\Phi \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ Wb/s = $\dfrac{P\Phi N}{60}$ V (by Faraday's law).

**Step 4:** Number of conductors in series per path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$

**Step 5:** Total EMF:
$$E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{P \Phi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 6:** For lap winding ($A = P$): $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N}{60}$

**Step 7:** For wave winding ($A = 2$): $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{120}$

**b) A 250 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 600 conductors. The armature resistance is $0.2 \ \Omega$ and the field resistance is $250 \ \Omega$. It takes 40 A from the supply at a certain load. The flux per pole is 30 mWb. Calculate: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Speed, (iii) Developed torque. (7 Marks)**

*Step-by-step model solution:*

**Step 1:** Find shunt field current.
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** Find armature current.
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 40 - 1 = 39 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 (i): Back EMF.**
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (39)(0.2) = 250 - 7.8 = 242.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 (ii): Speed.** For wave winding, $A = 2$.
$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$$
$$242.2 = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-3})(600)(N)(4)}{60 \times 2}$$
$$242.2 = \frac{72 N}{120} = 0.6 N$$
$$N = \frac{242.2}{0.6} = 403.67 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5 (iii): Developed torque.**
$$T_a = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A} = \frac{(4)(30 \times 10^{-3})(600)(39)}{2 \pi (2)}$$
$$T_a = \frac{2808}{4\pi} = \frac{2808}{12.566} \approx 223.42 \text{ N·m}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]`

**a) Explain with neat sketches the external characteristics of (i) Shunt, (ii) Series, and (iii) Cumulative compound DC generators. (7 Marks)**

*Step-by-step model solution:*

**Definition:** The external characteristic of a DC generator is the curve plotted between **terminal voltage (V)** on the Y-axis and **load current ($I_L$)** on the X-axis.

**(i) Shunt Generator:**

The terminal voltage equation: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$

As $I_L$ increases, the terminal voltage **decreases** due to:
- Armature resistance drop: $I_a R_a$
- Armature reaction (demagnetizing effect reducing $E_g$)
- Reduced shunt field current (since $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ drops as V drops)

**Curve Behavior:** Starts at $E_0$ (no-load EMF), drops **slightly and linearly**, with a small droop at higher loads. Good voltage regulation.

**(ii) Series Generator:**

The terminal voltage: $V = E_g - (R_a + R_{se}) I_a$

As $I_L$ increases:
- Initially, $E_g$ rises rapidly as $\Phi$ increases (unsaturated region).
- After saturation, $E_g$ rises very little, but the $I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ drop dominates.
- Curve **rises and then falls** (chest-shaped curve).

**(iii) Cumulative Compound Generator:**

Series and shunt fields **aid** each other.

Based on the strength of the series winding:
- **Over-compounded:** Terminal voltage at full load > no-load voltage.
- **Level-compounded:** Full load voltage = no-load voltage.
- **Under-compounded:** Full load voltage < no-load voltage (similar shape to shunt but more drop).

**Sketches to be drawn (described verbally for the solution):**
- Shunt: Drooping curve from top-left to bottom-right.
- Series: Rising curve that peaks and then falls.
- Cumulative compound: Above shunt curve, rises initially then falls.

**b) Compare DC shunt motor and DC series motor under the following heads: (i) Speed Regulation, (ii) Torque-Speed characteristic, (iii) Starting torque, (iv) Application, (v) Field winding connection, (vi) No-load behavior. (7 Marks)**

*Step-by-step model solution:*

| # | Parameter | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| (i) | **Speed Regulation** | Good (2-5%) — nearly constant | Poor — wide variation |
| (ii) | **Torque-Speed Curve** | Nearly horizontal (slight drop) | Steeply falling (inverse) |
| (iii) | **Starting Torque** | Moderate (1.2-2× FL) | Very high (5-7× FL) |
| (iv) | **Application** | Lathes, fans, pumps, line shafts | Traction, cranes, hoists |
| (v) | **Field Winding** | Parallel to armature | In series with armature |
| (vi) | **No-load Behavior** | Safe (limited speed rise) | **Dangerous** (unbounded speed rise) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the direction of shift of MNA in armature reaction — it is **opposite for motor and generator**. This is a common error and costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **For wave winding** in numerical problems, students often forget that $A = 2$ is **always constant** regardless of the number of poles. This is a recurring mistake.
> 3. In the **EMF equation derivation**, do NOT skip the step where flux cut per revolution is justified. Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for this conceptual step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DC Generators and DC Motors (Module 3)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ A DC machine is **reversible** — same construction works as motor or generator.
- ☐ EMF equation: $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$; for lap $A=P$, for wave $A=2$.
- ☐ Motor back EMF: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$; Generator: $V = E_g - I_a R_a$.
- ☐ Speed equation: $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$ — inversely proportional to flux.
- ☐ Torque equation: $T_a = \dfrac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$ — proportional to flux × current.

**Classification Mastery:**
- ☐ **Shunt:** Field parallel; **Series:** Field in series; **Compound:** Both.
- ☐ **Cumulative compound:** MMFs add; **Differential compound:** MMFs oppose.
- ☐ **Over-compounded / Level-compounded / Under-compounded** — based on series field strength.

**Characteristic Curves:**
- ☐ Shunt motor: Speed nearly constant; Series motor: Speed inversely proportional to current.
- ☐ Series motor must NEVER be started on no-load (mechanical destruction).
- ☐ Compound motor (cumulative) combines high starting torque with safe no-load operation.

**Armature Reaction:**
- ☐ MNA shift direction: Generator — in direction of rotation; Motor — opposite to rotation.
- ☐ Remedies: Interpoles, compensating windings, shifting brush position.

**Application Memory Map:**
- ☐ Shunt motor → Constant speed drives (lathes, fans, pumps)
- ☐ Series motor → High starting torque (traction, cranes, hoists)
- ☐ Cumulative compound → Heavy load with intermittent operations (rolling mills)
- ☐ DC series generator → Rare; used as boosters

**Numerical Problem Solving Sequence:**
- ☐ Step 1: Identify winding type → determine $A$ (lap = $P$, wave = 2).
- ☐ Step 2: Find $I_{sh}$ from $V$ and $R_{sh}$.
- ☐ Step 3: Find $I_a$ from line current and $I_{sh}$.
- ☐ Step 4: Find $E_b$ or $E_g$ from voltage equation.
- ☐ Step 5: Substitute in EMF formula to find $N$, or use torque formula to find $T_a$.

**Common Units Quick Reference:**
- $\Phi$ in Wb; $Z$ dimensionless; $N$ in RPM; $P$ dimensionless; $A$ dimensionless.
- $T_a$ in N·m; $P$ (power) in Watts; $V$ in Volts; $I$ in Amperes.
- $k$ (machine constant) = $\dfrac{ZP}{60A}$ for SI units.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always draw the **circuit diagram FIRST** before starting calculations. This fetches **2-3 marks** of credit on its own, even if your numerical solution has minor errors. Also, **underline final answers** in the numerical portion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-Phase Induction Motors: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Three-Phase Induction Motors: Principle of Operation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Three-Phase Induction Motor (Asynchronous Motor):** A *self-starting*, robust, *singly-fed* AC rotating electrical machine that converts **electrical energy into mechanical energy** through the interaction of a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** produced by the three-phase stator winding and the induced currents in the short-circuited rotor conductors — governed by the principle of **electromagnetic induction** discovered by Michael Faraday (1831) and **Lenz's Law**. The motor has no separate DC excitation for the rotor (no slip rings / brushes in squirrel-cage type), and the rotor *never* rotates at the synchronous speed — hence it is called an **Asynchronous Machine**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rotating Horseshoe Magnet" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you hold a bar magnet and slowly rotate it above a metal compass needle placed on a table. The compass needle will *try* to chase the magnet but will always lag behind slightly because it must overcome friction (load). The rotating magnet is the **stator's RMF**, and the compass needle is the **rotor**. Because the rotor can never catch up (no relative motion = no induced EMF = no torque), it always slips — this *slip* is what makes the motor "induction" type. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards - **Synchronous Speed of RMF:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ (in RPM) - **Supply frequency in India:** $f = \mathbf{50 \, Hz}$ (standard) - **Standard pole configurations:** 2, 4, 6, 8 poles (giving 3000, 1500, 1000, 750 RPM synchronous speeds) - **Slip range (typical loaded operation):** $\mathbf{2\% \le s \le 6\%}$ for standard industrial motors - **Efficiency range (large motors):** $\mathbf{85\%}$ to $\mathbf{96\%}$ ### 1.4 Classification Snapshot (Construction) | Type | Description | KTU Relevance | |------|-------------|---------------| | **Squirrel-Cage IM** | Rotor bars short-circuited by end-rings; rugged, cheap, maintenance-free | Most common industrial motor | | **Slip-Ring (Wound-Rotor) IM** | Rotor has 3-phase winding connected via slip-rings; external resistance can be added | High starting torque applications | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Construction — The Anatomy #### A. Stator (Stationary Part) - **Stator Core:** Laminated silicon-steel stamping (to reduce **eddy current losses**), slots punched on the inner periphery. - **Stator Winding:** Three identical single-phase windings — **R, Y, B** — physically displaced by **120° electrical** in space. - **Frame:** Cast-iron / fabricated steel housing for mechanical support and flux path completion. #### B. Rotor (Rotating Part) - **Two structural variants:** 1. **Squirrel-Cage Rotor:** Copper/Aluminum bars placed in skewed rotor slots, all bars permanently short-circuited at both ends by conducting end-rings. 2. **Wound / Slip-Ring Rotor:** Three-phase insulated winding (similar to stator) star-connected internally, with the three open ends brought out to **slip rings**. #### C. Shaft, Bearings, Cooling Fan - Mechanical coupling with the load, supported by ball / roller bearings; forced air cooling via shaft-mounted fan. ### 2.2 Production of Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) — The Heart of the Motor > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Conditions Required (Board-Favourite Question):** > 1. **Three-phase balanced supply** (equal magnitudes, 120° phase-displaced currents). > 2. **Three spatially-displaced windings** (120° electrical apart on the stator). > 3. **Time-varying (sinusoidal) currents** — the "rotation" of the field is mathematically a result of $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$. #### Logical Breakdown — How RMF Emerges - **Step 1 — Pulsating Fields:** Each individual phase winding, when energised, produces a **stationary, alternating, sinusoidally-pulsating magnetic field** along its own magnetic axis. These are *not* rotating — they grow and collapse in place. - **Step 2 — Spatial Displacement:** Because the three windings are 120° apart in space, the three pulsating flux-axes (along R, Y, B axes) are also 120° apart in space. - **Step 3 — Temporal Displacement:** The phase currents are 120° apart in *time* because the supply is a 3-phase system. - **Step 4 — Vector Sum:** The **resultant flux** at any instant is the **phasor sum** of the three pulsating fluxes. By the mathematical identity proved in §3, this sum yields a **single flux vector of constant magnitude** that **rotates** in space at angular velocity $\omega_s = \dfrac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4\pi f}{P}$ rad/s. ### 2.3 Principle of Operation — Why the Rotor Spins 1. The stator RMF rotates at synchronous speed $N_s$. 2. The rotor is initially *at rest* → there is **relative velocity** between RMF and rotor conductors. 3. By **Faraday's Law**, this relative motion **induces an EMF** in each rotor bar (just like in a transformer — stator is primary, rotor is short-circuited secondary). 4. Rotor bars being short-circuited → **induced current flows**. 5. By **Lenz's Law**, the direction of induced current is such that it *opposes the cause* (the relative motion). Hence the rotor experiences a **force** ($F = BIL$). 6. The sum of these forces produces a **net torque** that drags the rotor in the **same direction as the RMF**. 7. The rotor accelerates in that direction, but as it speeds up, *relative speed decreases*, hence induced EMF, current, and torque all reduce. 8. **Steady-state equilibrium:** Rotor settles at a speed $N_r < N_s$ such that the developed torque exactly balances the load torque. The relative speed $(N_s - N_r)$ is called the **slip speed**. > [!NOTE] > **Why rotor can never reach $N_s$?** If $N_r = N_s$, then relative velocity is zero → no induced EMF → no rotor current → no torque → rotor would decelerate. Hence slip is a *physical necessity* for torque production. ### 2.4 Concept of Slip (s) $$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ - At **standstill** (start): $N_r = 0 \Rightarrow s = 1$ (i.e., $\mathbf{100\%}$ slip). - At **synchronous speed** (hypothetical): $N_r = N_s \Rightarrow s = 0$. - At **no-load**: $s \approx 0.001$ to $0.005$ (very small). - At **full-load**: $s \approx 0.02$ to $0.06$ for standard motors. #### Rotor Frequency & Induced EMF Frequency $$f_r = s \cdot f$$ $$E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$$ where $E_2$ is the standstill rotor EMF (when $s = 1$). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Condition | |---|----------|---------|------------------| | 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$ | RPM | | 2 | Synchronous Angular Velocity | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | rad/s | | 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | per-unit (0 to 1) | | 4 | Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM | | 5 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | | 6 | Rotor EMF | $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ | Volts | | 7 | Slip Speed | $N_{slip} = N_s - N_r = s \, N_s$ | RPM | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Slip is *not a defect* — it is the *enabling parameter*. Without slip, no torque, no rotation. The induction motor's working principle is fundamentally tied to relative motion (transformer analogy with a moving secondary). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Mathematical Proof of Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF) — The Board Standard > [!NOTE] > **Assumptions:** Balanced 3-phase sinusoidal currents with peak value $I_m$, supplied to 3 identical stator windings displaced 120° electrical in space (axes $\Phi_R, \Phi_Y, \Phi_B$). **Step 1 — Write the three-phase currents** $$i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ **Step 2 — Instantaneous fluxes produced (each axis has its own pulsating flux proportional to its current; assume unit proportionality for clarity)** $$\phi_R = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$\phi_Y = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$\phi_B = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ **Step 3 — Resolve each flux along two perpendicular axes (say x and y) using the fact that the windings are spaced 120° in space** Taking the R-axis along x-direction, the Y-axis makes $+120°$ with it, and the B-axis makes $-120°$ (or $+240°$). $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_x &= \phi_R \cos(0°) + \phi_Y \cos(120°) + \phi_B \cos(240°) \\ \phi_y &= \phi_R \sin(0°) + \phi_Y \sin(120°) + \phi_B \sin(240°) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the flux expressions and evaluate each component using the identity $\cos(240°) = \cos(-120°) = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ and $\sin(240°) = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_x &= \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot 1 + \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right) \\ \phi_y &= \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot 0 + \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \cdot \left(-\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Use the identity: $\sin A + \sin(A - 120°) + \sin(A - 240°) = 0$ for any A (proved by Euler's formula or simple phasor sum)** By direct trigonometric addition: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_x &= \phi_m \left[ \sin(\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t - 120°) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(\omega t - 240°) \right] \\ &= \phi_m \cdot \tfrac{3}{2} \sin(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_y &= \phi_m \left[ \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin(\omega t - 120°) - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin(\omega t - 240°) \right] \\ &= \phi_m \cdot \tfrac{3}{2} \sin(\omega t - 90°) \\ &= -\phi_m \cdot \tfrac{3}{2} \cos(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ > The factor $\tfrac{3}{2}$ emerges because the three phasors — when balanced — sum to a phasor of magnitude 1.5 times the individual phasor. (This is a known identity for balanced 3-phase systems.) **Step 6 — Compute the resultant flux magnitude** $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_{res} &= \sqrt{\phi_x^{\,2} + \phi_y^{\,2}} \\ &= \sqrt{ \left(\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m \sin\omega t\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m \cos\omega t\right)^2 } \\ &= \tfrac{3}{2} \, \phi_m \, \sqrt{ \sin^2\omega t + \cos^2\omega t } \\ &= \mathbf{\tfrac{3}{2} \, \phi_m \quad \text{(CONSTANT magnitude!)}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Compute the angular position of the resultant flux vector** $$ \begin{aligned} \tan(\theta) &= \dfrac{\phi_y}{\phi_x} = \dfrac{-\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m \cos(\omega t)}{\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m \sin(\omega t)} = -\cot(\omega t) = \tan\left(\omega t - 90°\right) \\ &\Rightarrow \theta = \omega t - 90° \end{aligned} $$ > Since $\theta$ varies **linearly with time** at rate $\omega$, the flux vector is **rotating at constant angular velocity $\omega$** in the +ve direction (R → Y → B phase sequence, standard convention). ### 3.2 Derivation of the Slip Relation (Energy Transfer Foundation) Starting from the basic definition of slip: $$ \begin{aligned} s &= \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \\ N_s - N_r &= s \, N_s \\ N_r &= N_s - s \, N_s \\ \therefore \quad N_r &= N_s (1 - s) \end{aligned} $$ **Frequency of induced rotor EMF** (since EMF is induced by relative motion;
