relative speed = slip speed): $$ \begin{aligned} f_r &= \left(\dfrac{P}{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{N_{slip}}{60} = \left(\dfrac{P}{2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{s N_s}{60} = s \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{60}\right) \\ &= s \cdot f \end{aligned} $$ **Rotor Induced EMF (at slip s):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{2r} &= 4.44 \, f_r \, N_2 \, \phi_m \, k_w = 4.44 \, (s f) \, N_2 \, \phi_m \, k_w \\ &= s \cdot E_2 \end{aligned} $$ where $E_2 = 4.44 \, f \, N_2 \, \phi_m \, k_w$ is the **standstill rotor EMF** (when $s = 1$). ### 3.3 Direction of Rotation — Phase-Sequence Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule (Board-Favourite):** The RMF rotates from the **leading phase** to the **lagging phase**. > - If phase sequence is **R → Y → B**, RMF rotates from R-axis to Y-axis to B-axis. > - **Reversing any two phases** (e.g., supplying R-B-Y) reverses the direction of rotation. This is the standard method of *motor reversal* in industries. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Three-Phase Induction Motor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3-Phase AC Supply
50 Hz, 400V] --> B[Stator Winding
R, Y, B 120 deg apart] B --> C{Rotating Magnetic Field
RMF, speed Ns} C --> D[Faraday Induction
in Rotor Bars] D --> E[Induced EMF and
Rotor Current Ir] E --> F[Lorentz Force
F equals B I L] F --> G[Net Electromagnetic
Torque Te] G --> H{Rotor Accelerates
Nr less than Ns} H --> I[Slip s equals Ns minus Nr / Ns] I --> J[Mechanical Output
to Load Shaft] ``` ### 4.2 Step-by-Step RMF Production Timeline ```mermaid flowchart TD T0["t equals 0"] -->|"ia equals 0"| A1["Flux along R axis equals 0"] A1 --> B1["ib negative, ic positive"] B1 --> C1["Resultant flux along Y axis"] T1["t equals T by 12"] --> D1["ia positive max"] D1 --> E1["Resultant at 30 deg from R toward Y"] T2["t equals T by 6"] --> F1["ia equals 0, ib positive max"] F1 --> G1["Resultant at 60 deg from R toward Y"] T3["t equals T by 4"] --> H1["ia negative max"] H1 --> I1["Resultant at 90 deg from R toward Y"] T4["t equals T by 2"] --> J1["ia equals 0, ib negative max"] J1 --> K1["Resultant at 120 deg from R toward Y equals along B axis"] T5["t equals 7T by 12"] --> L1["ia positive max again"] L1 --> M1["Full cycle complete, RMF returned 360 deg"] ``` ### 4.3 Rotor Slip-Speed Feedback Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Rotor at Standstill, Nr equals 0] --> P1[Slip s equals 1 maximum] P1 --> P2[Max relative velocity] P2 --> P3[Max induced EMF and current] P3 --> P4[Max starting torque Tst] P4 --> P5[Rotor accelerates, Nr increases] P5 --> P6[Slip s decreases] P6 --> P7[Induced EMF and torque reduce] P7 --> P8{Te equals Tload?} P8 -->|No| P5 P8 -->|Yes| P9[Steady State Operating Point] ``` ### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Simplified ASCII Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STATOR[STATOR Outer Frame] SA[Slot with R-Winding, Axis 0 deg] SB[Slot with Y-Winding, Axis 120 deg] SC[Slot with B-Winding, Axis 240 deg] end subgraph AIRGAP[UNIFORM AIR GAP] AG[Rotating Flux Density B varying with space and time] end subgraph ROTOR[ROTOR Inner Drum] RA[Squirrel-Cage Bars, Skewed Slots] RB[End Rings Short-Circuited] end SA --> AG SB --> AG SC --> AG AG --> RA RA --> RB ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Note:** In the above Mermaid block diagrams, the three stator windings (R, Y, B) are physically placed 120° apart in the stator slots. The RMF (block "AG") cuts across the air gap and links the rotor conductors, which are short-circuited (block "RB"). The induced current in the rotor bars interacts with the air-gap flux to produce torque per the Lorentz force law. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define slip of an induction motor. Why is the slip never zero in practice?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Slip is defined as the relative speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor, expressed as a fraction of synchronous speed. $$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ where $N_s = 120f/P$ is the synchronous speed of the RMF and $N_r$ is the actual rotor speed in RPM. **Why slip is never zero (1 mark):** For torque to be developed, there *must* be a relative motion between the RMF and the rotor conductors so that an EMF is induced (Faraday's law). If $s = 0$, then $N_r = N_s$, relative velocity vanishes, induced EMF and rotor current become zero, and no torque is produced. Hence the rotor would decelerate. Therefore, $s$ is a physical necessity and is *never* zero in practice. **(Full 3 marks for clear, formula + 1-line physical reasoning.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] List any three essential conditions for the production of a rotating magnetic field in a three-phase induction motor.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Three-phase balanced AC supply** with equal magnitudes and 120° electrical phase displacement. 2. **Three identical stator windings** physically displaced by **120° electrical in space**. 3. The currents must be **sinusoidal and time-varying** (not DC) so that the resultant phasor sum is a *rotating* vector and not a stationary one. *(Bonus credit: mention that 2-phase with 90° displacement or multiphase with appropriate phase-angle/winding-angle also works — the general condition is $\text{phase angle} = \dfrac{360°}{n}$ both in time and space.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] — OPTION A (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat schematic, explain the **construction** of a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Label the major parts. **(b)** [7 Marks] **Derive the mathematical expression** to prove that a three-phase supply produces a **rotating magnetic field of constant magnitude** $\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m$. --- #### Model Solution — Q3 (a) [7 marks — Squirrel-Cage IM Construction] The three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor consists of two main parts: **1. Stator (Stationary Part)** — *(2 marks)* - **Stator Frame:** A robust cast-iron or fabricated steel outer body that houses the core and provides mechanical protection and a return path for flux. - **Stator Core:** Built up of thin (0.35–0.5 mm) **laminated silicon-steel stampings** to minimise **eddy-current losses**. The laminations are insulated from each other by a thin varnish film. Slots are punched on the inner periphery to accommodate the winding. - **Stator Winding:** A **three-phase double-layer** (usually) distributed winding placed in the stator slots, with the three phases (R, Y, B) physically displaced by **120° electrical**. It is connected in either **star (Y)** or **delta (Δ)** to the three-phase AC supply. **2. Rotor (Rotating Part)** — *(2 marks)* - **Rotor Core:** Also made of **laminated silicon-steel stampings**, with **skewed slots** on the outer surface. Skewing prevents cogging, reduces harmonics, and provides smoother torque. - **Squirrel-Cage Winding:** Each rotor slot contains an **uninsulated bar** of copper or (more commonly in standard motors) **aluminium**. All rotor bars are **permanently short-circuited at both ends** by **conducting end-rings**, forming a closed, self-sustaining cage — hence the name *"squirrel cage."* - **Shaft:** A high-grade steel shaft runs through the centre of the rotor to transmit mechanical power to the load. **3. Auxiliary Parts** — *(1 mark)* - **Bearings:** Ball or roller bearings support the shaft and allow free rotation with minimal friction. - **Cooling Fan:** A fan mounted on the shaft provides forced air circulation for heat dissipation. - **End-Covers / Bearing Housings:** Provide mechanical enclosure and bearing seats. **Advantages** *(1 mark for completeness)*: Simple, rugged, cheap, low maintenance, self-starting, no slip rings or brushes. --- #### Model Solution — Q3 (b) [7 marks — RMF Derivation] **Given:** Three balanced sinusoidal phase currents, peak value $I_m$, angular frequency $\omega$, supply frequency $f$. **Step 1 — Phase currents:** $(1 \text{ mark})$ $$i_R = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$i_Y = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$i_B = I_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ **Step 2 — Instantaneous fluxes along the three spatially-displaced axes (R, Y, B), each axis 120° apart in space:** $(1 \text{ mark})$ $$\phi_R = \phi_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$\phi_Y = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$\phi_B = \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ **Step 3 — Resolve each flux into two perpendicular components (along x and y axes).** $(2 \text{ marks})$ Taking the R-axis along the x-direction: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_x &= \phi_R + \phi_Y \cos 120° + \phi_B \cos 240° \\ &= \phi_m \sin(\omega t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) - \tfrac{1}{2}\phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \\ &= \tfrac{3}{2} \phi_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(using the identity } \sin A + \sin(A-120°) + \sin(A-240°) = 0 \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_y &= 0 + \phi_Y \sin 120° + \phi_B \sin 240° \\ &= \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) - \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \\ &= \tfrac{3}{2} \phi_m \sin(\omega t - 90°) = -\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m \cos(\omega t) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Magnitude and angular position of the resultant flux vector:** $(2 \text{ marks})$ $$ \begin{aligned} \phi_{res} &= \sqrt{\phi_x^{\,2} + \phi_y^{\,2}} \\ &= \sqrt{ \left(\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m\right)^2 \left[\sin^2(\omega t) + \cos^2(\omega t)\right] } \\ &= \mathbf{\tfrac{3}{2} \, \phi_m} \quad \text{(constant magnitude — proved)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \tan\theta &= \dfrac{\phi_y}{\phi_x} = -\cot(\omega t) = \tan(\omega t - 90°) \\ \Rightarrow \theta &= \omega t - 90° \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $(1 \text{ mark})$ Since $\theta$ is a **linear function of time**, the flux vector rotates at a **uniform angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi f$ rad/s** with **constant magnitude $\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m$**. The number of pole-pairs of the winding arrangement (P/2) scales the mechanical rotation to $\omega_s = 2\omega/P$ rad/s, equivalent to $N_s = 120f/P$ RPM. **∎** --- **Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified] — OPTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice** **(a)** [7 Marks] A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole induction motor runs at **1440 RPM** at full load. Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed $N_s$, **(ii)** Slip $s$ at full load, **(iii)** Rotor frequency $f_r$, **(iv)** Slip speed $N_{slip}$. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **principle of operation** of a three-phase induction motor. Why is the rotor not able to reach synchronous speed? Justify your answer using the transformer analogy. --- #### Model Solution — Q4 (a) [7 marks — Numerical] **Given:** $f = 50 \text{ Hz}, P = 4, N_r = 1440 \text{ RPM}$ **(i) Synchronous Speed:** $(2 \text{ marks})$ $$N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = \mathbf{1500 \text{ RPM}}$$ **(ii) Slip at Full Load:** $(2 \text{ marks})$ $$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = \mathbf{0.04 \; pu} \; (\text{or } 4\%)$$ **(iii) Rotor Frequency:** $(1.5 \text{ marks})$ $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = \mathbf{2 \text{ Hz}}$$ **(iv) Slip Speed:** $(1.5 \text{ marks})$ $$N_{slip} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1440 = \mathbf{60 \text{ RPM}}$$ *(Equivalently, $N_{slip} = s \cdot N_s = 0.04 \times 1500 = 60$ RPM ✓)* --- #### Model Solution — Q4 (b) [7 marks — Principle of Operation] **Working Principle** *(5 marks)*: When a balanced three-phase AC supply is given to the three-phase stator winding (physically displaced by 120° in space), it produces a **Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)** of constant magnitude rotating at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ (proved in §3.1 above). At the moment of starting, the rotor is *stationary* ($N_r = 0$). The RMF cuts across the stationary rotor conductors at a *high relative speed*, inducing an EMF in each rotor bar by **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**: $$e = B \, l \, v$$ where $v = N_s - N_r$ is the relative velocity of the RMF with respect to the rotor conductor. Since the rotor bars are **short-circuited** (squirrel cage) or connected through external resistances (wound rotor), a **rotor current $I_r$ flows**. By **Lenz's law**, the direction of $I_r$ is such that it *opposes the cause* (the relative motion), i.e., it tries to drag the rotor in the same direction as the RMF. The current-carrying rotor conductors now lie in the stator's magnetic field, and a **mechanical force** is exerted on each conductor per the **Lorentz force law**: $$F = B \, I_r \, l$$ The cumulative effect of these forces is a **net electromagnetic torque** $T_e$ that accelerates the rotor in the same direction as the RMF. As $N_r$ increases, the relative speed $(N_s - N_r)$ decreases, hence the induced EMF, rotor current, and torque all reduce until $T_e$ equals the load torque — at this point, the motor runs at a **steady-state speed $N_r < N_s$**. **Why $N_r$ Cannot Reach $N_s$ (Transformer Analogy)** *(2 marks)*: A three-phase induction motor is essentially a **rotating transformer**, where the stator acts as the *primary* and the short-circuited rotor as the *secondary*. Energy transfer in a transformer (and hence in the induction motor) requires a **rate of change of flux linkage** — i.e., *relative motion* between the field and the conductor. If the rotor were to attain $N_r = N_s$, the relative velocity would be zero, the rate of flux change in the rotor would be zero, and the induced EMF and rotor current would vanish — leading to **zero torque**. With no torque to overcome friction and load, the rotor would immediately slow down. Hence the rotor *must* always rotate at a speed slightly less than $N_s$, i.e., a finite slip $s > 0$ is a **physical necessity** for torque production. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > 1. **Confusing "rotor speed" with "synchronous speed":** Many students write $N_r = N_s$ in the standstill condition. *Standstill* means $N_r = 0$, **not** $N_s$. ($N_s$ is the *field* speed, $N_r$ is the *rotor* speed.) > 2. **Forgetting units:** $f_r$ is in **Hz**, $N_s$ and $N_r$ are in **RPM**, $s$ is a **dimensionless per-unit ratio**. Mixing units is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty. > 3. **RMF magnitude:** It is $\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m$, **not** $\phi_m$. The factor 1.5 arises from the phasor sum of three balanced vectors — students often drop this factor. > 4. **Skewing in rotor slots:** Many students omit *why* the rotor slots are skewed (to reduce cogging, slot harmonics, and magnetic locking). This is a 1-mark question by itself. > 5. **Reactor analogy misuse:** Saying "the rotor is *attracted* to the stator field" is incomplete. Correct phrasing: *"the rotor *follows* the RMF by induced-current interaction (Lorentz force) — analogous to a short-circuited secondary of a transformer."* > 6. **Slip direction:** Slip is defined as $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$, not the reverse. In *generator* operation, the formula becomes $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ with $N_r > N_s$ giving **negative slip** — but for motors, $s$ is always positive. > 7. **Direction reversal question:** Examiners love to ask: *"How do you reverse the direction of rotation?"* Answer: *Interchange any two of the three stator phase connections to the supply.* Many students mistakenly say "reverse the supply voltage" — this is wrong. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Induction motor = Transformer with rotating, short-circuited secondary.** Always start every principle-based answer with this analogy. - **Synchronous speed formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — *the only RMF speed in the picture.* - **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is *always positive* for motoring mode and lies between **0.01 (no-load)** and **1 (standstill)**. - **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$ — directly proportional to slip. At standstill, $f_r = f$ (transformer-like); at full load, $f_r$ is very small (1–3 Hz). - **Three conditions for RMF:** (i) 3-phase balanced supply, (ii) 120° spatial winding displacement, (iii) time-varying sinusoidal currents. *Memorise verbatim for 3-mark questions.* - **RMF magnitude = $\tfrac{3}{2}\phi_m$** — constant, not pulsating. The factor 1.5 is the phasor sum of three balanced unit vectors. - **RMF rotates at $\omega_s$ rad/s (mechanical) = $2\omega/P$** where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the electrical angular frequency. - **Direction of RMF** = leading-phase to lagging-phase direction. *Reversing any two phase connections reverses rotation.* - **Induced rotor EMF** $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ — *directly proportional to slip.* - **Why rotor never reaches $N_s$:** Relative motion is essential for EMF induction; zero relative velocity → zero EMF → zero torque → rotor must slip. *State this as a single logical chain for full marks.* - **Squirrel-cage** = rugged, cheap, maintenance-free, no slip rings, no brushes. **Wound rotor** = external resistance control, high starting torque, more expensive. - **Laminated construction** (stator + rotor) is mandatory to limit **eddy-current losses**. - **Skewed rotor slots** = reduces cogging torque, slot harmonics, and magnetic locking tendency. - **Industrial dominance:** ~70–80% of all industrial motors worldwide are 3-phase squirrel-cage induction motors — a fact often quoted in viva questions. > [!TIP] > **One-Liner Memory Trick — "**FIST**"** for the four equations: **F**requency in rotor, **I**nduced EMF, **S**lip speed, **T**ruly proportional to $s$ — *all four scale linearly with $s$.* Use $f_r = sf$, $E_{2r} = sE_2$, $N_{slip} = sN_s$, and the torque equation (next module) follows the same pattern.

topics.title: Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DC Machine** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that operates on the principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and **Lorentz Force**, converting between direct-current electrical energy and mechanical rotational energy. Its **construction** refers to the physical arrangement, material selection, and geometric configuration of its stationary and rotating sub-assemblies designed to establish a uniform magnetic field, support current-carrying conductors, and provide a unidirectional output via mechanical commutation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Construction is the foundational topic for understanding DC Machine operation. Examiners expect students to identify **each part, its function, and the material used**. Marks are frequently awarded for diagrammatic representation and labelling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — DC Machine Construction:**
> The DC machine consists of two main assemblies:
> 1. **Stator (Stationary Part):** Yoke, Pole Core, Pole Shoe, Field Winding, End Covers, Brush Holders.
> 2. **Rotor (Rotating Part — Armature):** Armature Core, Armature Winding, Commutator, Shaft, Bearings, Cooling Fan.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel with a Twist"

Imagine a **water wheel** in a river — the water pushes the blades (rotor), and the wheel spins to grind grain. Now, replace water with **magnetic push** (Lorentz force on current-carrying conductors), and add a clever mechanical device called a **commutator** that flips the direction of current every half-turn so the rotor always experiences a force in the same rotational direction. The stationary part (yoke + poles) is like the **riverbank and pillar holding the magnets**, and the rotating part is the **wheel with conducting coils**. Without the **commutator**, the wheel would just oscillate back and forth (not rotate continuously) — this is the genius of DC machine design.

## 1.3 Why Construction Matters in Engineering

The construction of a DC machine directly determines:
- **Power Rating** (kW/MW capacity)
- **Voltage Regulation** behaviour
- **Cooling Efficiency** (affects insulation class — A, B, F, H)
- **Commutation Quality** (spark-free operation)
- **Maintenance Cycle** and **Service Life**

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application Examples:** DC machines are used in **electric traction (locomotives like Indian Railways WAP-5, WAG-9)**, **steel rolling mills**, **cranes and elevators**, **paper mills**, and **battery-operated electric vehicles**. The constructional features vary based on the application (e.g., series motors for traction, shunt motors for constant-speed applications).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard Operating Frequency:** DC (0 Hz)
- **Standard Voltage Ratings:** **220 V DC**, **440 V DC**, **600 V DC** (industrial)
- **Insulation Class:** Typically **Class B (130°C)** or **Class F (155°C)**
- **Maximum Speed:** Typically **1500–3000 RPM** for industrial machines
- **Air Gap Flux Density:** Typically **0.7 to 1.2 Tesla (Wb/m²)**
- **Specific Loading (Armature):** **15,000 to 50,000 A/m** (ampere-conductors per metre of periphery)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of DC Machine Construction

### 2.1.1 Stator (Stationary Assembly)

The stator provides the **magnetic framework** and supports the field system. It consists of:

#### (a) Yoke (Magnetic Frame)
- **Function:** Serves as the **outer cylindrical frame** that supports the pole cores and provides a **low-reluctance return path** for the magnetic flux.
- **Material:** Made of **cast iron** (for small machines) or **cast steel / rolled steel** (for large machines — because cast steel has higher permeability and lower reluctance).
- **Why this material?** It must carry magnetic flux (low reluctance) AND provide mechanical strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "Yoke = Young's Frame"**: Yoke = Young's frame for magnetic flux.

#### (b) Pole Core
- **Function:** The **cylindrical core** attached to the yoke, around which the field winding is wound. It concentrates the magnetic flux and directs it towards the armature.
- **Material:** Made of **laminated silicon steel (annealed)** — laminations reduce **eddy current losses**.
- **Shape:** Generally **rectangular cross-section**, tapering slightly towards the pole shoe.

#### (c) Pole Shoe
- **Function:** The **curved (arc-shaped) extension** at the bottom of the pole core. Its purposes are:
  1. To **spread the flux uniformly** over the armature surface.
  2. To **support the field coils** mechanically.
  3. To reduce the **reluctance of the air gap** (since it has a large cross-sectional area facing the armature).
- **Material:** Same laminated silicon steel as the pole core.
- **Shape:** Curved/arc-shaped with a **pole arc** typically 60–70% of the **pole pitch**.

#### (d) Field Winding (Field Coils)
- **Function:** When DC current flows, it produces the **working magnetic flux** in the machine. The number of turns and current determine the **MMF (magnetomotive force)** and hence the flux.
- **Material:** **Enameled copper wire** (insulated) wound on a former, then placed around the pole core.
- **Types (Based on Connection with Armature):**
  - **Shunt Winding:** Many turns of thin wire, connected in **parallel** with the armature. High resistance, low current.
  - **Series Winding:** Few turns of thick wire, connected in **series** with the armature. Low resistance, high current.
  - **Compound Winding:** Both shunt and series windings on each pole.

### 2.1.2 Rotor (Armature — Rotating Assembly)

#### (a) Armature Core
- **Function:** Houses the armature conductors and provides a low-reluctance path for the flux from the field poles.
- **Material:** **Laminated silicon steel (electrical steel, e.g., CRGO — Cold Rolled Grain Oriented)**. The laminations (typically 0.35–0.5 mm thick) are **insulated from each other** by a thin varnish or paper layer to reduce **eddy current losses**.
- **Construction:** The laminations are stamped with **slots** (open, semi-closed, or closed type) on the outer periphery. These slots house the armature winding.
- **Ventilation:** Radial and axial **ventilating ducts** are provided for cooling.

#### (b) Armature Winding
- **Function:** The **current-carrying conductors** placed in the armature slots. When current flows through these conductors in the presence of the magnetic field, the Lorentz force **F = BIL** produces torque.
- **Types:**
  - **Lap Winding:** Used for **high-current, low-voltage** machines. Number of parallel paths **A = P** (number of poles).
  - **Wave Winding:** Used for **low-current, high-voltage** machines. Number of parallel paths **A = 2** (always).
- **Material:** **Enameled copper conductors** (or aluminium for low-cost machines). Each conductor is **insulated** and the slots are lined with **slot insulation** (mica, fibre, or synthetic material like Nomex).

#### (c) Commutator
- **Function:** The most **critical mechanical component**. It is a **mechanical rectifier** that converts the **AC induced in the armature coils** to **DC at the brushes**. It also reverses the current direction in each coil at the precise moment it passes the brush position, ensuring **continuous unidirectional torque**.
- **Construction:** Made of **hard-drawn copper segments** (wedge-shaped) separated by thin **mica insulation** (typically 0.5–0.8 mm thick). The segments are clamped between two V-shaped steel rings with insulated clamping bolts.
- **Key Feature:** Mica insulation is **lower than copper** (in modern machines, "mica undercutting" is done) to prevent wear and ensure good commutation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Heart" of a DC Machine:** The **Commutator** is often called the heart of a DC machine because it is the unique component that distinguishes DC machines from AC machines.

#### (d) Brushes and Brush Holders
- **Function:** Brushes are **stationary contact blocks** that press against the rotating commutator, providing the electrical connection between the rotating armature winding and the external circuit.
- **Material:** **Carbon, Graphite, or Carbon-Graphite composition** (sometimes with copper dust for high-current applications). The choice balances conductivity, wear resistance, and commutation quality.
- **Brush Holders:** Maintain **constant spring pressure** on the commutator, typically 1.5–2.5 N/cm² of brush contact area.

#### (e) Shaft, Bearings, and Cooling Fan
- **Shaft:** Made of **high-grade steel** (e.g., EN8, EN9), transmits mechanical power to the load.
- **Bearings:** **Ball or roller bearings** (most common) or **sleeve bearings** (for very large machines) — support the rotor and allow smooth rotation.
- **Cooling Fan:** Mounted on the shaft, it draws cool air over the armature and field coils. Cooling methods: **Self-ventilated (open)**, **Forced-ventilated (separately cooled)**, or **Closed-circuit with heat exchanger**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Value** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Flux per Pole | $\Phi$ | Magnetic flux per pole (constant in steady state) | Weber (Wb) |
| Pole Pitch | $\tau_p = \dfrac{\pi D}{P}$ | Distance between two adjacent pole centres on the armature periphery | Metre (m) |
| Number of Poles | $P$ | Total number of field poles | — |
| Number of Conductors | $Z$ | Total armature conductors | — |
| Number of Parallel Paths (Lap) | $A = P$ | For simplex lap winding | — |
| Number of Parallel Paths (Wave) | $A = 2$ | For simplex wave winding | — |
| Back EMF (Generated EMF) | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$ | EMF induced in the armature | Volts (V) |
| Armature Torque | $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi \, Z \, I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Electromagnetic torque developed | N·m |
| Power Developed (Mechanical) | $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$ | Converted from electrical to mechanical | Watts (W) |
| Reluctance of Air Gap | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l_g}{\mu_0 \cdot A_g}$ | Dominant reluctance in magnetic circuit | AT/Wb |
| Air Gap Flux Density | $B = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_g}$ | Flux density under pole face | Tesla (T) |
| Lorentz Force per Conductor | $F = B \cdot I \cdot L$ | Tangential force on armature conductor | Newton (N) |
| Armature Resistance (per path) | $R_a$ | Total resistance of all parallel paths combined | Ohm (Ω) |
| Coefficient of Self-Induction | $L$ | Self-inductance of armature coil | Henry (H) |
| Commutator Segment Pitch | $Y_c = \dfrac{C}{P}$ | Distance between commutator segments | segments |

> [!TIP]
> **How to Remember EMF Equation:** The formula $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ comes from the rate of cutting flux. Time for one revolution = 60/N seconds. Total flux cut per second per conductor = $\Phi P N/60$ ... extending to Z conductors in A parallel paths gives the standard form.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Series Motors:** Used in **traction (trains), cranes, and hoists** because of high starting torque. Construction features **thick series field windings** that can handle high currents.
- **DC Shunt Motors:** Used in **lathes, fans, centrifugal pumps, and printing machines** for nearly constant-speed operation. Construction features **many turns of fine wire** for the shunt field.
- **DC Compound Motors:** Used in **elevators, rolling mills, and presses** where both high starting torque and good speed regulation are required. Construction includes **both series and shunt windings** on the same pole.
- **Permanent Magnet (PM) DC Machines:** Used in **small toys, automotive starters, robotics, and servos** — uses **rare-earth magnets (NdFeB)** instead of field windings, eliminating the need for field excitation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Back EMF Equation $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$

This is the **most important derivation** for any KTU question on DC machine construction-related performance.

### Step 1: Flux Cut Per Conductor Per Revolution

Let:
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (in Weber)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $N$ = Speed of armature in RPM (revolutions per minute)

For one revolution of the armature, each conductor cuts the flux under **all $P$ poles** (i.e., $P \times \Phi$ weber).

> **Conversion Logic:** Since there are $P$ poles and each pole contributes $\Phi$ weber, in one full revolution, a conductor passes under all $P$ poles, cutting a total flux = $P \cdot \Phi$ weber.

### Step 2: Time for One Revolution

Time taken for one revolution = $\dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds (since $N$ revolutions occur in 60 seconds).

> **Conversion Logic:** If the armature makes $N$ revolutions in 60 seconds, then 1 revolution takes $\dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

### Step 3: Average EMF Induced per Conductor

By **Faraday's Law**, average EMF = rate of change of flux:

$$e_{conductor} = \dfrac{\text{Total flux cut per revolution}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi}{60 / N} = \dfrac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

### Step 4: EMF per Parallel Path

In a **single parallel path**, the conductors are connected in **series**. The number of conductors per parallel path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$, where $A$ is the number of parallel paths.

> **Conversion Logic:** The total $Z$ conductors are divided equally among $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in one path = $Z/A$. These are in series within the path.

### Step 5: Total EMF (Back EMF)

The voltage across each parallel path is the **back EMF** $E_b$. All $A$ paths are in parallel and have the same voltage:

$$\boxed{E_b = \left(\dfrac{Z}{A}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\Phi \, P \, N}{60} = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} \text{ volts}}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Standard Winding Cases

- **Lap Winding** ($A = P$):

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, P} = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N}{60}$$

- **Wave Winding** ($A = 2$):

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{120}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these substitutions matter:** In KTU problems, you will often be given the winding type. Always substitute $A = P$ for lap and $A = 2$ for wave winding before plugging in numerical values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Armature Torque Equation $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \dfrac{P}{A}$

### Step 1: Force on a Single Conductor

By **Biot-Savart / Lorentz Law**, the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field:

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \text{ newtons}$$

where:
- $B$ = Flux density under the pole (Tesla)
- $I$ = Current through the conductor (Amperes)
- $L$ = Active length of the conductor (metres)

### Step 2: Total Force on All Conductors

The total tangential force is developed by conductors **under the pole** (in the active region). For a useful average value, we consider the **average force per conductor** integrated over all $Z$ conductors carrying total armature current $I_a$:

$$F_{total} = \left(\dfrac{\Phi \, P}{2\pi \, D}\right) \cdot Z \cdot I_a \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{A}\right) \cdot \text{ (geometric factors)}$$

For simplicity, we use the well-known result. The total torque is:

$$T_a = F_{total} \times \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)$$

where $D$ is the armature diameter, and the $D$ cancels out after substitution.

### Step 3: Final Torque Equation

After standard derivation:

$$\boxed{T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, I_a \, P}{A} \text{ N·m}}$$

### Step 4: Power Cross-Check (Sanity Verification)

Electrical power converted to mechanical = $E_b \cdot I_a$:

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} \cdot I_a$$

Mechanical power = $T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

$$P_m = T_a \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \left(\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A}\right) \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P \cdot I_a}{60 \, A}$$

Both expressions match ✓ — **the derivation is consistent**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt machine has 500 armature conductors. The armature is wave-wound. The flux per pole is **0.02 Wb** and runs at **1500 RPM**. The armature resistance is **0.5 Ω**. Calculate:
1. The generated EMF
2. The armature current when delivering rated load
3. The electromagnetic torque

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step solution** (showing every transition, as expected in KTU answer sheets):

### Part (a): Generated EMF

**Given:** $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb, $Z = 500$, $N = 1500$ RPM, $P = 4$, **Wave Winding $\Rightarrow A = 2$**

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} = \dfrac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step-wise evaluation:**

$$E_b = \dfrac{0.02 \times 500 \times 1500 \times 4}{120}$$

$$E_b = \dfrac{0.02 \times 500 \times 6000}{120}$$

$$E_b = \dfrac{0.02 \times 3,000,000}{120}$$

$$E_b = \dfrac{60,000}{120} = 500 \text{ V}$$

> **Note:** The generated EMF (500 V) > terminal voltage (250 V) — this means the machine acts as a **generator** delivering power to the load. If $E_b < V$, it acts as a **motor**.

### Part (b): Armature Current at Rated Load

$$V = E_b - I_a R_a \text{ (generator mode)} \Rightarrow I_a = \dfrac{E_b - V}{R_a}$$

$$I_a = \dfrac{500 - 250}{0.5} = \dfrac{250}{0.5} = 500 \text{ A}$$

### Part (c): Electromagnetic Torque

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, I_a \, P}{A}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{0.02 \times 500 \times 500 \times 4}{2}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{20,000}{2} = \dfrac{10,000}{2\pi}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{10,000}{6.2832} \approx 1591.55 \text{ N·m}$$

**Cross-verification via Power:**

$$P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 500 \times 500 = 250,000 \text{ W} = 250 \text{ kW}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega} = \dfrac{250,000}{50\pi} = \dfrac{5000}{\pi} \approx 1591.55 \text{ N·m} \checkmark$$

> **Both methods match exactly** — this is the **gold standard of self-verification** expected in KTU answer sheets for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — DC Machine Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords). Labels are clean text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DCMachineAssembly] --> B[StatorStationary]
    A --> C[RotorArmatureRotating]
    A --> D[AirGapMagneticCoupling]

    B --> B1[YokeMagneticFrame]
    B --> B2[PoleCoreAndShoeAssembly]
    B --> B3[FieldWindingExcitation]
    B --> B4[BrushHolderAssembly]
    B --> B5[EndCoversAndBearings]

    B1 --> B1a[Material: Cast Iron or Cast Steel]
    B1 --> B1b[Function: Flux Return Path + Mechanical Support]

    B2 --> B2a[PoleCore Laminated Silicon Steel]
    B2 --> B2b[Pole Shoe Arc-Shaped Spread Flux]
    B2 --> B2c[Reduces Air Gap Reluctance]

    B3 --> B3a[Shunt Winding: Many Turns Thin Wire Parallel]
    B3 --> B3b[Series Winding: Few Turns Thick Wire In Series]
    B3 --> B3c[Compound: Both Shunt and Series]

    B4 --> B4a[Brushes Carbon or Graphite Blocks]
    B4 --> B4b[Spring Pressure 1.5 to 2.5 N per cm2]
    B4 --> B4c[Stationary Contact with Rotating Commutator]

    C --> C1[Shaft HighGrade Steel]
    C --> C2[ArmatureCore LaminatedCRGO]
    C --> C3[ArmatureWinding CopperConductors]
    C --> C4[Commutator HardDrawnCopperSegments]
    C --> C5[CoolingFan MountedOnShaft]

    C2 --> C2a[Slots Open or SemiClosed or Closed]
    C2 --> C2b[VentilatingDucts Radial and Axial]

    C3 --> C3a[Lap Winding A equals P for HighCurrent]
    C3 --> C3b[Wave Winding A equals 2 for HighVoltage]

    C4 --> C4a[Mica Insulation Between Segments]
    C4 --> C4b[Mechanical Rectifier AC to DC Conversion]

    D --> D1[Working Flux Density 0.7 to 1.2 Tesla]
    D --> D2[Lorentz Force F equals B I L]
    D --> D3[Induced EMF E equals B L v]
```

## 4.2 Material-Function Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[DC Machine Part] --> Q[Material Used]
    P --> R[Primary Function]
    P --> S[Failure Mode if Material Mismatched]

    P1[Yoke] --> Q1[Cast Iron or Cast Steel]
    P1 --> R1[Flux Return Path]
    P1 --> S1[Excessive Hysteresis Loss if wrong steel grade]

    P2[Pole Core and Pole Shoe] --> Q2[Laminated Silicon Steel]
    P2 --> R2[Conduct and Spread Flux to Armature]
    P2 --> S2[Eddy Current Heating if not laminated]

    P3[Field Winding] --> Q3[Enameled Copper Wire]
    P3 --> R3[Produce MMF and Working Flux]
    P3 --> S3[Insulation Failure leading to short circuit]

    P4[Armature Core] --> Q4[Laminated CRGO Silicon Steel]
    P4 --> R4[Carry Flux and Host Conductors]
    P4 --> S4[High Iron Loss if laminations damaged]

    P5[Armature Winding] --> Q5[Enameled Copper or Aluminium]
    P5 --> R5[Cut Flux and Generate EMF]
    P5 --> S5[Hot Spot Failure due to overloading]

    P6[Commutator] --> Q6[Hard Drawn Copper with Mica]
    P6 --> R6[Mechanical Rectification AC to DC]
    P6 --> S6[Flashover due to poor commutation]

    P7[Brushes] --> Q7[Carbon Graphite Composite]
    P7 --> R7[Transfer Current to External Circuit]
    P7 --> S7[Commutator Scoring and Sparking]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Conceptual — ASCII Layout)

```
        +--------------------+     <-- Yoke (outer frame)
        |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
        |  N  S  N  S  N  S  |     <-- Pole Cores with Field Winding
        |__|__|__|__|__|__|__|
        |                    |
        |    [ARMATURE]      |     <-- Rotor (rotates inside)
        |   Slot-|-Winding   |
        |   Slot-|-Winding   |
        |   Slot-|-Winding   |
        |   Slot-|-Winding   |
        |                    |
        |   o---COMMUTATOR   |     <-- Copper segments on shaft
        |   o---COMMUTATOR   |
        |   o---COMMUTATOR   |
        +--------------------+
           / | BRUSHES | \         <-- Carbon brushes contact commutator
        [+]            [-]         <-- External terminals
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to draw a sectional view of a DC machine, you must show: **(1) Yoke, (2) Pole core + shoe, (3) Field winding on each pole, (4) Air gap, (5) Armature core with slots, (6) Armature winding in slots, (7) Commutator, (8) Brushes.** Any missing component = loss of 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"State the functions of the yoke and pole shoe in a DC machine. What materials are used for their construction?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **yoke** serves two primary functions:
1. It provides **mechanical support** for the pole cores and the entire machine.
2. It acts as a **low-reluctance return path** for the magnetic flux, completing the magnetic circuit from one pole to another.

**Material:** **Cast iron** (for small machines) or **cast steel/rolled steel** (for large machines, due to higher permeability).

The **pole shoe** has the following functions:
1. It **spreads the magnetic flux uniformly** over the armature surface (flux distribution).
2. It **supports the field coils** mechanically.
3. Its large cross-section facing the armature **reduces the air-gap reluctance**.

**Material:** **Laminated silicon steel** (annealed).

> **Keywords for full marks:** *Low-reluctance return path, mechanical support, flux distribution, cast iron, cast steel, laminated silicon steel.*

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Explain the function of a commutator in a DC machine. Why is it called a mechanical rectifier?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **commutator** performs two critical functions:
1. It **collects the current** from the armature winding and delivers it to the external circuit via brushes.
2. It **reverses the direction of current** in each armature coil as the coil passes the brush axis, ensuring that the torque on the armature remains **unidirectional** (continuous rotation).

It is called a **mechanical rectifier** because it converts the **alternating current (AC) induced in the armature coils** into **direct current (DC)** in the external circuit, but it does this through **mechanical switching action** (segments + brushes), unlike electronic rectifiers that use solid-state devices (diodes, thyristors).

The commutator is constructed from **hard-drawn copper segments** insulated from each other by **mica** and clamped between two V-shaped steel rings.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the **constructional details of a DC machine**. State the function of each part.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A **4-pole, 250 V DC shunt generator** has **480 armature conductors**. The armature is **lap-wound**, with flux per pole = **0.025 Wb** and runs at **1000 RPM**. Armature resistance is **0.2 Ω** and shunt field resistance is **125 Ω**. Calculate:
1. The induced EMF
2. The armature current
3. The electromagnetic torque

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Diagram (Labelled Description):**

A DC machine consists of a **stator** (stationary) and **rotor** (rotating) separated by an **air gap**. The main parts are:

**Stator Parts:**
1. **Yoke** — Made of **cast iron/cast steel**, provides mechanical support and a flux return path.
2. **Pole Core** — Made of **laminated silicon steel**, carries the field winding and concentrates flux.
3. **Pole Shoe** — Arc-shaped laminated extension, spreads flux uniformly and supports the field coils.
4. **Field Winding** — **Copper wire coils** wound on pole cores; produces working magnetic flux when excited.
5. **Brush Holders** — Hold the carbon brushes at the commutator surface with spring pressure.
6. **End Covers** — Enclose the bearings and support the rotor.

**Rotor Parts:**
7. **Armature Core** — Built of **laminated CRGO silicon steel** with slots; reduces eddy current losses.
8. **Armature Winding** — Insulated **copper conductors** placed in the slots; cuts flux and generates EMF.
9. **Commutator** — Cylindrical structure of **hard-drawn copper segments** separated by **mica**; mechanical rectifier.
10. **Shaft** — **High-grade steel** shaft that transmits mechanical power.
11. **Bearings** — **Ball or roller bearings** for smooth rotation with minimal friction.
12. **Cooling Fan** — Mounted on the shaft, provides self-ventilation.

> **Marking Scheme Note:** Each correctly identified part with function + material = 0.5 mark. Diagram carries 2 marks.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- $P = 4$, $V = 250$ V, $Z = 480$, **Lap winding** $\Rightarrow A = P = 4$
- $\Phi = 0.025$ Wb, $N = 1000$ RPM, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $R_{sh} = 125$ Ω

**Step 1: Induced EMF**

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 480 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 4}$$

$$E_b = \dfrac{0.025 \times 480 \times 1000}{60} = \dfrac{12,000}{60} = 200 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Armature Current**

The shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}} = \dfrac{250}{125} = 2 \text{ A}$$

Let load current = $I_L$, armature current $I_a = I_L + I_{sh}$.

For a **generator**, $E_b = V + I_a R_a$:

$$200 = 250 + I_a \times 0.2$$

$$I_a \times 0.2 = -50 \Rightarrow I_a = -250 \text{ A}$$

> **Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that the machine actually acts as a **motor** under these conditions (i.e., $E_b < V$). For a true generator, we need $E_b > V$.

**Step 3: Electromagnetic Torque**

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, I_a \, P}{A} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{0.025 \times 480 \times (-250) \times 4}{4}$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times 0.025 \times 480 \times (-250)$$

$$T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \times (-3000) = \dfrac{-3000}{6.2832} \approx -477.46 \text{ N·m}$$

The negative torque indicates **motor action** (opposing motion is being driven by the supply).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **construction and material of a commutator** in a DC machine with the **slip rings of an AC machine**. Why is the commutator necessary only in DC machines?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **6-pole DC generator** delivers **200 A** to the load at **500 V**. The armature is **wave-wound** with **720 conductors**, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb. Find the **speed (N)**. If the machine is now **rewound as lap**, what is the new speed for the same generated EMF and flux?

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

| **Feature** | **Commutator (DC Machine)** | **Slip Rings (AC Machine)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Multiple segmented copper bars (segments) | Continuous solid copper rings |
| **Segments** | Segmented — each connected to one coil end | No segmentation — continuous rings |
| **Insulation** | Mica between segments | No insulation between rings (each ring isolated) |
| **Function** | **Rectifies** AC in armature to DC output | Only **transfers** AC to/from external circuit (no rectification) |
| **Number** | Many segments (equal to coils) | Usually 2 or 3 rings |
| **Brush Contact** | Brush contacts **change segments** as commutator rotates (switching) | Brushes maintain **continuous contact** with same ring |
| **Material** | Hard-drawn copper + mica | Copper or bronze alloy |

**Why commutator is necessary only in DC machines:**
The armature winding in a DC machine always has **AC induced** in its coils (because each coil cuts both N and S poles alternately during rotation). To obtain **DC output** at the brushes, the connections to the external circuit must be **reversed periodically** — this is the role of the **commutator**. In an AC machine, the output is intended to be AC, so no rectification is needed, and **slip rings** suffice to merely transfer the AC current to the stationary external circuit.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- $P = 6$, $I_L = 200$ A, $V = 500$ V, $Z = 720$, $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb
- **Wave Winding** $\Rightarrow A = 2$
- Generator mode: $E_b = V + I_a R_a$... but $R_a$ is **not given**, so we assume $E_b \approx V = 500$ V (neglecting armature resistance drop — standard KTU simplification).

> **Note:** When $R_a$ is not given, examiners expect students to assume $E_b \approx V$ for generators. This is a common KTU convention.

**Case 1: Wave Winding**

$$E_b = \dfrac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

$$500 = \dfrac{0.03 \times 720 \times N \times 6}{60 \times 2}$$

$$500 = \dfrac{0.03 \times 720 \times 6 \times N}{120} = \dfrac{129.6 \, N}{120}$$

$$500 = 1.08 \, N \Rightarrow N = \dfrac{500}{1.08} \approx 462.96 \text{ RPM}$$

**Case 2: Lap Winding (Rewound)**

For the same $E_b = 500$ V and $\Phi = 0.03$ Wb, with $A = P = 6$:

$$500 = \dfrac{0.03 \times 720 \times N_{new} \times 6}{60 \times 6}$$

$$500 = \dfrac{0.03 \times 720 \times N_{new}}{60} = 0.36 \, N_{new}$$

$$N_{new} = \dfrac{500}{0.36} \approx 1388.89 \text{ RPM}$$

> **Key Engineering Insight:** For the **same generated EMF, flux, and conductors**, a **wave-wound machine runs at ~3× the speed of a lap-wound machine**. This is why wave windings are used in **high-voltage applications** where the speed is moderate, while lap windings are used in **high-current applications** where lower speed is acceptable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in DC Machine Construction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Yoke Material:** Students often write "yoke is made of iron" without specifying **cast iron** or **cast steel**. Always state the type.
> 2. **Forgetting Lamination:** Many students write "pole core is made of steel" but **fail to mention laminations** to reduce eddy current losses. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.
> 3. **Commutator Wording:** Writing "commutator converts DC to AC" is a **fatal error**. It converts **AC (induced in coils) to DC (at the brushes)**.
> 4. **Winding Equation Errors:** Mixing up $A = P$ (lap) and $A = 2$ (wave). Always check the winding type **before** substituting in the EMF equation.
> 5. **Diagram Labelling:** Skipping the **air gap** in the sectional view loses a mark. The air gap is where energy conversion happens.
> 6. **Sign Conventions in Generator vs Motor:** Forgetting that $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ in **generator** mode and $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ in **motor** mode leads to wrong current direction.
> 7. **Brush Material:** Saying "copper brushes" is incorrect for standard machines — brushes are **carbon or graphite** (copper brushes are used only in special low-voltage applications).
> 8. **Insulation Specificity:** Writing "insulation" without specifying **mica, varnish, or enamel** is considered incomplete. Be specific.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DC Machine Construction (Module 4):**
>
> ✓ A DC machine converts electrical to mechanical energy (motor) or vice versa (generator), requiring **commutation** for unidirectional output.
>
> ✓ **Stator parts:** Yoke (cast iron/steel), Pole Core (laminated Si-steel), Pole Shoe (arc-shaped, laminated), Field Winding (enameled Cu), Brush Holders, End Covers.
>
> ✓ **Rotor parts:** Armature Core (laminated CRGO, slotted), Armature Winding (Cu/Al conductors in slots), Commutator (Cu segments + mica), Shaft (steel), Bearings (ball/roller), Cooling Fan.
>
> ✓ **Functions of key parts:** Yoke = flux return + support; Pole shoe = flux spread + reduce reluctance; Field winding = MMF production; Armature winding = EMF generation; Commutator = AC→DC conversion.
>
> ✓ **Materials matter:** Laminations → reduce eddy current losses; Cast steel → high permeability for flux; Mica → insulation at commutator; Carbon/graphite → brush material for low wear and good commutation.
>
> ✓ **EMF Equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — valid for both generator and motor.
>
> ✓ **Torque Equation:** $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a P}{A}$.
>
> ✓ **Winding types:** Lap → $A = P$ (high current, low voltage); Wave → $A = 2$ (low current, high voltage).
>
> ✓ **Commutator = "heart" of DC machine**, mechanically rectifies AC to DC via segmented copper bars.
>
> ✓ **Cooling methods:** Self-ventilated (open), Forced-ventilated (separately excited fan), Closed-circuit (heat exchanger).
>
> ✓ **Insulation classes** used in DC machines: Class A (105°C), Class B (130°C), Class F (155°C), Class H (180°C).
>
> ✓ **Standard voltage ratings:** 220 V, 440 V, 600 V (industrial) and 110 V, 220 V (domestic/small machines).
>
> ✓ **Real-world applications:** Traction motors (series), Cranes/Elevators (compound), Fans/Lathes (shunt), PMDC for toys/automotive.
>
> ✓ **Cross-verification trick:** Always verify torque calculation using $T = P_m / \omega$ and $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a$.
>
> ✓ **Diagram must include:** Yoke + Pole core/shoe + Field winding + Air gap + Armature core + Slots + Winding + Commutator + Brushes + Shaft.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are Electrical Machines? An **electrical machine** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy into mechanical energy (motor action) or mechanical energy into electrical energy (generator action). The conversion is governed by the fundamental principles of **electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law)** and **force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field (Lorentz Force)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4):** > Electrical machines are classified based on the nature of supply (AC/DC), mode of operation (motor/generator), and construction (stator/rotor arrangement). Module 4 emphasizes the **types, constructional features, and operating principles** of DC machines, synchronous machines, and induction machines. ## 1.2 The Two Grand Categories Electrical machines are broadly classified into **two functional families**: | Functional Family | Energy Direction | KTU Terminology | |---|---|---| | **Generator** | Mechanical → Electrical | Energy **Source** mode | | **Motor** | Electrical → Mechanical | Energy **Sink** mode | > [!IMPORTANT] > A single physical machine can operate as **both** a generator and a motor — only the direction of power flow changes. This is called the **reversibility of electrical machines**, a core KTU concept. ## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Plain English) Think of an electrical machine like a **reversible water pump**: - A water pump pushes water uphill using electricity → equivalent to a **motor** (electrical input → mechanical output). - Now imagine water flowing downhill through the same pump, turning its impeller and generating electricity → equivalent to a **generator** (mechanical input → electrical output). - The *type* of machine determines which "impeller design" is used: some are optimized for **direct current (DC) flow**, some for **alternating current (AC) flow**, and some for **variable speed (induction)**. ## 1.4 The Master Classification Tree The "Types" in Module 4 of PERUT417 refers to the **hierarchical classification of rotating electrical machines** into the following families: ### A. DC Machines 1. **DC Generator** (Mechanical → DC Electrical) 2. **DC Motor** (DC Electrical → Mechanical) ### B. AC Machines 1. **Synchronous Machines** - Synchronous Generator (Alternator) — Mechanical → AC Electrical - Synchronous Motor — AC Electrical → Mechanical 2. **Asynchronous (Induction) Machines** - Induction Generator — Mechanical → AC Electrical - Induction Motor — AC Electrical → Mechanical 3. **Special Machines** - Single-phase induction motor - Universal motor - Stepper motor, Servo motor (overview only) > [!TIP] > For KTU 2024 PERUT417, focus on the **3 main types**: DC machines, Synchronous machines, and Induction (Asynchronous) machines, along with their subtypes. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Type 1 — DC Machines ### 2.1.1 Constructional Foundation A DC machine has two main parts: - **Stator (Field System):** Produces the main magnetic flux. It carries the *field winding*. - **Armature (Rotor):** The rotating part where EMF is induced. It carries the *armature winding*. - **Commutator + Brushes:** The signature component of a DC machine that converts AC (internal) to DC (external) in a generator, or vice versa in a motor. ### 2.1.2 Types of DC Machines (Based on Field Excitation) The classification of DC machines depends on how the field winding is connected to the armature: | Type | Field Connection | Key Property | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Separately Excited DC Machine** | Field winding supplied from an **independent DC source** | Independent control of field flux | Laboratory, precise speed control | | **Self-Excited DC Machine** | Field winding supplied **from its own armature** | No external source needed | Industrial applications | | ↳ **Shunt Wound** | Field in **parallel** with armature | Nearly constant speed, good regulation | Lathes, fans, blowers | | ↳ **Series Wound** | Field in **series** with armature | High starting torque, variable speed | Traction (trains, cranes, elevators) | | ↳ **Compound Wound** | Both shunt + series field windings | Combines advantages of both | Rolling mills, presses, elevators | | ↳  • **Cumulative Compound** | Series flux **aids** shunt flux | High starting torque + good regulation | Heavy industrial loads | | ↳  • **Differential Compound** | Series flux **opposes** shunt flux | Very stable, narrow application | Special purpose drives | > [!NOTE] > **Why does field connection matter?** > Because the **field flux (Φ)** directly controls the **back-EMF (Eb)** and **torque (T)** in a DC machine. By changing how Φ is created, the speed-torque characteristics of the machine change dramatically. ### 2.1.3 EMF Equation of a DC Generator (Core KTU Formula) For any DC machine, the **generated EMF per parallel path** is given by: $$E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$ Where every variable has a precise meaning and SI unit. ### 2.1.4 Torque Equation of a DC Motor The **electromagnetic torque developed** in a DC motor is given by: $$T_a = \frac{E_b \, I_a \, 60}{2\pi \, N} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$ Equivalently, in terms of flux and armature current: $$T_a = 0.159 \, \Phi \, Z \, P \, I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}$$ This is the most-tested formula on DC machines in KTU examinations. ### 2.1.5 Back-EMF and Armature Voltage Equations For a **DC Motor** (counter-EBM opposes applied voltage): $$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$ For a **DC Generator** (generated EMF adds to voltage drop): $$V = E_g - I_a R_a$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Students often confuse whether $E_b$ is added to or subtracted from $V$. **Remember:** in a **motor**, $E_b$ is the *back-EMF* (opposes supply), so it is **subtracted** from $V$ across $R_a$. In a **generator**, $E_g$ is the *generated EMF* (drives the current), so it is **greater than** the terminal $V$ by the $I_aR_a$ drop. --- ## 2.2 Type 2 — Synchronous Machines ### 2.2.1 Definition A **synchronous machine** is an AC machine whose rotor rotates at the **synchronous speed**, given by: $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$ Where $f$ is the supply frequency (Hz) and $P$ is the number of poles. The rotor speed is **constant** regardless of load (within synchronous capacity). ### 2.2.2 Two Sub-Types Based on Rotor Construction | Sub-Type | Rotor Design | Field Excitation | Application | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Salient Pole Machine** | Projecting (sticking-out) poles | DC field from slip rings & brushes | Low-speed, **hydropower generators**, large alternators | High | | **Non-Salient (Cylindrical) Pole Machine** | Smooth cylindrical rotor | DC field from slip rings & brushes | High-speed, **steam/turbine generators** (turbo-alternators) | High | > [!NOTE] > **Why two rotor designs?** > Salient poles are mechanically robust but suffer high windage loss at high speeds → ideal for **low-speed** applications (e.g., hydro plants where turbines rotate at 100–300 rpm). Smooth cylindrical rotors are mechanically balanced and aerodynamic → ideal for **high-speed** applications (e.g., steam turbines at 1500/3000 rpm). ### 2.2.3 EMF Equation of a Synchronous Generator The RMS value of the EMF induced per phase in a synchronous machine: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph} \, k_w$$ ### 2.2.4 Synchronous Motor — The Two-Mode Excitation Behavior - **Under-excited:** Field current is *low* → machine draws **lagging** reactive power (acts like an inductor). - **Over-excited:** Field current is *high* → machine draws **leading** reactive power (acts like a capacitor). - **Normal excitation:** Unity power factor. > [!IMPORTANT] > This unique property of the synchronous motor — being able to **absorb or supply reactive power** by varying its field excitation — is heavily tested in KTU and is the basis for its use as a **synchronous condenser** in power-factor correction. --- ## 2.3 Type 3 — Asynchronous (Induction) Machines ### 2.3.1 Definition An **induction machine** is an AC machine in which the rotor does **not** receive electrical power by conduction; instead, current is **induced** in the rotor by transformer-like action from the stator field. Consequently, the rotor can never reach synchronous speed — it always runs at a speed slightly below $N_s$. ### 2.3.2 Slip — The Defining Parameter $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$ Where $s$ is the per-unit slip, $N_s$ is synchronous speed, and $N_r$ is rotor speed. At starting, $N_r = 0$, so $s = 1$; at no-load, $N_r \approx N_s$, so $s \approx 0$. ### 2.3.3 Types of Induction Motors (Critical for KTU) | Type | Rotor Design | Starting Torque | Speed Control | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Squirrel Cage Induction Motor** | Solid bars shorted by end rings | Moderate (~1.5–2× full load) | Limited (pole-changing, V/f) | Fans, pumps, compressors, conveyors | | **Slip Ring (Wound Rotor) Induction Motor** | Wound 3-phase rotor with slip rings | High (~2.5–3× full load) | Excellent (rheostatic control) | Cranes, hoists, elevators, mills | ### 2.3.4 Rotor EMF and Frequency The rotor EMF is induced at the **slip frequency**: $$f_r = s \, f$$ $$E_{2r} = s \, E_2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > At start, $s = 1$, so rotor frequency equals stator frequency. At full load, $s \approx 0.03$–$0.05$, so rotor frequency drops to a few Hz. This frequency transformation is the heart of the induction machine's operation. ### 2.3.5 The Rotor Equivalent Circuit Element $$\frac{R_2}{s} = R_2 + \frac{R_2(1-s)}{s}$$ The right-hand term $\frac{R_2(1-s)}{s}$ is the **mechanical load resistance** as seen from the stator side — the slip-dependent conversion of electrical to mechanical power. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Quantity | Typical KTU Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Generated EMF (DC machine) | Generator numerical problems | | 2 | $T_a = 0.159 \, \Phi Z P \, I_a$ | Torque (DC motor) | Motor numerical problems | | 3 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Motor voltage equation | Back-EMF numericals | | 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | All AC machine problems | | 5 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi_m \, T_{ph} \, k_w$ | Synchronous machine EMF | Alternator numericals | | 6 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip | Induction motor numericals | | 7 | $f_r = s \, f$ | Rotor frequency | Induction motor analysis | | 8 | $\dfrac{R_2}{s} = R_2 + \dfrac{R_2(1-s)}{s}$ | Rotor resistance referred to stator | Equivalent circuit analysis | | 9 | $P_{mech} = 3 I_2^2 \dfrac{R_2(1-s)}{s}$ | Mechanical power developed (3-phase) | Power-flow numericals | | 10 | $P_{gap} = 3 \dfrac{E_2^2 \cdot s}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2} \cdot R_2$ | Air-gap power (per-phase model) | Torque-slip derivations | > [!NOTE] > **Variable legend (all in SI units unless noted):** > $\Phi$ = flux per pole [Wb], $Z$ = total armature conductors, $N$ = speed [rpm], $P$ = number of poles, $A$ = parallel paths, $I_a$ = armature current [A], $R_a$ = armature resistance [Ω], $E_b$ = back-EMF [V], $T_{ph}$ = turns per phase, $k_w$ = winding factor, $s$ = slip [per-unit], $f$ = supply frequency [Hz], $f_r$ = rotor frequency [Hz], $E_2$ = standstill rotor EMF [V], $R_2, X_2$ = rotor resistance and reactance referred to stator [Ω]. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Machine Type | Industry Domain | Why It Is Used | |---|---|---| | **DC Shunt Motor** | Lathes, machine tools, printing | Constant-speed characteristic | | **DC Series Motor** | Electric locomotives, cranes | High starting torque | | **Synchronous Generator (Alternator)** | Power stations (thermal, hydro, nuclear) | Generates bulk electrical power | | **Synchronous Motor** | Power-factor correction, large compressors | Leading reactive power capability | | **Squirrel Cage IM** | Pumps, fans, conveyors | Rugged, low-maintenance, cheap | | **Slip Ring IM** | Cranes, hoists, mills | High starting torque + smooth speed control | # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation of a DC Generator ### 3.1.1 Given A DC machine with $P$ poles, $Z$ total armature conductors, $A$ parallel paths, flux per pole $\Phi$ [Wb], and running at $N$ revolutions per minute. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Flux cut per revolution.** In one revolution, each conductor cuts the flux of all $P$ poles. The total flux cut per conductor per revolution is therefore: $$\text{Flux cut per conductor per rev} = \Phi \cdot P \quad \text{[Wb]}$$ **Step 2: Time for one revolution.** If the armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the time for one revolution is: $$T = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$ **Step 3: Average EMF per conductor (Faraday's Law).** By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is: $$e_{\text{avg per conductor}} = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{\Phi P}{60/N} = \frac{\Phi P N}{60} \quad \text{[V]}$$ **Step 4: Total conductors in series per parallel path.** In a DC machine, the total number of conductors $Z$ is distributed equally among $A$ parallel paths. Hence the number of conductors in series per path is: $$Z_{\text{per path}} = \frac{Z}{A}$$ **Step 5: EMF per parallel path = Generated EMF.** All conductors in a single path are in series, so the total EMF of one path (and hence the generated EMF between the brushes) is: $$E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \frac{\Phi P N}{60}$$ $$\boxed{\,E_g = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A} \quad \text{[V]}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** > $E_g \propto \Phi N$ — the generated EMF is **directly proportional** to both the flux per pole and the speed. This is the single most important relationship in DC machine analysis. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Equation of a DC Motor ### 3.2.1 Given A DC motor armature supplied with current $I_a$ [A], armature resistance $R_a$ [Ω], applied voltage $V$ [V], back-EMF $E_b$ [V], and running at $N$ [rpm]. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Mechanical power developed.** The electromagnetic power converted from electrical to mechanical form is: $$P_{\text{conv}} = E_b \cdot I_a \quad \text{[W]}$$ **Step 2: Relation between mechanical power, torque, and speed.** Mechanical power is also expressed as the product of torque $T_a$ [N·m] and angular velocity $\omega$ [rad/s]: $$P_{\text{conv}} = T_a \cdot \omega = T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$ **Step 3: Equate the two expressions.** $$E_b \cdot I_a = T_a \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$ **Step 4: Solve for torque.** $$T_a = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a \cdot 60}{2\pi N} = \frac{60}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{N}$$ Since $\frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 9.55$ and $E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$: $$T_a = 9.55 \cdot \frac{I_a}{N} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A} = \frac{9.55 \, \Phi Z P I_a}{60 A}$$ A common compact form uses $9.55/60 \approx 0.159$ (valid for the simplex wave-wound case where $A = P$ for some derivations, but more universally): $$\boxed{\,T_a = 0.159 \, \Phi \, Z \, P \, I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}\,}$$ **Step 5: Crucial proportionalities for KTU.** - $T_a \propto \Phi$ (torque proportional to flux) - $T_a \propto I_a$ (torque proportional to armature current) > [!IMPORTANT] > **For a DC Series Motor:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ (field is in series with armature), so $T_a \propto I_a^2$ — this quadratic relationship explains its **enormous starting torque**. > **For a DC Shunt Motor:** $\Phi$ is approximately constant, so $T_a \propto I_a$ — a linear relationship gives **predictable, stable operation**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Synchronous Speed of an AC Machine ### 3.3.1 Given A multi-pole AC machine supplied at frequency $f$ [Hz] with $P$ poles. ### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Cycles per revolution.** In one mechanical revolution of the rotor, it passes through $\frac{P}{2}$ complete pairs of poles. Each pair of poles corresponds to one complete AC cycle. $$\text{Cycles per revolution} = \frac{P}{2}$$ **Step 2: Time per revolution.** If supply frequency is $f$ cycles/second, the time per cycle is $\frac{1}{f}$ seconds. The time per revolution is: $$T_{\text{rev}} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{f} = \frac{P}{2f} \quad \text{[seconds]}$$ **Step 3: Revolutions per second.** $$N \text{ [rev/s]} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{2f}{P}$$ **Step 4: Revolutions per minute (rpm).** $$N_s = \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 f}{P}$$ $$\boxed{\,N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{[rpm]}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical examples (KTU frequently tested):** > - $P = 2, f = 50$ Hz → $N_s = 3000$ rpm (turbo-alternator) > - $P = 4, f = 50$ Hz → $N_s = 1500$ rpm (most industrial motors) > - $P = 6, f = 50$ Hz → $N_s = 1000$ rpm > - $P = 24, f = 50$ Hz → $N_s = 250$ rpm (hydro-generator) --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Slip Relation for Induction Motor ### 3.4.1 Given A 3-phase induction motor with synchronous speed $N_s$ and actual rotor speed $N_r$. ### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Define slip speed.** The difference between synchronous speed and rotor speed is called the **slip speed**: $$N_{\text{slip}} = N_s - N_r \quad \text{[rpm]}$$ **Step 2: Define per-unit slip.** $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{N_{\text{slip}}}{N_s}$$ **Step 3: Solve for rotor speed.** $$N_r = N_s(1 - s) \quad \text{[rpm]}$$ **Step 4: Frequency of rotor EMF.** Because the rotor EMF is induced by the relative motion between the rotating stator field and the rotor, the rotor EMF frequency is the slip frequency: $$f_r = s \cdot f \quad \text{[Hz]}$$ **Step 5: Rotor EMF magnitude.** Since EMF is proportional to frequency (Faraday's law): $$E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$$ where $E_2$ is the standstill rotor EMF (when $s = 1$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** > - At start: $N_r = 0 \Rightarrow s = 1 \Rightarrow f_r = f, E_{2r} = E_2$ (maximum rotor EMF, maximum rotor frequency) ✔ > - At synchronous speed: $N_r = N_s \Rightarrow s = 0 \Rightarrow f_r = 0, E_{2r} = 0$ (no relative motion, no induction) ✔ > - At typical full load: $s \approx 0.04 \Rightarrow f_r \approx 2$ Hz, $E_{2r} \approx 0.04 E_2$ ✔ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Electrical Machines ```mermaid flowchart TD EM["Electrical Machines"] EM --> DIRC["Based on Energy Direction"] DIRC --> GEN["Generator
Mechanical to Electrical"] DIRC --> MOT["Motor
Electrical to Mechanical"] EM --> DTYPE["Based on Supply Type"] DTYPE --> DCM["DC Machines"] DTYPE --> ACM["AC Machines"] DCM --> DCG["DC Generator"] DCM --> DCMOT["DC Motor"] DCG --> DSEP["Separately Excited"] DCG --> DSELF["Self Excited"] DSELF --> DSH["Shunt Wound"] DSELF --> DSR["Series Wound"] DSELF --> DCMP["Compound Wound"] DCMP --> DCUM["Cumulative"] DCMP --> DDIF["Differential"] DCMOT --> DMSEP["Separately Excited Motor"] DCMOT --> DMSELF["Self Excited Motor"] DMSELF --> DMSH["Shunt Motor"] DMSELF --> DMSR["Series Motor"] DMSELF --> DMCMP["Compound Motor"] ACM --> SYN["Synchronous Machine"] ACM --> ASYN["Asynchronous Induction Machine"] SYN --> SALT["Synchronous Generator Alternator"] SYN --> SMOT["Synchronous Motor"] SALT --> SP1["Salient Pole"] SALT --> SP2["Cylindrical Non Salient Pole"] SMOT --> SM1["Under Excited"] SMOT --> SM2["Over Excited"] SMOT --> SM3["Normal Excitation"] ASYN --> IM1["Squirrel Cage IM"] ASYN --> IM2["Slip Ring Wound Rotor IM"] IM1 --> SPH["Single Phase Variant"] IM2 --> TPH["Three Phase Variant"] ``` ## 4.2 Power-Flow Block Diagram of a DC Machine (Universal) ```mermaid flowchart LR PE["Mechanical Power Input Pin"] --> MECH["Mechanical Coupling"] MECH --> ARM["Armature Rotor Assembly"] ARM --> EBMF["Induces EMF Eb"] EBMF --> BRUSH["Commutator and Brush Assembly"] BRUSH --> TERM["Terminal Electrical Power Pout"] FW["Field Winding Excited by Vf"] --> FMF["Produces Flux Phi"] FMF --> ARM ARM -.->|Back EMF Eb| ARMV["Armature Voltage Equation V = Eb + IaRa"] ``` ## 4.3 Induction Motor — Torque vs Slip Curve (Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR AX["Slip s axis"] --> S0["s equals 0
Synchronous speed"] AX --> SS["s equals 1
Standstill"] S0 --> NOP["No Load Point
Low slip low torque"] SS --> ST["Starting Torque Tst"] NOP --> MX["Maximum Torque Tmax
at slip sm"] MX --> ST ST --> PULL["Pull out region
Unstable operation"] NOP --> REG["Stable Operating Region
Full Load to No Load"] ``` ## 4.4 Synchronous Motor — V-Curve Functional Block ```mermaid flowchart TD SUB["Synchronous Motor at Constant Load"] SUB --> UND["Under Excited
Field current low"] SUB --> NOM["Normal Excitation
Unity power factor"] SUB --> OVR["Over Excited
Field current high"] UND --> IND["Machine draws Lagging Reactive Power
Inductor like behavior"] NOM --> UNI["Unity Power Factor
Purely real power"] OVR --> CAP["Machine draws Leading Reactive Power
Capacitor like behavior"] CAP --> PFP["Used for Power Factor Correction
Synchronous Condenser Mode"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read the V-Curve:** Plot armature current $I_a$ on the Y-axis and field current $I_f$ on the X-axis at constant load. The curve resembles the letter "V" — minimum $I_a$ at normal excitation, $I_a$ rises on both sides as excitation deviates. This V-curve is a frequently-tested KTU concept for synchronous motors. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Classify DC machines based on field excitation. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Model Answer:** DC machines are classified based on the manner in which the field winding is excited, i.e., how the field winding receives its DC supply: 1. **Separately Excited DC Machine:** The field winding is supplied from an **independent external DC source**. Flux $\Phi$ can be controlled independently of armature current. Used where precise control of speed/torque is required (e.g., Ward-Leonard system). 2. **Self-Excited DC Machine:** The field winding is supplied from the **machine's own armature output**. It has three subtypes: - **(a) Shunt Wound:** Field winding connected in **parallel** with the armature. High field resistance, nearly constant flux, constant-speed characteristic. Used in lathes, fans, blowers. - **(b) Series Wound:** Field winding connected in **series** with the armature. Low field resistance, flux proportional to armature current, very high starting torque. Used in traction, cranes, elevators. - **(c) Compound Wound:** Carries **both** shunt and series field windings on the same pole. Further divided into: - **Cumulative Compound** — series flux aids shunt flux (high starting torque, good regulation). - **Differential Compound** — series flux opposes shunt flux (rare, special applications). ### Q2. Differentiate between synchronous and induction (asynchronous) machines. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Synchronous Machine | Induction (Asynchronous) Machine | |---|---|---| | **Rotor speed** | Exactly $N_s = 120f/P$ (constant) | Always less than $N_s$ (varies with load) | | **Slip** | $s = 0$ always | $s > 0$ (typically 0.02–0.06) | | **Rotor excitation** | Needs **separate DC excitation** via slip rings | **No external excitation**; rotor EMF is *induced* | | **Construction** | Salient pole or cylindrical rotor; needs slip rings and brushes | Squirrel cage (simple) or slip ring (wound rotor) | | **Starting** | **Not self-starting** (needs damper windings or pony motor) | **Self-starting** (single-phase and three-phase variants both available) | | **Power factor control** | Can operate at leading, lagging, or unity p.f. by varying field | Always operates at **lagging** power factor | | **Use as generator** | Used as **alternator** in all power stations | Rarely used as generator (in wind farms) | | **Cost & maintenance** | Higher (brushes, slip rings, DC source) | Lower (cage type is maintenance-free) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — DC/AC Machine Problems:** > (1) Always specify the **type of DC machine** (shunt/series/compound) at the start of the answer — students lose 1 mark for not mentioning it. > (2) In numericals, never write the final numerical answer without the **unit**. A bare number with no unit → 0.5 mark deducted. > (3) In torque/speed problems, use the **same units consistently** (N·m for torque, rpm for speed, W for power). Mixing SI and CGS loses marks. > (4) When asked for "types", **do not just list names** — write the **constructional difference** and **one distinguishing application** for each. Listing without explanation → at most 50% marks. ### Question A (14 Marks) — DC Machines Focus [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024] **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **DC shunt motor**. List **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of the same. (7 Marks) **(b)** A **250 V DC shunt motor** has an armature resistance of **0.5 Ω** and a field winding resistance of **250 Ω**. The motor draws an armature current of **20 A** at no-load and **60 A** at full load. Calculate: (7 Marks) 1. The back-EMF at no-load and at full load. 2. The speed of the motor at full load if the no-load speed is **1200 rpm**. Assume flux is constant. #### Model Solution — (a) **Construction:** A DC shunt motor has the following main parts: 1. **Yoke (Frame):** The outer cylindrical structure made of cast iron or cast steel. It supports the pole cores and provides a return path for flux. 2. **Pole Cores and Pole Shoes:** Made of laminated silicon steel, bolted to the yoke. The pole shoes spread the flux uniformly over the armature surface. 3. **Field Winding (Shunt Field):** Wound with a large number of turns of **fine-gauge insulated copper wire** of high resistance, connected in **parallel** with the armature. This produces the working flux $\Phi$. 4. **Armature Core:** A cylindrical drum built from **laminated silicon steel** to reduce eddy current losses. Slots are cut on its outer surface to hold the armature winding. 5. **Armature Winding:** A closed winding of insulated copper conductors placed in the armature slots. Lap or wave winding is used. 6. **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of **hard-drawn copper segments** insulated from each other by mica.
