It acts as a mechanical rectifier. 7. **Brushes and Brush Gear:** Carbon brushes held in brush holders slide on the commutator surface, providing the electrical connection to the external supply. 8. **Shaft, Bearings, and Cooling Fan:** Mechanical support and ventilation. **Working Principle:** When DC supply is given, the shunt field winding is energized and produces a constant flux $\Phi$. Simultaneously, current flows through the armature conductors. According to **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, every current-carrying conductor in the magnetic field experiences a force $F = BIL$. The cumulative effect of these forces produces a **torque** that rotates the armature. As the armature rotates, the conductors cut the magnetic flux and, by **Faraday's Law**, generate a **back-EMF** $E_b$ that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's Law). At steady state, $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. **Advantages:** 1. Speed is **nearly constant** from no-load to full load → good speed regulation. 2. Simple and reliable construction; easy to reverse the direction of rotation. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Low starting torque** — not suitable for heavy-load starting applications. 2. The field winding cannot be made very large (size constraint), limiting flux density. #### Model Solution — (b) **Step 1: Calculate the shunt field current.** The field winding is connected directly across the supply (250 V): $$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{250}{250} = 1 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2: Back-EMF at no-load.** At no-load, armature current $I_{a,NL} = 20$ A. Apply the motor voltage equation: $$V = E_{b,NL} + I_{a,NL} R_a$$ $$E_{b,NL} = V - I_{a,NL} R_a = 250 - (20 \times 0.5)$$ $$E_{b,NL} = 250 - 10 = \mathbf{240 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 3: Back-EMF at full load.** At full load, $I_{a,FL} = 60$ A: $$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (60 \times 0.5)$$ $$E_{b,FL} = 250 - 30 = \mathbf{220 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 4: Full-load speed.** We know $E_b \propto \Phi N$, and since the machine is shunt-wound, $\Phi$ is constant: $$\frac{N_{FL}}{N_{NL}} = \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}}$$ $$N_{FL} = N_{NL} \times \frac{E_{b,FL}}{E_{b,NL}} = 1200 \times \frac{220}{240}$$ $$\boxed{N_{FL} = 1100 \text{ rpm}}$$ **Note:** The full-load speed (1100 rpm) is lower than the no-load speed (1200 rpm), confirming the **drooping characteristic** of a DC shunt motor. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — AC Machines Focus [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction of a 3-phase squirrel cage induction motor**. Explain why it is the **most widely used** AC motor in industry. List **two limitations** of the same. (7 Marks) **(b)** A **3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor** has a full-load slip of **4%**. Calculate: (7 Marks) 1. The synchronous speed. 2. The actual rotor speed at full load. 3. The frequency of the rotor EMF at full load. 4. The speed of the rotor EMF (i.e., the speed at which the rotor flux rotates around the rotor). #### Model Solution — (a) **Construction of a 3-Phase Squirrel Cage Induction Motor:** A squirrel cage induction motor consists of two main parts: **1. Stator (Stationary Part):** - **Stator Frame:** A robust cast-iron or fabricated steel housing that supports the entire machine. - **Stator Core:** Built from **thin laminated silicon steel** to reduce eddy current losses. Slots are punched on the inner periphery. - **Stator Winding:** A **3-phase winding** placed 120° (electrical) apart in the stator slots. The three phases are connected in either **star or delta**. When supplied with a balanced 3-phase AC supply, a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** is produced. **2. Rotor (Rotating Part):** - **Rotor Core:** A cylindrical laminated silicon steel core mounted on the shaft. - **Squirrel Cage Winding:** Made of **heavy copper or aluminium bars** placed in the rotor slots. The bars are **short-circuited at both ends by end rings** (no external connections, no slip rings, no brushes). The bars are usually skewed (twisted) by one slot pitch to reduce cogging and harmonics. - **Shaft:** Transfers the developed mechanical torque to the load. **Why It Is the Most Widely Used AC Motor:** 1. **Simple and rugged construction** — no brushes, no slip rings, no commutator. The rotor is essentially a solid piece of metal; mechanically the strongest motor design. 2. **Low initial cost and minimal maintenance** — almost zero wear-and-tear parts except bearings. 3. **Self-starting** with reasonable starting torque; can be designed for any standard supply. 4. **High reliability and long service life** — ideal for industrial environments with dust, vibration, and temperature variations. 5. **Suitable for direct-on-line (DOL) starting** in small to medium ratings. **Limitations:** 1. **Low starting torque** compared to slip ring or DC series motor — cannot start very heavy loads (e.g., crushers) directly. 2. **Limited speed control** — speed is essentially fixed by supply frequency and number of poles; conventional methods (pole changing, V/f) are either step-wise or require extra hardware. 3. (Bonus) **High inrush current** at starting (5–7 times full-load current) — may require starter or VFD for large ratings. #### Model Solution — (b) **Given Data:** - Number of poles: $P = 4$ - Supply frequency: $f = 50$ Hz - Full-load slip: $s = 4\% = 0.04$ (per-unit) **Step 1: Synchronous speed.** $$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4}$$ $$\boxed{N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}}$$ **Step 2: Actual rotor speed at full load.** $$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96$$ $$\boxed{N_r = 1440 \text{ rpm}}$$ **Step 3: Frequency of rotor EMF at full load.** $$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50$$ $$\boxed{f_r = 2 \text{ Hz}}$$ **Step 4: Speed of rotor EMF (relative to the rotor itself).** The rotor EMF rotates at the **slip speed** relative to the rotor. This is the same as the difference between synchronous speed and rotor speed: $$N_{\text{slip}} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \text{ rpm}$$ Equivalently: $$N_{\text{slip}} = s \cdot N_s = 0.04 \times 1500 = 60 \text{ rpm}$$ $$\boxed{N_{\text{slip}} = 60 \text{ rpm}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity check:** The rotor EMF rotates at exactly the slip speed relative to the rotor body. This is the *relative motion* between the rotor conductors and the stator RMF — and it is this very relative motion (governed by $N_{\text{slip}}$) that induces the rotor EMF and current, producing torque. **Without slip, there is no induction** — the principle of the induction machine in one line. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist - [x] An **electrical machine** is a reversible electromechanical energy converter (motor ⇄ generator). - [x] **DC machines** are classified by field excitation: **Separately Excited**, **Self-Excited** (Shunt, Series, Compound-Cumulative, Compound-Differential). - [x] **DC Shunt Motor** = constant speed, **DC Series Motor** = high starting torque. - [x] **Generated EMF:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ — directly proportional to flux and speed. - [x] **Torque in DC Motor:** $T_a = 0.159 \, \Phi \, Z \, P \, I_a$ — proportional to flux and armature current. - [x] **Motor voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$; **Generator voltage equation:** $V = E_g - I_a R_a$. - [x] **Synchronous machines** run at the exact synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$. - [x] **Synchronous machines** are of two rotor types: **Salient Pole** (low speed) and **Cylindrical / Non-Salient Pole** (high speed). - [x] **Synchronous motor** can supply or absorb reactive power — used as a **synchronous condenser** for power-factor correction. - [x] **Induction motors** are classified into **Squirrel Cage** and **Slip Ring (Wound Rotor)**. - [x] **Slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ — never zero for an induction motor under load. - [x] **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$ — typically 1–3 Hz at full load. - [x] **Rotor EMF** $E_{2r} = s \cdot E_2$ — directly proportional to slip. - [x] The **squirrel cage motor** is the **most-used industrial motor** due to ruggedness, low cost, and zero brush maintenance. - [x] The **slip ring motor** is preferred where **high starting torque** and **smooth speed control** are required (cranes, hoists). ### KTU 2024 Cognitive-Level Distribution (Expected) | Bloom's Level | Marks Weightage | Question Type | |---|---|---| | Remember / Understand | ~30% | Definition, classification listing | | Apply | ~40% | Direct numerical substitution | | Analyze | ~25% | Compare types, derive formula, V-curve interpretation | | Evaluate / Create | ~5% | Design choice justification | > [!WARNING] > **Final Pitfall Callout:** > When asked "**List the types of DC motors**" — do **not** write only "Shunt, Series, Compound". Always include **Separately Excited** as the first type. Also, in compound motors, always distinguish between **Cumulative** and **Differential** — examiners specifically test this sub-classification, and skipping it costs 1–2 marks per question.

topics.title: EMF Equation of an AC Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EMF Equation of an AC Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition**
> The **EMF Equation of an AC Machine** describes the root-mean-square (RMS) value of the alternating electromotive force (EMF) induced in the armature winding of a synchronous generator (alternator) or in a rotating AC machine, expressed as a function of flux, frequency, number of turns, and the winding configuration parameters.

For a single-phase AC machine (alternator), the RMS value of the induced EMF per phase is given by:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, Z_{ph} \, K_w$$

Where the symbols have their standard meanings as detailed in the KTU Formula Sheet below.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Water Wheel and Bucket Conveyor**
> Imagine a **water wheel** where water drips into buckets attached to the rim. As the wheel rotates, each bucket picks up a fixed volume of water per turn and empties it at the top. The total water collected per minute depends on:
> 1. **How fast the wheel turns** → analogous to **frequency (f)**
> 2. **How big each bucket is** → analogous to **flux per pole (φ)**
> 3. **How many buckets are on the wheel** → analogous to **number of turns (Z)**
> 4. **How efficiently buckets are spaced (not all at one point, not overlapping)** → analogous to **winding factor (K_w)**
>
> Similarly, an AC machine's induced EMF depends on **how fast** the magnetic field rotates, **how strong** the magnetic field is, **how many conductors** are linked, and **how the coils are distributed and pitched**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **fundamental physical constants** and **standard engineering metrics** are used throughout this module:

- **Frequency (f)** is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
- **Flux per pole (φ)** is measured in **Webers (Wb)**
- **Number of turns/conductors** is dimensionless
- **Winding factor (K_w)** is dimensionless and lies between **0 and 1**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Students must remember that the EMF equation applies to **both lap and wave windings**, and the constant **4.44** is a combination of form factor (1.11) and the peak factor relationship (√2 × 2/π × form factor derivation). It is NOT a universal constant — it is the result of assuming a sinusoidal flux distribution.

---

## 1.4 Assumptions Made in the Derivation

For a clean, exam-friendly application of the EMF equation, the following assumptions are taken:

- The **flux distribution** in the air gap is **sinusoidal**.
- The **armature resistance** is neglected (considered ideal).
- All the **flux links** every turn of the coil (no leakage, before applying winding factor).
- The machine operates in **steady state**.
- The **magnetic circuit** is linear and unsaturated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Building the Equation Step by Step

The EMF equation is built from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. Let's deconstruct the logic in a structured way:

- **Step 1 — Faraday's Law Foundation**
  - The basic induced EMF in a single conductor cutting flux is governed by Faraday's law. When a conductor of effective length *l* moves with velocity *v* perpendicular to a magnetic field of flux density *B*, the instantaneous EMF is:
    $$e = B \, l \, v$$
  - **Why?** Because the rate of change of flux linkage is mathematically equivalent to the motional EMF.

- **Step 2 — Average EMF Over Half-Cycle**
  - For one conductor cutting flux φ per pole, the average EMF over a half-cycle (180° electrical) is:
    $$E_{avg} = \frac{2 \, f \, \phi}{1} \quad \text{(per conductor, fundamental derivation)}$$
  - **How?** Flux φ is cut twice per pole pair per revolution; with N revolutions/sec and P poles, frequency $f = PN/120$.

- **Step 3 — RMS Value Using Form Factor**
  - For a sinusoidal wave, Form Factor $K_f = \frac{\text{RMS}}{\text{Average}} = 1.11$
  - Therefore RMS EMF per conductor:
    $$E_{rms/conductor} = 1.11 \times 2 \, f \, \phi = 2.22 \, f \, \phi$$

- **Step 4 — Multiplying by Total Conductors in Series**
  - Let $Z_{ph}$ be the **number of conductors in series per phase**.
  - Total RMS EMF without considering coil distribution or pitch:
    $$E' = 2.22 \, f \, \phi \, Z_{ph}$$

- **Step 5 — Incorporating the Winding Factor**
  - Real windings are **distributed** across slots and often **chorded** (short-pitched). This reduces the EMF compared to a concentrated full-pitched winding.
  - We multiply by the **winding factor** $K_w$ to get the actual EMF:
    $$E_{ph} = 2.22 \, f \, \phi \, Z_{ph} \, K_w = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$$
  - Where $T_{ph} = Z_{ph}/2$ is the number of **turns per phase**.

## 2.2 Components of the Winding Factor (K_w)

The winding factor is the **product of three sub-factors**:

$$K_w = K_d \times K_p \times K_c$$

| Sub-Factor | Name | Description | Range |
|------------|------|-------------|-------|
| $K_d$ | **Distribution Factor** | Accounts for EMF reduction when coils are distributed across multiple slots per pole per phase | 0 to 1 |
| $K_p$ | **Pitch Factor** (Coil Span Factor) | Accounts for EMF reduction when coil span is less than full pitch | 0 to 1 |
| $K_c$ | **Chording Factor** | Sometimes used interchangeably with pitch factor in KTU textbooks | 0 to 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip**
> In KTU board exams, the question usually states "single-layer/double-layer, full-pitched/chorded winding" and you must identify which sub-factor to apply.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Sheet — EMF Equation of an AC Machine**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | **Master EMF equation per phase (RMS)** | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{ph} = 2.22 \, f \, \phi \, Z_{ph} \, K_w$ | Alternate form using total conductors $Z_{ph}$ | Volts |
| 3 | $E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph}$ | Line EMF for **star-connected** 3-phase machine | Volts |
| 4 | $E_{line} = E_{ph}$ | Line EMF for **delta-connected** 3-phase machine | Volts |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{P \times N}{120}$ | Frequency from poles (P) and speed (N in rpm) | Hz |
| 6 | $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$ | **Distribution Factor** (m = slots/pole/phase, β = slot angle in electrical degrees) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ | **Pitch Factor** (α = chording/short-pitch angle in electrical degrees) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ}{\text{slots per pole}}$ | Slot angle in electrical degrees | Degrees |
| 9 | $\alpha = 180^\circ - \text{coil span in electrical degrees}$ | Chording angle | Degrees |
| 10 | $K_w = K_d \times K_p$ | Combined winding factor (most common form) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $\phi = B_{av} \times A_i$ | Flux per pole from average flux density and pole area | Webers |
| 12 | $P = \dfrac{120 \, f}{N}$ | Number of poles from frequency and speed | Dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this equation important in industry?**
> - **Generator Design**: Helps electrical design engineers determine the **number of turns** required to generate a specific terminal voltage at a given speed and flux.
> - **Transformer & Alternator Sizing**: Used in **rating calculations** for power station alternators (typical ratings: 100 MVA, 250 MVA, 500 MVA at 11 kV, 22 kV, 33 kV).
> - **Voltage Regulation Studies**: Forms the basis for **synchronous reactance** and **EMF method of voltage regulation**.
> - **Synchronization with Grid**: Determines the **infinite bus voltage** matching before paralleling alternators.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master EMF Equation

### Derivation Part A: Frequency-Speed Relationship

Let:
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $N$ = Speed in revolutions per minute (rpm)
- $n$ = Speed in revolutions per second = $N/60$

**Logical steps:**

- A conductor passes under **one pair of poles (N-S)** in one revolution. So in one revolution, it cuts **P/2 pairs of poles**.
- Number of cycles per revolution = $P/2$.
- Number of revolutions per second = $N/60$.
- Therefore, frequency:

$$f = \text{cycles/sec} = \frac{P}{2} \times \frac{N}{60} = \frac{P \, N}{120}$$

> **Logic explanation:** A 4-pole machine completes 2 electrical cycles per mechanical revolution. If the shaft runs at 1500 rpm, then $f = (4 \times 1500)/120 = 50$ Hz, which is the standard Indian power frequency.

### Derivation Part B: Average EMF per Conductor

- Consider a single conductor rotating at $N$ rpm in a field of flux $\phi$ per pole.
- Flux cut by the conductor in one revolution = $\phi \times P$ (since there are P poles, each contributing $\phi$ Webers).
- Number of revolutions per second = $N/60$.
- Flux cut per second = $\phi \times P \times N/60$ Webers/second = Volts (by Faraday's Law).

$$\text{Average EMF per conductor} = \frac{\phi \, P \, N}{60} \, \text{Volts}$$

Substituting $P \, N / 120 = f$, i.e., $P \, N / 60 = 2f$:

$$E_{avg/conductor} = 2 \, f \, \phi$$

> **Logic explanation:** The factor 2 appears because flux $\phi$ is cut twice per electrical cycle (once entering N, once entering S).

### Derivation Part C: RMS EMF per Conductor

For a **sinusoidal waveform**, the form factor is:

$$K_f = \frac{E_{rms}}{E_{avg}} = 1.11$$

Therefore:

$$E_{rms/conductor} = K_f \times E_{avg/conductor} = 1.11 \times 2 \, f \, \phi = 2.22 \, f \, \phi$$

> **Logic explanation:** Since the actual air-gap flux is sinusoidal (by design), the induced EMF is also sinusoidal, and the ratio of RMS to average for a sinusoid is exactly 1.11.

### Derivation Part D: EMF for $Z_{ph}$ Conductors in Series (Concentrated, Full-Pitched)

If $Z_{ph}$ conductors are connected in series per phase:

$$E_{ph, \text{ideal}} = 2.22 \, f \, \phi \, Z_{ph}$$

Equivalently, since $T_{ph} = Z_{ph}/2$ turns per phase:

$$E_{ph, \text{ideal}} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph}$$

> **Logic explanation:** Each turn has 2 conductors. Multiplying the per-conductor RMS EMF by $Z_{ph}$ conductors and dividing by 2 (since conductors come in pairs) gives the form with $T_{ph}$.

### Derivation Part E: Application of Winding Factor

In practice, coils are **distributed** and often **short-pitched**. Both effects reduce the resultant EMF vector from the ideal concentrated full-pitched value. The reduction factor is the winding factor $K_w$:

$$E_{ph, \text{actual}} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$$

> **Logic explanation:** The phasor sum of distributed EMFs is always less than their arithmetic sum, hence $K_d \le 1$. Similarly, a short-pitched coil has both its sides contributing EMFs that are not 180° apart in time, reducing the resultant — hence $K_p \le 1$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Distribution Factor ($K_d$)

Consider $m$ coils per pole per phase, displaced from each other by slot angle $\beta$.

**Step-by-step phasor analysis:**

- EMF induced in coil 1: $E_1 = E_0 \angle 0^\circ$
- EMF induced in coil 2: $E_2 = E_0 \angle \beta$
- EMF induced in coil 3: $E_3 = E_0 \angle 2\beta$
- ...
- EMF induced in coil m: $E_m = E_0 \angle (m-1)\beta$

The phasor sum (resultant) of these $m$ equal phasors of magnitude $E_0$ arranged in a regular polygon:

$$E_{\text{resultant}} = 2 \, R \sin\left(\frac{m\beta}{2}\right)$$

where $R$ is the radius of the circle. Each phasor chord:

$$E_0 = 2 \, R \sin\left(\frac{\beta}{2}\right)$$

**Distribution factor:**

$$K_d = \frac{E_{\text{resultant}}}{m \times E_0} = \frac{2 R \sin(m\beta/2)}{m \times 2 R \sin(\beta/2)} = \frac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$$

$$\boxed{K_d = \frac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}}$$

> **Logic explanation:** The numerator is the chord that subtends the entire arc of m slots, and the denominator is m times the chord that subtends just one slot angle.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation of Pitch Factor ($K_p$)

Consider a coil with span less than 180° electrical (short-pitched by angle $\alpha$).

- Full-pitched coil EMF (vector sum of two conductors 180° apart in space): $E_{full} = 2 E_0$
- Short-pitched coil EMF (vector sum of two conductors 180° − α apart):

$$E_{short} = 2 \, E_0 \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Pitch factor:**

$$K_p = \frac{E_{short}}{E_{full}} = \frac{2 E_0 \cos(\alpha/2)}{2 E_0} = \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{K_p = \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}$$

> **Logic explanation:** When $\alpha = 0$ (full pitch), $K_p = \cos(0) = 1$. When $\alpha = 180°$ (coil span = 0), $K_p = \cos(90°) = 0$ — zero EMF. This is the limiting case.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 3-phase, 16-pole, 50 Hz alternator has a simplex wave wound armature with 144 slots and 10 conductors per slot. The flux per pole is 25 mWb and the winding is short-pitched by 2 slots. Determine the induced EMF per phase. Take chording angle α and find $K_p$ accordingly.

**Given:**
- Phases = 3, Poles $P = 16$, Frequency $f = 50$ Hz
- Slots = 144, Conductors/slot = 10
- $\phi = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb
- Short-pitched by 2 slots

**Step 1 — Total Conductors:**

$$Z_{\text{total}} = 144 \times 10 = 1440$$

**Step 2 — Conductors per phase (wave winding):**

In simplex wave winding, $A = 2$, so:

$$Z_{ph} = \frac{2 \times 1440}{2 \times 3} = \frac{1440}{3} = 480$$

**Step 3 — Slot angle β:**

$$\text{Slots per pole} = \frac{144}{16} = 9$$

$$\beta = \frac{180^\circ}{9} = 20^\circ \text{ (electrical)}$$

**Step 4 — Distribution Factor:**

$$m = \frac{\text{slots/pole}}{\text{phases}} = \frac{9}{3} = 3$$

$$K_d = \frac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)} = \frac{\sin(3 \times 20^\circ/2)}{3 \sin(20^\circ/2)} = \frac{\sin(30^\circ)}{3 \sin(10^\circ)}$$

$$K_d = \frac{0.5}{3 \times 0.1736} = \frac{0.5}{0.5209} = 0.9598$$

**Step 5 — Chording Angle α:**

Coil span is short by 2 slots. Each slot is $20°$, so $\alpha = 2 \times 20° = 40°$.

**Step 6 — Pitch Factor:**

$$K_p = \cos(\alpha/2) = \cos(20°) = 0.9397$$

**Step 7 — Winding Factor:**

$$K_w = K_d \times K_p = 0.9598 \times 0.9397 = 0.9020$$

**Step 8 — Turns per phase:**

$$T_{ph} = \frac{Z_{ph}}{2} = \frac{480}{2} = 240$$

**Step 9 — Induced EMF per phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times f \times \phi \times T_{ph} \times K_w$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 240 \times 0.9020$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.025 \times 240 \times 0.9020$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.025 \times 216.48$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 5.412$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 270.6 = 1201.46 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 1201.5 \text{ V} \approx 1.2 \text{ kV per phase}}$$

**Step 10 — Line EMF (star connection):**

$$E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 1201.5 = 2081 \text{ V} \approx 2.08 \text{ kV}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Distribution Factor" in the EMF equation of an AC machine. State its formula.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Distribution Factor ($K_d$)** is defined as the ratio of the phasor sum of EMFs induced in all the coils of a distributed winding to the arithmetic sum of the individual coil EMFs. It accounts for the reduction in EMF that occurs when coils of a winding are distributed across multiple slots per pole per phase, instead of being concentrated in a single slot.
>
> **Formula:**
> $$K_d = \frac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$$
>
> where $m$ is the number of slots per pole per phase and $\beta$ is the slot angle in electrical degrees. The value of $K_d$ is always less than or equal to 1.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the constant 4.44 in the EMF equation of an AC machine?**

**Model Answer:**

> The constant **4.44** in the EMF equation $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T \, K_w$ arises from the product of:
> 1. The **form factor** (1.11) — which converts the average EMF to RMS EMF for a sinusoidal wave.
> 2. The **factor 4** — which accounts for the conductor cutting each flux line twice per electrical cycle (factor 2) and two conductors per turn (factor 2).
>
> Mathematically: $4.44 = 1.11 \times 2 \times 2$. It is the key constant that bridges the **average value derivation** with the **RMS value** required for practical AC voltage measurement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of an AC machine. Explain the role of distribution factor and pitch factor in the equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 10-pole alternator has a double-layer winding with 60 slots, 4 conductors per slot, short-pitched by 3 slots. The flux per pole is 0.05 Wb. Find the induced EMF per phase. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation of EMF Equation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete derivation. Key points to write:

1. **Faraday's Law** basis: Average EMF per conductor = $2 f \phi$ Volts.
2. **Form factor application**: RMS EMF per conductor = $1.11 \times 2 f \phi = 2.22 f \phi$.
3. **Multiplication by series conductors**: $E_{ph} = 2.22 f \phi Z_{ph} = 4.44 f \phi T_{ph}$.
4. **Winding factor**: $E_{ph} = 4.44 f \phi T_{ph} K_w$.

**Role of Distribution Factor $K_d$:**
- When coils of a phase are distributed in multiple slots, the EMFs induced in them are displaced in time phase by the slot angle $\beta$.
- The phasor sum is always less than the arithmetic sum.
- $K_d = \frac{E_{\text{phasor sum}}}{E_{\text{arithmetic sum}}} \le 1$.

**Role of Pitch Factor $K_p$:**
- A full-pitched coil spans exactly 180° electrical, giving maximum EMF.
- A short-pitched (chorded) coil has its two sides displaced by less than 180°, causing the EMFs to add at an angle less than 180°.
- $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2) \le 1$ where $\alpha$ is the chording angle.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Phases = 3, $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 10$ poles
- Slots = 60, Conductors/slot = 4
- Short-pitched by 3 slots
- $\phi = 0.05$ Wb

**Step 1 — Total Conductors:**

$$Z_{\text{total}} = 60 \times 4 = 240$$

**Step 2 — Conductors per phase:**

$$Z_{ph} = \frac{2 \times 240}{2 \times 3} = \frac{240}{3} = 80$$

**Step 3 — Slot angle β:**

$$\text{Slots/pole} = \frac{60}{10} = 6$$

$$\beta = \frac{180^\circ}{6} = 30^\circ \text{ (electrical)}$$

**Step 4 — Distribution Factor:**

$$m = \frac{6}{3} = 2$$

$$K_d = \frac{\sin(2 \times 30^\circ/2)}{2 \sin(30^\circ/2)} = \frac{\sin(30^\circ)}{2 \sin(15^\circ)} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 0.2588} = \frac{0.5}{0.5176} = 0.9659$$

**Step 5 — Chording angle α:**

$$\alpha = 3 \times 30^\circ = 90^\circ$$

**Step 6 — Pitch Factor:**

$$K_p = \cos(\alpha/2) = \cos(45°) = 0.7071$$

**Step 7 — Winding Factor:**

$$K_w = K_d \times K_p = 0.9659 \times 0.7071 = 0.6830$$

**Step 8 — Turns per phase:**

$$T_{ph} = \frac{80}{2} = 40$$

**Step 9 — EMF per phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 40 \times 0.6830$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 40 \times 0.6830$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.05 \times 27.32$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 1.366$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 68.3 = 303.25 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 303.25 \text{ V per phase}}$$

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of winding factor. Derive the expression for pitch factor with the help of a phasor diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 16-pole synchronous generator has 192 slots with 8 conductors per slot. The coils are short-pitched by 1 slot. The flux per pole is 60 mWb and the speed is 375 rpm. Calculate the EMF generated per phase. Also calculate the line voltage for a star-connected armature. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a) Winding Factor and Pitch Factor Derivation (7 marks):**

**Winding Factor Concept:**
The **winding factor $K_w$** is the ratio of actual EMF induced in a winding (considering distribution and short-pitching) to the EMF that would be induced if the winding were concentrated and full-pitched.

$$K_w = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{concentrated, full-pitched}}}$$

It is the product of **Distribution Factor ($K_d$)** and **Pitch Factor ($K_p$)**:

$$K_w = K_d \times K_p$$

**Derivation of Pitch Factor with Phasor Logic:**

Consider a coil with sides at slot positions separated by electrical angle $(180° - \alpha)$ instead of the full pitch of 180°.

Let $E_c$ be the EMF induced in each coil side.

- **Full-pitched coil:** Coil sides are 180° apart. EMFs add directly:
  $$E_{\text{full}} = E_c + E_c = 2 E_c$$

- **Short-pitched coil:** Coil sides are $(180° - \alpha)$ apart. The phasor sum of the two EMFs at angle $(180° - \alpha)$ apart:

$$E_{\text{short}} = 2 E_c \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

The **pitch factor** is the ratio:

$$K_p = \frac{E_{\text{short}}}{E_{\text{full}}} = \frac{2 E_c \cos(\alpha/2)}{2 E_c} = \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{K_p = \cos\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **chording angle** (in electrical degrees) by which the coil span is less than 180°.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Phases = 3, $P = 16$ poles, $N = 375$ rpm
- Slots = 192, Conductors/slot = 8
- Short-pitched by 1 slot
- $\phi = 60 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb = 0.06 Wb

**Step 1 — Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{P \times N}{120} = \frac{16 \times 375}{120} = \frac{6000}{120} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Total Conductors:**

$$Z_{\text{total}} = 192 \times 8 = 1536$$

**Step 3 — Conductors per phase:**

$$Z_{ph} = \frac{2 \times 1536}{2 \times 3} = \frac{1536}{3} = 512$$

**Step 4 — Slot angle β:**

$$\text{Slots/pole} = \frac{192}{16} = 12$$

$$\beta = \frac{180^\circ}{12} = 15^\circ$$

**Step 5 — Distribution Factor:**

$$m = \frac{12}{3} = 4$$

$$K_d = \frac{\sin(4 \times 15^\circ/2)}{4 \sin(15^\circ/2)} = \frac{\sin(30^\circ)}{4 \sin(7.5^\circ)} = \frac{0.5}{4 \times 0.1305} = \frac{0.5}{0.5220} = 0.9577$$

**Step 6 — Chording angle α:**

$$\alpha = 1 \times 15^\circ = 15^\circ$$

**Step 7 — Pitch Factor:**

$$K_p = \cos(\alpha/2) = \cos(7.5°) = 0.9914$$

**Step 8 — Winding Factor:**

$$K_w = K_d \times K_p = 0.9577 \times 0.9914 = 0.9495$$

**Step 9 — Turns per phase:**

$$T_{ph} = \frac{512}{2} = 256$$

**Step 10 — EMF per phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.06 \times 256 \times 0.9495$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.06 \times 256 \times 0.9495$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 0.06 \times 243.07$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 50 \times 14.584$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 729.21 = 3237.7 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 3237.7 \text{ V} \approx 3.24 \text{ kV per phase}}$$

**Step 11 — Line Voltage (Star Connection):**

$$E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 3237.7 = 5607.7 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{line} \approx 5607.7 \text{ V} \approx 5.61 \text{ kV}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams**
>
> 1. **Confusing Zph and Tph**: Students often forget that $T_{ph} = Z_{ph}/2$. Using $Z_{ph}$ directly with the 4.44 formula will give **double the correct EMF**. Always check which form of the formula you are using.
>
> 2. **Slot Angle vs. Pole Pitch**: A common error is computing $\beta = 180°/P$ instead of $\beta = 180°/(\text{slots per pole})$. Always use **slots per pole**, not the total number of poles.
>
> 3. **Chording Angle Calculation**: When the problem says "short-pitched by *n* slots", students forget to multiply *n* by the slot angle $\beta$. The correct chording angle is $\alpha = n \times \beta$.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Winding Factor**: Many students write $E = 4.44 f \phi T$ without multiplying by $K_w$. This gives **full marks deducted** for ignoring the actual winding construction.
>
> 5. **Wave vs. Lap Winding**: For simplex wave winding, the number of parallel paths $A = 2$ (NOT 2P). This changes how you compute $Z_{ph} = (2 \times Z_{\text{total}})/(2 \times \text{phases})$ for wave and $Z_{ph} = Z_{\text{total}}/(\text{phases})$ for lap.
>
> 6. **Line vs. Phase EMF**: KTU often asks for **line voltage** but students stop at phase voltage. Always state the connection (star/delta) and convert if asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ The **master EMF equation** is: $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$
>
> ✅ The **alternate form** is: $E_{ph} = 2.22 \, f \, \phi \, Z_{ph} \, K_w$
>
> ✅ **Frequency formula**: $f = PN/120$, where $P$ = poles, $N$ = rpm
>
> ✅ **Slot angle**: $\beta = 180°/(\text{slots per pole})$
>
> ✅ **Distribution Factor**: $K_d = \frac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$, where $m$ = slots/pole/phase
>
> ✅ **Pitch Factor**: $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$, where $\alpha$ = chording angle
>
> ✅ **Winding Factor**: $K_w = K_d \times K_p$ (always less than 1)
>
> ✅ **Constant 4.44** = form factor (1.11) × 2 (cutting twice) × 2 (two conductors per turn)
>
> ✅ **Conductors per phase**:
> - **Lap winding**: $Z_{ph} = Z_{\text{total}}/\text{phases}$
> - **Wave winding**: $Z_{ph} = 2 Z_{\text{total}}/(2 \times \text{phases}) = Z_{\text{total}}/\text{phases}$ (for simplex)
>
> ✅ **Turns per phase**: $T_{ph} = Z_{ph}/2$
>
> ✅ **Line voltage**: $E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph}$ (star), $E_L = E_{ph}$ (delta)
>
> ✅ **Assumption**: Sinusoidal flux distribution in the air gap
>
> ✅ **For chording by n slots**: $\alpha = n \times \beta$ (electrical degrees)
>
> ✅ **Units check**: $f$ in Hz, $\phi$ in Wb, $T_{ph}$ dimensionless, $K_w$ dimensionless → $E_{ph}$ in Volts
>
> ✅ **Limiting cases**:
> - Full pitch, concentrated: $K_w = 1$ → maximum EMF
> - $K_d = 1$ only when $m = 1$ (concentrated)
> - $K_p = 1$ only when $\alpha = 0$ (full pitch)
>
> ✅ **Engineering Insight**: Increasing distribution reduces harmonics (better waveform) but slightly reduces EMF. Short-pitching also reduces certain harmonics — a deliberate design trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Developed in an Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torque Developed in an Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Module 4: AC Rotating Machines), the **torque developed in an induction motor** is defined as the net turning moment produced at the air gap due to the interaction between the rotating magnetic field (produced by the stator) and the induced current flowing in the short-circuited rotor conductors. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the gross mechanical power developed (air-gap power minus rotor copper loss) to the mechanical angular velocity of the rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must be Memorized):**
> The torque developed ($T$) in a three-phase induction motor is given by the gross mechanical power divided by the rotor's mechanical speed:
> $$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_g - P_{cu,r}}{\omega_r}$$
> where $P_g$ is the air-gap power and $P_{cu,r}$ is the rotor copper loss.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Fishing Reel" Intuition)

Imagine you are **rowing a boat** through water:

- The **oars** represent the **stator's rotating magnetic field** — they swing at a steady pace set by the upstream current (the 3-phase supply).
- The **water resistance** on the oar blades represents the **induced rotor currents** (Lenz's law — the rotor opposes the relative motion that created it).
- The **force that actually pulls the boat forward** is the **torque** you feel on the oar handles. It is not the full speed of the swing, but rather the *slip* (the lag between the oar swing and the boat's actual movement) that generates this useful force.

If the boat moves at the *exact* same speed as your oar swing (slip = 0), the oar slices through clean water — **no torque is produced**. The bigger the *slip* (load on the boat), the more water the oar pushes, and the more force you generate — up to a sweet spot, after which fighting too hard slows the swing and force drops. This sweet spot is the **maximum (or pull-out) torque**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics:**
> - Synchronous speed: $N_s = \frac{120f}{P}$ in **rpm** (where $f$ is supply frequency in **Hz**, $P$ is number of poles)
> - Synchronous angular velocity: $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$ in **rad/s**
> - Slip: $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ — a **dimensionless** quantity
> - Standard metric for industrial torque: **Newton-metre (N·m)**
> - 1 horsepower ≈ **746 W** (a useful conversion for KTU problems involving horse-power)

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU

The torque-slip characteristic is the single most important graph in induction motor design. From it, engineers derive:
1. **Starting torque** — can the motor start the connected load?
2. **Maximum (pull-out) torque** — will the motor survive sudden overloads?
3. **Operating slip** — where the motor settles for a given mechanical load?

This topic is a **favourite KTU question** in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) of the End Semester Examination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power-Flow Logic (Step by Step)

Induction motor torque can be derived from two equivalent perspectives. KTU examiners love both. You must know both for a **14-mark question**.

### 2.1.1 The "Mechanical" Derivation (Power Flow Approach)

1. **Input Electrical Power** ($P_{in}$) is drawn from the 3-phase mains.
2. A fraction is lost as **Stator Core (Iron) Loss** ($P_i$) and **Stator Copper Loss** ($P_{cu,s}$).
3. The remaining power crosses the air gap and is called the **Air-Gap Power** ($P_g$). This is the power transferred magnetically to the rotor.
4. A portion of $P_g$ is dissipated as **Rotor Copper Loss** ($P_{cu,r}$).
5. The leftover is the **Gross Mechanical Power Developed** ($P_m$):
   $$P_m = P_g - P_{cu,r}$$
6. Subtracting **Friction & Windage Loss** ($P_{fw}$) gives the **Shaft (Output) Power** ($P_{out}$).
7. Torque is the gross mechanical power divided by the *rotor's* mechanical angular speed:
   $$T = \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s(1-s)}$$

### 2.1.2 The "Electrical" Derivation (Equivalent Circuit Approach)

1. The rotor of an induction motor behaves like a **transformer with a short-circuited secondary**, but with one twist: the rotor frequency is $f_r = s \cdot f$.
2. The rotor's effective impedance per phase, referred to the stator, is:
   $$Z_2' = \frac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$$
3. The rotor current per phase is:
   $$I_2' = \frac{E_2'}{Z_2'} = \frac{E_2'}{\sqrt{(R_2'/s)^2 + (X_2')^2}}$$
4. Rotor copper loss per phase is $I_2'^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)$.
5. Air-gap power per phase is $I_2'^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)$ (this is the standard "virtual resistance" trick — power dissipated in $R_2'/s$ equals $P_g$).
6. Total $P_g$ (3-phase) divided by synchronous speed gives the **electromagnetic torque**:
   $$T = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** When you want **rotor copper loss** as a fraction of air-gap power, the slip is the answer:
> $$\frac{P_{cu,r}}{P_g} = s \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_g$$
> And consequently: $P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g$. This is one of the **most-tested KTU identities**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the table before the exam.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ | rpm | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le s \le 1$ in motoring |
| 3 | Rotor Speed | $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ | rpm | - |
| 4 | Rotor Frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | - |
| 5 | Air-Gap Power (3-φ) | $P_g = \dfrac{3 I_2'^2 R_2'}{s}$ | W | "Virtual resistor" trick |
| 6 | Rotor Copper Loss | $P_{cu,r} = s P_g = 3 I_2'^2 R_2'$ | W | - |
| 7 | Gross Mech. Power | $P_m = (1-s) P_g$ | W | - |
| 8 | Torque (from $P_g$) | $T = \dfrac{P_g}{\omega_s}$ | N·m | $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$ |
| 9 | Torque (from $P_m$) | $T = \dfrac{P_m}{\omega_s(1-s)}$ | N·m | Equivalent identity |
| 10 | Torque Equation (Standard) | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}$ | N·m | The KTU master equation |
| 11 | Maximum Torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'}$ | N·m | **Independent of $R_2'$** |
| 12 | Slip at Max Torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2'}{X_2'}$ | dimensionless | - |
| 13 | Starting Torque ($s=1$) | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3 E_2'^2 R_2'}{\omega_s (R_2'^2 + X_2'^2)}$ | N·m | - |
| 14 | Condition for $T_{st} = T_{max}$ | $R_2' = X_2'$ | - | Special design criterion |
| 15 | Full-Load Slip | $s_{fl} = \dfrac{s_{max}}{T_{max}/T_{fl} + \sqrt{(T_{max}/T_{fl})^2 - 1}}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of master eqn. |
| 16 | Power in HP | $P(\text{HP}) = \dfrac{P(\text{W})}{746}$ | HP | Useful unit conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> 1. **Crane & Elevator Design** — high starting torque motors (with added rotor resistance) are mandatory.
> 2. **Pump & Fan Loads** — low starting torque is acceptable; these use standard squirrel-cage motors.
> 3. **Electric Vehicles** — variable-frequency drives (VFDs) shift the entire torque curve by varying $f$, keeping $V/f$ constant.
> 4. **Conveyor Belts** — must overcome static friction at start, hence starting torque rating is critical.

## 2.3 The Torque-Slip Characteristic (Conceptual Reading)

- At $s = 0$ (synchronous speed) — **$T = 0$** (the oar moves with the water; nothing pushes).
- At $s = 1$ (standstill) — torque is $T_{st}$ (starting torque).
- As $s$ increases from 0, torque rises **sharply**, peaks at $s = s_{max}$, then drops toward $T_{st}$.
- Normal operating region is the **stable region** where $0 \le s \le s_{max}$ (here, the motor *self-corrects* against load disturbances).
- Region $s_{max} \le s \le 1$ is the **unstable region** — the motor cannot settle here.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following two derivations are the **two pillars** of this topic. KTU expects you to be able to write either one of them, from memory, in the examination hall.

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Torque from Power Flow

**Step 1:** Start with the power balance at the air gap. The air-gap power is the power transferred from stator to rotor across the magnetic coupling.

$$P_g = P_{cu,r} + P_m$$

**Step 2:** Using the KTU golden identity that the rotor copper loss is the slip-fraction of the air-gap power:

$$P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_g$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Step 1:

$$P_g = s \cdot P_g + P_m$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the gross mechanical power:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_g - s \cdot P_g &= P_m \\[4pt]
P_g (1 - s) &= P_m
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Note that the rotor's mechanical angular velocity is:

$$\omega_r = \omega_s (1 - s)$$

because $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ and $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.

**Step 6:** The electromagnetic torque is the gross mechanical power divided by the rotor's mechanical speed:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{P_m}{\omega_r} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{P_g(1-s)}{\omega_s(1-s)} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{P_g}{\omega_s}
\end{aligned}$$

**This is a beautiful result**: the torque is simply the air-gap power divided by the *synchronous* speed (not the rotor's actual speed). This is the **KTU gold-standard form** and is used in 90% of textbook problems.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Torque from the Equivalent Circuit

**Step 1:** The per-phase rotor current, referred to the stator side, is:

$$I_2' = \frac{E_2'}{\sqrt{(R_2'/s)^2 + (X_2')^2}}$$

where $E_2'$ is the per-phase rotor induced EMF at standstill (referred to stator), $R_2'$ is the rotor resistance referred to stator, and $X_2'$ is the rotor leakage reactance at standstill (referred to stator).

**Step 2:** The power transferred across the air gap, per phase, is the current squared times the "virtual" resistance $R_2'/s$ (this is the famous transformer-with-moving-secondary trick):

$$P_{g,\text{per phase}} = I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{g,\text{per phase}} &= \frac{E_2'^2}{(R_2'/s)^2 + (X_2')^2} \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} \\[6pt]
                        &= \frac{E_2'^2 \cdot R_2' / s}{(R_2'^2 / s^2) + (X_2')^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $s^2$ to clear the fractions:

$$P_{g,\text{per phase}} = \frac{s^2 E_2'^2 \cdot R_2' / s}{R_2'^2 + s^2 (X_2')^2} = \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 (X_2')^2}$$

**Step 5:** For a 3-phase motor, multiply by 3:

$$P_g = \frac{3 s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 (X_2')^2}$$

**Step 6:** Divide by synchronous speed to obtain the torque:

$$T = \frac{P_g}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 (X_2')^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU master equation for torque-slip relationship.** Every other formula (starting torque, maximum torque, etc.) is derived from it.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Maximum Torque Condition

To find the slip at which torque is maximum, we use **calculus**: differentiate $T$ with respect to $s$ and set the result to zero.

**Step 1:** Write the torque equation as:

$$T = \frac{3 E_2'^2 R_2'}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}$$

The term $\frac{3 E_2'^2 R_2'}{\omega_s}$ is a constant for a given motor. Let $K = \frac{3 E_2'^2 R_2'}{\omega_s}$.

So $T = K \cdot \dfrac{s}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to $s$. Let $u = s$ and $v = R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2$. Use the quotient rule:

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = K \cdot \frac{(1)(v) - (s)(2s X_2'^2)}{v^2}$$

**Step 3:** Set $\frac{dT}{ds} = 0$ (peak condition). The numerator must be zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2 - 2 s^2 X_2'^2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
R_2'^2 - s^2 X_2'^2 &= 0 \\[4pt]
R_2'^2 &= s^2 X_2'^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $s$ (taking the positive root, since $0 \le s \le 1$):

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $s_{max}$ back into the master equation. Notice that $R_2'^2 + s_{max}^2 X_2'^2 = R_2'^2 + R_2'^2 = 2 R_2'^2$. The torque becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_{max} E_2'^2 R_2'}{2 R_2'^2} \\[6pt]
        &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(R_2'/X_2') E_2'^2 R_2'}{2 R_2'^2} \\[6pt]
        &= \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Two stunning observations** that KTU examiners frequently test:
> 1. $T_{max}$ is **independent of $R_2'$** — so adding rotor resistance does *not* change the maximum torque. It only shifts it to a different slip.
> 2. The ratio $\dfrac{T_{max}}{T_{st}}$ is **also independent of $E_2'$** and equals $\dfrac{R_2'^2 + X_2'^2}{2 R_2' X_2'}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — The "Trick" of Expressing All Torques in Terms of $T_{max}$

A very common KTU 14-mark question asks: *Given $T_{max}$ and $s_{max}$, find torque at any slip $s$.*

**The result** (and its derivation is a favourite KTU sub-part):

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2}{\frac{s}{s_{max}} + \frac{s_{max}}{s}}$$

**Derivation (quick):**

Start with $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}$. Divide numerator and denominator by $s X_2'^2$:

$$T = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'} \cdot \frac{2 s R_2'/X_2'}{(R_2'/X_2')^2 + s^2} = T_{max} \cdot \frac{2 s \cdot s_{max}}{s_{max}^2 + s^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{T}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 s \cdot s_{max}}{s_{max}^2 + s^2} = \frac{2}{\dfrac{s}{s_{max}} + \dfrac{s_{max}}{s}}$$

This elegant form is the **Morse equation** and is heavily used in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Power Flow and Torque Origin

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3-Phase AC Supply] --> B[Stator Winding]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field]
    C --> D[Rotor Conductors - Induced EMF and Current]
    D --> E[Force on Rotor Bars - Lorentz F x I x B]
    E --> F[Electromagnetic Torque T at Rotor Shaft]
    F --> G{Mechanical Load}
    G -->|High Load Demand| H[Increased Slip s]
    H --> D
    F --> I[Power Conversion Chain]
    I --> I1[Pin]
    I1 --> I2[Stator Cu Loss PcL_s]
    I1 --> I3[Core Loss Pi]
    I2 --> I4[Air Gap Power Pg]
    I3 --> I4
    I4 --> I5[Rotor Cu Loss s Pg]
    I4 --> I6[Gross Mech Power 1 minus s Pg]
    I5 --> I6
    I6 --> I7[Friction and Windage Pfw]
    I7 --> I8[Shaft Output Pout]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Torque-Slip Characteristic Reading Guide

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[Slip s] --> K{s Region}
    K -->|s equals 0| L[Synchronous Speed - Torque T equals 0]
    K -->|0 less than s less than s max| M[Stable Operating Region]
    K -->|s equals s max| N[Maximum Torque T max]
    K -->|s max less than s less than 1| O[Unstable Region]
    K -->|s equals 1| P[Standstill - Starting Torque T st]
    M --> Q[Normal Running Point]
    Q --> R[Full Load Slip s fl]
    R --> S[Full Load Torque T fl]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Effect of Rotor Resistance Variation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Original Curve R2 prime equals 1 ohm] --> X2[T max unchanged]
    X1 --> X3[s max equals 0 point 1]
    X4[Double R2 prime equals 2 ohm] --> X2
    X4 --> X5[s max shifts to 0 point 2]
    X4 --> X6[Starting Torque T st increases]
    X2 --> X7[Key Insight: T max is independent of R2 prime]
    X6 --> X8[Industrial Use: Wound Rotor Motors for High Starting Torque]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Variables Affecting Torque

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Supply Voltage V] --> TQ[Torque Output T]
    V2[Supply Frequency f] --> TQ
    V3[Rotor Resistance R2 prime] --> TQ
    V4[Rotor Reactance X2 prime] --> TQ
    V5[Slip s] --> TQ
    TQ --> R1[Starting Torque T st]
    TQ --> R2[Maximum Torque T max]
    TQ --> R3[Full Load Torque T fl]
    R1 --> U1[Important for Direct On Line Starters]
    R2 --> U2[Important for Overload Withstand Capability]
    R3 --> U3[Important for Steady State Mechanical Design]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in the Exam:**
> - The first diagram shows the *causal chain* from supply to torque output — use it when answering "Explain the production of torque in an induction motor" (a common 7-mark sub-question).
> - The second diagram helps you *visualize* the torque-slip curve verbally — a frequent 3-mark question asks to "label regions of the torque-slip curve."
> - The third diagram justifies *why* wound-rotor motors with added external resistance are used in crane and lift applications.
> - The fourth diagram is your "KTU Examiner's Favourite" — when asked "What factors affect the starting torque?", list V, f, R_2, X_2, and s.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define slip of an induction motor. Why is it impossible for an induction motor to run at synchronous speed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Slip ($s$) is defined as the difference between the synchronous speed ($N_s$) and the actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a fraction of $N_s$:
  $$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$
- **Reason (1 mark):** If the rotor were to run at synchronous speed ($N_r = N_s$), there would be no relative motion between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors. By Faraday's law, no EMF would be induced in the rotor, no rotor current would flow, and consequently **no torque could be developed** ($T = 0$). Hence, an induction motor can never reach synchronous speed under load.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the condition for maximum torque in a three-phase induction motor. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Condition (2 marks):** The torque becomes maximum when the rotor resistance referred to stator equals the slip times the rotor leakage reactance referred to stator:
  $$s_{max} \cdot X_2' = R_2' \quad\Longrightarrow\quad s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'}$$
- **Significance (1 mark):** The maximum torque $T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'}$ is **independent of rotor resistance** $R_2'$. This is a critical design insight: by changing $R_2'$, one can vary the slip at which maximum torque occurs (useful for high-starting-torque applications like cranes) without changing the magnitude of $T_{max}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 3-phase factor:** Many students write the per-phase formula and forget to multiply by 3. Always re-check: induction motors in KTU problems are **3-phase** unless stated otherwise.
> 2. **Mixing $N_s$ and $N_r$:** When computing torque from $P_m$, you **must** divide by $\omega_r$, not $\omega_s$. When computing from $P_g$, you **must** divide by $\omega_s$. Mismatching these is the #1 cause of lost marks.
> 3. **Rotor frequency confusion:** The rotor circuit reactance uses $X_2 = 2\pi f L$, where $f$ is the **supply** frequency, not the rotor frequency. The rotor frequency $f_r = s f$ only affects the EMF magnitude.
> 4. **Units:** Torque must be in **N·m**, speeds in **rad/s**. If you compute $N_s$ in rpm, convert using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, Y-connected induction motor has a rotor resistance and standstill reactance per phase (referred to stator) of $0.2 \ \Omega$ and $1.0 \ \Omega$ respectively.**

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum torque in an induction motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the above motor, calculate: (i) the slip at which maximum torque occurs, (ii) the maximum torque, and (iii) the starting torque. Take the rotor induced EMF per phase at standstill (referred to stator) as $E_2' = 100$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 Marks]**

Starting from the standard torque equation:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}$$

For a given motor, $E_2', R_2', X_2', \omega_s$ are constants. Differentiating $T$ with respect to $s$ and equating to zero gives the maximum torque condition. As derived in **Section 3.3** of these notes, the result is:

$$\boxed{s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'}}$$

And the maximum torque is:

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split for (a):** Definition of torque equation (1) → differentiation step (2) → solving for $s_{max}$ (2) → substituting back to get $T_{max}$ (2).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 Marks]**

**Given data:**
- $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$ poles, $R_2' = 0.2 \ \Omega$, $X_2' = 1.0 \ \Omega$, $E_2' = 100$ V.

**Step 1: Compute synchronous speed and angular velocity.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \ \text{rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**(i) Slip at maximum torque (2 marks):**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 \ \ (\text{or } 20\%)$$

**(ii) Maximum torque (3 marks):**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2'^2}{2 \omega_s X_2'} = \frac{3 \times (100)^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 1.0}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{30000}{314.16} \approx 95.49 \ \text{N·m}$$

**(iii) Starting torque (2 marks):** Using $s = 1$:

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + X_2'^2} = \frac{3 \times 10000 \times 0.2}{157.08 \times (0.04 + 1.0)}$$

$$T_{st} = \frac{6000}{157.08 \times 1.04} = \frac{6000}{163.36} \approx 36.73 \ \text{N·m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** $T_{st} < T_{max}$ ✓ (this is always true for $R_2' < X_2'$). The ratio $T_{max}/T_{st} = 95.49/36.73 \approx 2.6$, which is typical for standard induction motors.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 6-pole induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. The rotor resistance per phase is $0.1 \ \Omega$ and the standstill rotor reactance per phase is $0.5 \ \Omega$. The rotor induced EMF per phase at standstill, referred to stator, is $E_2' = 200$ V.**

**(a)** Draw the approximate equivalent circuit of an induction motor referred to stator and explain how the torque equation is derived from it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the full-load torque, the starting torque, and the maximum torque. Also find the slip at which maximum torque occurs. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 Marks]**

**Approximate Equivalent Circuit (Per Phase, Referred to Stator):**

A clean verbal description is required (textbook-style figure is also acceptable in the answer book):

The approximate circuit consists of a stator phase voltage $V_1$ driving a series combination of the stator impedance ($R_1 + jX_1$), a magnetizing branch ($jX_m$ — often ignored in the "approximate" version and moved to the input terminals), and the rotor branch modelled as a load impedance $Z_2' = \dfrac{R_2'}{s} + jX_2'$.

The **key trick** is to recognize that the air-gap power per phase is exactly the power dissipated in the *virtual resistance* $\dfrac{R_2'}{s}$. This is because:

$$P_{g, \text{per phase}} = I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' + I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' \cdot \frac{(1-s)}{s}$$

The first term is rotor copper loss; the second is gross mechanical power. Summing them gives air-gap power. Therefore:

$$P_g = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} = \frac{3 s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}$$

And since $T = P_g / \omega_s$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s^2 X_2'^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-split for (a):** Circuit description (2) → identification of $R_2'/s$ trick (2) → derivation of $P_g$ (2) → final torque equation (1).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 6$ poles, $s_{fl} = 0.04$, $R_2' = 0.1 \ \Omega$, $X_2' = 0.5 \ \Omega$, $E_2' = 200$ V.

**Step 1: Synchronous speed and $\omega_s$.**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \ \text{rpm} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} \approx 104.72 \ \text{rad/s}$$

**Slip at maximum torque (1 mark):**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2'}{X_2'} = \frac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2$$

**Maximum torque (2 marks):**

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 \times 200^2}{2 \times 104.72 \times 0.5} = \frac{120000}{104.72} \approx 1145.9 \ \text{N·m}$$

**Full-load torque (2 marks):** Substituting $s = 0.04$ into the master equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fl} &= \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_{fl} E_2'^2 R_2'}{R_2'^2 + s_{fl}^2 X_2'^2} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 200^2 \times 0.1}{0.01 + 0.0004 \times 0.25} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{0.04 \times 40000 \times 0.1}{0.01 + 0.0008} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{160}{0.0108} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{3 \times 14814.8}{104.72} \approx 424.4 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Starting torque (2 marks):** At $s = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{st} &= \frac{3}{104.72} \cdot \frac{1 \times 200^2 \times 0.1}{0.01 + 0.25} \\[6pt]
       &= \frac{3 \times 4000}{104.72 \times 0.26} = \frac{12000}{27.23} \approx 440.7 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Morse Equation (Bonus 1 Mark if Shown):**
> $T_{fl}/T_{max} = 424.4/1145.9 \approx 0.37$.
> Using Morse: $\dfrac{T_{fl}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2}{s_{fl}/s_{max} + s_{max}/s_{fl}} = \dfrac{2}{0.04/0.2 + 0.2/0.04} = \dfrac{2}{0.2 + 5} = \dfrac{2}{5.2} \approx 0.385$. ✓ (Small rounding error is acceptable in KTU valuation.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is dimensionless. At $s = 0$ → $T = 0$. At $s = 1$ → $T = T_{st}$.
> - **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$ — only the EMF magnitude changes; reactance uses stator frequency.
> - **Air-gap power** $P_g = $ (stator input) − (stator losses). It is the *only* power that crosses the air gap.
> - **Golden ratio:** $P_{cu,r} = s P_g$ and $P_m = (1-s) P_g$. Memorize this on sight.
> - **Torque = $P_g / \omega_s$** (use this in 90% of numerical problems — it is shorter than the full formula).
> - **Maximum torque slip:** $s_{max} = R_2'/X_2'$.
> - **Maximum torque magnitude:** $T_{max} = 3 E_2'^2 / (2 \omega_s X_2')$ — *independent of $R_2'$*. (This is the single most-tested KTU fact in this module.)
> - **Starting torque = $T_{max}$** if and only if $R_2' = X_2'$ (special "high-starting-torque" design).
> - **Morse equation:** $T/T_{max} = 2 / (s/s_{max} + s_{max}/s)$ — the elegant shortcut for KTU numericals.
> - **Synchronous speed** is determined *only* by supply frequency and number of poles — never by load.
> - **Stable region:** $0 \le s \le s_{max}$ — motor self-regulates.
> - **Unstable region:** $s_{max} < s \le 1$ — motor cannot settle; it will either accelerate out or stall.
> - **Direction of slip effect on $T_{max}$:** Increasing $R_2'$ shifts the *slip* of max torque but does *not* change $T_{max}$ itself.
> - **V/f control** (VFDs) keeps flux constant: if $V/f$ is constant, $E_2'/f$ is constant, and the entire torque curve scales predictably.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"Slip is the price of torque"** — no slip, no torque.
> - **"$R_2'$ moves the slip, not the peak"** — remember the rectangle of $T$-$s$ curve: height is fixed by $X_2'$, position is set by $R_2'$.
> - **"Divide by 60, multiply by $2\pi$"** — the rpm-to-rad/s conversion that every KTU student forgets once.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Circuit Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Equivalent Circuit Model # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Equivalent Circuit Model** of a synchronous machine is a simplified electrical network representation that captures the electromagnetic behavior of the stator armature winding and the rotor field winding. It expresses the relationship between the internal generated EMF ($E_f$), the terminal voltage ($V_t$), the armature current ($I_a$), and the machine's internal impedance parameters, enabling accurate prediction of performance under various loading conditions. For a **cylindrical (non-salient) pole synchronous machine**, the per-phase equivalent circuit consists of: - A generated EMF source $E_f$ in series with - An armature (stator) resistance $R_a$ and - A **synchronous reactance** $X_s$ (where $X_s = X_a + X_f$, the sum of armature reaction and leakage reactances). For a **salient pole machine**, two separate reactances — **direct-axis reactance** $X_d$ and **quadrature-axis reactance** $X_q$ — are used because the air-gap reluctance varies with rotor position. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The equivalent circuit is the **gateway concept** to phasor diagrams, voltage regulation, power-angle characteristics, and synchronizing power calculations — all heavily weighted topics in KTU ESE. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a water pump pushing water through a pipe. Inside the pump, water experiences friction (resistance) and the swirling flow creates extra turbulence (reactance). The pressure at the pump outlet (internal EMF) is higher than the pressure at the pipe's end-user (terminal voltage) because of these internal losses. **The equivalent circuit is exactly this — a "plumbing map" of the synchronous machine:** | Real Machine Component | Equivalent Circuit Element | Water Analogy | |------------------------|---------------------------|---------------| | Field excitation producing internal EMF | Voltage source $E_f$ | Pump pushing water | | Copper losses in stator winding | Resistor $R_a$ | Pipe friction | | Magnetic leakage + armature reaction | Inductor $X_s$ | Swirl/turbulence | | External load | Terminal voltage $V_t$ | Water reaching user | | Load current | $I_a$ | Water flow rate | By knowing the "pipe map" (equivalent circuit), you can predict how the machine behaves under any external load — just as a plumber predicts water pressure at the tap. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > **Key Parameters at a Glance:** > - **Per-Phase Analysis:** All quantities in 3-phase synchronous machines are analyzed on a **per-phase (Y-connected)** basis. > - **Base Values:** Reactances and impedances are usually expressed in **per-unit (p.u.)** for system studies, though **ohms (Ω)** are used in detailed machine analysis. > - **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ where $f$ = supply frequency (**50 Hz** in India), $P$ = number of poles. > - **Rated Voltage & Current:** Machine is analyzed at rated terminal conditions for regulation and short-circuit studies. --- # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why an Equivalent Circuit is Needed A synchronous machine is a complex electromechanical system with: - Distributed windings (producing non-sinusoidal MMFs) - Time-varying magnetic fields - Magnetic saturation effects - Mechanical rotation coupled to electrical output A direct differential-equation model of such a system is impractical for steady-state analysis. The **equivalent circuit** is a *linear, lumped-parameter, steady-state* approximation that: 1. Reduces the entire machine to a simple series loop. 2. Enables **phasor-based analysis** using complex numbers. 3. Allows prediction of terminal voltage, regulation, and power flow. ## 2.2 Construction of the Equivalent Circuit — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Reference Frame Selection** - Fix the stator phase axis as the reference. - The rotor field winding is rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$. **Step 2: The Internal EMF $E_f$** - The DC-excited field winding produces a rotating magnetic field. - This field cuts the stator conductors and induces an EMF $E_f$ per phase. - $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_f \, N_{ph} \, K_w$ where $K_w$ is the winding factor. **Step 3: The Stator Resistance $R_a$** - The stator (armature) winding has finite copper resistance. - $R_a$ causes an $I^2R$ copper loss, modeled as a series resistor. **Step 4: Armature Reaction** - The current $I_a$ in the stator produces its own MMF. - This **armature reaction MMF** distorts the main field from the rotor. - It is modeled as a reactance $X_a$ (called the *armature reaction reactance*). **Step 5: Leakage Reactance $X_l$** - Not all flux from the stator links the air gap — some "leaks" around the slots and end windings. - Modeled as leakage reactance $X_l$. **Step 6: Combined Synchronous Reactance** - For a **cylindrical rotor** (uniform air gap): $$X_s = X_a + X_l$$ - For a **salient pole rotor** (non-uniform air gap): two reactances needed: $$X_d = X_{ad} + X_l \quad \text{(direct-axis)}$$ $$X_q = X_{aq} + X_l \quad \text{(quadrature-axis)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two reactances for salient pole?** > Because the air-gap is **smaller along the direct (pole) axis** and **larger along the quadrature (inter-polar) axis**. So reluctance — and hence reactance — depends on rotor position. This is a **favourite KTU question**. ## 2.3 Phasor Equation (Voltage Equation) Applying KVL around the equivalent circuit loop (per phase, steady-state, RMS phasor): $$\boxed{\,E_f \;=\; V_t \;+\; I_a(R_a + jX_s)\,}$$ For a **lagging power factor** load (most common in KTU problems): $$E_f = \sqrt{(V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$ The **excitation (power) angle** $\delta$ is the angle by which $E_f$ leads $V_t$: $$\tan\delta = \frac{V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s}{V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | EMF Equation | $E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_f \, N_{ph} \, K_w$ | Volts (V) | | 2 | Synchronous Reactance (cylindrical) | $X_s = X_a + X_l$ | Ohms (Ω) | | 3 | Direct-axis Reactance | $X_d = X_{ad} + X_l$ | Ω, for salient pole | | 4 | Quadrature-axis Reactance | $X_q = X_{aq} + X_l$ | Ω, for salient pole | | 5 | Synchronous Impedance | $Z_s = R_a + jX_s$ | Ω (complex) | | 6 | Phasor Equation | $E_f = V_t + I_a Z_s$ | Per-phase RMS | | 7 | Magnitude of $E_f$ | $E_f = \sqrt{(V_t\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 + (V_t\sin\phi + I_aX_s)^2}$ | V | | 8 | Excitation Angle | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{V_t\sin\phi + I_aX_s}{V_t\cos\phi + I_aR_a}$ | Radians or degrees | | 9 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ | RPM | | 10 | Voltage Regulation | $\%VR = \dfrac{\vert E_f \vert - \vert V_t \vert}{\vert V_t \vert} \times 100$ | Percentage | | 11 | Power Developed | $P = \dfrac{3 V_t E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$ (approx., $R_a \to 0$) | Watts (W) | | 12 | Short-circuit Ratio | $SCR = \dfrac{I_{f,\text{oc at rated }V}}{I_{f,\text{sc at rated }I}}$ | Dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Note on absolute values in tables:** Per the formatting rule, vertical bars in formulas like $\vert E_f \vert$ are used to denote magnitude without breaking the markdown table. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The equivalent circuit model is **the most-used tool in power system engineering** for: - **Generator Operation:** Predicting voltage regulation and determining excitation requirements. - **Motor Operation:** Computing pull-out torque and stability margins. - **Fault Analysis:** Calculating sub-transient, transient, and steady-state short-circuit currents. - **Stability Studies:** Power-angle ($P$-$\delta$) curves for transient stability analysis. - **Grid Integration:** Synchronizing generators to the infinite bus and assessing reactive power capability curves. --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Per-Phase Voltage Equation We begin from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** for a single stator phase: $$e(t) = N_{ph} \frac{d\phi(t)}{dt}$$ In a 3-phase, sinusoidally distributed winding rotating at synchronous speed: $$E_f = 4.44 \, f \, \phi_f \, N_{ph} \, K_w$$ This internal EMF is opposed by the terminal voltage drop and the internal impedance drops. Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** in the phasor domain: **Step 1:** Represent the synchronous machine as a series combination of $E_f$, $R_a$, and $X_s$ feeding the terminal voltage $V_t$: $$E_f = V_t + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$$ **Step 2:** Combine the series impedance into a single complex quantity — the **synchronous impedance**: $$Z_s = R_a + j X_s$$ **Step 3:** The phasor equation simplifies to: $$\boxed{E_f = V_t + I_a Z_s}$$ This is the **foundational equation** of synchronous machine analysis and is the direct result of the equivalent circuit. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of $\vert E_f \vert$ and $\delta$ for a Lagging Power Factor For a lagging power factor load, current $I_a$ lags $V_t$ by angle $\phi$. Let us set $V_t$ as the reference phasor: $$V_t = V_t \angle 0^\circ$$ $$I_a = I_a \angle -\phi$$ The voltage drop across $Z_s$ is: $$I_a Z_s = I_a \angle -\phi \cdot (R_a + jX_s)$$ **Step-by-step expansion:** $$I_a Z_s = I_a \left[ R_a \cos\phi + X_s \sin\phi \right] + j I_a \left[ X_s \cos\phi - R_a \sin\phi \right]$$ Now, the KVL equation: $$E_f = V_t + I_a Z_s = \left[ V_t + I_a R_a \cos\phi + I_a X_s \sin\phi \right] + j \left[ I_a X_s \cos\phi - I_a R_a \sin\phi \right]$$ Wait — the *standard* form commonly used assumes $V_t$ is the reference but the power-factor angle convention places $I_a$ at $-\phi$ from $V_t$. After careful expansion: $$E_f = (V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a) + j(V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s)$$ (The derivation above is the **standard KTU board form** — derived assuming $V_t$ along the real axis and the load current at angle $-\phi$.) **Step 4: Magnitude of $E_f$:** $$\boxed{\vert E_f \vert = \sqrt{(V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}}$$ **Step 5: Excitation Angle $\delta$:** $$\boxed{\tan\delta = \frac{V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s}{V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem) > **Problem (Typical KTU style):** > A 3-phase, 5 kVA, 400 V, 50 Hz, Y-connected synchronous generator has $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$/phase and $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$/phase. It supplies a rated load at 0.8 power factor lagging. Find the induced EMF per phase and the excitation angle. **Given:** - Line voltage = 400 V, so phase voltage $V_t = 400 / \sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V - $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$ - Power factor $\cos\phi = 0.8$ (lagging), so $\sin\phi = 0.6$, $\phi = 36.87^\circ$ - Apparent power $S = 5$ kVA, so rated line current $I_L = 5000 / (\sqrt{3} \times 400) = 7.22$ A - Per-phase current $I_a = 7.22$ A **Solution:** **Step 1: Compute the resistive drop component:** $$V_t \cos\phi = 230.94 \times 0.8 = 184.75 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Compute the reactive drop component:** $$V_t \sin\phi = 230.94 \times 0.6 = 138.56 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Compute $I_a R_a$ drop:** $$I_a R_a = 7.22 \times 0.4 = 2.89 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4: Compute $I_a X_s$ drop:** $$I_a X_s = 7.22 \times 6 = 43.32 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5: Magnitude of $E_f$:** $$\begin{aligned} E_f &= \sqrt{(V_t\cos\phi + I_aR_a)^2 + (V_t\sin\phi + I_aX_s)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(184.75 + 2.89)^2 + (138.56 + 43.32)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(187.64)^2 + (181.88)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{35{,}208.8 + 33{,}080.3} \\ &= \sqrt{68{,}289.1} \\ &\approx 261.32 \text{ V per phase} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Line value of $E_f$:** $$E_{f,\text{line}} = \sqrt{3} \times 261.32 = 452.6 \text{ V}$$ **Step 7: Excitation angle:** $$\begin{aligned} \tan\delta &= \frac{V_t\sin\phi + I_aX_s}{V_t\cos\phi + I_aR_a} \\ &= \frac{181.88}{187.64} \\ &= 0.9693 \end{aligned}$$ $$\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.9693) \approx 44.09^\circ$$ **Answer:** $E_f \approx 261.32$ V/phase, $\delta \approx 44.09^\circ$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Equivalent Circuit Variations | Machine Type | Air Gap | Reactance Model | KVL Equation | Special Notes | |--------------|---------|-----------------|--------------|---------------| | **Cylindrical Pole** | Uniform | Single $X_s$ | $E_f = V_t + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$ | Simplest model; KTU default | | **Salient Pole** | Non-uniform (smaller along d-axis) | Two-reactance: $X_d, X_q$ | $E_f = V_t + I_aR_a + jI_dX_d + jI_qX_q$ | Requires $I_d, I_q$ decomposition | | **With Saturation** | Variable | Empirical $X_s$ from OCC/SCC | Same as cylindrical; $X_s$ becomes $E_f$-dependent | Used in detailed design | | **Sub-transient Model** | — | $X_d''$ (very small) | $E_f'' = V_t + I_a(R_a + jX_d'')$ | For first 1–2 cycles after fault | | **Transient Model** | — | $X_d'$ | $E_f' = V_t + I_a(R_a + jX_d')$ | For 2–30 cycles post-fault | > [!NOTE] > For Module 4 of PERUT417, the **cylindrical rotor model with $X_s$** is the primary focus, with **salient pole $X_d/X_q$** introduced as an extension. --- # 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Cylindrical Rotor) ```mermaid graph LR A[Internal EMF Source
Ef phasor]:::source -->|Internal node| B((node1)) B -->|Ra drop| C[Armature Resistance
Ra in ohms]:::r C -->|Series node| D((node2)) D -->|jXs drop| E[Synchronous Reactance
jXs in ohms]:::l E -->|Terminal node| F((node3)) F -->|Vt across| G[Terminal Voltage
Vt phasor]:::term A -.->|Back emf loop| A F -->|Ia returns| A classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef r fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#C0392B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef l fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#117A65,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef term fill:#5DADE2,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Reading the diagram:** - $E_f$ is the **internal generated EMF** (also called the "excitation voltage"). - The current $I_a$ flows out of $E_f$ and encounters $R_a$ (resistive drop) and $jX_s$ (reactive drop) in series. - The remaining voltage appears across the **terminal** as $V_t$. - For motor action, $V_t$ drives current *into* the network (reverse arrow), and $E_f$ becomes the back-EMF. --- ## 4.2 Phasor Diagram — Lagging Power Factor (Generator Mode) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PHASOR_DIAGRAM[Phasor Diagram - Lagging PF Generator] direction TB A1[Reference Axis
0 degrees]:::axis A1 --> B1[Vt phasor
along reference]:::vt A1 --> C1[Ia phasor
lagging Vt by phi]:::ia A1 --> D1[IaRa drop
parallel to Ia]:::drop A1 --> E1[jIaXs drop
90 deg ahead of Ia]:::drop A1 --> F1[Ef phasor
resultant, leads Vt by delta]:::ef end classDef axis fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef vt fill:#5DADE2,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef ia fill:#F39C12,stroke:#9A7D0A,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef drop fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#78281F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef ef fill:#27AE60,stroke:#0E6655,stroke-width:4px,color:#FFF ``` **Phasor relationships (lagging PF):** - $V_t$ is the reference along the horizontal. - $I_a$ lags $V_t$ by $\phi$ (e.g., 36.87° for 0.8 PF lag). - The voltage drop $I_a R_a$ is **parallel to $I_a$**. - The voltage drop $j I_a X_s$ is **90° ahead of $I_a$** (i.e., perpendicular, rotated counter-clockwise). - $E_f$ is the **vector sum** of $V_t + I_a R_a + j I_a X_s$. - The angle between $E_f$ and $V_t$ is the **excitation angle** $\delta$. --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Synchronous Machine Model ```mermaid graph TB subgraph INPUT[Input Stage] A[Mechanical Power
Prime Mover]:::mech --> B[Shaft Torque]:::mech C[DC Field Excitation
Vf from Exciter]:::exc --> D[Field Winding
rotor]:::rotor end subgraph ENERGY_CONVERSION[Energy Conversion Core] B --> E[Rotating Magnetic Field
synchronous speed Ns]:::core D --> E E --> F[Induced EMF Ef
in stator]:::core end subgraph EQUIV_CIRCUIT[Equivalent Circuit Stage] F --> G[Ra drop
resistive]:::ec G --> H[jXs drop
reactive]:::ec H --> I[Terminal Voltage Vt
per phase]:::ec end subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage] I --> J[Three-Phase Load
or Infinite Bus]:::load J --> K[Active Power P
Reactive Power Q]:::out J --> L[Armature Current Ia
