feedback]:::fb end L -.-> G classDef mech fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#2874A6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef exc fill:#F8C471,stroke:#B9770E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rotor fill:#D7BDE2,stroke:#6C3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef core fill:#F1948A,stroke:#922B21,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef ec fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#196F3D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#F7DC6F,stroke:#9A7D0A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#BB8FCE,stroke:#5B2C6F,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef fb fill:#AEB6BF,stroke:#566573,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Interpretation of the block diagram:** - **Input stage:** Mechanical power from the prime mover and DC excitation are the two energy inputs. - **Energy conversion:** The rotating magnetic field cuts the stator conductors, inducing EMF $E_f$. - **Equivalent circuit stage:** Internal drops $I_a R_a$ and $j I_a X_s$ are subtracted from $E_f$ to give the terminal voltage. - **Output stage:** Power flows to the load; the load current $I_a$ is fed back into the equivalent circuit. --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define synchronous reactance of a synchronous machine. How is it modeled in the equivalent circuit?** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Model Answer:** Synchronous reactance $X_s$ is an **imaginary reactance** used to model the combined effect of (i) armature reaction reactance $X_a$ and (ii) stator leakage reactance $X_l$ in a synchronous machine. $$X_s = X_a + X_l$$ In the per-phase equivalent circuit, $X_s$ is represented as a **series inductive element** in series with the armature resistance $R_a$ and the internal EMF source $E_f$. It accounts for the voltage drop that occurs due to the magnetizing effect of the armature current and the leakage flux. > **Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition (1M), Formula (1M), Equivalent circuit placement (1M). --- ### **Q2. Why are two reactances $X_d$ and $X_q$ used for salient pole machines instead of a single $X_s$?** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Answer:** In salient pole machines, the **air gap is non-uniform** — it is smaller (low reluctance) along the **direct axis (d-axis)** passing through the pole center, and larger (high reluctance) along the **quadrature axis (q-axis)** between the poles. Since reactance is inversely proportional to permeance: $$X_d = X_{ad} + X_l \quad (\text{high, as air gap is small})$$ $$X_q = X_{aq} + X_l \quad (\text{low, as air gap is large})$$ Therefore, a single $X_s$ cannot represent the rotor's behavior at all positions, and the stator current must be decomposed into **direct-axis component $I_d$** and **quadrature-axis component $I_q$** for accurate modeling. > **Marks Distribution:** Reason (1M), Formulas (1M), Justification (1M). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake #1:** Using **line values** in formulas that demand **per-phase values** (e.g., $V_t = V_L/\sqrt{3}$). This single error costs **3–4 marks**. > - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting that $V_t \sin\phi$ (not $V_t \cos\phi$) is paired with $I_a X_s$ in the phasor equation for **lagging PF**. Mix-up here = 0 marks for the derivation. > - **Common Mistake #3:** Conflating $\phi$ (load power-factor angle) with $\delta$ (excitation angle). These are **fundamentally different** angles — $\phi$ is between $V_t$ and $I_a$; $\delta$ is between $E_f$ and $V_t$. > - **Common Mistake #4:** Skipping units in the final answer. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for missing or inconsistent units. > - **Common Mistake #5:** Not stating the assumption $R_a \ll X_s$ when using the simplified power equation $P = (3V_t E_f / X_s) \sin\delta$. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`** A 3-phase, 10 kVA, 415 V, 50 Hz, star-connected synchronous generator has armature resistance of $0.5 \, \Omega$/phase and synchronous reactance of $5 \, \Omega$/phase. Compute the open-circuit voltage per phase and the excitation angle when it delivers full load at: **(a)** Unity power factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** 0.8 power factor lagging. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $S = 10$ kVA, $V_L = 415$ V, $f = 50$ Hz - $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$/phase, $X_s = 5 \, \Omega$/phase - Phase voltage: $V_t = 415 / \sqrt{3} = 239.6$ V - Rated line current: $I_L = 10000 / (\sqrt{3} \times 415) = 13.91$ A - Per-phase current: $I_a = 13.91$ A --- **Part (a): Unity Power Factor ($\cos\phi = 1.0$, $\sin\phi = 0$)** **Step 1:** Compute the real-axis component: $$V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a = 239.6 \times 1.0 + 13.91 \times 0.5 = 239.6 + 6.955 = 246.555 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the imaginary-axis component: $$V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s = 239.6 \times 0 + 13.91 \times 5 = 69.55 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $|E_f|$: $$\begin{aligned} E_f &= \sqrt{(246.555)^2 + (69.55)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{60{,}789.4 + 4{,}837.2} \\ &= \sqrt{65{,}626.6} \\ &\approx 256.18 \text{ V/phase} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Excitation angle: $$\tan\delta = \frac{69.55}{246.555} = 0.2821 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \approx 15.74^\circ$$ **Answer (a):** $E_f \approx 256.18$ V/phase, $\delta \approx 15.74^\circ$. --- **Part (b): 0.8 Power Factor Lagging ($\cos\phi = 0.8$, $\sin\phi = 0.6$)** **Step 1:** Real-axis component: $$V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a = 239.6 \times 0.8 + 13.91 \times 0.5 = 191.68 + 6.955 = 198.635 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Imaginary-axis component: $$V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s = 239.6 \times 0.6 + 13.91 \times 5 = 143.76 + 69.55 = 213.31 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $|E_f|$: $$\begin{aligned} E_f &= \sqrt{(198.635)^2 + (213.31)^2} \\ &= \sqrt{39{,}455.8 + 45{,}501.2} \\ &= \sqrt{84{,}957.0} \\ &\approx 291.47 \text{ V/phase} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Excitation angle: $$\tan\delta = \frac{213.31}{198.635} = 1.0738 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \approx 47.0^\circ$$ **Answer (b):** $E_f \approx 291.47$ V/phase, $\delta \approx 47.0^\circ$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`** **(a)** Draw the per-phase equivalent circuit of a cylindrical rotor synchronous machine. Explain the significance of each element. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3-phase, 1500 kVA, 6.6 kV, star-connected synchronous generator has $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$ and $X_s = 4.5 \, \Omega$ per phase. Find the percentage voltage regulation at full load 0.85 power factor lagging. Also find the load angle $\delta$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Equivalent Circuit Diagram and Element Significance** The per-phase equivalent circuit of a cylindrical rotor synchronous machine is: ``` +-----[ Ra ]-----[ jXs ]-----+ | | ( Ef ) | | | +------------- Vt ------------+ ``` | Element | Symbol | Significance | |---------|--------|--------------| | Internal EMF source | $E_f$ | EMF induced in the stator by the rotating DC-excited field. | | Armature resistance | $R_a$ | Models the stator copper (ohmic) losses. | | Synchronous reactance | $X_s$ | Models the combined effect of armature reaction and stator leakage flux. | | Terminal voltage | $V_t$ | The voltage delivered to the external load per phase. | **Key equation:** $E_f = V_t + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$ --- **Part (b): Numerical Computation** **Given:** - $S = 1500$ kVA, $V_L = 6.6$ kV - $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $X_s = 4.5 \, \Omega$/phase - Power factor = 0.85 lagging → $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $\sin\phi = 0.5268$, $\phi = 31.79^\circ$ **Step 1:** Per-phase voltage: $$V_t = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Full-load current: $$I_a = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = 131.22 \text{ A}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the drops: - $I_a R_a = 131.22 \times 0.3 = 39.37$ V - $I_a X_s = 131.22 \times 4.5 = 590.5$ V **Step 4:** Components for the phasor magnitude: - Real: $V_t \cos\phi + I_a R_a = 3810.5 \times 0.85 + 39.37 = 3238.9 + 39.37 = 3278.3$ V - Imaginary: $V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s = 3810.5 \times 0.5268 + 590.5 = 2007.4 + 590.5 = 2597.9$ V **Step 5:** Induced EMF: $$E_f = \sqrt{(3278.3)^2 + (2597.9)^2} = \sqrt{10{,}747{,}256 + 6{,}749{,}071} = \sqrt{17{,}496{,}327} \approx 4183.0 \text{ V/phase}$$ **Step 6:** Percentage Voltage Regulation: $$\%VR = \frac{E_f - V_t}{V_t} \times 100 = \frac{4183.0 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100 = \frac{372.5}{3810.5} \times 100 \approx 9.78\%$$ **Step 7:** Load (excitation) angle: $$\tan\delta = \frac{2597.9}{3278.3} = 0.7925 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta \approx 38.4^\circ$$ **Answer (b):** Voltage regulation $\approx 9.78\%$, Load angle $\delta \approx 38.4^\circ$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Equivalent Circuit Model** - ✅ The **per-phase equivalent circuit** is always drawn for a **Y-connected** stator winding. - ✅ **Phasor equation:** $E_f = V_t + I_a(R_a + jX_s)$ — memorize this as the **first line** of any answer. - ✅ **$X_s = X_a + X_l$** for cylindrical rotor; **$X_d$ and $X_q$** for salient pole. - ✅ For **lagging PF loads**, the imaginary part is **$V_t \sin\phi + I_a X_s$**; for **leading PF**, it becomes **$V_t \sin\phi - I_a X_s$**. - ✅ The **excitation angle** $\delta$ is between $E_f$ and $V_t$ — *not* between $V_t$ and $I_a$ (that's $\phi$). - ✅ **Voltage regulation** is always positive for **lagging and unity PF** loads; it can be **negative** for highly **leading PF** loads. - ✅ The **approximate formula** $P = (3 V_t E_f / X_s) \sin\delta$ is valid **only when $R_a \to 0$** — state this assumption. - ✅ Always convert **line voltage to phase voltage** by dividing by $\sqrt{3}$ before applying the per-phase equation. - ✅ The **phasor diagram** for a generator has $E_f$ leading $V_t$ by $\delta$; for a motor, $V_t$ leads $E_f$. - ✅ **$SCR = 1 / X_{s,\text{p.u.}}$** — a critical relation in design problems. - ✅ Maximum power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90^\circ$ (cylindrical rotor, simplified model) — beyond this, the machine **loses stability**. ---

topics.title: Torque-Speed Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torque-Speed Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque-Speed Characteristic** is the graphical and mathematical relationship between the electromagnetic (developed) torque $T_e$ produced by an electrical machine (motor) and its rotational speed $N$ (or angular velocity $\omega$) under steady-state operating conditions, while all other input parameters (terminal voltage, field current, armature current) are held constant.

For a motor, the characteristic is fundamentally defined by the **mechanical equation of motion**:

$$T_e = T_L + J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$$

where:
- $T_e$ = Electromagnetic (developed) torque in **N·m**
- $T_L$ = Load torque (shaft torque) in **N·m**
- $J$ = Moment of inertia in **kg·m²**
- $B$ = Viscous friction coefficient in **N·m·s/rad**
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity in **rad/s**

In steady state ($\frac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$) and neglecting friction ($B = 0$):

$$T_e = T_L = T_{shaft}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to identify the **shape, slope, and intercepts** of the T–N curve for **DC Shunt, DC Series, DC Compound (Cumulative & Differential)**, and **3-Phase Induction Motors** in Module 4.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **riding a bicycle** on a flat road:

- When you start pedaling from rest, the bike needs a **huge push** (high torque) to overcome inertia and get moving. This is the **starting torque** region.
- As the bicycle gains speed, your legs no longer need to push as hard — the **torque demand drops**, but the **speed increases**.
- This inverse relationship between *effort (torque)* and *speed* is exactly what a **DC Series Motor** does — it gives massive starting torque and slows down under heavy loads, but speeds up dangerously when unloaded.
- A **DC Shunt Motor**, in contrast, behaves like a car on cruise control — it maintains nearly the same speed whether going uphill or downhill, because its torque-speed curve is almost **flat (constant speed)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** The **fan regulator** in your house works on this principle. As you turn the knob, the **voltage** changes, which shifts the entire torque-speed curve, allowing you to control fan speed (and hence torque delivery) precisely.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Torque unit:** **N·m** (Newton-meter) — S.I. standard
- **Speed unit:** **rpm** (revolutions per minute) in industry, **rad/s** in SI
- **Power relation:** $P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (in Watts when $N$ is in rpm and $T$ in N·m)
- **Flux unit:** **Wb** (Weber) for DC machines, **Wb/turn** or **line integral** for AC machines
- **Standard base speed reference:** $N_0$ = no-load speed; $N_{fl}$ = full-load speed

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Governing Equations of a DC Motor

The torque-speed relationship is derived from two fundamental DC machine equations:

#### 2.1.1 EMF Equation (Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction)

$$E_b = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 \, A}$$

where:
- $E_b$ = Back-EMF in **Volts**
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole in **Wb**
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed in **rpm**
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in armature winding

#### 2.1.2 Torque Equation (Lorentz Force Principle)

$$T_e = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2\pi A}$$

where:
- $T_e$ = Electromagnetic torque in **N·m**
- $I_a$ = Armature current in **Amperes**

#### 2.1.3 Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### 2.2 Systematic Derivation of Torque-Speed Relationship

**Step 1 — Express Back-EMF in terms of speed:**

From EMF equation: $E_b = k_1 \Phi N$ where $k_1 = \frac{PZ}{60A}$ (machine constant)

**Step 2 — Express Torque in terms of armature current:**

From torque equation: $T_e = k_2 \Phi I_a$ where $k_2 = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A}$ (machine constant)

**Step 3 — Eliminate $I_a$ and $E_b$ using voltage equation:**

$$V = k_1 \Phi N + \frac{T_e R_a}{k_2 \Phi}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$ (Speed):**

$$N = \frac{V}{k_1 \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2} T_e$$

This is the **general linear torque-speed equation** valid for **all DC motors**. The difference between motor types lies entirely in **how $\Phi$ depends on $I_a$**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table thoroughly — it is the single most-tested content in Module 4 examinations.

| Motor Type | Flux Equation | Torque Equation | Speed Equation | No-Load Speed | Characteristic Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Shunt** | $\Phi = \text{const}$ | $T \propto I_a$ | $N = N_0 - k T$ | Finite ($V/k_1\Phi$) | **Drooping straight line** |
| **DC Series** | $\Phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated) | $T \propto I_a^2$ | $N \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ | Infinity (ideal) | **Inverse square-root curve (rectangular hyperbola)** |
| **DC Compound (Cumulative)** | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}(I_a)$ | Between shunt & series | Between both | Finite, slightly higher than shunt | **Drooping curve** |
| **DC Compound (Differential)** | $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}(I_a)$ | Highly non-linear | May rise with load | Finite | **Rising curve (unstable)** |
| **3-Phase Induction** | $\Phi \approx \text{const}$ (air-gap flux) | $T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$ | $N = N_s(1-s)$ | Synchronous speed $N_s$ | **Parabolic with pull-out torque** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical relationship to remember:** $\frac{k_1}{k_2} = \frac{2\pi}{60}$, so the constants in EMF and torque equations are interconvertible: $E_b = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot \frac{T}{I_a} = \omega \cdot \frac{T}{I_a}$

### 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

- **Selection of motor for a load:** The torque-speed curve of the motor must intersect the load curve at the desired operating point with **stable equilibrium**.
- **Speed regulation analysis:** $\% \text{Reg} = \frac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100$ — critical for KTU numerical problems.
- **Stability criterion:** $\frac{dT_m}{dN} < \frac{dT_L}{dN}$ for stable operation.
- **Industrial applications:**
  - **Shunt motor** → Lathes, centrifugal pumps, blowers, printing machines (constant speed)
  - **Series motor** → Cranes, hoists, electric traction, hair dryers (high starting torque)
  - **Cumulative compound** → Rolling mills, elevators, punches (medium starting torque with finite no-load speed)
  - **Induction motor** → Most industrial drives, fans, compressors

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque-Speed Characteristic of DC Shunt Motor

**Given:** Field winding is connected in parallel with the armature, with a constant field current $I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}}$ (assuming constant supply voltage).

**Step 1:** Since the field winding is supplied with a constant voltage across a practically constant resistance:

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, neglecting armature reaction:

$$\Phi = \text{constant} = \Phi_{sh}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting constant $\Phi$ into the general equation:

$$N = \frac{V}{k_1 \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2} T_e$$

**Step 4:** Let us define the **no-load speed** as the intercept (when $T_e = 0$):

$$N_0 = \frac{V}{k_1 \Phi}$$

**Step 5:** Let us define the **slope** of the straight line:

$$N = N_0 - \left(\frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2}\right) T_e$$

**Step 6:** Converting to angular speed using $N = \frac{60\omega}{2\pi}$:

$$\omega = \frac{V}{k_1' \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_1'^2 \Phi^2} T_e \quad \text{where } k_1' = \frac{PZ}{2\pi A}$$

**Step 7:** The torque-speed curve is a **straight line with negative slope** — the signature of the DC shunt motor.

> [!NOTE]
> Since $R_a$ is small, the slope is small — the curve is **nearly flat**, which is why shunt motors are known as **"constant speed motors."**

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque-Speed Characteristic of DC Series Motor

**Given:** Field winding is connected in series with the armature, so the same current flows through both:

$$I_f = I_a = I_s$$

**Step 1:** Before magnetic saturation, flux is directly proportional to field current:

$$\Phi = k_f I_a \quad \text{(linear region)}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting into the torque equation:

$$T_e = k_2 \Phi I_a = k_2 (k_f I_a) I_a = k_2 k_f I_a^2$$

Therefore:

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T_e}{k_2 k_f}}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting into the voltage equation:

$$V = k_1 \Phi N + I_a R_a = k_1 (k_f I_a) N + I_a R_a$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $N$:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_1 k_f I_a}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $I_a$ from Step 2:

$$N = \frac{V - R_a \sqrt{\frac{T_e}{k_2 k_f}}}{k_1 k_f \sqrt{\frac{T_e}{k_2 k_f}}}$$

**Step 6:** Simplifying (let $K = k_1 k_f \sqrt{k_2 k_f}$):

$$N = \frac{V}{K \sqrt{T_e}} - \frac{R_a}{K}$$

**Step 7:** At no-load ($T_e \to 0$):

$$N_0 \to \infty \quad \text{(ideal case)}$$

In practice, residual flux $\Phi_r$ limits this to:

$$N_{0,\text{actual}} = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_1 \Phi_r}$$

**Step 8:** The curve is an **inverse square-root function (rectangular hyperbola)** — the signature of a DC series motor.

> [!WARNING]
> The series motor must **never be run at no-load** — it will mechanically self-destruct due to runaway speed. This is why series motors are **mechanically coupled** to their loads (e.g., in traction motors).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque-Speed Characteristic of 3-Phase Induction Motor

**Starting equation (rotor power flow):**

The electromagnetic torque in an induction motor is given by:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$$

where:
- $V_1$ = Stator phase voltage
- $R_2$ = Rotor resistance referred to stator
- $X_2$ = Rotor leakage reactance referred to stator
- $s$ = Slip $= \frac{N_s - N}{N_s}$
- $\omega_s$ = Synchronous angular velocity $= \frac{2\pi N_s}{60}$

**Step 1:** Define slip in terms of speed:

$$N = N_s (1 - s)$$

**Step 2:** Express torque as a function of $s$ (the standard form):

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s V_1^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum (Pull-out) Torque:** Differentiate with respect to $s$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \implies s_m = \frac{R_2}{X_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $s_m$ back into the torque equation:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **$T_{max}$ is independent of $R_2$** — it depends only on supply voltage and rotor reactance. However, the **slip at which it occurs** depends on $R_2$.

**Step 5 — Starting Torque** (at $s = 1$):

$$T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Step 6 — Full-load Torque** at slip $s_{fl}$:

$$T_{fl} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s_{fl} V_1^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_{fl} X_2)^2}$$

**Step 7:** Since $s_{fl}$ is small (typically 2–5%), $R_2^2 \gg (s_{fl} X_2)^2$, so the **operating region is approximately linear**:

$$T_e \approx \frac{3 s V_1^2}{\omega_s R_2} \implies s \propto T_e$$

**Step 8:** Therefore, in the normal operating region:

$$N = N_s(1 - s) = N_s - N_s \cdot \frac{R_2 \omega_s T_e}{3 V_1^2}$$

This is a **linear, gently drooping straight line**, very similar to a DC shunt motor.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Speed Regulation of DC Shunt Motor

**Step 1:** From the torque-speed equation:

$$N_0 = \frac{V}{k_1 \Phi}, \quad N_{fl} = N_0 - \left(\frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2}\right) T_{fl}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $T_{fl} = k_2 \Phi I_{a,fl}$:

$$N_{fl} = \frac{V}{k_1 \Phi} - \frac{R_a I_{a,fl}}{k_1 \Phi} = \frac{V - I_{a,fl} R_a}{k_1 \Phi} = \frac{E_{b,fl}}{k_1 \Phi}$$

**Step 3:** Percentage speed regulation:

$$\% \text{Reg} = \frac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100 = \frac{I_{a,fl} R_a}{V - I_{a,fl} R_a} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant result shows that the speed regulation of a DC shunt motor depends only on **$I_a R_a$ drop** and the supply voltage. Typically 5–10% — making it an excellent constant-speed machine.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Torque-Speed Characteristics Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ROTATING ELECTRICAL MACHINES"] --> B["DC MOTORS"]
    A --> C["AC MOTORS"]

    B --> B1["SHUNT MOTOR"]
    B --> B2["SERIES MOTOR"]
    B --> B3["COMPOUND MOTOR"]

    C --> C1["3-PHASE INDUCTION MOTOR"]

    B1 --> D1["LINEAR DROOP: N = N0 - kT"]
    B2 --> D2["INVERSE SQUARE ROOT: N proportional to 1 over sqrt T"]
    B3 --> D3["CUMULATIVE: Between Shunt and Series"]
    B3 --> D4["DIFFERENTIAL: Rising and Unstable"]

    C1 --> E1["PARABOLIC: Three Regions - Low Slip, Pull-out, High Slip"]
    E1 --> E2["Tmax at slip sm = R2 by X2"]
    E1 --> E3["Linear Approximation in Operating Region"]

    D1 --> F["CONSTANT SPEED APPLICATIONS"]
    D2 --> G["HIGH STARTING TORQUE APPLICATIONS"]
    D3 --> H["MEDIUM STARTING TORQUE WITH SAFE NO-LOAD"]
    D4 --> I["RARE - SERVO APPLICATIONS"]
    E1 --> J["INDUSTRIAL DRIVES - PUMPS, FANS, COMPRESSORS"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Flow of Torque-Speed Characteristic Generation

```mermaid
graph LR
    V["SUPPLY VOLTAGE V"] --> N1["KVL ON ARMATURE LOOP"]
    RA["ARMATURE RESISTANCE Ra"] --> N1
    EB["BACK EMF Eb = k1 x Phi x N"] --> N1

    IF["FIELD CURRENT If"] --> FLUX["AIR GAP FLUX Phi"]
    IA["ARMATURE CURRENT Ia"] --> TE1["TORQUE Te = k2 x Phi x Ia"]
    FLUX --> TE1
    FLUX --> EB

    TE1 --> N2["N = V minus IaRa divided by k1 x Phi"]
    IA --> N2

    N2 --> CHART["TORQUE-SPEED CURVE"]
    IA --> CHART

    CHART --> P1["SHUNT: STRAIGHT LINE"]
    CHART --> P2["SERIES: RECTANGULAR HYPERBOLA"]
    CHART --> P3["COMPOUND: MODIFIED CURVE"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Stability Analysis of Operating Point

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["MOTOR T-N CURVE"] --> S2["LOAD T-N CURVE"]
    L1["FAN TYPE: TL proportional to N squared"] --> S2
    L2["LINEAR: TL proportional to N"] --> S2
    L3["CONSTANT TORQUE: TL = constant"] --> S2
    L4["HOIST TYPE: TL constant independent of N"] --> S2

    S2 --> OP["OPERATING POINT INTERSECTION"]
    OP --> STB["STABILITY CHECK: dTe/dN must be less than dTL/dN"]

    S1 --> STB
    S2 --> STB
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic: DC Shunt Motor Circuit Representation

```
   +---[ R_sh ]---+
   |              |
   |              |
  (+)             |
  V ---+--------[ R_a ]--- (A) ----+
        |                          |
        +--------[ Field ]---------+
                                      |
                          +--- N (speed) ---+
                          |                  |
                          +--- Te (torque) --+
                                 to load
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define the term "torque-speed characteristic" of a DC motor. Why is it considered the most important performance curve of a motor?

**Model Answer:**

The torque-speed characteristic of a DC motor is the **graphical relationship** between the electromagnetic torque developed ($T_e$) by the motor and its rotational speed ($N$) at a given constant supply voltage.

It is considered the most important performance curve because:

1. It reveals the **starting torque** (torque at $N = 0$), which determines whether the motor can drive a given load from rest.
2. It indicates the **no-load speed** ($N_0$), which defines the upper speed limit of operation.
3. It determines the **speed regulation** under varying load conditions.
4. It establishes the **stability** of the operating point when intersected with the load characteristic.
5. It is the key to **motor selection** for any specific application (e.g., traction, pumps, lathes).

$$\text{Mathematically: } T_e = f(N) \text{ at } V = \text{const}, \Phi = \text{condition dependent}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
State the torque and speed equations of a DC motor. What is the function of the back-EMF in a DC motor?

**Model Answer:**

The two fundamental equations of a DC motor are:

**Torque equation (developed by Lorentz force):**

$$T_e = \frac{P \Phi Z I_a}{2 \pi A} = k_2 \Phi I_a \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Speed equation (from EMF equation):**

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_1 \Phi} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

**Function of Back-EMF ($E_b$):**
- $E_b$ is the **counter-voltage** generated by the rotating armature conductors cutting the magnetic field flux, in accordance with **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.
- It always **opposes the applied voltage** (Lenz's Law), hence the name "back-EMF."
- It is the **mechanism of self-regulation** in a DC motor — as speed increases, $E_b$ rises, reducing the net armature current $I_a = \frac{V - E_b}{R_a}$, which in turn reduces the torque.
- Without back-EMF, the armature current would be limited only by $R_a$ (very small), causing the motor to draw dangerous currents and burn out.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks — this is the standard KTU ESE 2024 Scheme pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the torque-speed characteristic equation of a DC shunt motor. Show that the curve is a **straight line with negative slope**. What is the significance of the slope?

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of $0.3 \, \Omega$ and a field resistance of $110 \, \Omega$. At no-load, the motor draws 5 A from the supply and runs at 1200 rpm. At full-load, the motor draws 50 A. Calculate:
1. The no-load speed $N_0$
2. The full-load speed $N_{fl}$
3. The percentage speed regulation

Neglect armature reaction.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 above for full derivation)*

For a DC shunt motor, the field current $I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \text{constant}$, so $\Phi = \text{constant}$.

The torque-speed equation becomes:

$$N = \frac{V}{k_1 \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2} T_e$$

This is of the form $N = N_0 - mT_e$ where $m = \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2}$ is the slope.

**Significance of slope:** The slope $m$ represents the **drop in speed per unit increase in torque**. A smaller slope means a **flatter characteristic** (better speed regulation). Since $R_a$ is small and $\Phi$ is large, the slope is small, making the shunt motor a near-constant-speed machine.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Field current:**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — No-load armature current:**

$$I_{a0} = I_{L0} - I_{sh} = 5 - 2 = 3 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — No-load back-EMF:**

$$E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (3)(0.3) = 220 - 0.9 = 219.1 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Full-load armature current:**

$$I_{a,fl} = I_{L,fl} - I_{sh} = 50 - 2 = 48 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Full-load back-EMF:**

$$E_{b,fl} = V - I_{a,fl} R_a = 220 - (48)(0.3) = 220 - 14.4 = 205.6 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — No-load speed** (using $N \propto E_b/\Phi$ with $\Phi$ = const):

$$N_0 = \frac{E_{b0}}{E_{b,fl}} \times N_{fl} = \frac{219.1}{205.6} \times 1200 = 1278.5 \, \text{rpm}$$

Alternatively, using the no-load condition directly:
$$N_0 = \frac{E_{b0}}{k_1 \Phi} = \frac{219.1}{k_1 \Phi}$$
where $k_1 \Phi = \frac{E_{b,fl}}{N_{fl}} = \frac{205.6}{1200} = 0.1713$
Therefore $N_0 = \frac{219.1}{0.1713} = 1278.5 \, \text{rpm}$

**Step 7 — Full-load speed** (given):

$$N_{fl} = 1200 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Step 8 — Percentage speed regulation:**

$$\% \text{Reg} = \frac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100 = \frac{1278.5 - 1200}{1200} \times 100 = 6.54\%$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the torque-speed relationship of a DC series motor. Why is a series motor unsuitable for belt-driven applications? Explain the reason for **runaway speed** at no-load.

**(b)** A 200 V DC series motor has an armature resistance of $0.25 \, \Omega$ and a series field resistance of $0.15 \, \Omega$. At a certain load, the motor draws 40 A and runs at 800 rpm. If the load is increased such that the motor draws 80 A, calculate:
1. The new speed
2. The ratio of developed torques ($T_2/T_1$)

Assume magnetic circuit to be in the unsaturated region.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(See Section 3.2 above for full derivation)*

For a DC series motor, in the unsaturated region:

$$\Phi = k_f I_a \quad \text{and} \quad T_e = k_2 k_f I_a^2$$

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_1 k_f I_a} = \frac{V}{k_1 k_f I_a} - \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_f}$$

In terms of torque:

$$N = \frac{V}{k_1 \sqrt{k_2 k_f T_e}} - \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_f}$$

**Why series motor is unsuitable for belt-driven applications:**
In belt drives, the belt may **slip or break**, suddenly removing the load. With no mechanical load, the speed tends to **infinity** (practically very high), causing **mechanical damage** to the armature, bearings, and commutator.

**Reason for runaway speed at no-load:**
At no-load, the torque required is minimal (only to overcome friction). The armature current drops to a very small value, which in turn causes the flux $\Phi = k_f I_a$ to become very small. From the speed equation $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k_1 \Phi}$, a very small $\Phi$ results in an extremely large value of $N$ — the motor "runs away."

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V = 200 \, \text{V}$
- $R_a = 0.25 \, \Omega$
- $R_{se} = 0.15 \, \Omega$
- Total series resistance: $R_t = R_a + R_{se} = 0.40 \, \Omega$
- Initial condition: $I_1 = 40 \, \text{A}$, $N_1 = 800 \, \text{rpm}$
- Final condition: $I_2 = 80 \, \text{A}$, $N_2 = ?$

**Step 1 — Back-EMF at initial condition:**

$$E_{b1} = V - I_1 R_t = 200 - (40)(0.40) = 200 - 16 = 184 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Back-EMF at final condition:**

$$E_{b2} = V - I_2 R_t = 200 - (80)(0.40) = 200 - 32 = 168 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Use the unsaturated series motor relation:** $E_b \propto \Phi N$ and $\Phi \propto I_a$

$$E_b = k_1 \Phi N = k_1 (k_f I_a) N = (k_1 k_f) I_a N$$

Therefore: $E_b = C \cdot I_a N$ where $C = k_1 k_f$ is a constant.

**Step 4 — Apply at both conditions:**

$$E_{b1} = C \cdot I_1 \cdot N_1 \implies C = \frac{E_{b1}}{I_1 N_1} = \frac{184}{(40)(800)} = \frac{184}{32000} = 0.00575$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $N_2$:**

$$N_2 = \frac{E_{b2}}{C \cdot I_2} = \frac{168}{(0.00575)(80)} = \frac{168}{0.46} = 365.2 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Step 6 — Torque ratio** (in unsaturated region, $T \propto I_a^2$):

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{80}{40}\right)^2 = 4$$

**Result Summary:**
- $N_2 = 365.2 \, \text{rpm}$ (speed drops as load increases — typical of series motor)
- $\frac{T_2}{T_1} = 4$ (torque quadruples when current doubles)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract field current** in shunt motor numericals — students often use total load current as armature current, losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_a$ and $R_t$** in series motor problems — must use $R_t = R_a + R_{se}$ in the voltage equation.
> 3. **Assuming saturation in series motor** when the problem states "unsaturated" — leads to incorrect $T \propto I_a$ instead of $T \propto I_a^2$.
> 4. **Not writing units** in the final answer (rpm, N·m, A) — KTU evaluators deduct **0.5–1 mark per missing unit**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention armature reaction** assumption — always state "neglecting armature reaction" explicitly for full marks.
> 6. **Confusing the slope sign** in derivation — speed DECREASES with increasing torque, so the slope is NEGATIVE.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Torque-Speed Characteristics**

- [ ] **Two fundamental equations** to remember: $T_e = k_2 \Phi I_a$ and $E_b = k_1 \Phi N$
- [ ] **Voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (always holds, for all DC motors)
- [ ] **General T-N equation:** $N = \frac{V}{k_1\Phi} - \frac{R_a}{k_1 k_2 \Phi^2} T_e$ — memorize this!
- [ ] **DC Shunt:** $\Phi = \text{const}$ → **Linear, drooping straight line** → constant speed
- [ ] **DC Series:** $\Phi \propto I_a$ (unsaturated) → **Inverse square root curve** → high starting torque, dangerous at no-load
- [ ] **DC Compound Cumulative:** $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} + \Phi_{se}$ → between shunt and series → most versatile
- [ ] **DC Compound Differential:** $\Phi = \Phi_{sh} - \Phi_{se}$ → rising characteristic → unstable, rarely used
- [ ] **Induction Motor:** $T \propto \frac{s}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ → parabolic with $T_{max}$ at $s_m = R_2/X_2$
- [ ] **Pull-out torque** is independent of $R_2$ — depends only on $V_1$ and $X_2$
- [ ] **Stability criterion:** $\frac{dT_m}{dN} < \frac{dT_L}{dN}$ at the operating point
- [ ] **Speed regulation formula:** $\% \text{Reg} = \frac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100$ — typically 5–10% for shunt, 10–15% for compound, undefined for series
- [ ] **Power relation:** $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (with $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m → $P$ in W)
- [ ] **Series motor MUST NOT be run at no-load** — always beltless, gear-coupled, or directly coupled
- [ ] **Stable operating regions:** Shunt motor operating region is stable for all loads; Series motor is stable throughout; Differential compound is inherently UNSTABLE

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Induction Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Induction Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Single Phase Induction Motor (SPIM)** is a self-starting (with auxiliary means) alternating current motor that operates from a **single-phase AC supply** and produces mechanical rotation through electromagnetic induction between the stator and a short-circuited rotor (squirrel-cage rotor). The stator carries a distributed single-phase winding that produces a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field** rather than a truly rotating magnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A single-phase induction motor is **NOT inherently self-starting**. It requires an auxiliary starting winding and a phase-splitting mechanism to produce a rotating magnetic field at the instant of starting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Supply Frequency:** $f = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz}}$ in India (KTU Kerala region). The synchronous speed in RPM is given by $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$, where $P$ is the number of poles.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pedal-Bicycle" Explanation

Imagine you are sitting on a stationary bicycle. If someone pushes you exactly forward, you move forward — but if you are perfectly balanced, you will not automatically lean and pedal. Now, imagine two friends pushing you simultaneously: one from the **left-rear** and one from the **right-rear** (with a slight time delay). You will start to rotate forward smoothly. 

The single-phase induction motor has a similar story:
- A **single-phase AC supply** alone produces only a *pulsating* magnetic field (like the single forward push) — this cannot start rotation from rest.
- A **second (auxiliary) winding**, displaced in space and energized with a **time-displaced current** (like the two friends with delay), creates a *rotating* magnetic field — the motor can now start.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Anchor — Why SPIMs are preferred for domestic use:**
> 1. Most homes have only single-phase AC supply.
> 2. SPIMs are simple, rugged, low-cost, and maintenance-free.
> 3. Squirrel-cage rotor has **no brushes, no commutator, no slip rings** — extremely reliable.

## 1.3 Constructional Features

| Component | Description | Material |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Stator Core** | Laminated silicon steel with semi-closed slots | CRGO steel |
| **Main Winding (M)** | Distributed winding covering 2/3 of stator slots | Copper |
| **Auxiliary / Starting Winding (A)** | Placed physically 90° (electrical) apart from main winding | Copper (thinner) |
| **Rotor** | Squirrel-cage type — skewed bars, shorted at both ends via end-rings | Aluminum / Copper |
| **Centrifugal Switch** | Disconnects auxiliary winding after ~75% of synchronous speed | Mechanical |
| **Bearings & Frame** | Provide mechanical support and cooling | Cast iron / Aluminum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention that the auxiliary winding is **displaced by 90° electrical** in space from the main winding, and is fed with a current that is **time-displaced (phase-shifted)** from the main winding current. Both space and time displacement are essential for producing a rotating magnetic field.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Single-Phase Winding Alone Cannot Start the Motor

When a single-phase AC supply is fed to the main winding, the resulting flux $\phi(t)$ is:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_{m} \cos(\omega t)$$

This flux **alternates** along a fixed axis — it does not rotate. According to the **Double Revolving Field Theory**, this pulsating flux can be resolved into **two equal, oppositely rotating magnetic fields**, each of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$.

- Forward rotating field: $\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \cdot e^{j\omega t}$ (produces forward torque $T_f$)
- Backward rotating field: $\phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} \cdot e^{-j\omega t}$ (produces reverse torque $T_b$)

At standstill ($s = 1$), the slip with respect to both fields is the same → $T_f = T_b$ → **Net starting torque = 0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** Once the rotor is given an initial push in either direction, the slip with respect to that direction's field decreases, while the slip with respect to the opposite field increases. The forward torque dominates → motor accelerates to near synchronous speed.

## 2.2 Double Revolving Field Theory (DRFT)

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. A pulsating flux $\phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ is mathematically equivalent to **two counter-rotating flux vectors** of equal magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$.
2. Each rotating field induces EMF in the rotor; the rotor reacts by producing its own magnetic field.
3. The interaction between each stator field and its induced rotor field produces a torque.
4. The net torque is the algebraic sum: $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$.

At any slip $s$:
- Slip w.r.t. forward field = $s$
- Slip w.r.t. backward field = $(2 - s)$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Condition |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM, $f$ in Hz, $P$ = poles |
| 2 | Actual rotor speed | $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$ | RPM, $s$ = slip |
| 3 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Dimensionless, $0 < s < 1$ |
| 4 | Stator pulsating flux | $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$ | Webers |
| 5 | Two counter-rotating flux components | $\phi_f = \phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ | Webers |
| 6 | Slip w.r.t. forward field | $s_f = s$ | — |
| 7 | Slip w.r.t. backward field | $s_b = (2 - s)$ | — |
| 8 | Torque $\propto \dfrac{s \cdot E_2^2 \cdot R_2 / s}{R_2^2/s^2 + (X_2)^2}$ | Standard induction torque | N·m |
| 9 | Starting Torque of SPIM | $T_{st} = 0$ (without auxiliary) | N·m |
| 10 | Condition for starting torque production | Two windings displaced by 90° in space, currents displaced by $\alpha$ in time | $\alpha \neq 0$ and $\neq 180°$ |
| 11 | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C}$ | Ohms |
| 12 | Phase angle between $I_m$ and $I_a$ | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_C}{R_a}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$ | degrees |
| 13 | Starting torque (split phase) | $T_{st} \propto I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin \alpha$ | N·m |
| 14 | Forward field reactance | $X_f = X_2$ at $s = 1$ (forward) | Ohms |
| 15 | Backward field effective reactance | $X_b \approx 2 X_2$ (approximate) | Ohms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Never confuse the single-phase motor's rotor with that of a three-phase motor. The rotor circuit effectively has **two rotor circuits** (one for forward, one for backward field) connected in series — this is reflected in the equivalent circuit which is unique to SPIMs.

## 2.4 Cross-Field Theory (Alternative View)

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. The main winding produces a pulsating flux $\phi_m$ along the main axis.
2. The rotating rotor cuts this flux, inducing a **rotor EMF** in the quadrature (cross) axis.
3. Since the rotor is short-circuited, a **rotor current** $I_r$ flows in the cross-axis.
4. This rotor current produces its own flux $\phi_r$ in the cross-axis.
5. The interaction between $\phi_m$ (main axis) and $\phi_r$ (cross axis) produces a **torque** along the direction of rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Cross-field theory is more accurate for analysis under loaded conditions. DRFT is simpler and is preferred for explaining why SPIMs are not self-starting.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic fans** → Capacitor-run (single-phase) induction motors.
- **Refrigerator compressors** → Capacitor-start induction motors.
- **Washing machines** → Capacitor-start capacitor-run motors.
- **Ceiling / table fans** → Split-phase or shaded-pole motors.
- **Small pumps and blowers** → Single-phase induction motors dominate.
- **HVAC systems, food mixers, blowers** → Universal SPIMs.

## 2.6 Starting Methods Overview

| Method | Starting Torque | Cost | Application |
|--------|-----------------|------|-------------|
| **Split-phase (Resistance start)** | Low (1.5–2× full load) | Low | Fans, blowers, small tools |
| **Capacitor-start** | High (3–4.5× full load) | Moderate | Compressors, pumps |
| **Capacitor-run (PSC)** | Moderate (1.5× full load) | Low | Ceiling fans, HVAC |
| **Capacitor-start Capacitor-run** | Very high (4–5× full load) | High | Washing machines, compressors |
| **Shaded-pole** | Very low (0.5–1× full load) | Very low | Small fans, toys, hair dryers |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Double Revolving Field Theory

**Given:** Stator flux $\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$, which is a real-valued, single-axis pulsating field.

**Step 1 — Express as a phasor sum of two rotating vectors:**

Using Euler's identity, $\cos(\omega t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}}{2}$.

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \cdot \dfrac{e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two counter-rotating components:**

$$\phi(t) = \underbrace{\dfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t}}_{\text{Forward field} \, \phi_f} + \underbrace{\dfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}}_{\text{Backward field} \, \phi_b}$$

**Step 3 — Define slip with respect to each field:**

Let the rotor rotate at speed $\omega_r$ (mechanical) corresponding to electrical frequency $\omega_r$. Synchronous electrical angular velocity is $\omega_s = 2\pi f / (\text{pole pairs})$.

$$\text{Forward slip: } s_f = \dfrac{\omega_s - \omega_r}{\omega_s} = s$$

$$\text{Backward slip: } s_b = \dfrac{\omega_s - (-\omega_r)}{\omega_s} = 2 - s$$

**Step 4 — Write the standard induction-motor torque expression for each field:**

For the forward field, the torque is:

$$T_f = \dfrac{K \cdot s_f \cdot \phi_f^2 \cdot R_2 / s_f}{R_2^2 / s_f^2 + X_2^2} = \dfrac{K \cdot \phi_f^2 \cdot s_f \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_f X_2)^2}$$

For the backward field, by replacing $s_f$ with $s_b$:

$$T_b = \dfrac{K \cdot \phi_b^2 \cdot s_b \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s_b X_2)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net torque:**

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b$$

Since $\phi_f = \phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$:

$$T_{net} = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \left[ \dfrac{s \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2} - \dfrac{(2-s) R_2}{R_2^2 + [(2-s) X_2]^2} \right]$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate at standstill ($s = 1$):**

Substituting $s = 1$ into the equation:

$$\dfrac{s \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}\bigg\vert_{s=1} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

$$\dfrac{(2-s) R_2}{R_2^2 + [(2-s) X_2]^2}\bigg\vert_{s=1} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

These are **identical** → $T_{net} = 0$. The single-phase motor produces **zero starting torque** without an auxiliary mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Standard Derivation:** This exact 6-step derivation using DRFT is a regular question in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the steps, the substitutions, and the final condition $T_{st} = 0$ at $s = 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Split-Phase Motor Starting Torque

**Given:**
- Main winding impedance: $\bar{Z}_m = R_m + jX_m$
- Auxiliary winding impedance: $\bar{Z}_a = R_a + jX_a$ (designed with higher $R_a / X_a$ ratio)
- Auxiliary winding in series with a starting resistance $R_{ext}$ (or with high inherent resistance)

**Step 1 — Compute the magnitude and phase of each winding's current:**

$$I_m = \dfrac{V}{Z_m}, \quad \phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right)$$

$$I_a = \dfrac{V}{Z_a}, \quad \phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the phase difference between the two currents:**

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Write the starting torque expression:**

For a two-phase motor with windings in space quadrature, the starting torque is:

$$T_{st} \propto \phi_m \cdot \phi_a \cdot \sin \alpha$$

In terms of winding currents and assuming flux $\propto$ current:

$$\boxed{T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — For a split-phase motor**, typically $\alpha \approx 25° - 30°$, giving moderate starting torque (1.5–2 × full-load torque).

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacitor-Start Motor Starting Torque

**Step 1 — Auxiliary winding is in series with a capacitor of reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$.**

**Step 2 — Total auxiliary winding impedance:**

$$\bar{Z}_a = R_a + j(X_a - X_C)$$

**Step 3 — Phase angle of auxiliary current:**

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Phase difference between $I_m$ and $I_a$:**

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Starting torque:**

$$T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin \alpha$$

**Step 6 — Optimal capacitor value** for $\alpha = 90°$ (maximum torque):

$$\tan \alpha = \tan(90°) = \infty \implies X_C = X_a + \dfrac{R_a \cdot X_m}{R_m}$$

The value of $C$ that makes the auxiliary current lead the main current by exactly $90°$:

$$\boxed{C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \left(X_a + \dfrac{R_a \cdot X_m}{R_m}\right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** When $\alpha = 90°$, $\sin \alpha = 1$ (maximum), and the motor behaves like a balanced two-phase motor at starting. This is the design goal for capacitor-start and capacitor-start capacitor-run motors.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | Split-Phase | Capacitor-Start | Capacitor-Run (PSC) | Shaded-Pole |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|--------------------|-------------|
| Auxiliary winding | Yes (high R) | Yes + Capacitor | Yes + Capacitor (no switch) | Copper ring |
| Phase displacement $\alpha$ | 25° – 30° | 80° – 90° | 80° – 90° | < 30° |
| Starting torque (× F.L.) | 1.5 – 2 | 3 – 4.5 | 1.5 – 2 | 0.5 – 1 |
| Starting current | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Power factor (running) | Poor | Poor | Good | Very poor |
| Efficiency | Moderate | Moderate | High | Low |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Very low |
| Reversible? | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| Application | Fans, blowers | Compressors | Ceiling fans | Small fans, toys |
| Capacitor value | — | $C \approx 50 - 100 \, \mu F$ (start) | $C \approx 2 - 12 \, \mu F$ (run) | — |
| Centrifugal switch | Yes | Yes | No | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Single-Phase Induction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SupplyA[Single Phase AC Supply 230V 50Hz] --> SwitchA[SPST Main Switch]
    SwitchA --> Node1{Junction to Windings}
    Node1 --> MainW[Main Winding M - Distributed Copper]
    Node1 --> AuxW[Auxiliary Winding A - 90 deg Space Displaced]
    AuxW --> CentSw[Centrifugal Switch NC at start]
    CentSw --> CapBank[Capacitor Bank or Resistor]
    CapBank --> AuxTerm[Aux Terminal returns to Supply]
    MainW --> MainTerm[Main Terminal returns to Supply]
    MainW --> AirGap[Stator Rotor Air Gap]
    AuxW --> AirGap
    AirGap --> SquirrelRotor[Squirrel Cage Rotor - Aluminum Bars]
    SquirrelRotor --> Shaft[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    SquirrelRotor --> BackEMF[Induced Rotor EMF and Current]
    BackEMF --> TorqueProd[Rotating Magnetic Field Interaction]
    TorqueProd --> MechOut[Mechanical Power at Shaft]
    Shaft --> Load[Connected Load Fan Pump Compressor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Starting Method Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA[SPIM Starting Method Selection] --> Decision1{Required Starting Torque}
    Decision1 --> LowT[Low Torque 1.5x FL]
    Decision1 --> MedT[Medium Torque 2 to 3x FL]
    Decision1 --> HighT[High Torque 3 to 5x FL]
    Decision1 --> VLowT[Very Low Torque less than 1x FL]
    LowT --> SplitP[Split Phase Resistance Start Motor]
    MedT --> CapRun[Capacitor Run PSC Motor]
    HighT --> CapStart[Capacitor Start Motor]
    HighT --> CapBoth[Capacitor Start Capacitor Run]
    VLowT --> ShadedP[Shaded Pole Motor]
    SplitP --> App1[Fans Blowers Small Tools]
    CapRun --> App2[Ceiling Fans HVAC Units]
    CapStart --> App3[Refrigerator Compressors Pumps]
    CapBoth --> App4[Washing Machines High Inertia Loads]
    ShadedP --> App5[Toys Hair Dryers Small Table Fans]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit of Single-Phase Induction Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vsrc[Supply Voltage V] --> Zm[Stator Impedance Z1 R1 jX1]
    Zm --> JMagnet[Junction to Magnetising Branch]
    JMagnet --> Xm[Magnetising Reactance jXm as jX0]
    JMagnet --> Jrotor[Junction to Rotor Block]
    Jrotor --> R2f[R2 by s for Forward Field]
    R2f --> X2f[jX2 for Forward Field]
    Jrotor --> R2b[R2 by 2 minus s for Backward Field]
    R2b --> X2b[jX2 for Backward Field]
    X2b --> GroundA[Return to Source]
    R2f --> GroundA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The unique feature of the SPIM equivalent circuit is the **two parallel rotor branches** — one representing the rotor's response to the forward rotating field (slip $s$) and another to the backward rotating field (slip $2-s$). The total rotor impedance at any slip is the **parallel combination** of these two branches.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Speed–Torque Characteristics of SPIM Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GraphA[Speed Torque Curve Comparison] --> CurveA[Split Phase]
    GraphA --> CurveB[Capacitor Start]
    GraphA --> CurveC[Capacitor Run]
    GraphA --> CurveD[Capacitor Start Capacitor Run]
    GraphA --> CurveE[Shaded Pole]
    CurveA --> LabelA[Low starting torque smooth acceleration]
    CurveB --> LabelB[High starting torque 3 to 4x FL]
    CurveC --> LabelC[Moderate torque high running PF]
    CurveD --> LabelD[Highest torque high efficiency]
    CurveE --> LabelE[Lowest torque low cost simple construction]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *(3 Marks)*
**Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting? Explain briefly using double revolving field theory.**

**Model Answer:**

A single-phase induction motor is not self-starting because the alternating current in the main winding produces a **pulsating magnetic field** along a fixed axis — not a rotating one. According to the **double revolving field theory**, this pulsating field can be resolved into two equal and oppositely rotating magnetic fields of magnitude $\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$ each.

At standstill, the rotor's slip with respect to both the forward and backward rotating fields is the **same** ($s = 1$). Therefore, the **forward torque** $T_f$ and **backward torque** $T_b$ produced by the two fields are **equal in magnitude but opposite in direction**. Their algebraic sum gives:

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b = 0$$

Hence the **net starting torque is zero**, and the rotor fails to start on its own. An auxiliary winding with a phase-displaced current is required to produce a true rotating field that initiates rotation. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *(3 Marks)*
**List any three starting methods used for single-phase induction motors. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Starting Method | Application |
|---|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Split-phase (Resistance start)** | Table fans, small blowers |
| 2 | **Capacitor-start** | Refrigerator compressors, water pumps |
| 3 | **Capacitor-run (PSC)** | Ceiling fans, HVAC fans |
| 4 *(any one)* | **Shaded-pole** | Hair dryers, small toys, table fans |
| 5 *(any one)* | **Capacitor-start Capacitor-run** | Washing machines, high-inertia loads |

Each method produces a phase-displaced rotating magnetic field using an auxiliary winding, enabling self-starting. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** — **Module 4**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **construction and working of a capacitor-start single-phase induction motor** with a neat sketch. Why is the centrifugal switch used? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with two windings:
   - **Main winding (M):** Distributed in 2/3 of stator slots, low resistance, high inductance.
   - **Auxiliary (starting) winding (A):** Placed 90° electrical apart from main winding, high resistance, low inductance.
2. **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type — skewed aluminum bars short-circuited by end-rings.
3. **Capacitor $C_s$:** Connected in series with the auxiliary winding.
4. **Centrifugal switch (S):** Normally closed; mounted on the shaft.

**Working:**
1. When the motor is switched on, both windings are energized simultaneously.
2. The main winding current $I_m$ lags the supply voltage by a large angle (highly inductive).
3. The auxiliary winding current $I_a$ leads the supply voltage (because of the series capacitor).
4. The phase angle $\alpha$ between $I_m$ and $I_a$ is approximately **80°–90°**, producing a strong **rotating magnetic field**.
5. The rotor experiences a high starting torque (3–4.5 × full-load torque) and accelerates.
6. When the rotor reaches about **70%–80% of synchronous speed**, the centrifugal switch **opens**, disconnecting the auxiliary winding and capacitor from the supply.
7. The motor then continues to run on the main winding alone as a single-phase motor.

**Why a centrifugal switch is used:**
- To **protect the auxiliary winding and capacitor** from prolonged current flow (they are designed only for short-duration starting duty).
- To **improve running efficiency** and power factor (the auxiliary winding has higher resistance and would cause extra losses if left in the circuit).
- The auxiliary winding is not designed for continuous duty, so it must be disconnected once the motor picks up speed. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q. (b)** With the help of the **double revolving field theory**, derive the expression showing that a single-phase induction motor has zero starting torque. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The flux produced by a single-phase stator winding is:

$$\phi(t) = \phi_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Using Euler's identity, this pulsating flux is resolved into two counter-rotating fields:

$$\phi(t) = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t} + \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$$

The first term represents a **forward rotating field** $\phi_f = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$, and the second a **backward rotating field** $\phi_b = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2}$, each rotating at synchronous speed $\omega_s$.

**Step 3:** Each rotating field induces a torque on the rotor. For a rotor rotating at slip $s$:

- Slip w.r.t. forward field: $s_f = s$
- Slip w.r.t. backward field: $s_b = 2 - s$

**Step 4:** The torque produced by each field follows the standard induction motor torque relation:

$$T_f = K \cdot \phi_f^2 \cdot \dfrac{s \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}$$

$$T_b = K \cdot \phi_b^2 \cdot \dfrac{(2 - s) R_2}{R_2^2 + [(2 - s) X_2]^2}$$

**Step 5:** Net torque on the rotor:

$$T_{net} = T_f - T_b$$

**Step 6:** At standstill, $s = 1$. Substituting $s = 1$:

$$T_f = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{1 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

$$T_b = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{\phi_m}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{1 \cdot R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$$

**Step 7:** Since $\phi_f = \phi_b$, $T_f = T_b$ → $T_{net} = 0$.

$$\boxed{T_{st} = T_f - T_b = 0}$$

**Conclusion:** A single-phase induction motor produces **zero starting torque** without an auxiliary starting mechanism, confirming that it is **not self-starting**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** — **Module 4**

**Q. (a)** Describe the **construction, working, and applications of a shaded-pole induction motor**. Why is its starting torque very low? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
1. **Stator:** Salient-pole type construction with concentrated windings.
2. Each pole has a **shading band** — a copper ring (short-circuited) that encircles approximately **1/3 of the pole face** (the trailing edge).
3. **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage type.

**Working:**
1. When the AC supply is given to the stator winding, an alternating flux $\phi$ is produced in the main pole.
2. A small portion $\phi_2$ links the shading band, inducing an EMF and circulating current in it (Lenz's law).
3. This induced current opposes the change in $\phi_2$, delaying its buildup and collapse.
4. The unshaded portion flux $\phi_1$ leads $\phi_2$ in time.
5. The result is a **shifting (rotating) magnetic field** that moves from the unshaded region to the shaded region of the pole.
6. The rotor experiences a small torque in the direction of flux shift.

**Why starting torque is very low:**
- The phase difference between $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ is **less than 30°**, producing a very weak rotating field.
- The copper shading band has limited cross-section → small induced current → weak field shift.
- The starting torque is typically only **0.5× to 1× full-load torque**.

**Applications:**
- Small table fans
- Hair dryers
- Toy motors
- Refrigerator evaporator fans
- Indicating instruments
- Vending machines, photocopy machines

The motor is **non-reversible** (direction is fixed by the shading band position) and is **not suitable for high-inertia loads**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q. (b)** A **capacitor-start single-phase induction motor** has the following data:
- Main winding impedance at standstill: $Z_m = (4 + j5) \, \Omega$
- Auxiliary winding impedance (without capacitor): $Z_a = (8 + j2) \, \Omega$
- Supply voltage: $V = 230 \, V, \, 50 \, \text{Hz}$
- A capacitor of $C = 30 \, \mu F$ is connected in series with the auxiliary winding.

**Calculate:**
- (i) The phase angle of main winding current. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) The phase angle of auxiliary winding current. *(2 marks)*
- (iii) The phase difference $\alpha$ between the two currents. *(2 marks)*
- (iv) Comment on whether this gives good starting torque. *(1 mark)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Capacitive reactance:**

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 30 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1}{0.00942} \approx 106.1 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Auxiliary winding impedance with capacitor:**

$$\bar{Z}_a' = R_a + j(X_a - X_C) = 8 + j(2 - 106.1) = 8 - j104.1 \, \Omega$$

$$Z_a' = \sqrt{8^2 + 104.1^2} = \sqrt{64 + 10838.81} = \sqrt{10902.81} \approx 104.4 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Phase angle of main winding current:**

$$\phi_m = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_m}{R_m}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{5}{4}\right) = 51.34° \, (\text{lagging})$$

**Step 4 — Phase angle of auxiliary winding current:**

$$\phi_a = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_a - X_C}{R_a}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-104.1}{8}\right) = -85.6° \, (\text{leading})$$

**Step 5 — Phase difference:**

$$\alpha = \phi_m - \phi_a = 51.34° - (-85.6°) = 136.94°$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that $\alpha$ must be measured as the **smaller of the two** possible differences. Here, $180° - 136.94° = 43.06°$. The effective phase displacement is $\boxed{\alpha = 43.06°}$. Many students lose 1–2 marks by not taking the proper angle.

**Step 6 — Comment on starting torque:**

Since the effective $\alpha = 43.06°$ is small (ideal would be 80°–90°), the starting torque would be **modest** — this capacitor value is **not optimal**. To improve, **reduce the capacitance** to increase the phase angle difference towards 90°.

**Verification using $\sin \alpha$:** $\sin(43.06°) = 0.683$ — produces only about 68% of maximum possible starting torque. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 (Single Phase Induction Motors)**

- ✅ A **single-phase induction motor** is **not self-starting** — the pulsating flux produces zero net starting torque (DRFT proof required).
- ✅ The **two pre-requisites for self-starting** are: (i) **spatial displacement of 90°** between main and auxiliary windings, and (ii) **temporal phase displacement $\alpha$** between their currents.
- ✅ **DRFT** states: $\phi_{pulsating} = \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{j\omega t} + \dfrac{\phi_m}{2} e^{-j\omega t}$ — two counter-rotating equal fields.
- ✅ At standstill, $s = 1$, so $T_f = T_b$ → $T_{st} = 0$.
- ✅ The **equivalent circuit** of an SPIM has **two parallel rotor branches**: one for forward ($s$) and one for backward ($2-s$) fields.
- ✅ **Split-phase motor:** Uses high-resistance auxiliary winding → $\alpha \approx 25°$–$30°$ → low starting torque.
- ✅ **Capacitor-start motor:** Capacitor in series with auxiliary winding → $\alpha \approx 80°$–$90°$ → high starting torque; centrifugal switch disconnects aux at 75% $N_s$.
- ✅ **Capacitor-run (PSC) motor:** Smaller capacitor permanently in circuit → better running power factor, smoother operation; no centrifugal switch.
- ✅ **Capacitor-start Capacitor-run motor:** Two capacitors — large $C_s$ (start, switched out) + small $C_r$ (run, permanent) → highest starting + running performance.
- ✅ **Shaded-pole motor:** Copper shading ring on pole face → very low starting torque, non-reversible, cheapest construction.
- ✅ **Centrifugal switch** is used in split-phase and capacitor-start motors to disconnect the auxiliary winding after starting.
- ✅ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$; **actual speed** $N_r = N_s(1-s)$.
- ✅ **Slip w.r.t. forward field** = $s$; **slip w.r.t. backward field** = $2 - s$.
- ✅ **Optimum capacitor** for 90° phase shift: $C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f \left(X_a + \dfrac{R_a \cdot X_m}{R_m}\right)}$.
- ✅ **Universal motor** = series-wound motor that works on both AC and DC (not the focus of SPIM module but is single-phase).
- ✅ SPIMs are used in **fans, pumps, refrigerators, washing machines, hair dryers, mixers, blowers** — virtually all household appliances.
- ✅ **Cross-field theory** is the alternative explanation — based on rotor-induced flux in quadrature axis interacting with main flux.
- ✅ **Starting torque formula:** $T_{st} = K \cdot I_m \cdot I_a \cdot \sin \alpha$ — maximize $\alpha$ for maximum torque.
- ✅ **Phase angle calculation pitfall:** When $\alpha > 90°$, take $180° - \alpha$ as the effective angle.
- ✅ **Reverse rotation** of split-phase and capacitor-start motors is achieved by **interchanging the auxiliary winding terminals** (reverses the rotating field direction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, always draw a **neat labeled diagram** (3 marks), state the **constructional features** (2 marks), explain **working** with equations (5 marks), state **applications** (2 marks), and add a **comparative analysis** or **numerical solution** (2 marks). Diagrams are non-negotiable in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Single-Phase Induction Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Single-Phase Induction Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single-Phase Induction Motor (SPIM)** is an alternating current (AC) motor that operates on a **single-phase AC supply** and uses electromagnetic induction as its operating principle, where the rotor does not receive electrical power through conduction (no slip rings, no commutator) but has currents induced in it by the rotating/stationary stator magnetic field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim spirit):** A single-phase induction motor is essentially a **non-self-starting** AC motor requiring auxiliary starting mechanisms to produce a rotating magnetic field (RMF) or its equivalent, because a single-phase stator winding alone produces only a **pulsating (alternating) magnetic field**, not a rotating one.

The motor essentially consists of:
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel core with a single-phase main winding (and sometimes an auxiliary starting winding).
- **Rotor:** Squirrel-cage rotor (most common) — identical in construction to a three-phase induction motor rotor.
- **End Shields / Bearings / Frame:** Mechanical support structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The rotor of a single-phase induction motor is identical to that of a three-phase induction motor (squirrel-cage). The difference lies entirely in the **stator winding arrangement and the starting mechanism**.

## 1.2 The Fundamental Problem: Why a Single-Phase Stator Cannot Self-Start

If only a single-phase winding is energized with sinusoidal AC, the resulting air-gap flux is:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_{max} \sin(\omega t)$$

This flux is **purely alternating (pulsating)** along a fixed axis. Mathematically, it has **zero net rotational component** at the instant of starting. Hence, the rotor experiences no directional torque at standstill.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Point:** The most frequently asked 3-mark question is: *"Why is a single-phase induction motor not self-starting?"* Always answer in terms of the absence of a rotating magnetic field and the resulting zero starting torque.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swinging Pendulum"

Imagine you push a child on a swing (pendulum). If you push the child in a fixed forward-and-backward direction (alternating motion) without any sideways push, the swing will oscillate but **will not produce circular motion**. However, if you add a slight sideways nudge (the "starting" component) at the right moment, circular swinging becomes possible — analogous to how a split-phase or capacitor provides a **second phase-shifted winding** to convert the swinging (pulsating) field into a rotating one.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"A single-phase supply is like one leg of a 3-legged stool. You need at least a second leg (auxiliary winding) to make the system stable (self-starting)."*

## 1.4 The Two Classical Theories Explaining SPIM Operation

There are **two equivalent mathematical frameworks** to describe the working of a single-phase induction motor:

| Theory | Physical Interpretation | Best Used For |
|--------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Double Revolving Field Theory** (Ferraris, 1888) | The pulsating flux = sum of two equal counter-rotating fluxes | Conceptual analysis of starting problem |
| **Cross-Field Theory** (Potier, 1900s) | Forward & backward flux components are coupled via rotor-induced EMF | Derivation of actual torque-speed curve |

> Both theories yield **identical** final torque equations; they are mathematically equivalent.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Supply frequency (India):** $f = 50$ Hz → $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.16$ rad/s
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ rpm
- **Slip:** $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$
- **Air-gap flux density (typical):** $\mathbf{0.4 \text{ to } 0.8 \text{ T}}$
- **Power factor (typical, single-phase IM):** $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \text{ lagging}}$

---

> **End of Section 1** — Continue to Section 2 for theoretical analysis and the high-yield formula sheet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Double Revolving Field Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

The single-phase stator winding produces a flux:

$$\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$, we get:

$$\Phi(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\Phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t - \theta)}_{\text{Forward component (rotating at }+\omega_s)} + \underbrace{\frac{\Phi_m}{2} \sin(\omega t + \theta)}_{\text{Backward component (rotating at }-\omega_s)}$$

### Logical Interpretation:
1. The **pulsating flux** can be mathematically split into **two equal rotating fluxes** of magnitude $\Phi_m / 2$.
2. One rotates **clockwise (forward, $f$)** at synchronous speed $N_s$; the other rotates **counter-clockwise (backward, $b$)** at $-N_s$.
3. Each rotating flux induces its own torque in the rotor:
   - **Forward torque** $T_f$ — acts in the direction of forward rotation.
   - **Backward torque** $T_b$ — acts in the direction opposing rotation.
4. **Net torque** $T = T_f - T_b$.
5. At **standstill (s = 1):** $T_f = T_b$ → **net starting torque = 0**. (This is *why* it is not self-starting.)
6. Once the rotor is **pushed in either direction**, the corresponding component flux increases (due to lower slip), and the opposite flux decreases (higher slip) → net torque becomes nonzero → motor accelerates in that direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The motor runs in the direction it is pushed.** This is the cornerstone of all starting methods — they "push" the rotor in a preferred direction at standstill.

## 2.2 Classification of Single-Phase Induction Motors

KTU 2024 syllabus broadly classifies SPIMs into the following types:

### A. Split-Phase Motors
1. **Resistance Start (Split-Phase) Motor**
2. **Capacitor-Start Motor**
3. **Capacitor-Run (Permanent Capacitor) Motor**
4. **Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run (Two-Value Capacitor) Motor**

### B. Shaded-Pole Motor

### C. Reluctance-Start Motor / Repulsion-Start Motor (Special types)

## 2.3 Construction & Operating Principle of Each Type

### 2.3.1 Resistance-Start (Split-Phase) Motor
- **Stator:** Two windings — **Main (Running) winding** (thick wire, low resistance, high inductance) and **Starting (Auxiliary) winding** (thin wire, high resistance, low inductance).
- **Phase shift achieved by:** Difference in **R/X ratio** between the two windings.
- **Typical phase difference:** 25°–30° (not 90°, hence "split-phase," not two-phase).
- **Starting switch:** Centrifugal switch (disconnects auxiliary winding at ~75% of synchronous speed).
- **Applications:** Fans, washing machines, small machine tools, blowers.

### 2.3.2 Capacitor-Start Motor
- **Stator:** Main winding + Auxiliary winding **in series with a starting capacitor**.
- **Phase shift achieved by:** Capacitor introduces a leading current in the auxiliary winding.
- **Typical phase difference:** 80°–90° (much better than resistance-start).
- **Starting torque:** Very high (2.5 to 4.5 times full-load torque).
- **Capacitor:** Electrolytic-type (used for short duration only).
- **Applications:** Compressors, pumps, conveyors, heavy-duty fans.

### 2.3.3 Capacitor-Run (Permanent Capacitor) Motor
- **Stator:** Auxiliary winding **and its capacitor remain in circuit permanently**.
- **Capacitor type:** Oil-filled, paper, or MPP-type (rated for continuous duty).
- **Phase shift:** 80°–90° even at running condition.
- **Advantages:** High power factor, smooth operation, quiet running, good running torque.
- **Disadvantages:** Low starting torque (50%–100% of full-load).
- **Applications:** Ceiling fans, exhaust fans, blowers requiring quiet operation.

### 2.3.4 Capacitor-Start Capacitor-Run Motor
- **Stator:** Two capacitors — a large **electrolytic starting capacitor** (disconnected via centrifugal switch) AND a smaller **oil-filled running capacitor** (permanently in circuit).
- **Best performance:** High starting torque AND high running torque AND high power factor.
- **Applications:** Refrigerators, air conditioners, machine tools, commercial refrigeration.

### 2.3.5 Shaded-Pole Motor
- **Stator:** Salient poles with a **shading coil** (a single short-circuited copper ring) around a small portion of each pole.
- **Working:** The shading coil causes a **time lag** in the flux linking the shaded portion → produces a weak rotating field sweeping from unshaded to shaded region.
- **Starting torque:** Very low (40%–50% of full-load).
- **Features:** Simplest, cheapest, robust, no centrifugal switch.
- **Applications:** Small fans, hair dryers, toy motors, record players, timers.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute values inside table cells to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|--------------------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 2 | $s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip (per-unit, 0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \frac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous angular speed | rad/s |
| 4 | $\omega_r = (1-s)\,\omega_s$ | Rotor mechanical angular speed | rad/s |
| 5 | $f_2 = s f$ | Rotor-induced frequency (running) | Hz |
| 6 | $f_2 = f$ | Rotor frequency at standstill (s=1) | Hz |
| 7 | $\Phi(t) = \Phi_m \sin(\omega t) = \frac{\Phi_m}{2}\bigl[\sin(\omega t - \theta) + \sin(\omega t + \theta)\bigr]$ | Double revolving field decomposition | Wb |
| 8 | $T = \frac{k \cdot s \cdot r_2' \cdot E_2^2}{(r_2')^2 + (s x_2')^2}$ | Torque-slip (forward component) | N·m |
| 9 | $T_b = \frac{k \cdot s_b \cdot r_2' \cdot E_2^2}{(r_2')^2 + (s_b x_2')^2}$ | Backward torque component | N·m |
| 10 | $s_b = 2 - s$ | Effective slip seen by backward field | dimensionless |
| 11 | $T_{net} = T_f - T_b$ | Net electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 12 | $T_{max} = \frac{k E_2^2}{2 x_2'}$ | Maximum forward torque (occurs at $s = r_2' \vert x_2'$) | N·m |
| 13 | $P_{gap} = \frac{2 \pi N_s T}{60}$ | Air-gap (developed) mechanical power | W |
| 14 | $\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{losses}}$ | Efficiency (typical 60–75% for fractional kW SPIM) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $C = \frac{I_m \sin\phi_m}{2 \pi f \, V_c \, \omega^2}$ (approx. sizing relation) | Approximate starting capacitor value | Farads |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, problem-solving questions often test the **double revolving field theory** and the calculation of **slip with respect to forward and backward fields**. Memorize the relation $s_b = 2 - s$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single-phase induction motors are the **workhorses of low-power applications** worldwide. They dominate the:
- **Domestic appliance market** (ceiling fans, washing machines, refrigerators, mixers, air conditioners).
- **Small commercial equipment** (pumps, blowers, office machinery).
- **Agricultural and small industrial tools** (threshers, grinders, lathes, drills).

> Single-phase induction motors constitute over **80% of the total fractional-kilowatt motors manufactured globally**, making them the single largest category of AC motors in production.
