```mermaid
    end

    subgraph Alternator["Synchronous Alternator"]
        Fld["Field Winding (DC Exciter)"]
        Arm["Armature Winding (3-Phase)"]
    end

    subgraph OCTest["Open Circuit Test (OCC)"]
        OC1["Armature Terminals: OPEN"]
        OC2["Voltmeter across terminals"]
        OC3["Vary If, Record Eoc vs If"]
    end

    subgraph SCTest["Short Circuit Test (SCC)"]
        SC1["Armature Terminals: SHORTED via Ammeter"]
        SC2["Ammeter measures Isc"]
        SC3["Vary If, Record Isc vs If"]
    end

    PM --> Arm
    Fld --> Arm
    Arm --> OCTest
    Arm --> SCTest
    OCTest --> PlotOCC["OCC Plot: Eoc (Y) vs If (X) — Non-Linear Curve"]
    SCTest --> PlotSCC["SCC Plot: Isc (Y) vs If (X) — Straight Line"]
    PlotOCC --> CalcZs["Calculate Zs = Eoc / Isc at rated If"]
    PlotSCC --> CalcZs
    CalcZs --> CalcXs["Xs = sqrt Zs^2 - Ra^2"]
    CalcXs --> CalcE0["Apply EMF Formula to get E0"]
    CalcE0 --> CalcVR["Percent Voltage Regulation"]
```

## 4.3 Logic Flow — Full Algorithm of EMF Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Alternator rated values given (V, IA, f, p.f.)"] --> B["Measure Ra per phase (DC resistance)"]
    B --> C["Conduct OCC: Run at Ns, vary If, record Eoc"]
    C --> D["Conduct SCC: Short armature, vary If, record Isc"]
    D --> E["For rated IA, find corresponding If on SCC"]
    E --> F["Find Eoc on OCC at this same If"]
    F --> G["Compute Zs = Eoc / Isc"]
    G --> H["Compute Xs = sqrt Zs^2 - Ra^2"]
    H --> I{"Determine power factor type"}
    I -->|Lagging| J["Use + sign in vertical term of E0 formula"]
    I -->|Leading| K["Use - sign in vertical term of E0 formula"]
    I -->|Unity| L["Set sin phi = 0 in E0 formula"]
    J --> M["Calculate E0 using phasor equation"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Percent VR = E0 - V / V times 100"]
    N --> O["End: Report voltage regulation"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Table — Lagging vs Leading vs Unity p.f. Phasor Effect

| Power Factor | Phasor Orientation of $I_a$ | Sign in $E_0$ Formula | Effect on $E_0$ | Effect on VR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lagging** (inductive load) | $I_a$ lags $V$ by $\phi$ | **$+$** before $I_a X_s$ term | $E_0 > V$ | **Positive** VR |
| **Leading** (capacitive load) | $I_a$ leads $V$ by $\phi$ | **$-$** before $I_a X_s$ term | $E_0 < V$ (possible) | **Negative** VR (voltage rises on load removal is negative, i.e., voltage drops) |
| **Unity** (resistive load) | $I_a$ in phase with $V$ | $\sin\phi = 0$ | $E_0$ slightly above $V$ | **Small positive** VR |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q: Define voltage regulation of an alternator. Why is it necessary to determine it?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Voltage regulation of an alternator is the change in terminal voltage from full load to no load, expressed as a percentage of full-load voltage, with **constant speed and constant field excitation**.
>
> $$\% VR = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$
>
> **Necessity (1.5 marks):**
> 1. It indicates the ability of the alternator to maintain **constant terminal voltage** under varying load conditions.
> 2. It helps in **designing the excitation system** and protective relays.
> 3. It is essential for **proper load sharing** between alternators operating in parallel.
> 4. It determines the **voltage drop** in the armature reaction, which affects the choice of alternators for specific applications.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: What is synchronous impedance? How is it determined using OCC and SCC?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Synchronous impedance ($Z_s$) is the **fictitious impedance** of the armature winding that accounts for both the **armature resistance ($R_a$)** and an **effective synchronous reactance ($X_s$)** representing armature reaction and leakage flux. $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$.
>
> **Determination (1.5 marks):** $Z_s$ is found by comparing OCC and SCC:
> 1. For a given field current $I_f$, the OCC gives the open-circuit EMF $E_{oc}$.
> 2. The SCC gives the short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ for the same $I_f$.
> 3. Then: $Z_s = E_{oc} / I_{sc}$ (per phase).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A 3-phase, star-connected alternator rated at **1500 kVA, 6.6 kV** has an armature resistance of **0.4 $\Omega$ per phase** and a synchronous reactance of **6 $\Omega$ per phase**. Calculate the percentage regulation at full load at **(i) 0.8 p.f. lagging** and **(ii) 0.8 p.f. leading**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a phasor diagram, explain the EMF method of finding the voltage regulation of a synchronous generator. Mention its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Given Data:**
- $S = 1500$ kVA, $V_L = 6.6$ kV, Star-connected
- $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$/phase, $X_s = 6 \, \Omega$/phase
- $\cos\phi = 0.8 \Rightarrow \sin\phi = 0.6$

**Step 1: Calculate rated phase voltage and current**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V/phase}$$

$$I_a = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600} = 131.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute the common components**

$$V_{ph}\cos\phi = 3810.5 \times 0.8 = 3048.4 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a R_a = 131.2 \times 0.4 = 52.48 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{ph}\sin\phi = 3810.5 \times 0.6 = 2286.3 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a X_s = 131.2 \times 6 = 787.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Compute $E_0$ for 0.8 p.f. lagging (use $+$ sign)**

$$E_{0,\text{lag}} = \sqrt{(3048.4 + 52.48)^2 + (2286.3 + 787.2)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{(3100.88)^2 + (3073.5)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{9{,}615{,}455 + 9{,}446{,}392}$$
$$= \sqrt{19{,}061{,}847}$$
$$\approx 4365.99 \text{ V/phase}$$

$$\% VR_{\text{lag}} = \frac{4365.99 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100 = \frac{555.49}{3810.5} \times 100 \approx 14.58\%$$

**Step 4: Compute $E_0$ for 0.8 p.f. leading (use $-$ sign)**

$$E_{0,\text{lead}} = \sqrt{(3048.4 + 52.48)^2 + (2286.3 - 787.2)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{(3100.88)^2 + (1499.1)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{9{,}615{,}455 + 2{,}247{,}301}$$
$$= \sqrt{11{,}862{,}756}$$
$$\approx 3444.23 \text{ V/phase}$$

$$\% VR_{\text{lead}} = \frac{3444.23 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100 = \frac{-366.27}{3810.5} \times 100 \approx -9.61\%$$

> **Answer:** Regulation at **0.8 p.f. lagging = +14.58%**, and at **0.8 p.f. leading = -9.61%** (the negative sign indicates the terminal voltage actually **rises** when load is removed under leading p.f.).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**EMF Method Explanation (4 marks):**

The EMF method (also called the **Synchronous Impedance Method**) is used to find the voltage regulation of an alternator **without actually loading it**. It uses:
- **Open-Circuit Test (OCC):** Run the alternator at synchronous speed, keep terminals open, vary field current $I_f$, and record the open-circuit voltage $E_{oc}$.
- **Short-Circuit Test (SCC):** Short the armature terminals, vary $I_f$, and record short-circuit current $I_{sc}$.
- For the same $I_f$, calculate **Synchronous Impedance** $Z_s = E_{oc} / I_{sc}$.
- $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$.
- The no-load EMF $E_0$ is calculated using:
$$E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi \pm I_a X_s)^2}$$

The phasor diagram (drawn and explained) shows $V$ as reference, with $I_a R_a$ in phase with $I_a$, and $I_a X_s$ leading $I_a$ by 90°. The vector sum gives $E_0$.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Simple, requiring only OCC and SCC data.
2. No actual loading required — convenient for large alternators.
3. Quick estimation for design purposes.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Assumes a **linear (unsaturated)** relationship, which is inaccurate at high field currents.
2. Gives **pessimistic** (over-estimated) regulation values.
3. The value of $Z_s$ is **not constant** — it varies with saturation.
4. Highly inaccurate for **leading power factor** loads.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A 3-phase, 10 kVA, 400 V, star-connected synchronous generator has an armature resistance of **0.6 $\Omega$ per phase**. The open-circuit and short-circuit characteristics at synchronous speed are given below:

| $I_f$ (A) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OCC: $E_{oc}$ (V/phase) | 80 | 160 | 230 | 280 | 320 | 345 |
| SCC: $I_{sc}$ (A/phase) | 12 | 24 | 36 | 48 | 60 | 72 |

Calculate the synchronous impedance and reactance per phase. Hence, find the full-load regulation at **0.9 p.f. lagging**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain why the EMF method gives pessimistic results. Compare the EMF method with the Potier Triangle (ZPF) method. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** Star-connected, $S = 10$ kVA, $V_L = 400$ V, $R_a = 0.6 \, \Omega$/phase, $\cos\phi = 0.9$ (lagging)

**Step 1: Rated phase values**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V/phase}$$

$$I_a = \frac{10{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} = 14.43 \text{ A/phase}$$

**Step 2: Find $I_f$ for $I_{sc} = I_{a,\text{rated}} = 14.43$ A from SCC**

From the table, the SCC is a **straight line** through the origin. Using the slope:
$$I_{sc} = 6 \cdot I_f \Rightarrow I_f = \frac{14.43}{6} \approx 2.405 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Find $E_{oc}$ from OCC at $I_f = 2.405$ A**

Using linear interpolation between $I_f = 2$ A and $I_f = 4$ A:

At $I_f = 2$ A: $E_{oc} = 80$ V
At $I_f = 4$ A: $E_{oc} = 160$ V

Slope of OCC in this region: $(160 - 80)/(4 - 2) = 40$ V/A

$$E_{oc} \text{ at } I_f = 2.405 \text{ A} \approx 80 + 40 \times (2.405 - 2) = 80 + 16.2 = 96.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Synchronous Impedance**

$$Z_s = \frac{E_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{96.2}{14.43} = 6.67 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Synchronous Reactance**

$$X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2} = \sqrt{6.67^2 - 0.6^2} = \sqrt{44.49 - 0.36} = \sqrt{44.13} \approx 6.64 \, \Omega/\text{phase}$$

**Step 6: Calculate $E_0$ for 0.9 p.f. lagging**

$\cos\phi = 0.9 \Rightarrow \sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.81} = \sqrt{0.19} = 0.4359$

$$V_{ph}\cos\phi = 230.94 \times 0.9 = 207.85 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a R_a = 14.43 \times 0.6 = 8.66 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{ph}\sin\phi = 230.94 \times 0.4359 = 100.66 \text{ V}$$
$$I_a X_s = 14.43 \times 6.64 = 95.82 \text{ V}$$

$$E_0 = \sqrt{(207.85 + 8.66)^2 + (100.66 + 95.82)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{(216.51)^2 + (196.48)^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{46{,}876 + 38{,}604}$$
$$= \sqrt{85{,}480}$$
$$\approx 292.4 \text{ V/phase}$$

**Step 7: Percentage Regulation**

$$\% VR = \frac{292.4 - 230.94}{230.94} \times 100 = \frac{61.46}{230.94} \times 100 \approx 26.61\%$$

> **Answer:** $Z_s \approx 6.67 \, \Omega$, $X_s \approx 6.64 \, \Omega$, $\% VR \approx 26.61\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in This Question:**
> 1. Using **line** voltage instead of **phase** voltage in the $E_0$ formula — KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. Computing $I_f$ at rated voltage on OCC (should be on **SCC** at rated current).
> 3. Forgetting to interpolate $E_{oc}$ correctly — using $E_{oc}$ at $I_f = 2$ A instead of 2.405 A.
> 4. Using $-$ sign for $X_s$ term when load is **lagging**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Why EMF Method Gives Pessimistic Results (3.5 marks):**

The EMF method treats the synchronous reactance $X_s$ as if it represents the **actual voltage drop** in the machine under load. However, in reality:

1. **Armature reaction** in a synchronous machine is **demagnetizing** (for lagging p.f.) and acts on the **rotating field**, not as a leakage reactance drop.
2. The EMF method **assumes the entire armature reaction as a fictitious reactance drop**, ignoring that the **magnitude of armature reaction depends on saturation**.
3. When calculating $Z_s$ from OCC and SCC, the OCC is taken in the **linear (unsaturated) region** for simplicity, but **at rated voltage, the machine is saturated**, meaning the actual $X_s$ is **lower** than calculated.
4. As a result, the calculated $E_0$ is **higher** than the true no-load EMF, leading to an **over-estimated (pessimistic)** voltage regulation.

**Comparison: EMF Method vs Potier Triangle Method (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | EMF Method | Potier Triangle (ZPF) Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Other Name** | Synchronous Impedance Method | Zero Power Factor Method |
| **Data Required** | OCC + SCC | OCC + ZPF Curve |
| **Saturation Considered** | NO (assumes linear) | YES (uses Potier reactance $X_p$ and Potier shift) |
| **Armature Reaction** | Assumed to be a constant reactance | Separated from leakage reactance |
| **Accuracy** | Low (pessimistic) | High (closer to actual) |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Moderately complex |
| **KTU Result Type** | Over-estimates regulation | Closer to actual regulation |
| **Test Required** | OCC + SCC | OCC + ZPF (loads run at zero p.f. for various $I_f$) |
| **Result Validity for Leading p.f.** | Poor | Better |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (Loss: 2–3 marks):** Students often use line voltage $V_L$ directly in the per-phase $E_0$ formula. Always convert $V_L \to V_{ph}$ first (using $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$ for star; $V_{ph} = V_L$ for delta).
>
> 2. **Wrong Sign in $X_s$ Term (Loss: 2–4 marks):** For **lagging** p.f., use **$+$** sign; for **leading** p.f., use **$-$** sign. This single error can give a wildly wrong (and often negative) regulation.
>
> 3. **Confusion Between $R_a$ and $Z_s$ (Loss: 1–2 marks):** $R_a$ is the **DC armature resistance** (measured directly), while $Z_s$ is the **fictitious synchronous impedance** (derived from OCC and SCC). They are NOT the same.
>
> 4. **Misreading OCC/SCC Data (Loss: 1–2 marks):** OCC is a curve (non-linear), SCC is a straight line. Always pick $E_{oc}$ corresponding to the **field current that produces rated short-circuit current on SCC**, not at rated voltage on OCC.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Convert Per-Phase Values to Line (when needed):** If the question asks for line-to-line regulation, multiply the per-phase $E_0$ by $\sqrt{3}$.
>
> 6. **No Units in Final Answer (Loss: 0.5 mark):** Always write $V$ for voltages, $\Omega$ for impedance/resistance/reactance, and **%** for regulation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: Voltage regulation = $(E_0 - V)/V \times 100$% — the rise in terminal voltage from full load to no load at constant speed and field excitation.
- **EMF Method** = Synchronous Impedance Method — proposed by **Rothert**.
- **Tests Required**: **OCC (Open-Circuit Test)** + **SCC (Short-Circuit Test)**.
- **OCC** is plotted as $E_{oc}$ vs. $I_f$ — a **non-linear (saturating) curve**.
- **SCC** is plotted as $I_{sc}$ vs. $I_f$ — a **straight line** (linear region).
- **Synchronous Impedance**: $Z_s = E_{oc} / I_{sc}$ (per phase, at rated $I_f$).
- **Synchronous Reactance**: $X_s = \sqrt{Z_s^2 - R_a^2}$.
- **Standard $E_0$ Formula (Lagging p.f.)**:
$$E_0 = \sqrt{(V\cos\phi + I_a R_a)^2 + (V\sin\phi + I_a X_s)^2}$$
- **Standard $E_0$ Formula (Leading p.f.)**: Replace $+$ with $-$ before the $I_a X_s$ term in the vertical component.
- **Pessimistic Result**: EMF method **over-estimates** voltage regulation because it ignores saturation and treats armature reaction as a fictitious reactance.
- **Sign Convention for Power Factor**:
  - **Lagging** → $+$ sign for $X_s$ term
  - **Leading** → $-$ sign for $X_s$ term
  - **Unity** → $\sin\phi = 0$
- **KTU Convention**: All calculations are **per phase**. Convert $V_L$ to $V_{ph}$ first, then compute.
- **Typical Numerical Ranges**:
  - $R_a$: 0.1 to 1 $\Omega$/phase
  - $X_s$: 1 to 10 $\Omega$/phase
  - $\% VR$ at full load (lagging): typically **10% to 30%** for medium-sized alternators.
- **Why is it called "EMF Method"?** Because the regulation is computed from the EMF values (open-circuit and short-circuit), not from actual loading.
- **KTU Exam Tip**: Always show OCC and SCC diagrams (sketches) and label key points — examiners award **partial credit** for correct visual representation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Angle Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Angle Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Angle Characteristic (δ-Characteristic)** of a synchronous machine is the mathematical/graphical relationship that describes how the real (active) electrical power transferred between the rotor and the stator varies as a function of the **load angle (δ)** — the angular displacement between the internal generated EMF (E) phasor of the rotor and the terminal voltage (V) phasor of the stator.

For a cylindrical-rotor synchronous machine, the fundamental power-angle equation is:

$$P_e = \frac{E \cdot V}{X_s} \sin(\delta)$$

where:
- $P_e$ = Real electrical power transferred (in **Watts** or per-unit on the machine's MVA base)
- $E$ = Internal generated EMF per phase (in **Volts**)
- $V$ = Terminal voltage per phase (in **Volts**)
- $X_s$ = Synchronous reactance per phase (in **Ohms**)
- $\delta$ = Load (power) angle (in **electrical radians** or **degrees**)

For a salient-pole synchronous machine, the two-reaction theory yields a second-harmonic term:

$$P_e = \frac{E \cdot V}{X_d} \sin(\delta) + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin(2\delta)$$

where $X_d$ and $X_q$ are the direct-axis and quadrature-axis synchronous reactances respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tug-of-War of Magnetic Fields"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine two magnets tied together by a stretched spring — one magnet (rotor) tries to drag the other (stator field) along. The spring's stretch is the **load angle δ**. If you pull too hard (large δ), the spring may snap — this is the **steady-state stability limit**. The power-angle curve is essentially the "stretching energy stored in that magnetic spring."

- When δ = **0°** (rotor and stator fields perfectly aligned): No power transfer — the machine floats in a state of magnetic equilibrium.
- When δ increases up to **90°** (for cylindrical rotor): Power transfer rises sinusoidally to its maximum $P_{max} = EV/X_s$.
- Beyond δ = 90°: The magnetic "spring" begins to lose grip, and the machine **loses synchronism**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Synchronicity Benchmark (50 Hz Indian Grid):**
> - Synchronous speed: $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ where $P$ is the number of poles.
> - One mechanical revolution corresponds to $P/2$ electrical cycles, hence $\delta_{electrical} = (P/2) \cdot \delta_{mechanical}$.

The **steady-state stability limit** is universally defined as the point where $\frac{dP}{d\delta} = 0$, which for the cylindrical rotor case gives $\delta = 90°$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of the Power-Angle Relationship

The power-angle characteristic arises directly from the **synchronous machine's phasor diagram** and the electromagnetic torque produced by the interaction of two magnetic fields:

- **Stator Armature Reaction Field** — created by the balanced three-phase stator currents.
- **Rotor Field** — produced by the DC-excited rotor winding (or permanent magnets).

The electromagnetic torque in any rotating machine is given by the incremental change of co-energy with respect to angular displacement. For a synchronous machine, this manifests as the cross-product of the two MMF vectors, projected onto the air-gap permeance.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Reasoning Behind the Equation

1. **Step 1 — Define the phasor geometry:** The internal EMF $\vec{E}$ leads the terminal voltage $\vec{V}$ by the load angle $\delta$ under generator action.
2. **Step 2 — Express the air-gap power:** The real power crossing the air gap equals the product of the quadrature-axis component of armature current and the internal EMF.
3. **Step 3 — Substitute the reactance relation:** The armature current $I_a$ flowing through synchronous reactance $X_s$ produces a voltage drop $I_a X_s$ perpendicular to $I_a$.
4. **Step 4 — Resolve into components:** Splitting $I_a$ into its d-axis and q-axis components, only the q-axis component contributes to real power.
5. **Step 5 — Trigonometric simplification:** Applying $\sin(A)\cos(B) - \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(A-B)$ yields the clean sinusoidal form.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula needed to solve KTU 2024 Scheme university examination problems on Power Angle Characteristics. Note the deliberate use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve table rendering.

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| $P_e = \dfrac{E V}{X_s} \sin(\delta)$ | Cylindrical rotor power-angle equation | Turbo-alternators with uniform air gap |
| $P_{max} = \dfrac{E V}{X_s}$ | Maximum transferable power (cylindrical) | Occurs at $\delta = 90°$ |
| $P_e = \dfrac{E V}{X_d}\sin(\delta) + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin(2\delta)$ | Salient-pole power-angle equation | Hydro-generators with salient poles |
| $P_{max,salient} \approx \dfrac{E V}{X_d} + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{X_q} - \dfrac{1}{X_d}\right)$ | Approximate peak of salient-pole curve | Between $\delta = 65°$ and $75°$ |
| $P_{synchronizing} = \dfrac{dP_e}{d\delta}\bigg\vert_{\delta_0}$ | Synchronizing power coefficient | Used in stability and oscillation studies |
| $\dfrac{dP}{d\delta} = \dfrac{E V}{X_s}\cos(\delta)$ | Slope of power-angle curve (cylindrical) | Determines stiffness of electrical coupling |
| $\delta_{critical} = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{1-\left(\dfrac{P_{applied}}{P_{max}}\right)^{2}}\right)$ | Critical load angle for a given load | Used in pull-out / load-shedding analysis |
| $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed in RPM | Convert between mechanical and electrical angles |
| $T_e = \dfrac{P_e}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{P_e}{2\pi n_s / 60}$ | Electromagnetic torque in Nm | Motor/generator torque calculations |
| $E^2 = V^2 + (I_a X_s)^2 - 2 V I_a X_s \cos(\theta + 90°)$ | Phasor-diagram relation (generator) | For excitation / power-factor calculations |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Stability Studies:** The δ-curve is the cornerstone of transient stability assessment in grid-integrated generators. The **Equal-Area Criterion** uses the area under this curve to determine whether a machine will remain stable after a fault.
- **Governor and AVR Tuning:** The slope $dP/d\delta$ at the operating point dictates the natural frequency of electromechanical oscillations (typically 0.1–2 Hz in large interconnected grids).
- **Synchronizing Procedure:** Before closing the paralleling breaker of an alternator to the infinite bus, the operator checks that $\delta \approx 0°$ and $f_{machine} \approx f_{bus}$ — this corresponds to zero power transfer, ensuring a smooth synchronization.
- **Pull-out Torque of Synchronous Motors:** The maximum torque a synchronous motor can deliver without losing step is directly obtained from the peak of its power-angle curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cylindrical-Rotor Power-Angle Equation

**Starting point — Per-phase phasor diagram (Generator action):**

The internal EMF $\vec{E}$ leads the terminal voltage $\vec{V}$ by the load angle $\delta$. The armature current $\vec{I_a}$ lags $\vec{V}$ by the load power-factor angle $\phi$ (for a lagging load). The synchronous reactance drop $j\vec{I_a}X_s$ is perpendicular to $\vec{I_a}$.

**Step 1:** Express the voltage equation per phase (neglecting armature resistance $R_a \ll X_s$):

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + j X_s \vec{I_a}$$

**Step 2:** Resolve $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{V}$ along the d-q axes. Choose the q-axis along $\vec{V}$, so $\vec{V} = V \angle 0°$. Then $\vec{E} = E \angle \delta$, leading $\vec{V}$ by $\delta$.

**Step 3:** Substitute $\vec{I_a} = (\vec{E} - \vec{V}) / (j X_s)$. The complex power per phase is:

$$S = 3 \vec{V} \vec{I_a}^{\,*}$$

**Step 4:** The real (active) power per phase is $\text{Re}\{S\}$, but for the total three-phase power:

$$P_e = 3 \, \text{Re}\{\vec{V} \vec{I_a}^{\,*}\}$$

**Step 5:** Expanding $\vec{I_a}^{\,*}$ from Step 1:

$$\vec{I_a} = \frac{\vec{E} - \vec{V}}{j X_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{I_a}^{\,*} = \frac{\vec{E}^{\,*} - \vec{V}^{\,*}}{-j X_s}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the complex power becomes:

$$S = 3 \vec{V} \cdot \frac{\vec{E}^{\,*} - \vec{V}^{\,*}}{-j X_s} = \frac{3j}{X_s} \vec{V}\left(\vec{E}^{\,*} - \vec{V}^{\,*}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $\vec{V} = V \angle 0°$ and $\vec{E} = E \angle \delta$:

$$S = \frac{3j}{X_s}\left[V E \angle(-\delta) - V^2\right]$$

**Step 8:** The real part comes from the imaginary cross-term. Using $j e^{-j\delta} = \sin(\delta) + j\cos(\delta)$:

$$\text{Re}\{S\} = \frac{3 V E \sin(\delta)}{X_s}$$

**Step 9:** Hence the three-phase real power:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{3 E V}{X_s} \sin(\delta)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For line-to-line voltage ratings in per-unit form, or for star-connected machines where $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$, the same $EV/X_s$ formulation is used directly with phase quantities, and the factor of 3 converts to total three-phase power.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Salient-Pole Power-Angle Equation

In salient-pole machines, the air-gap permeance is **not uniform**. The d-axis (pole-axis) has a high permeance, while the q-axis (inter-polar axis) has a lower permeance. The armature MMF must therefore be resolved into two independent components:

- $F_d$ along the d-axis: linked to $X_d$ (direct-axis synchronous reactance)
- $F_q$ along the q-axis: linked to $X_q$ (quadrature-axis synchronous reactance)

**Step 1:** Armature current components from the phasor diagram:

$$I_q = \frac{E - V \cos(\delta)}{X_d}$$

$$I_d = \frac{V \sin(\delta)}{X_q}$$

**Step 2:** Real power contributed by each axis component:

$$P_e = 3 \left[ E I_q + (X_d - X_q) I_d I_q \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The second term arises from the **reluctance torque** — additional torque produced purely due to the non-uniformity of the air-gap permeance, even when field excitation is absent.

**Step 3:** Substitute the expressions for $I_d$ and $I_q$:

$$P_e = 3 \left[ E \cdot \frac{E - V \cos(\delta)}{X_d} + (X_d - X_q) \cdot \frac{V \sin(\delta)}{X_q} \cdot \frac{E - V \cos(\delta)}{X_d} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Expand and group terms. After algebraic simplification (which the student is expected to perform in the exam):

$$P_e = \frac{3 E V}{X_d} \sin(\delta) + \frac{3 V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin(2\delta)$$

**Step 5:** Maximum power occurs where $dP_e / d\delta = 0$. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP_e}{d\delta} = \frac{3 E V}{X_d}\cos(\delta) + 3 V^2\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\cos(2\delta) = 0$$

This transcendental equation has no closed-form solution for $\delta$, but for typical machine parameters the maximum occurs in the range $\delta \in [65°, 80°]$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Synchronizing Power Coefficient

The **synchronizing power** is defined as the small-signal change in electrical power per unit change in load angle, evaluated at the steady-state operating point $\delta_0$:

$$P_{syn} = \left. \frac{dP_e}{d\delta} \right|_{\delta = \delta_0}$$

For a cylindrical-rotor machine:

$$P_{syn} = \frac{E V}{X_s} \cos(\delta_0)$$

**Physical significance:** A high $P_{syn}$ means a small perturbation in $\delta$ produces a large restoring power change — the machine is **stiffly coupled** to the grid. A low $P_{syn}$ (operating near $\delta = 90°$) means the system is on the verge of instability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The undamped natural frequency of electromechanical oscillations between a generator and the infinite bus is given by:
>
> $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{\omega_s \, P_{syn}}{2 H}}$$
>
> where $H$ is the per-unit inertia constant (in seconds) and $\omega_s$ is the synchronous angular frequency. This is the basis of **small-signal rotor-angle stability analysis** in modern power systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Rotor Types

| **Parameter** | **Cylindrical Rotor (Turbo-Alternator)** | **Salient-Pole Rotor (Hydro-Generator)** |
|---|---|---|
| Air-gap permeance | Uniform | Non-uniform (high on d-axis, low on q-axis) |
| Synchronous reactance | Single value $X_s$ | Two values: $X_d > X_q$ |
| Power-angle equation | $P = (EV/X_s)\sin(\delta)$ | $P = (EV/X_d)\sin(\delta) + \text{reluctance term}$ |
| Peak power angle | Exactly **90°** | Between **65° and 80°** (machine-dependent) |
| Reluctance power | Zero | Present (helps stability) |
| Typical application | Thermal, nuclear, gas turbo-alternators (2-pole, 3000/3600 RPM) | Hydro-electric generators (multi-pole, low RPM) |
| Construction | Solid forged steel rotor with slots | Laminated pole pieces bolted to spider hub |
| Rotor diameter | Small (high speed, large centrifugal forces) | Large (low speed, shorter axial length) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Power Transfer in a Synchronous Generator

The following block diagram maps the functional signal flow from mechanical input to electrical output, highlighting where the load angle enters the system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Power Input P_mech] --> B[Turbine / Prime Mover]
    B --> C[Rotor Field Excitation DC]
    C --> D[Synchronous Machine Air-Gap Coupling]
    E[Stator Armature Three-Phase Windings] --> D
    D --> F[Power Angle delta Formation]
    F --> G[EMF Vector E leads V by delta]
    G --> H[Power Transfer Pe = EV over Xs times sin delta]
    H --> I[Electrical Output Terminals 3-Phase AC]
    I --> J[External Load / Grid Infinite Bus]
    J --> K[Feedback: Power Factor Angle phi]
    K --> F
```

### 4.2 Synchronizing Sequence Flowchart

This diagram illustrates the standard procedure an operator follows when synchronizing a generator to an infinite bus, which is fundamentally an exercise in bringing the load angle to a safe value before closing the breaker.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1([Start: Generator on Standby]) --> S2[Bring Generator to Rated Voltage via AVR]
    S2 --> S3[Adjust Speed via Governor to match Grid Frequency]
    S3 --> S4{Synchronoscope and Lamp Check}
    S4 -- Phase Sequence Correct --> S5[Adjust Excitation to match Grid Voltage]
    S4 -- Phase Sequence Wrong --> S3
    S5 --> S6{Delta approx 0 degrees?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Fine-tune Speed to drive Delta toward 0]
    S7 --> S6
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Close Paralleling Breaker]
    S8 --> S9[Generator now supplies Pe = EV over Xs times sin delta]
    S9 --> S10([End: Synchronized to Grid])
```

### 4.3 Power-Angle Curve Visualization (Block Schematic of Curve Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region 1: 0 to 90 degrees] -->|Stable Operation| R2[Pe rises sinusoidally]
    R2 --> R3[Maximum Pe at delta equals 90 degrees]
    R3 --> R4[Region 2: 90 to 180 degrees]
    R4 -->|Unstable| R5[Pe decreases, machine pulls out of step]
    R5 --> R6[Loss of Synchronism Protection Trips]
```

### 4.4 Phasor Diagram Representation (Textual Block Layout)

For examination purposes, the power-angle characteristic is best understood alongside the per-phase phasor diagram. The functional relationships in the phasor diagram can be represented as:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Terminal Voltage V along Real Axis] --> B[Internal EMF E leads V by delta]
    A --> C[Armature Current Ia lags V by phi]
    C --> D[jIaXs Drop Perpendicular to Ia]
    D --> E[Vector Sum gives E from V]
    B --> F{Delta is the Load Angle}
    F --> G[Quadrature Component of Ia times E gives Pe]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The phasor-diagram approach used in the block schematic above replaces the need for a hand-drawn diagram in KTU answer scripts. Students are advised to use a similar annotated block representation when they cannot reproduce exact phasor geometry on paper.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
*Define load angle of a synchronous machine. What is its significance in power system stability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **load angle δ** of a synchronous machine is the angle by which the internal generated EMF phasor $\vec{E}$ leads the terminal voltage phasor $\vec{V}$ in the steady state. For a generator operating in isolation, δ is measured with respect to an arbitrary reference; for a machine connected to an infinite bus, δ is the angle between the machine's rotor axis and a synchronously rotating reference frame tied to the grid. **Significance in stability:** The active power transferred is $P_e = (EV/X_s)\sin(\delta)$, so δ directly determines the operating point on the power-angle curve. The slope $dP/d\delta = (EV/X_s)\cos(\delta)$ at the operating point defines the synchronizing power — a key indicator of the machine's ability to remain in synchronism following a disturbance. Operating too close to δ = 90° (the steady-state stability limit) risks loss of synchronism.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*Why is the power-angle characteristic of a salient-pole machine non-sinusoidal? Mention the additional term that appears.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The power-angle characteristic of a salient-pole machine is non-sinusoidal because the air-gap **permeance is non-uniform** — the d-axis (along the pole) has high permeance while the q-axis (inter-polar) has lower permeance. As a result, the armature reaction must be split into two components ($I_d$ and $I_q$) that see **different reactances** ($X_d$ and $X_q$). The additional term that appears is the **reluctance power term** $\frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin(2\delta)$, which is a second-harmonic sinusoid representing torque produced by the rotor's tendency to align with the minimum-reluctance (high-permeance) axis. The complete equation is $P = (EV/X_d)\sin(\delta) + (V^2/2)(1/X_q - 1/X_d)\sin(2\delta)$.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget the **factor of 3** when converting per-phase power to three-phase power. The equation $P = (EV/X)\sin(\delta)$ is for three-phase total; the per-phase version is $P_{ph} = (EV/X)\sin(\delta)$ but the more common form is the three-phase form. **Always verify the problem statement's units** (per-phase or line values).
> - In salient-pole problems, students often drop the **reluctance term** entirely, losing up to **3 marks**. Memorize both terms.
> - When asked to find "maximum power," do not blindly state δ = 90° for salient-pole machines — the actual peak is **less than 90°** and is found by setting $dP/d\delta = 0$ numerically.
> - The angle δ is in **electrical radians/degrees**, not mechanical. For a P-pole machine, $\delta_{electrical} = (P/2) \cdot \delta_{mechanical}$.

---

**Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**
**(a)** Derive the power-angle characteristic of a cylindrical-rotor synchronous machine from the per-phase phasor diagram. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 2-pole, star-connected cylindrical-rotor synchronous generator has a synchronous reactance of 8 Ω per phase. It delivers a load of 1200 kW at 6.6 kV (line) at 0.8 power factor lagging. Find the load angle δ and the internal EMF per phase. Neglect armature resistance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The starting per-phase voltage equation for a synchronous generator (neglecting $R_a$) is:

$$\vec{E} = \vec{V} + j X_s \vec{I_a}$$

The complex power per phase is $S_{ph} = \vec{V}\vec{I_a}^{\,*}$. Multiplying both sides of the voltage equation by $\vec{I_a}^{\,*}$:

$$\vec{E}\vec{I_a}^{\,*} = \vec{V}\vec{I_a}^{\,*} + j X_s I_a^2$$

Taking the real part (real power per phase):

$$P_{ph} = \text{Re}\{\vec{V}\vec{I_a}^{\,*}\} = E I_a \cos(\theta) - X_s I_a^2 \sin(\phi)$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{I_a}$. From the phasor diagram, the armature current is:

$$\vec{I_a} = \frac{\vec{E} - \vec{V}}{j X_s}$$

Substituting and expanding with $\vec{V} = V\angle 0°$ and $\vec{E} = E\angle \delta$:

$$\vec{I_a} = \frac{E\angle \delta - V\angle 0°}{j X_s}$$

$$\vec{I_a}^{\,*} = \frac{E\angle(-\delta) - V\angle 0°}{-j X_s} = \frac{j}{X_s}\left(E\angle(-\delta) - V\right)$$

Therefore:

$$S_{ph} = \vec{V}\vec{I_a}^{\,*} = V \cdot \frac{j}{X_s}\left(E\angle(-\delta) - V\right) = \frac{j V E}{X_s}\angle(-\delta) - \frac{j V^2}{X_s}$$

The real part of $S_{ph}$ comes from expanding $j e^{-j\delta} = \sin(\delta) - j\cos(\delta)$:

$$P_{ph} = \frac{V E \sin(\delta)}{X_s}$$

For three phases:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{3 E V}{X_s} \sin(\delta)}$$

**Mark distribution:** Phasor equation (2), complex power setup (2), algebraic expansion (2), final boxed result (1).

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert line values to per-phase values:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total power and per-phase current:**

Total three-phase power $P = 1200$ kW at 0.8 p.f. lagging.

$$I_a = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \, V_L \, \cos(\phi)} = \frac{1200 \times 10^3}{\sqrt{3} \times 6600 \times 0.8} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{9145.4} = 131.22 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the synchronous reactance drop:**

$$I_a X_s = 131.22 \times 8 = 1049.76 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Step 4 — Use the phasor diagram relation to find δ:**

The horizontal (real-axis) component: $E \cos(\delta) = V_{ph} + I_a X_s \sin(\phi)$ (since the jX_s drop is perpendicular to $I_a$, its real projection aligns with $V$ for a lagging load).

$$\sin(\phi) = \sin(\cos^{-1}(0.8)) = 0.6$$

$$E \cos(\delta) = 3810.5 + 1049.76 \times 0.6 = 3810.5 + 629.86 = 4440.36 \text{ V}$$

The vertical (quadrature-axis) component: $E \sin(\delta) = I_a X_s \cos(\phi)$:

$$E \sin(\delta) = 1049.76 \times 0.8 = 839.81 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Compute δ and E:**

$$\tan(\delta) = \frac{E \sin(\delta)}{E \cos(\delta)} = \frac{839.81}{4440.36} = 0.1891$$

$$\delta = \tan^{-1}(0.1891) = 10.72° \text{ (electrical)}$$

$$E = \sqrt{(4440.36)^2 + (839.81)^2} = \sqrt{19{,}716{,}800 + 705{,}361} = \sqrt{20{,}422{,}161} \approx 4519.1 \text{ V per phase}$$

**Verification using the power-angle formula:**

$$P_e = \frac{3 E V}{X_s} \sin(\delta) = \frac{3 \times 4519.1 \times 3810.5}{8} \sin(10.72°) = \frac{51{,}658{,}720}{8} \times 0.1860 = 6{,}457{,}340 \times 0.1860 \approx 1{,}201{,}065 \text{ W}$$

This matches the given load of 1,200,000 W within rounding tolerance. ✓

**Final Answers:** $\delta \approx 10.72°$ and $E \approx 4519 \text{ V/phase}$

---

**Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)**
**(a)** With the help of two-reaction theory, derive the power-angle equation of a salient-pole synchronous machine. Explain the origin of the reluctance power term. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 3-phase salient-pole synchronous generator has $X_d = 0.9$ pu and $X_q = 0.6$ pu on its own base. It operates at terminal voltage $V = 1.0$ pu and internal EMF $E = 1.4$ pu. Compute the maximum power the machine can deliver and the corresponding load angle. Use per-unit values directly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

In a salient-pole machine, the air-gap reluctance varies with rotor position. The armature MMF $\vec{F_a}$ is therefore resolved into two orthogonal components along the direct axis (d-axis) and quadrature axis (q-axis) — this is the **two-reaction theory** of Blondel.

$$F_d = F_a \sin(\psi) \quad \text{and} \quad F_q = F_a \cos(\psi)$$

where $\psi$ is the angle between $\vec{F_a}$ and the q-axis. Equivalently, for currents:

$$I_d = I_a \sin(\psi) \quad \text{and} \quad I_q = I_a \cos(\psi)$$

Each component produces a flux that crosses the air gap with different permeance. The d-axis flux links the high-permeance path (through the iron pole), and the q-axis flux crosses the inter-polar air space.

The voltage equations along the d-q axes:

$$E = V \cos(\delta) + I_d X_d \quad \text{(d-axis)}$$

$$0 = V \sin(\delta) - I_q X_q \quad \text{(q-axis)}$$

From the second equation:

$$I_q = \frac{V \sin(\delta)}{X_q}$$

Substituting into the first:

$$I_d = \frac{E - V \cos(\delta)}{X_d}$$

The real power transferred is the dot product of $\vec{E}$ with $\vec{I_a}$, evaluated per phase and multiplied by 3. After working through the algebra (as in Section 3.2), the result is:

$$P_e = \frac{3 E V}{X_d} \sin(\delta) + \frac{3 V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin(2\delta)$$

**Origin of the Reluctance Power Term:** The term $(V^2/2)(1/X_q - 1/X_d)\sin(2\delta)$ arises from the fact that the rotor iron, when displaced from alignment with the stator field, experiences a net torque that pulls it back toward the high-permeance axis. This is the same physical principle as a solenoid plunger being drawn into a coil — it is **reluctance torque** and exists even when $E = 0$ (i.e., with no field excitation). This is why a salient-pole machine can run as a **reluctance motor** with no DC excitation.

**Mark distribution:** Two-reaction concept (2), d-q axis equations (2), algebra leading to final form (2), physical explanation of reluctance term (1).

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $V = 1.0$ pu, $E = 1.4$ pu, $X_d = 0.9$ pu, $X_q = 0.6$ pu.

**Step 1 — Write the power-angle equation in per-unit form:**

$$P_e = \frac{E V}{X_d}\sin(\delta) + \frac{V^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{X_q} - \frac{1}{X_d}\right)\sin(2\delta)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$P_e = \frac{1.4 \times 1.0}{0.9}\sin(\delta) + \frac{(1.0)^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{0.6} - \frac{1}{0.9}\right)\sin(2\delta)$$

$$P_e = 1.5556 \sin(\delta) + 0.5 \times (1.6667 - 1.1111)\sin(2\delta)$$

$$P_e = 1.5556 \sin(\delta) + 0.2778 \sin(2\delta)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero to find maximum:**

$$\frac{dP_e}{d\delta} = 1.5556 \cos(\delta) + 0.5556 \cos(2\delta) = 0$$

Using $\cos(2\delta) = 2\cos^2(\delta) - 1$:

$$1.5556 \cos(\delta) + 0.5556 (2\cos^2(\delta) - 1) = 0$$

$$1.1112 \cos^2(\delta) + 1.5556 \cos(\delta) - 0.5556 = 0$$

Let $u = \cos(\delta)$:

$$1.1112 u^2 + 1.5556 u - 0.5556 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic:**

$$u = \frac{-1.5556 \pm \sqrt{(1.5556)^2 + 4 \times 1.1112 \times 0.5556}}{2 \times 1.1112}$$

$$u = \frac{-1.5556 \pm \sqrt{2.4199 + 2.4692}}{2.2224} = \frac{-1.5556 \pm \sqrt{4.8891}}{2.2224}$$

$$u = \frac{-1.5556 \pm 2.2112}{2.2224}$$

Taking the positive root (since $\cos(\delta)$ must be in $[-1, 1]$ for real $\delta$):

$$u = \frac{-1.5556 + 2.2112}{2.2224} = \frac{0.6556}{2.2224} = 0.2950$$

**Step 5 — Compute δ and P_max:**

$$\delta_{max} = \cos^{-1}(0.2950) = 72.84°$$

$$P_{max} = 1.5556 \sin(72.84°) + 0.2778 \sin(2 \times 72.84°)$$

$$P_{max} = 1.5556 \times 0.9554 + 0.2778 \times \sin(145.68°)$$

$$P_{max} = 1.4862 + 0.2778 \times 0.5661 = 1.4862 + 0.1572 = 1.6434 \text{ pu}$$

**Final Answers:** $P_{max} \approx 1.64$ pu and $\delta_{max} \approx 72.8°$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that $\delta_{max} = 72.8°$ is **less than 90°**, confirming the theoretical expectation for salient-pole machines. Also, the maximum power (1.64 pu) is **greater** than what a cylindrical rotor with the same $X_d$ would deliver ($EV/X_d = 1.556$ pu) — this is the contribution of the reluctance term.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Power Angle Characteristics:**

- **Definition:** Load angle δ is the angular lead of the internal EMF $\vec{E}$ over the terminal voltage $\vec{V}$.
- **Cylindrical Rotor Equation:** $P = (3EV/X_s)\sin(\delta)$ — single sinusoid, peak at exactly 90°.
- **Salient-Pole Equation:** $P = (3EV/X_d)\sin(\delta) + (3V^2/2)(1/X_q - 1/X_d)\sin(2\delta)$ — fundamental + second harmonic, peak at 65°–80°.
- **Reluctance Power:** The $\sin(2\delta)$ term exists only for salient-pole machines; it is the power developed purely by the rotor's tendency to align with the stator field's high-permeance axis.
- **Maximum Power Transfer:** Determined by $dP/d\delta = 0$. For cylindrical rotor, $P_{max} = EV/X_s$. For salient pole, must be solved numerically from a transcendental equation.
- **Steady-State Stability Limit:** The point where $dP/d\delta = 0$. Beyond this point, any further increase in mechanical input torque cannot be balanced by an increase in electrical torque — the machine pulls out of step.
- **Synchronizing Power Coefficient:** $P_{syn} = (dP/d\delta)\vert_{\delta_0}$. A high value indicates a stiff system with good damping of rotor oscillations.
- **Three-Phase vs. Per-Phase:** The standard $P = (EV/X)\sin(\delta)$ form gives three-phase total power. For per-phase power, divide by 3.
- **Unit Conventions:** E and V must be in the same units (V or pu), and X in consistent units (Ω or pu) for the equation to be dimensionally valid.
- **Converting Mechanical to Electrical Angles:** $\delta_{electrical} = (P/2) \cdot \delta_{mechanical}$.
- **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$, where $f$ is in Hz and $P$ is the number of poles.
- **Operating Point Discipline:** Always check the operating point's distance from the stability limit during fault analysis. The closer to 90°, the more vulnerable the machine.
- **Infinite Bus Assumption:** When δ is measured against an infinite bus, the terminal voltage magnitude and frequency are constants; only δ varies with mechanical input.
- **Synchronizing Procedure Recap:** Match voltage → match frequency → match phase sequence → minimize δ → close breaker.
- **Equal-Area Criterion:** Beyond the scope of this module but always pairs with power-angle analysis for transient stability problems in higher semesters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous Motor: Principle, Starting, Hunting, Damper Windings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT417 - ELECTRICAL CIRCUITS AND MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Motor: Principle, Starting, Hunting, Damper Windings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Synchronous Motor** is a doubly-excited AC machine whose rotor rotates at a constant speed (called **Synchronous Speed**, $N_s$) strictly synchronized with the frequency of the stator supply. The rotor is excited by a separate DC source via slip rings (or is of brushless design), and it does not rely on induced rotor currents for steady-state torque production. The electromagnetic torque is developed due to the magnetic locking between the stator's rotating magnetic field and the rotor's DC-excited field poles.

$$N_s = \frac{120\,f}{P} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

where **f** = supply frequency (Hz) and **P** = number of stator poles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of it like a "magnetic handshake":**
> Imagine a crowd of people walking in a perfectly synchronized circle, holding hands (the **stator rotating field**). A person standing still in the center (the **DC-excited rotor**) is **NOT** spinning on his own. He is *pulled* into the circle's rhythm. Once he locks hands with someone walking by, he MUST walk at the exact same speed as the crowd — neither faster nor slower. If the crowd speeds up or slows down, he is forced to change pace instantly. This "locked-in" behavior is the defining feature of a synchronous motor. Unlike an induction motor (which always slips a little), a synchronous motor has **zero slip at steady state**.

### 1.3 Key Identifiers
- The rotor speed is **independent of load** (within pull-out torque limits).
- The machine is **not self-starting** (a critical KTU favorite question).
- It can operate at any power factor (unity, lagging, or leading) by varying DC excitation — making it a useful **synchronous condenser**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 specifically demands an understanding of (1) the principle of operation, (2) the reason for non-self-starting behavior, (3) various starting methods, (4) the hunting phenomenon, and (5) the role of damper windings in suppressing hunting and enabling starting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Magnetic Locking Mechanism
The synchronous motor works on the principle of **magnetic locking (or magnetic coupling) between two fields**:

1. **Stator (Armature)**: A 3-phase winding supplied from a balanced 3-phase AC source produces a **uniform rotating magnetic field** of constant magnitude at synchronous speed $N_s$.
2. **Rotor (Field)**: Energized by a DC source. This creates **stationary N–S poles** of fixed polarity on the rotor.
3. **Interaction**: The rotating stator field drags the stationary rotor poles and pulls them into synchronism. Once synchronized, the rotor field poles are magnetically locked with the rotating stator field, and they continue to rotate together at $N_s$.

### 2.2 Why is a Synchronous Motor NOT Self-Starting?
Let us examine the forces acting on a stationary rotor (at $t = 0$):

- The stator field rotates at $N_s$ (e.g., 1500 rpm for a 4-pole, 50 Hz system).
- In **one half-cycle** of supply, the stator poles reverse polarity (i.e., the N-pole becomes S and vice versa).
- The rotor, being massive and inert, cannot reverse its own polarity (or accelerate) instantaneously.
- Hence, the rotor experiences **alternating torque in both directions** — the average torque over one full cycle is **ZERO**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students wrongly state that a synchronous motor "is not self-starting because it has high inertia." The actual reason is the **high inertia combined with the alternating nature of the stator's rotating field** — the rotor cannot follow the field's rapid alternation.

### 2.3 Starting Methods
To make the rotor approach synchronous speed before locking, the following methods are used:

| S.No | Method | Principle | Application |
|------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Pony Motor / Auxiliary Motor** | A separate small induction motor (or DC motor) mechanically coupled to the shaft drives the rotor up to (or near) $N_s$, after which DC excitation is applied. | Large synchronous motors & generators (used in power stations) |
| 2 | **Damper Winding (Amortisseur) Starting** | Rotor is initially started as an **induction motor** using short-circuited copper bars in pole faces. Once it reaches ~95% of $N_s$, DC excitation locks it into synchronism. | Most common industrial method |
| 3 | **Reduced-Frequency (Variable-Frequency) Starting** | The supply frequency is gradually increased from a low value (e.g., 5 Hz) to the rated 50 Hz. Rotor follows as the rotating field slowly accelerates. | Used with VFDs (Variable Frequency Drives) |
| 4 | **Slip-Ring Induction Motor Coupling** | An induction motor's rotor is mechanically coupled to the synchronous motor's shaft. The induction motor brings the rotor up to near-synchronous speed. | Special industrial drives |

### 2.4 Hunting Phenomenon

> [!NOTE]
> **Hunting** refers to the **oscillation of the rotor about its mean angular position** (i.e., about the synchronous position) when the load suddenly changes or when a periodic disturbance acts on the machine.

**Causes of Hunting:**
- Sudden changes in mechanical load (e.g., a crusher or rolling mill load).
- Faults in the supply system (short circuits, line tripping).
- Sudden change in excitation.
- Periodic pulsating loads (e.g., reciprocating compressors).

**Effects (if left uncontrolled):**
- The rotor may pull out of synchronism (loss of stability).
- Mechanical stress on shaft, bearings, and couplings.
- Abnormal noise and vibration.
- Severe damage to the machine and connected load.

**Mathematical Representation:**
Hunting is described as a **damped torsional oscillation** of the rotor, governed by the differential equation:

$$J\,\frac{d^2\delta}{dt^2} + D\,\frac{d\delta}{dt} + K\,\delta = 0$$

where **J** = moment of inertia, **D** = damping coefficient, **K** = synchronizing torque coefficient, and **δ** = torque angle (load angle).

> If **D = 0**, the oscillation is **undamped** and continues indefinitely.
> If **D > 0** (provided by damper windings), the oscillations decay exponentially.

### 2.5 Damper Windings (Amortisseur Windings)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Damper windings are **short-circuited copper (or brass) bars** embedded in slots cut into the pole faces of the salient-pole rotor. The bars are short-circuited at both ends by end rings, forming a complete squirrel-cage structure (similar to an induction motor cage).

**Dual Role of Damper Windings:**

| Phase | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **During Starting** | Acts as the **secondary of an induction motor**. The rotating stator field cuts these bars, induces EMF, and produces **starting torque** to accelerate the rotor to near $N_s$. |
| **During Running** | When the rotor is at synchronous speed, there is **no relative motion** between rotor and field; hence **no EMF is induced** in damper bars. They are inactive. |
| **During Hunting** | When the rotor oscillates about the synchronous position, there is a **small relative speed** between rotor and stator field. EMF is induced in damper bars, and currents flow that **oppose** the relative motion (Lenz's law). This produces a **damping torque** that kills the oscillation. |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = 120f/P$ | rpm |
| 2 | Synchronous Angular Velocity | $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s / 60 = 2\pi f / (P/2)$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Slip (at start) | $s = 1$ (rotor at standstill) | — |
| 4 | Slip (at synchronism) | $s = 0$ | — |
| 5 | Induced EMF per phase | $E_f = 4.44\,f\,\phi_m\,T_{ph}\,K_w$ | Volts |
| 6 | Synchronous Reactance | $X_s = X_a + X_{ar}$ | Ω/phase |
| 7 | Armature Current | $I_a = (V_t - E_f)/Z_s$ | Amperes |
| 8 | Mechanical Power Developed | $P_m = \sqrt{3}\,V_t\,E_f\,\sin\delta \,/\,X_s$ | Watts (for cylindrical rotor) |
| 9 | Synchronous Torque | $T_e = (3/\omega_s) \cdot V_t E_f \sin\delta / X_s$ | N·m |
| 10 | Damping Torque (in damper bars) | $T_d = K_d \cdot (d\delta/dt)$ | N·m |
| 11 | Frequency of Hunting Oscillation | $f_h = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{K/J}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Pull-out Torque | $T_{max} = (3/\omega_s) \cdot V_t E_f / X_s$ | N·m (occurs at $\delta = 90°$) |

> **KTU Tip:** The last two equations (10 and 11) are commonly asked in derivations. Memorize the **damping torque expression** and the **natural hunting frequency formula**.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Power Factor Correction**: Over-excited synchronous motors run as **synchronous condensers**, supplying reactive power (VAr) to the grid.
- **Constant-Speed Drives**: Used in textile mills, paper mills, and rolling mills where precise speed is required.
- **Power Generation**: The same machine, mechanically driven, acts as a **synchronous generator** (alternator) — the cornerstone of power generation.
- **Industrial Compressors, Pumps, Blowers**: Where constant speed and power factor control are essential.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Speed ($N_s$)

**Statement:** Prove that the synchronous speed of a rotating magnetic field produced by a 3-phase winding is given by $N_s = 120 f / P$.

**Given:**
- Supply frequency = $f$ (Hz)
- Number of stator poles = $P$

**Step 1 — Time period of supply:**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical angle traversed in one time period:**
$$\theta_e = 2\pi \quad \text{radians (electrical)}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical angle corresponding to one electrical revolution:**
$$\theta_m = \frac{2\pi}{(P/2)} = \frac{4\pi}{P} \quad \text{radians (mechanical)}$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical revolutions per second:**
$$\text{rev/s} = \frac{\theta_m}{\theta_e} \cdot f = \frac{4\pi/P}{2\pi} \cdot f = \frac{2f}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to rpm:**
$$N_s = 60 \times \frac{2f}{P} = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{rpm} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mechanical Power Developed (Cylindrical Rotor)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for mechanical power developed per phase in a cylindrical-rotor synchronous motor.

**Step 1 — Phasor relationship (per phase):**

The induced EMF $E_f$ lags the terminal voltage $V_t$ by the load angle $\delta$ (for a motor). The armature current is:

$$I_a = \frac{V_t - E_f \angle -\delta}{j X_s}$$

**Step 2 — Real power input per phase:**
$$P_{in} = V_t I_a \cos\phi$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify** (using the identity $\cos\phi = \cos(\delta - \phi)$):

After algebraic manipulation (using $V_t$, $E_f$, $X_s$, and $\delta$), the **total real mechanical power** developed is:

$$P_m = \frac{3 V_t E_f}{X_s} \sin\delta$$

**Step 4 — Converting to torque (Mechanical form):**

Since $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega_s$:

$$T_e = \frac{P_m}{\omega_s} = \frac{3 V_t E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \sin\delta \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** When $\delta = 90°$, $\sin\delta = 1$, giving **maximum (pull-out) torque**:
> $$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_t E_f}{\omega_s X_s}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Natural Frequency of Hunting (Damped Oscillation)

**Statement:** Derive the natural frequency of the rotor oscillation (hunting) and show how damper windings affect it.

**Step 1 — Equation of motion of the rotor under small disturbance:**

Applying Newton's second law for rotation about the load angle $\delta$:

$$J \frac{d^2\delta}{dt^2} = T_a - T_e$$

where $T_a$ = applied mechanical torque and $T_e$ = electromagnetic torque.

**Step 2 — For small perturbation** $\delta = \delta_0 + \Delta\delta$, linearize:

$$T_e \approx T_{e0} + \left(\frac{dT_e}{d\delta}\right)_{\delta_0} \cdot \Delta\delta = T_a + K \cdot \Delta\delta$$

where $K = \dfrac{dT_e}{d\delta} = \dfrac{3 V_t E_f}{\omega_s X_s} \cos\delta_0$ (synchronizing torque coefficient).

**Step 3 — Equation of motion reduces to:**

$$J \frac{d^2(\Delta\delta)}{dt^2} + D \frac{d(\Delta\delta)}{dt} + K (\Delta\delta) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solving the second-order linear ODE:**

The damped natural frequency is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{J - (D^2/4K)}}$$

For light damping ($D^2 \ll 4KJ$):

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K}{J}} \quad \text{Hz} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The damping coefficient $D$ is supplied almost entirely by the **damper windings**. Without damper windings, $D \approx 0$, and the rotor oscillates perpetually. This derivation is a **frequently-asked 14-mark question**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Damper Windings Provide Starting & Damping Torque

| Regime | Relative Speed $N_s - N_r$ | Frequency Induced in Damper Bars | Function |
|--------|-----------------------------|------------------------------------|----------|
| **At start** ($N_r = 0$) | $N_s$ | $f_s$ (full supply frequency) | Acts as induction motor cage — produces high starting torque. |
| **Approaching synchronism** ($N_r \to N_s$) | Small | Very small slip frequency | Continues to provide acceleration torque. |
| **At synchronism** ($N_r = N_s$) | $0$ | $0$ (no flux cutting) | No EMF, no current — bars are inactive. |
| **During hunting** ($N_r$ oscillates about $N_s$) | Small oscillatory | Slip frequency $\neq 0$ | Induced currents oppose relative motion — **damping torque**. |

### 3.5 Energy & Torque Angle Relationship

The maximum power transfer occurs at $\delta = 90°$. Stable operation is between $0 < \delta < 90°$ (assuming positive synchronizing coefficient $K > 0$). Beyond $\delta = 90°$, the machine **falls out of step (loss of synchronism / pole slipping)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall (Pole Slipping):** Pole slipping is **not** the same as hunting. Pole slipping is a one-time complete loss of synchronism; hunting is a damped/undamped oscillation. Do not confuse them in your answer.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Synchronous Motor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[3-Phase AC Supply] --> B[Stator Winding]
    B --> C[Rotating Magnetic Field at Ns]
    D[DC Excitation Source] --> E[Slip Rings]
    E --> F[Rotor Field Winding]
    F --> G[Stationary Rotor Poles N-S]
    G --> H{Magnetic Locking}
    C --> H
    H --> I[Rotor Rotates at Ns]
    I --> J[Mechanical Load]
    K[Damper Windings on Pole Faces] --> L{Operating Mode}
    L -->|Starting| M[Induction Motor Mode - Provides Starting Torque]
    L -->|Steady State| N[Inactive - No Flux Cutting]
    L -->|Hunting| O[Provides Damping Torque]
    P[Sudden Load Change] --> Q[Hunting Oscillation]
    Q --> K
    K --> R[Oscillation Decays - Stable Operation]
    I --> S[Synchronous Speed Output]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Working Principle (Magnetic Locking)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Stator: 3-phase AC] --> B[Rotating MMF - Bs]
    C[DC Supply via Slip Rings] --> D[Rotor MMF - Br]
    B --> E{Torque Angle Delta}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Electromagnetic Torque Te = K . Bs . Br . sin Delta]
    F --> G[Mechanical Power Output]
    H[Hunting Disturbance] --> I[Delta Oscillates]
    I --> J[Damper Bars Activate]
    J --> K[Damping Torque Opposes Motion]
    K --> L[Delta Settles to Steady Value]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Starting Methods Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Synchronous Motor - Not Self-Starting] --> B[Choose Starting Method]
    B --> C1[Pony Motor Method]
    B --> C2[Damper Winding Method]
    B --> C3[Variable Frequency Method]
    B --> C4[Slip Ring IM Coupling]
    C1 --> D1[Auxiliary Motor Drives Rotor Near Ns]
    D1 --> E1[DC Excitation Applied]
    E1 --> F1[Magnetic Locking - Synchronous Operation]
    C2 --> D2[Damper Bars Induce Starting Torque]
    D2 --> E2[Rotor Reaches 95% of Ns]
    E2 --> F2[DC Excitation Applied]
    F2 --> F1
    C3 --> D3[Supply Frequency Ramped Up Slowly]
    D3 --> E3[Rotor Accelerates with Stator Field]
    E3 --> F1
    C4 --> D4[External IM Drives Rotor]
    D4 --> F1
```

### 4.4 Physical Layout Description (Pole Face with Damper Bars)

For visualization of a **salient-pole rotor** with damper windings:

- The rotor is a laminated steel cylinder with **projecting pole shoes**.
- Each pole shoe has **slots** cut into its surface.
- **Copper (or brass) bars** are inserted into these slots.
- All bars are short-circuited at both ends by **copper end-rings**.
- The structure looks like the **squirrel-cage rotor of an induction motor**, but it is embedded in the pole faces only (not in the entire rotor surface).
- The DC field winding is wound around the pole core, beneath the damper bars.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a synchronous motor not self-starting? What modification is incorporated in the rotor to make it self-starting?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A 3-phase synchronous motor is not self-starting because the rotor is **stationary** at the instant of switching, while the stator's rotating magnetic field moves at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. In one half-cycle, the stator poles reverse polarity, producing alternating torque on the rotor. The rotor, due to its **inertia**, cannot respond to this rapid alternation, and the **average starting torque is zero**.

To make it self-starting, **damper windings (Amortisseur windings)** are embedded in the rotor pole faces. These short-circuited copper bars act as a squirrel cage, causing the rotor to initially start as an **induction motor**. When the rotor speed approaches $N_s$, DC excitation is applied to lock the rotor into synchronism. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is hunting in a synchronous motor? How can it be prevented?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Hunting** is the phenomenon of oscillation of the rotor about its mean angular position (i.e., about the synchronous position) due to a sudden change in load, fault, or disturbance. If sustained, it may cause loss of synchronism, mechanical damage, and abnormal noise.

Hunting is prevented by providing **damper windings (Amortisseur windings)** in the rotor pole faces. When the rotor oscillates, these short-circuited bars experience a changing flux, inducing EMF and currents that **oppose the oscillation** (Lenz's law), providing a **damping torque** that quickly kills the oscillation. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q. (a) With a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a 3-phase synchronous motor. Why is it not self-starting? Describe the damper winding method of starting. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction & Principle (4 marks):**
   - A synchronous motor has a **3-phase stator** (armature) winding and a **DC-excited rotor** (field) winding.
   - When 3-phase AC is applied, the stator produces a **uniform rotating magnetic field** at synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$.
   - The DC-excited rotor has fixed N–S poles. When the stator field rotates, the rotor field poles are pulled into **magnetic locking** with the stator field.
   - Once locked, the rotor rotates at $N_s$ — same speed as the stator field, irrespective of load.

2. **Non-self-starting nature (2 marks):**
   - At start, rotor is at rest. The stator field alternates direction every half-cycle.
   - Due to high rotor inertia, it cannot follow this rapid alternation.
   - Average starting torque = 0 → **not self-starting**.

3. **Damper Winding Method (1 mark):**
   - Copper bars are embedded in the pole faces, short-circuited at the ends.
   - At start, the rotating field induces currents in these bars, producing **induction-motor-type starting torque**.
   - Rotor accelerates to ~95% of $N_s$, then DC excitation is applied → **magnetic locking** → synchronous operation.

---

**Q. (b) Derive the expression for the natural frequency of hunting in a synchronous motor. Show that damper windings provide damping torque proportional to the slip frequency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Equation of motion of rotor (3 marks):**

   For a small disturbance, applying Newton's second law for rotation:

   $$J\frac{d^2\delta}{dt^2} + D\frac{d\delta}{dt} + K\delta = 0$$

   where $J$ = moment of inertia, $D$ = damping coefficient, $K = \dfrac{dT_e}{d\delta}$ = synchronizing torque coefficient.

2. **Natural frequency derivation (2 marks):**

   For the undamped case ($D = 0$):

   $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{J}} \implies f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{K}{J}} \text{ Hz}$$

3. **Damping torque from damper windings (2 marks):**

   During hunting, the rotor oscillates about $N_s$. The relative speed between rotor and stator field is $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_r$. This induces an EMF in the damper bars, and a current flows:

   $$I_d = \frac{E_{induced}}{R_{bar} + jX_{bar}} \approx \frac{E_{induced}}{R_{bar}} \text{ (low slip)}$$

   The damping torque is:

   $$T_d = K_d \cdot I_d^2 \propto \Delta\omega = \frac{d\delta}{dt}$$

   Hence, damper windings provide a **damping torque proportional to slip frequency**, which opposes the relative motion (Lenz's law) and damps out hunting.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q. (a) Explain the phenomenon of hunting in synchronous motors. List its causes and effects. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):**
   Hunting is the **mechanical oscillation of the rotor** about its mean synchronous angular position when the machine is subjected to a sudden change in load or other transient disturbance.

2. **Causes (3 marks):**
   - Sudden application or removal of mechanical load.
   - Faults in the power supply (short circuits, line-to-line faults).
   - Sudden change in excitation current.
   - Periodic pulsating loads (compressors, reciprocating pumps).
   - Malfunction of the governor in a prime mover (when acting as a generator).

3. **Effects (2 marks):**
   - Loss of synchronism if oscillations grow beyond pull-out limit.
   - Mechanical stress on shaft, coupling, and bearings.
   - Severe vibration and abnormal noise.
   - Possible damage to connected equipment.

---

**Q. (b) With a neat diagram, describe the construction and function of damper windings in a synchronous motor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):**
   - Damper windings consist of **copper/brass bars** placed in slots cut into the **pole faces** of the salient-pole rotor.
   - The bars are short-circuited at both ends by **conducting end-rings**, forming a closed squirrel-cage structure.
   - The DC field winding is wound around the pole core, beneath the damper bars.

2. **Function during starting (2 marks):**
   - At standstill, the rotating stator field cuts the damper bars, inducing EMF and current.
   - This produces an **induction-motor-type torque**, accelerating the rotor to near-synchronous speed.

3. **Function during hunting (2 marks):**
   - At synchronism, the rotor and field move together → no flux cutting → no current in damper bars (inactive).
   - During hunting, the rotor oscillates, creating a small relative speed. EMF and currents are induced in the damper bars, producing a **damping torque** that opposes the oscillation and quickly settles the rotor to a stable position.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Synchronous Motor Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "not self-starting" reason**: Writing only "high inertia" is **incomplete**. The correct reason is "high inertia + alternating stator field → average torque = 0".
> 2. **Pole Slipping vs. Hunting**: Do NOT use these terms interchangeably. Pole slipping is **complete loss of synchronism**; hunting is **oscillation about synchronism**.
> 3. **Damper winding at synchronism**: Many students wrongly claim damper windings are "always active". They are **inactive at synchronism** — only during start and hunting.
> 4. **Power factor sign convention**: In synchronous motor convention, $E_f$ lags $V_t$ (i.e., the rotor pole lags the stator pole). Don't draw the phasor the wrong way.
> 5. **Starting method choice**: A direct 14-marker on "starting methods" requires explaining **at least two methods** with a clear comparison table for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P$ — **independent of load** at steady state.
- ✅ Synchronous motor is **not self-starting** due to rotor inertia + alternating stator field.
- ✅ Most common starting method: **Damper winding** (acts as induction motor at start).
- ✅ Other starting methods: Pony motor, Variable frequency, Slip-ring IM coupling.
- ✅ **Damper windings serve dual purpose**: (1) Provide starting torque (2) Suppress hunting.
- ✅ **Hunting** = oscillation of rotor about its mean position. Caused by sudden load change, fault, or excitation change.
- ✅ Hunting frequency: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/J}$.
- ✅ **Damping torque** is proportional to slip frequency: $T_d = K_d \cdot (d\delta/dt)$.
- ✅ Pull-out torque occurs at **δ = 90°**. Beyond this, **pole slipping** occurs.
- ✅ Synchronous motor can operate at **leading, lagging, or unity power factor** by adjusting DC excitation.
- ✅ **Over-excited synchronous motor** = **synchronous condenser** → used for power factor correction.
- ✅ At synchronism, damper bars are **electrically inactive** (no flux cutting, no EMF).
- ✅ KTU frequently asks derivations of: synchronous speed, mechanical power, hunting frequency.
- ✅ **Brass bars** (not copper) are sometimes used in damper windings to limit starting current and torque.
- ✅ Damper windings are also called **Amortisseur windings** (from French *amortir* = to damp).
